Your company has an enterprise application running on Compute Engine that requires high availability and high
performance. The application has been deployed on two instances in two zones in the same region in active-
passive mode. The application writes data to a persistent disk. In the case of a single zone outage, that data should
be immediately made available to the other instance in the other zone. You want to maximize performance while
minimizing downtime and data loss.
What should you do?
A. 1. Attach a persistent SSD disk to the first instance. 2. Create a snapshot every hour. 3. In case of a zone
outage, recreate a persistent SSD disk in the second instance where data is coming from the created snapshot.
B. 1. Create a Cloud Storage bucket. 2. Mount the bucket into the first instance with gcs-fuse. 3. In case of a
zone outage, mount the Cloud Storage bucket to the second instance with gcs-fuse.
C. 1. Attach a regional SSD persistent disk to the first instance. 2. In case of a zone outage, force-attach the disk
to the other instance.
D. 1. Attach a local SSD to the first instance disk. 2. Execute an rsync command every hour where the target is a
persistent SSD disk attached to the second instance. 3. In case of a zone outage, use the second instance.
Answer: C
Explanation:
The correct answer is C, utilizing a regional SSD persistent disk. This approach directly addresses the
requirement for high availability, minimal downtime, and data consistency during a zone outage.
Here's why: Regional persistent disks synchronously replicate data across two zones within a region. This
inherent redundancy ensures that if one zone experiences an outage, the data is immediately available in the
other zone. With a regional persistent disk, you can simply force-attach the disk to the standby instance in the
healthy zone, enabling a near-instantaneous failover without requiring complex data restoration procedures.
This minimizes both downtime and potential data loss compared to other options.
Option A, relying on snapshots, introduces a delay during failover as the disk would need to be recreated from
the snapshot. This would lead to greater downtime and potential data loss depending on snapshot frequency.
Option B, using Cloud Storage, introduces latency because of network access and is not ideal for high-
performance database workloads. Option D, local SSDs coupled with rsync, will not meet the high availability

requirement. In addition, local SSDs are ephemeral and data would be lost if instance terminated. The rsync
approach is also not synchronous and involves a manual failover process that contributes to increased
downtime.
By using a regional persistent disk, the application can achieve rapid failover with no data loss, fulfilling the
need for both high availability and minimal disruption. It is also a low complexity solution that leverages built-
in GCP capabilities.
Authoritative links:
Regional persistent disks: https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/disks#regional_pd
Choosing a storage option: https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/choose-storage-option
Question: 148 CertyIQ
You are designing a Data Warehouse on Google Cloud and want to store sensitive data in BigQuery. Your company
requires you to generate the encryption keys outside of Google Cloud. You need to implement a solution. What
should you do?
A. Generate a new key in Cloud Key Management Service (Cloud KMS). Store all data in Cloud Storage using
the customer-managed key option and select the created key. Set up a Dataflow pipeline to decrypt the data
and to store it in a new BigQuery dataset.
B. Generate a new key in Cloud KMS. Create a dataset in BigQuery using the customer-managed key option and
select the created key.
C. Import a key in Cloud KMS. Store all data in Cloud Storage using the customer-managed key option and
select the created key. Set up a Dataflow pipeline to decrypt the data and to store it in a new BigQuery dataset.
D. Import a key in Cloud KMS. Create a dataset in BigQuery using the customer-supplied key option and select
the created key.
Answer: D
Explanation:
The correct answer is D because it directly addresses the requirement of using externally generated
encryption keys for BigQuery data while adhering to best practices. Options A, B, and C are less suitable due
to various reasons.
Option A utilizes Cloud KMS-generated keys, which contradicts the requirement of external key generation.
Storing data in Cloud Storage first and then decrypting through Dataflow adds unnecessary complexity and
latency. Option B also employs a Cloud KMS-generated key, failing to address the need for imported keys.
While it directly targets BigQuery, the key source is incorrect. Option C correctly identifies the need to import
a key into Cloud KMS but incorrectly suggests a Cloud Storage intermediary and Dataflow processing. This
adds unnecessary steps.
Option D, however, correctly addresses the requirements. Importing an external key into Cloud KMS satisfies
the need for externally generated keys. Using the customer-supplied key option (not "customer-managed" as
in Options A,B,C) directly within BigQuery allows for the seamless application of this key during dataset
creation, which then encrypts the data at rest within BigQuery. This method avoids complex data pipelines and
maintains a direct encryption approach.
In essence, option D utilizes the "customer-supplied encryption key" (CSEK) feature of BigQuery, where the
user imports their own key material into Cloud KMS, retaining control of key generation and storage outside
of Google Cloud. This offers the highest level of control over sensitive data encryption.
Authoritative Links:

BigQuery Customer-Managed Encryption Keys: https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/customer-
managed-encryption - While this page describes the CMK feature (which uses Cloud KMS-generated keys), it
provides context for where the user supplied key option is applicable.
Cloud KMS import key: https://cloud.google.com/kms/docs/importing-keys - This documentation explains
how external keys can be imported into Cloud KMS.
BigQuery Customer-Supplied Encryption Keys: https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/customer-supplied-
encryption - This is the specific documentation outlining the usage of CSEK in BigQuery.
Question: 149 CertyIQ
Your organization has stored sensitive data in a Cloud Storage bucket. For regulatory reasons, your company must
be able to rotate the encryption key used to encrypt the data in the bucket. The data will be processed in Dataproc.
You want to follow Google-recommended practices for security. What should you do?
A. Create a key with Cloud Key Management Service (KMS). Encrypt the data using the encrypt method of
Cloud KMS.
B. Create a key with Cloud Key Management Service (KMS). Set the encryption key on the bucket to the Cloud
KMS key.
C. Generate a GPG key pair. Encrypt the data using the GPG key. Upload the encrypted data to the bucket.
D. Generate an AES-256 encryption key. Encrypt the data in the bucket using the customer-supplied encryption
keys feature.
Answer: B
Explanation:
The correct answer is B. This approach leverages Cloud KMS, Google's managed key management service, to
control the encryption keys used for Cloud Storage buckets. Setting the encryption key on the bucket to a
Cloud KMS key ensures that data within the bucket is encrypted using the specified key. Crucially, Cloud KMS
facilitates key rotation, a key requirement of the prompt, enabling compliance with regulatory requirements
by regularly changing encryption keys, bolstering security, and reducing the impact of potential key
compromises. This is achieved through the built-in rotation capabilities of KMS where new key versions can be
created, and older versions are no longer used, while maintaining access to older encrypted data. This
automated rotation simplifies compliance and maintenance significantly. This approach also adheres to
Google's recommended security practices, using managed service to handle sensitive key management
instead of manually handling keys. It is a server-side encryption approach, meaning the data is encrypted by
the cloud service, not by the client. Option A is incorrect because it manually encrypts data using Cloud KMS
before upload. This approach adds complexity and is not how Cloud Storage's server-side encryption with
KMS is intended to be used. Options C and D involve creating and managing the encryption keys outside of
Google Cloud, losing the advantages of managed services and increasing operational complexity. They don't
readily provide easy key rotation as KMS does. Moreover, Cloud Storage has native integration with KMS.
Supporting Concepts and Links:
Cloud Key Management Service (KMS): A managed service for creating, managing, and rotating
cryptographic keys. https://cloud.google.com/kms/docs
Cloud Storage Encryption: Details on using customer-managed encryption keys (CMEK) with Cloud Storage,
including integration with Cloud KMS. https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/encryption/customer-managed-
keys
Key Rotation: Importance and best practices of rotating encryption keys for enhanced security and
compliance. https://cloud.google.com/kms/docs/key-rotation
Server-Side Encryption: How Google manages data at rest encryption.
https://cloud.google.com/security/encryption-at-rest

Question: 150 CertyIQ
Your team needs to create a Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) cluster to host a newly built application that
requires access to third-party services on the internet.
Your company does not allow any Compute Engine instance to have a public IP address on Google Cloud. You need
to create a deployment strategy that adheres to these guidelines. What should you do?
A. Configure the GKE cluster as a private cluster, and configure Cloud NAT Gateway for the cluster subnet.
B. Configure the GKE cluster as a private cluster. Configure Private Google Access on the Virtual Private Cloud
(VPC).
C. Configure the GKE cluster as a route-based cluster. Configure Private Google Access on the Virtual Private
Cloud (VPC).
D. Create a Compute Engine instance, and install a NAT Proxy on the instance. Configure all workloads on GKE
to pass through this proxy to access third-party services on the Internet.
Answer: A
Explanation:
The correct answer is A. Configure the GKE cluster as a private cluster, and configure Cloud NAT Gateway
for the cluster subnet. Here's why:
A private GKE cluster ensures that nodes do not have public IP addresses, fulfilling the requirement that no
Compute Engine instance should have a public IP. This enhances security by limiting exposure to the internet.
However, without public IPs, GKE nodes cannot directly access external services on the internet. This is where
Cloud NAT comes into play. Cloud NAT provides outbound internet connectivity for GKE nodes by performing
Network Address Translation. It allows private instances within a VPC to connect to the internet through a
shared public IP address associated with the NAT Gateway, avoiding direct exposure. This aligns perfectly
with the restriction on public IPs and ensures external service accessibility. Option B, configuring Private
Google Access, enables communication with Google services but does not provide access to third-party
services on the public internet. Option C, using a route-based cluster, doesn't address the public IP restriction,
and Private Google Access has the same limitation as option B. Option D, a NAT proxy, introduces a single
point of failure and is more complex to manage compared to Cloud NAT. Therefore, option A provides the
optimal combination of security and connectivity.
Supporting Links:
Private clusters: https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/how-to/private-clusters
Cloud NAT: https://cloud.google.com/nat/docs/overview
Private Google Access: https://cloud.google.com/vpc/docs/private-access-options#private-google-access
Question: 151 CertyIQ
Your company has a support ticketing solution that uses App Engine Standard. The project that contains the App
Engine application already has a Virtual Private
Cloud (VPC) network fully connected to the company's on-premises environment through a Cloud VPN tunnel. You
want to enable the App Engine application to communicate with a database that is running in the company's on-
premises environment. What should you do?
A. Configure private Google access for on-premises hosts only.
B. Configure private Google access.
C. Configure private services access.
D. Configure serverless VPC access.

Answer: D
Explanation:
The correct answer is D. Configure serverless VPC access. Here's why:
App Engine Standard environments, by default, do not directly communicate with resources within a VPC
network, whether it's hosted in Google Cloud or on-premises connected through a VPN. To enable this
communication, we need a mechanism to bridge the gap between the serverless App Engine environment and
the private VPC network. Serverless VPC Access, also known as VPC connector, provides this bridge. It allows
serverless environments like App Engine Standard to send requests to internal resources within a VPC
network. Private Google Access, while relevant for accessing Google APIs over private IP addresses, doesn't
address the need for connecting to on-premises resources or other private resources in your VPC. Private
services access is meant for Google managed services, not your own custom on-premises infrastructure.
Therefore, it is inappropriate in the context of this question. By creating a Serverless VPC Access connector,
you effectively route traffic from your App Engine instance to the on-premises database through the VPC
network and the Cloud VPN tunnel. This allows secure communication and avoids exposing the database to
the public internet. This connector needs to reside in the same region as App Engine application. This way,
App Engine application is able to establish a private connection with the internal on-premises database.
Authoritative Links:
Serverless VPC Access: https://cloud.google.com/vpc/docs/serverless-vpc-access
Choosing a private connectivity option: https://cloud.google.com/vpc/docs/choose-private-connectivity
Question: 152 CertyIQ
Your company is planning to upload several important files to Cloud Storage. After the upload is completed, they
want to verify that the uploaded content is identical to what they have on-premises. You want to minimize the cost
and effort of performing this check. What should you do?
A. 1. Use Linux shasum to compute a digest of files you want to upload. 2. Use gsutil -m to upload all the files to
Cloud Storage. 3. Use gsutil cp to download the uploaded files. 4. Use Linux shasum to compute a digest of the
downloaded files. 5. Compare the hashes.
B. 1. Use gsutil -m to upload the files to Cloud Storage. 2. Develop a custom Java application that computes
CRC32C hashes. 3. Use gsutil ls -L gs://[YOUR_BUCKET_NAME] to collect CRC32C hashes of the uploaded
files. 4. Compare the hashes.
C. 1. Use gsutil -m to upload all the files to Cloud Storage. 2. Use gsutil cp to download the uploaded files. 3.
Use Linux diff to compare the content of the files.
D. 1. Use gsutil -m to upload the files to Cloud Storage. 2. Use gsutil hash -c FILE_NAME to generate CRC32C
hashes of all on-premises files. 3. Use gsutil ls -L gs://[YOUR_BUCKET_NAME] to collect CRC32C hashes of the
uploaded files. 4. Compare the hashes.
Answer: D
Explanation:
Option D is the most efficient and cost-effective solution for verifying data integrity after uploading files to
Cloud Storage. It leverages built-in Google Cloud tools and avoids unnecessary data transfer. gsutil -m
enables parallel uploads for faster performance. gsutil hash -c FILE_NAME directly computes CRC32C hashes
of on-premises files, which are also used by Cloud Storage for integrity checks. This eliminates the need for
custom code or extra downloads. gsutil ls -L efficiently retrieves metadata, including CRC32C hashes, of the
uploaded objects directly from Cloud Storage. Comparing pre-computed and Cloud Storage-generated
hashes is a lightweight and highly reliable way to confirm content consistency. Options A and C both involve
downloading the uploaded files again, which is inefficient and generates unnecessary egress charges. Option

A requires an external tool like shasum, while Option D uses the readily available Google Cloud tool. Option B
also requires developing custom code, adding overhead. The simplicity and directness of Option D aligns with
the principle of minimizing operational effort and cost. By directly comparing checksums, the process is both
efficient and accurate for verifying data integrity. This approach avoids computationally expensive methods
and unnecessary data movement.
Relevant links for further research:
gsutil hash command: https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/gsutil/commands/hash
gsutil ls command: https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/gsutil/commands/ls
CRC32C checksum: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cyclic_redundancy_check
Cloud Storage data integrity: https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/data-integrity
Question: 153 CertyIQ
You have deployed an application on Anthos clusters (formerly Anthos GKE). According to the SRE practices at
your company, you need to be alerted if request latency is above a certain threshold for a specified amount of time.
What should you do?
A. Install Anthos Service Mesh on your cluster. Use the Google Cloud Console to define a Service Level
Objective (SLO), and create an alerting policy based on this SLO.
B. Enable the Cloud Trace API on your project, and use Cloud Monitoring Alerts to send an alert based on the
Cloud Trace metrics.
C. Use Cloud Profiler to follow up the request latency. Create a custom metric in Cloud Monitoring based on the
results of Cloud Profiler, and create an Alerting policy in case this metric exceeds the threshold.
D. Configure Anthos Config Management on your cluster, and create a yaml file that defines the SLO and
alerting policy you want to deploy in your cluster.
Answer: A
Explanation:
The correct answer is A. Installing Anthos Service Mesh (ASM) enables detailed observability within your
Anthos cluster, specifically concerning service-to-service communication. ASM captures crucial metrics like
request latency at the service level. These metrics are directly integrated into Google Cloud's monitoring
tools. By using the Cloud Console, you can then define Service Level Objectives (SLOs), which are specific,
measurable targets for service performance. Once an SLO is established for acceptable latency, you can
create an alerting policy based on this SLO. This policy will trigger when the latency exceeds your defined
threshold for a set duration, aligning with your SRE requirements.
Options B, C, and D are not ideal for this scenario. Cloud Trace provides detailed tracing for individual
requests, but it’s not the primary tool for overall latency alerting. While tracing is useful for debugging,
creating alerts based on individual traces is inefficient and lacks the service-level aggregation ASM provides.
Cloud Profiler is for performance analysis and resource consumption, not directly for monitoring latency at a
request level for alerting, thus it is not ideal for this scenario. Although you could potentially generate custom
metrics from profiler data, it is more complex than using ASM's native metrics. Anthos Config Management
manages configuration across clusters, but doesn't handle SLOs or alerting configurations directly in this
way. While one could store configurations, ASM provides direct integration with SLO and alerting
mechanisms. Thus, option A best integrates the required functionality with simplicity.
Here are some authoritative links to explore these concepts further:
Anthos Service Mesh: https://cloud.google.com/anthos/service-mesh
Service Level Objectives (SLOs) in Cloud Monitoring: https://cloud.google.com/monitoring/slo
Cloud Monitoring Alerts: https://cloud.google.com/monitoring/alerts

Cloud Trace: https://cloud.google.com/trace
Cloud Profiler: https://cloud.google.com/profiler
Anthos Config Management: https://cloud.google.com/anthos/config-management
Question: 154 CertyIQ
Your company has a stateless web API that performs scientific calculations. The web API runs on a single Google
Kubernetes Engine (GKE) cluster. The cluster is currently deployed in us-central1. Your company has expanded to
offer your API to customers in Asia. You want to reduce the latency for users in Asia.
What should you do?
A. Create a second GKE cluster in asia-southeast1, and expose both APIs using a Service of type LoadBalancer.
Add the public IPs to the Cloud DNS zone.
B. Use a global HTTP(s) load balancer with Cloud CDN enabled.
C. Create a second GKE cluster in asia-southeast1, and use kubemci to create a global HTTP(s) load balancer.
D. Increase the memory and CPU allocated to the application in the cluster.
Answer: C
Explanation:
The optimal solution is C. Create a second GKE cluster in asia-southeast1, and use kubemci to create a
global HTTP(s) load balancer. Here's why:
The primary goal is to reduce latency for users in Asia. Deploying a second GKE cluster in a region closer to
these users (asia-southeast1) is crucial for this. Option A, while creating a second cluster, suggests a simple
load balancer exposing both IPs via DNS. This approach lacks intelligent traffic routing based on user location
and doesn't offer the global scale needed. Option B is a contender, but directly using a global HTTP(s) load
balancer without a multi-cluster setup means traffic will still be routed back to the single us-central1 cluster,
not effectively minimizing latency for Asian users. Option D addresses resource constraints but does not
address the geographic latency issue.
Option C leverages a multi-cluster deployment along with a global load balancer. Kubernetes Multi-Cluster
Ingress (kubemci) is specifically designed to manage ingress across multiple GKE clusters, creating a single
global HTTP(s) load balancer that distributes traffic intelligently based on proximity. This ensures that users
in Asia are routed to the cluster in asia-southeast1, significantly reducing latency. Kubemci facilitates the
setup and maintenance of this global routing, abstracting the complexity of managing multiple load balancers
across different regions. This approach delivers both high availability and performance improvement based on
geographic location.
In essence, this approach provides both a geo-proximal service and efficient global load balancing.
Further Research:
Kubernetes Multi-Cluster Ingress (kubemci): https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/how-
to/multi-cluster-ingress
Global HTTP(s) Load Balancer: https://cloud.google.com/load-balancing/docs/https
Multi-Cluster Kubernetes Architecture: https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/cluster-
administration/federation/
Question: 155 CertyIQ
You are migrating third-party applications from optimized on-premises virtual machines to Google Cloud. You are
unsure about the optimum CPU and memory options. The applications have a consistent usage pattern across

multiple weeks. You want to optimize resource usage for the lowest cost. What should you do?
A. Create an instance template with the smallest available machine type, and use an image of the third-party
application taken from a current on-premises virtual machine. Create a managed instance group that uses
average CPU utilization to autoscale the number of instances in the group. Modify the average CPU utilization
threshold to optimize the number of instances running.
B. Create an App Engine flexible environment, and deploy the third-party application using a Dockerfile and a
custom runtime. Set CPU and memory options similar to your application's current on-premises virtual machine
in the app.yaml file.
C. Create multiple Compute Engine instances with varying CPU and memory options. Install the Cloud
Monitoring agent, and deploy the third-party application on each of them. Run a load test with high traffic
levels on the application, and use the results to determine the optimal settings.
D. Create a Compute Engine instance with CPU and memory options similar to your application's current on-
premises virtual machine. Install the Cloud Monitoring agent, and deploy the third-party application. Run a load
test with normal traffic levels on the application, and follow the Rightsizing Recommendations in the Cloud
Console.
Answer: D
Explanation:
The correct answer is D. Here's a detailed justification:
Option D provides the most pragmatic and cost-effective approach for optimizing resource usage for a
consistent workload being migrated from on-premises. It starts with a reasonable approximation of the on-
premises configuration, offering a familiar baseline. Installing the Cloud Monitoring agent allows for in-depth
observation of the application's actual resource utilization within Google Cloud. Crucially, leveraging the
Rightsizing Recommendations in the Cloud Console is the key optimization step. These recommendations,
based on actual resource consumption patterns, suggest the most efficient CPU and memory configurations,
which directly target the goal of achieving the lowest cost. This is a practical and iterative way to fine-tune
resources. Load testing with "normal traffic levels" is also essential to mirror a realistic usage pattern and
prevent over-provisioning based on artificial high loads.
Option A proposes an instance group with auto-scaling, which while beneficial, focuses on dealing with
variable loads rather than right-sizing an instance. It also starts from the smallest configuration and auto-
scales based on average CPU utilization, which might not adequately capture memory usage requirements
and could lead to temporary resource constraints. Option B uses App Engine flexible, which, while suitable for
many applications, might add unnecessary complexity for a straightforward migration from a virtual machine.
Additionally, it requires containerization which could be an unneeded overhead. Option C suggests a brute-
force approach by setting up multiple instances with varying configurations and running load tests, which is
resource-intensive and time-consuming. It lacks the guided approach provided by rightsizing
recommendations, making it a less efficient method of resource optimization. Therefore, the structured
approach of starting from a baseline and iteratively optimizing with Cloud Monitoring's Rightsizing
Recommendations makes D the best answer.
Authoritative links:
Cloud Monitoring Rightsizing Recommendations: https://cloud.google.com/monitoring/alerts/using-
resource-recommendations
Compute Engine Resource Recommendations:
https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/instances/recommendations
Cloud Monitoring Agent: https://cloud.google.com/monitoring/agent
Question: 156 CertyIQ
Your company has a Google Cloud project that uses BigQuery for data warehousing. They have a VPN tunnel

between the on-premises environment and Google
Cloud that is configured with Cloud VPN. The security team wants to avoid data exfiltration by malicious insiders,
compromised code, and accidental oversharing.
What should they do?
A. Configure Private Google Access for on-premises only.
B. Perform the following tasks: 1. Create a service account. 2. Give the BigQuery JobUser role and Storage
Reader role to the service account. 3. Remove all other IAM access from the project.
C. Configure VPC Service Controls and configure Private Google Access.
D. Configure Private Google Access.
Answer: C
Explanation:
The correct answer is C. Configure VPC Service Controls and configure Private Google Access. Here's why:
VPC Service Controls (VPC SC) is a powerful security feature that establishes a security perimeter around
Google Cloud resources, including BigQuery. It prevents data exfiltration by restricting access to services
based on network and identity. By configuring VPC SC, you can limit how data can be accessed, including
from authorized Google Cloud projects, limiting access even if IAM permissions were compromised. This
protects against data loss via malicious insider attacks or oversharing. https://cloud.google.com/vpc-service-
controls/docs/overview
Private Google Access allows on-premises systems to access Google Cloud APIs through private IP
addresses, bypassing the public internet. Combined with VPC SC, this ensures that access to BigQuery is
restricted to the intended private network. It prevents data access from untrusted networks.
https://cloud.google.com/vpc/docs/private-access-options
Option A, configuring Private Google Access alone, only secures the path to Google Cloud services, not the
data itself. It doesn’t prevent a compromised account within the private network from exporting data.
Option B, while employing a service account with restricted permissions, does not mitigate the risk of data
exfiltration. A compromised service account, regardless of permissions granted, could still move data from
BigQuery if not constrained by a perimeter. Removing all other IAM access from the project could disrupt
legitimate business operations.
Option D is similar to option A and is also insufficient by itself. Private Google Access only provides private
network connectivity; it does not establish a perimeter around data.
Therefore, option C provides the most comprehensive solution by restricting data access and egress to only
intended and permitted users and networks, which is the most secure approach. This combination protects
against accidental and malicious data leaks.
Question: 157 CertyIQ
You are working at an institution that processes medical data. You are migrating several workloads onto Google
Cloud. Company policies require all workloads to run on physically separated hardware, and workloads from
different clients must also be separated. You created a sole-tenant node group and added a node for each client.
You need to deploy the workloads on these dedicated hosts. What should you do?
A. Add the node group name as a network tag when creating Compute Engine instances in order to host each
workload on the correct node group.
B. Add the node name as a network tag when creating Compute Engine instances in order to host each
workload on the correct node.
C. Use node affinity labels based on the node group name when creating Compute Engine instances in order to

host each workload on the correct node group.
D. Use node affinity labels based on the node name when creating Compute Engine instances in order to host
each workload on the correct node.
Answer: D
Explanation:
The correct answer is D because it leverages the power of node affinity within Google Compute Engine to
ensure workloads are deployed on specific physical hosts (nodes) within a sole-tenant node group. Sole-
tenant nodes are designed to provide physical separation for security and compliance. Options A and B, which
propose using network tags, are incorrect because network tags are for identifying instances within a network
and don't affect host placement. Option C, while utilizing labels, incorrectly suggests using the node group
name. The goal is to control placement on specific nodes, not node groups. Sole-tenant nodes are organized
within node groups, and each client's workload must be on a specific node. Node affinity labels allow you to
define rules that dictate where an instance should run based on the metadata of the node. By labeling each
node with a unique identifier (e.g., corresponding to a client or workload type) and then using those same
labels in node affinity rules during instance creation, you can ensure instances are placed on their desired
dedicated hosts. This approach adheres to the policy of physical separation, and using node names rather
than node group names guarantees that workloads from different clients are isolated on distinct physical
hardware. It's a fine-grained control mechanism ensuring full control of instance placement on dedicated
hardware, therefore, it satisfies the requirements of the scenario.
For further research, consult the following Google Cloud documentation:
Sole-tenancy: https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/nodes/sole-tenant-nodes
Node Affinity: https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/instances/configuring-min-cpu-
platform#node_affinity
Creating Instances with Sole Tenancy: https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/instances/create-sole-
tenant-instances
Question: 158 CertyIQ
Your company's test suite is a custom C++ application that runs tests throughout each day on Linux virtual
machines. The full test suite takes several hours to complete, running on a limited number of on-premises servers
reserved for testing. Your company wants to move the testing infrastructure to the cloud, to reduce the amount of
time it takes to fully test a change to the system, while changing the tests as little as possible.
Which cloud infrastructure should you recommend?
A. Google Compute Engine unmanaged instance groups and Network Load Balancer
B. Google Compute Engine managed instance groups with auto-scaling
C. Google Cloud Dataproc to run Apache Hadoop jobs to process each test
D. Google App Engine with Google StackDriver for logging
Answer: B
Explanation:
The optimal solution is B. Google Compute Engine managed instance groups with auto-scaling. Here's why:
Managed instance groups (MIGs) are ideal for this scenario because they provide a way to automatically
manage and scale a collection of identical virtual machine instances. This directly addresses the company's
need to reduce testing time by adding more resources on demand. Auto-scaling, a key feature of MIGs,
dynamically adjusts the number of VM instances based on metrics like CPU utilization or custom metrics
related to the test suite workload. This means the infrastructure automatically scales up during peak testing

hours and scales down when demand is low, optimizing costs. MIGs also offer high availability by
automatically restarting VMs that fail or become unresponsive, ensuring the test suite runs reliably.
Option A, while offering load balancing, lacks the automatic scaling feature crucial for addressing the need to
speed up testing. Option C uses Dataproc for data processing, which is not suitable for running a C++ test
suite. Option D uses App Engine, a platform as a service that is not designed for directly running custom
executables, specifically a C++ application that requires direct access to the VM environment. Therefore,
managed instance groups with auto-scaling (Option B) offer the best balance of flexibility, performance, and
cost-efficiency for migrating the C++ test suite.
Further research:
Google Cloud Managed Instance Groups: https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/instance-groups/
Google Cloud Autoscaling: https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/autoscaling/
Question: 159 CertyIQ
A lead software engineer tells you that his new application design uses websockets and HTTP sessions that are
not distributed across the web servers. You want to help him ensure his application will run properly on Google
Cloud Platform.
What should you do?
A. Help the engineer to convert his websocket code to use HTTP streaming
B. Review the encryption requirements for websocket connections with the security team
C. Meet with the cloud operations team and the engineer to discuss load balancer options
D. Help the engineer redesign the application to use a distributed user session service that does not rely on
websockets and HTTP sessions.
Answer: C
Explanation:
The core issue is the application's reliance on non-distributed websockets and HTTP sessions, which creates a
single point of failure and hinders scalability in a cloud environment like Google Cloud Platform (GCP). Option
C, "Meet with the cloud operations team and the engineer to discuss load balancer options," is the most
appropriate first step. Load balancers in GCP, especially those offering session affinity (also called sticky
sessions), can route requests from the same client to the same backend server, effectively maintaining the
illusion of a persistent session. While not a true distributed solution, it addresses the immediate problem
without requiring a complete application redesign. Options A and D suggest major code changes or
architectural shifts, which are more complex and should be considered later if load balancer session affinity
proves inadequate. Option B, focusing on encryption requirements, is important but doesn't directly solve the
scalability and session management problems.
A discussion involving the cloud operations team is vital because they have expertise in configuring and
managing GCP's load balancing services, enabling them to advise on the optimal load balancer type, session
affinity settings, and other related parameters to suit the application's needs. This collaborative approach
allows for a practical solution that can be implemented relatively quickly, and provides the engineer with an
understanding of how infrastructure can be used to address application design issues.
Supporting Links:
Google Cloud Load Balancing Overview: https://cloud.google.com/load-balancing/docs/load-balancing-
overview
Session Affinity: https://cloud.google.com/load-balancing/docs/session-affinity

Question: 160 CertyIQ
The application reliability team at your company this added a debug feature to their backend service to send all
server events to Google Cloud Storage for eventual analysis. The event records are at least 50 KB and at most 15
MB and are expected to peak at 3,000 events per second. You want to minimize data loss.
Which process should you implement?
A. ¢ Append metadata to file body ¢ Compress individual files ¢ Name files with serverName " Timestamp ¢
Create a new bucket if bucket is older than 1 hour and save individual files to the new bucket. Otherwise, save
files to existing bucket.
B. ¢ Batch every 10,000 events with a single manifest file for metadata ¢ Compress event files and manifest file
into a single archive file ¢ Name files using serverName " EventSequence ¢ Create a new bucket if bucket is
older than 1 day and save the single archive file to the new bucket. Otherwise, save the single archive file to
existing bucket.
C. ¢ Compress individual files ¢ Name files with serverName " EventSequence ¢ Save files to one bucket ¢ Set
custom metadata headers for each object after saving
D. ¢ Append metadata to file body ¢ Compress individual files ¢ Name files with a random prefix pattern ¢ Save
files to one bucket
Answer: D
Explanation:
The correct answer is D. Here's why:
Option D prioritizes minimizing data loss and efficient handling of the event stream. Appending metadata
directly to the file body ensures that crucial information about the event is always tied to the event data itself,
avoiding potential inconsistencies or loss if metadata is handled separately. Compressing individual files
reduces storage costs and potentially speeds up the upload process to Cloud Storage. Using a random prefix
pattern for filenames helps distribute writes across the Cloud Storage infrastructure, preventing hot spots
and potential performance bottlenecks, crucial with a high write volume of 3000 events per second. Saving all
files to a single bucket simplifies management and avoids unnecessary complexity with bucket creation and
rotation.
Options A, B, and C have drawbacks. Option A’s bucket rotation every hour is excessive and adds unnecessary
overhead. Option B’s batching introduces potential latency and complexity, also, relying on a single archive
increases the risk of data loss in case of failure. While option C uses compression and metadata, it lacks the
crucial element of filename randomization to evenly distribute writes, thus increasing the risk of performance
issues. The use of custom metadata headers, suggested in C, would not resolve the issue of performance and
could be harder to maintain as metadata. Cloud Storage’s automatic metadata mechanism would make
custom headers redundant. In summary, option D is the optimal solution because it is designed for reliability,
performance, and simple management.
Further research:
Google Cloud Storage Performance Guidelines: https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/performance
Cloud Storage Naming Conventions: https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/naming
Google Cloud Storage Metadata: https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/metadata
Question: 161 CertyIQ
A recent audit revealed that a new network was created in your GCP project. In this network, a GCE instance has an
SSH port open to the world. You want to discover this network's origin.
What should you do?
A. Search for Create VM entry in the Stackdriver alerting console

B. Navigate to the Activity page in the Home section. Set category to Data Access and search for Create VM
entry
C. In the Logging section of the console, specify GCE Network as the logging section. Search for the Create
Insert entry
D. Connect to the GCE instance using project SSH keys. Identify previous logins in system logs, and match
these with the project owners list
Answer: C
Explanation:
Option C is the most effective approach to identify the origin of the new network and the GCE instance with an
open SSH port. Google Cloud Platform (GCP) logging provides a comprehensive audit trail of actions
performed within the project. Specifically, Cloud Logging captures API calls and events. Creating a network
and GCE instance are API-driven events. By filtering logs for "GCE Network" in the logging section and
searching for a "Create Insert" entry, you can pinpoint the user or service account responsible for creating the
network and consequently the potentially vulnerable GCE instance. This log entry will include metadata such
as timestamp, originating IP address, and user identity, crucial for audit purposes.
Options A, B, and D are less appropriate. Option A focuses on Stackdriver alerting, which triggers based on
configured thresholds and doesn't inherently track the origin of resource creation. Option B's "Activity page"
is designed for data access logging, typically relating to reading or modifying data stored in GCP services, and
not resource creation like virtual networks. Option D attempts to trace access via SSH logins which is
irrelevant for determining the creation source and involves directly interacting with the instance which might
not be ideal given security concerns. Instead, leveraging Cloud Logging provides a non-intrusive and complete
audit trail. Cloud Logging's audit logs are specifically designed for this type of investigation.
Therefore, option C utilizes the proper tool—Cloud Logging—and filtering capabilities to effectively pinpoint
the creation source of the network.
Supporting Links:
Cloud Logging Overview: https://cloud.google.com/logging/docs/overview
Audit Logs in Cloud Logging: https://cloud.google.com/logging/docs/audit/
GCE Logging: https://cloud.google.com/logging/docs/audit/reference/gce
Question: 162 CertyIQ
You want to make a copy of a production Linux virtual machine in the US-Central region. You want to manage and
replace the copy easily if there are changes on the production virtual machine. You will deploy the copy as a new
instance in a different project in the US-East region.
What steps must you take?
A. Use the Linux dd and netcat commands to copy and stream the root disk contents to a new virtual machine
instance in the US-East region.
B. Create a snapshot of the root disk and select the snapshot as the root disk when you create a new virtual
machine instance in the US-East region.
C. Create an image file from the root disk with Linux dd command, create a new virtual machine instance in the
US-East region
D. Create a snapshot of the root disk, create an image file in Google Cloud Storage from the snapshot, and
create a new virtual machine instance in the US-East region using the image file the root disk.
Answer: D
Explanation:

The correct answer is D. Here's why:
Option D is the most efficient and manageable method for creating a copy of a production VM in a different
project and region while ensuring easy replacement if the source changes.
1. Snapshot: Taking a snapshot of the source VM's root disk creates a point-in-time, consistent backup.
This is crucial for data integrity during the copy process. Snapshots are zonal resources and can't be
directly used in different regions or projects.
2. Image Creation from Snapshot: Creating an image from the snapshot is necessary because images
are global resources. Images are effectively templates for virtual machines, including the operating
system, and installed software. Images also allow efficient storage in Google Cloud Storage (GCS).
This step makes the copied VM's state accessible across regions and projects.
3. New Instance Creation: Finally, creating a new VM instance in the desired US-East region and
different project using the created image ensures the copy is deployed with the same configuration
as the source VM. When the original VM changes, you can easily repeat the process, creating new
snapshots and images. The new instance will have its own persistent disk.
Why other options are incorrect:
A. Using dd and netcat for direct streaming is complex, highly error-prone, and not recommended for
production environments. It also doesn't create a reusable template for quick replacement. It would involve a
live data transfer that is not manageable.
B. While creating a snapshot is a good first step, a snapshot itself cannot be used to create a new instance in a
different region/project. The snapshot must be used to create an image to accomplish this.
C. Using dd is also not recommended for creating the image in a manageable, repeatable, cloud-native way.
You would still need to bring the image into the Google Cloud environment and make it available as an image.
Key Cloud Concepts:
Snapshots: Point-in-time copies of disks. Zonal resources.
Images: Templates for creating virtual machines. Global resources. Stored in GCS.
Regions and Zones: Physical locations where resources are hosted.
Authoritative Links:
Google Cloud Documentation - Creating custom images:
https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/images/create-custom
Google Cloud Documentation - Snapshots: https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/disks/snapshots
Google Cloud Documentation - Image Storage: https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/images
In summary, option D provides the most reliable, scalable, and manageable solution for copying a virtual
machine instance between projects and regions in Google Cloud Platform while preserving the ability to easily
reproduce it.
Question: 163 CertyIQ
Your company runs several databases on a single MySQL instance. They need to take backups of a specific
database at regular intervals. The backup activity needs to complete as quickly as possible and cannot be allowed
to impact disk performance.
How should you configure the storage?
A. Configure a cron job to use the gcloud tool to take regular backups using persistent disk snapshots.

B. Mount a Local SSD volume as the backup location. After the backup is complete, use gsutil to move the
backup to Google Cloud Storage.
C. Use gcsfise to mount a Google Cloud Storage bucket as a volume directly on the instance and write backups
to the mounted location using mysqldump.
D. Mount additional persistent disk volumes onto each virtual machine (VM) instance in a RAID10 array and use
LVM to create snapshots to send to Cloud Storage
Answer: B
Explanation:
The correct answer is B. Mount a Local SSD volume as the backup location. After the backup is complete,
use gsutil to move the backup to Google Cloud Storage.
Here's the justification:
The question emphasizes speed and minimal impact on disk performance during backups. Local SSDs offer
the highest IOPS and lowest latency among storage options on Google Cloud, making them ideal for
temporary, high-performance operations like database backups. By writing backups to the Local SSD, the
primary MySQL instance's persistent disk is minimally impacted. This is because the read operations for
database backup will happen on the MySQL persistent disk and write operations to the Local SSD, eliminating
read/write contention. Once the backup is complete, the data can be transferred to durable Google Cloud
Storage using gsutil, a command-line utility optimized for data transfers.
Option A uses persistent disk snapshots. While persistent disk snapshots are a good option for full-disk
backups, taking frequent snapshots will add I/O load to the disk and consume space on the same disk. Option
C suggests using gcsfuse to directly write to Cloud Storage. While possible, this introduces latency as the
data needs to be transferred over the network, slowing down the backup process. Also, gcsfuse is not
designed for heavy write operations and introduces performance overhead when dealing with numerous and
large files. Option D suggests RAID10 with additional persistent disks and LVM snapshots which introduces
more complexity in configuration, provisioning and management, and adds more I/O impact on the primary
MySQL's persistent disk compared to Local SSD.
In summary, using a Local SSD for a temporary backup area satisfies the need for a fast, low-latency,
temporary storage location while the final backup is still stored on a durable, reliable Cloud Storage location.
Local SSDs provide temporary high-speed storage, which helps the database server not to experience I/O
bottlenecks caused by the backup operation.
Authoritative Links:
Google Cloud Local SSDs: https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/disks/local-ssd
Google Cloud Storage gsutil: https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/gsutil
Google Cloud Persistent Disk Snapshots: https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/disks/snapshots
Google Cloud gcsfuse: https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/gcsfuse
Question: 164 CertyIQ
You are helping the QA team to roll out a new load-testing tool to test the scalability of your primary cloud services
that run on Google Compute Engine with Cloud
Bigtable.
Which three requirements should they include? (Choose three.)
A. Ensure that the load tests validate the performance of Cloud Bigtable
B. Create a separate Google Cloud project to use for the load-testing environment
C. Schedule the load-testing tool to regularly run against the production environment

D. Ensure all third-party systems your services use is capable of handling high load
E. Instrument the production services to record every transaction for replay by the load-testing tool
F. Instrument the load-testing tool and the target services with detailed logging and metrics collection
Answer: ABF
Explanation:
The correct answer is ABF because these options address crucial aspects of effective load testing in a cloud
environment.
A. Ensure that the load tests validate the performance of Cloud Bigtable: This is essential because the
primary services rely on Cloud Bigtable. Load tests must explicitly evaluate its performance under simulated
high loads to identify potential bottlenecks or scaling limitations. Neglecting Bigtable in testing risks
overlooking critical issues.
B. Create a separate Google Cloud project to use for the load-testing environment: Using a dedicated
project ensures isolation from the production environment, preventing accidental disruption. It also allows for
controlled experimentation without affecting real users, promoting safer and more reliable testing. This is a
best practice for cloud resource management. (See: https://cloud.google.com/resource-
manager/docs/creating-managing-projects)
F. Instrument the load-testing tool and the target services with detailed logging and metrics collection:
Comprehensive logging and metrics are vital for understanding system behavior during load tests. This data
provides insights into performance bottlenecks, error rates, and resource utilization, allowing for informed
tuning and optimization. Without sufficient instrumentation, diagnosing issues after or during load tests can
be exceptionally difficult and time consuming. It also aligns with observability principles. (See:
https://cloud.google.com/monitoring/)
Options C, D, and E are not best practices or directly applicable: C is dangerous as it can directly impact
production. D is important but outside the direct scope of initial tool rollout and must be handled in iterative
phases. E is not effective and can generate huge amounts of data, and it's not the correct way to achieve
realistic load simulation.
Question: 165 CertyIQ
Your customer is moving their corporate applications to Google Cloud Platform. The security team wants detailed
visibility of all projects in the organization. You provision the Google Cloud Resource Manager and set up yourself
as the org admin.
What Google Cloud Identity and Access Management (Cloud IAM) roles should you give to the security team?
A. Org viewer, project owner
B. Org viewer, project viewer
C. Org admin, project browser
D. Project owner, network admin
Answer: B
Explanation:
The correct answer is B: Org viewer, project viewer. This choice provides the security team with the necessary
visibility without granting them excessive permissions. An Org Viewer role grants read-only access to the
entire organization, including its structure and policies, which is crucial for the security team to have an
overview of all projects. The Project Viewer role allows the team to see details within each project, such as

resources and configurations, without the ability to modify anything. This combination fulfills the requirement
of detailed visibility across all projects. Option A is incorrect because project ownership provides extensive
control, which is not needed for visibility purposes. Option C is incorrect as Organization admin is a highly
privileged role, far exceeding the necessary permissions for a security team needing only visibility. Option D is
also incorrect because a project owner has modify privileges and a network admin is a niche role, not
necessary for broad visibility. Granting the least privilege necessary principle, a critical aspect of cloud
security, is maintained by selecting option B.
Here are authoritative links to support this explanation:
Understanding roles: https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/understanding-roles - Learn about the purpose and
impact of IAM roles.
Predefined roles: https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/predefined-roles - Explore the specific capabilities of
the Organization Viewer and Project Viewer roles.
Principle of least privilege: https://cloud.google.com/security/best-practices/least-privilege - Understand the
importance of granting only necessary permissions.
Question: 166 CertyIQ
Your company places a high value on being responsive and meeting customer needs quickly. Their primary
business objectives are release speed and agility. You want to reduce the chance of security errors being
accidentally introduced.
Which two actions can you take? (Choose two.)
A. Ensure every code check-in is peer reviewed by a security SME
B. Use source code security analyzers as part of the CI/CD pipeline
C. Ensure you have stubs to unit test all interfaces between components
D. Enable code signing and a trusted binary repository integrated with your CI/CD pipeline
E. Run a vulnerability security scanner as part of your continuous-integration /continuous-delivery (CI/CD)
pipeline
Answer: BE
Explanation:
The correct answer is B and E. The company values release speed and agility while also aiming to reduce
security errors. Option B, using source code security analyzers in the CI/CD pipeline, addresses this by
automatically scanning code for vulnerabilities early in the development process. This prevents errors from
progressing further, aligning with agility as issues are caught sooner. Option E, running vulnerability security
scans in the CI/CD pipeline, ensures that built artifacts are checked for known security weaknesses before
deployment. This automated process helps maintain a balance between rapid release cycles and proactive
security. Option A, requiring peer review by a security SME for every check-in, can be a bottleneck and
doesn't align with the objective of agility. Option C, unit testing interfaces, is essential for software quality but
not specifically geared toward security. Option D, code signing and a trusted repository, adds security but it
does not proactively identify and prevent vulnerabilities during development phase. Instead, it is focused on
verifying the integrity of the deployed binary. Therefore, the automated and proactive approaches of options
B and E, integrated within the CI/CD pipeline, are the most suitable for achieving both rapid delivery and
robust security.
Supporting Links:
CI/CD Pipeline: https://cloud.google.com/devops/capabilities/ci-cd
DevSecOps: https://cloud.google.com/devops/capabilities/devsecops
Static Application Security Testing (SAST): https://owasp.org/www-project-top-ten/ (refer to the guidance

on automated security testing)
Dynamic Application Security Testing (DAST): https://owasp.org/www-project-top-ten/ (refer to the
guidance on automated security testing)
Question: 167 CertyIQ
You want to enable your running Google Kubernetes Engine cluster to scale as demand for your application
changes.
What should you do?
A. Add additional nodes to your Kubernetes Engine cluster using the following command: gcloud container
clusters resize CLUSTER_Name " -size 10
B. Add a tag to the instances in the cluster with the following command: gcloud compute instances add-tags
INSTANCE - -tags enable- autoscaling max-nodes-10
C. Update the existing Kubernetes Engine cluster with the following command: gcloud alpha container clusters
update mycluster - -enable- autoscaling - -min-nodes=1 - -max-nodes=10
D. Create a new Kubernetes Engine cluster with the following command: gcloud alpha container clusters create
mycluster - -enable- autoscaling - -min-nodes=1 - -max-nodes=10 and redeploy your application
Answer: C
Explanation:
The correct answer is C because it directly addresses enabling autoscaling on an existing Kubernetes Engine
cluster, fulfilling the question's requirement. Option A, while resizing the cluster, doesn't enable autoscaling;
it simply changes the static node count. Option B suggests tagging instances, which is insufficient for
enabling autoscaling as it doesn't configure the cluster to react dynamically to workload demands. Option D
proposes creating a new cluster with autoscaling enabled which is unnecessary and disruptive, considering
the question is about an existing running cluster. The gcloud alpha container clusters update command with the
--enable-autoscaling flag, along with specifying minimum and maximum node counts, directly configures the
cluster to automatically adjust the number of nodes based on resource utilization, ensuring the application
scales effectively with varying demands. This leverages Kubernetes' Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA) in
conjunction with the cluster autoscaler, enabling dynamic resource management. Option C is the most
efficient and least disruptive way to achieve the goal.
Further Research:
Google Cloud Documentation on Cluster Autoscaler: https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-
engine/docs/how-to/autoscaling/cluster-autoscaler
Google Cloud Documentation on gcloud container clusters update:
https://cloud.google.com/sdk/gcloud/reference/container/clusters/update
Kubernetes Documentation on Horizontal Pod Autoscaler: https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/run-
application/horizontal-pod-autoscale/
Question: 168 CertyIQ
Your marketing department wants to send out a promotional email campaign. The development team wants to
minimize direct operation management. They project a wide range of possible customer responses, from 100 to
500,000 click-through per day. The link leads to a simple website that explains the promotion and collects user
information and preferences.
Which infrastructure should you recommend? (Choose two.)
A. Use Google App Engine to serve the website and Google Cloud Datastore to store user data.
B. Use a Google Container Engine cluster to serve the website and store data to persistent disk.

C. Use a managed instance group to serve the website and Google Cloud Bigtable to store user data.
D. Use a single Compute Engine virtual machine (VM) to host a web server, backend by Google Cloud SQL.
Answer: AC
Explanation:
Here's a detailed justification for choosing options A and C, and why option B and D are less suitable for the
scenario:
Justification for Option A: Google App Engine and Cloud Datastore
App Engine is a fully managed, serverless platform. This aligns perfectly with the development team's desire
to minimize operational management, as it handles scaling, patching, and infrastructure maintenance
automatically. The provided scenario describes a highly variable load from 100 to 500,000 click-throughs per
day. App Engine's auto-scaling capabilities can seamlessly handle this fluctuating demand. Google Cloud
Datastore is a highly scalable NoSQL database service, ideal for storing user data generated from the
website. Its schemaless nature allows for flexible data models and efficient scaling, which is especially
important given the potentially large and fluctuating number of user interactions. This combination offers a
hands-off experience, allowing the development team to concentrate on application logic rather than
infrastructure.
Justification for Option C: Managed Instance Group and Cloud Bigtable
Managed Instance Groups (MIGs) offer automatic scaling of compute resources based on demand, addressing
the variable load effectively. MIGs distribute the website traffic across multiple virtual machine instances for
higher availability and fault tolerance. Google Cloud Bigtable, a scalable NoSQL database, is an ideal choice
for handling the potentially large volumes of data generated by the website, particularly if there's a need for
fast reads and writes across many user records. It is a good option for scaling reads/writes, and therefore a
good choice. The combination provides a robust solution with good autoscaling capabilities.
Why Option B is Less Suitable: Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) and Persistent Disk
While GKE provides great flexibility, it also requires more operational overhead than App Engine. Managing a
Kubernetes cluster includes tasks like node management, cluster upgrades, and scaling configurations, which
does not align with the requirement for minimizing direct operational management. Persistent disk isn't a
scalable data storage solution like Bigtable, thus limiting the performance and scalability needed for
potentially millions of user interactions per day.
Why Option D is Less Suitable: Single Compute Engine VM and Cloud SQL
A single Compute Engine VM creates a single point of failure, which is undesirable in a production setting that
needs to handle varied loads. The single VM also limits the scale of what can be done with the website. Cloud
SQL is suitable for relational data, but is less ideal for the scale and rapid data writes of 500,000 users in a
single day. Therefore, it is not a preferred database for this specific scenario.
In summary:
Options A and C provide solutions that emphasize minimizing operational management and high scalability,
perfectly aligning with the project requirements. Option B introduces more operational complexity, and Option
D creates a single point of failure, which are both less suitable.
Authoritative Links:
Google App Engine: https://cloud.google.com/appengine
Google Cloud Datastore: https://cloud.google.com/datastore
Google Managed Instance Groups: https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/instance-groups

Google Cloud Bigtable: https://cloud.google.com/bigtable
Question: 169 CertyIQ
Your company just finished a rapid lift and shift to Google Compute Engine for your compute needs. You have
another 9 months to design and deploy a more cloud-native solution. Specifically, you want a system that is no-ops
and auto-scaling.
Which two compute products should you choose? (Choose two.)
A. Compute Engine with containers
B. Google Kubernetes Engine with containers
C. Google App Engine Standard Environment
D. Compute Engine with custom instance types
E. Compute Engine with managed instance groups
Answer: BC
Explanation:
The question focuses on selecting compute products that offer no-ops and auto-scaling capabilities for a
cloud-native solution after a lift-and-shift to Compute Engine.
Option B, Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) with containers, is a strong choice because GKE is a managed
Kubernetes service. Kubernetes automates the deployment, scaling, and management of containerized
applications, significantly reducing operational overhead (no-ops). Its inherent auto-scaling features adjust
resources based on demand, ensuring applications remain performant without manual intervention.
https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine
Option C, Google App Engine Standard Environment, also aligns with the requirements. It is a fully managed,
serverless platform where Google handles infrastructure management, resulting in minimal operational
effort. App Engine automatically scales based on application traffic, ensuring high availability and resource
efficiency without manual configuration. https://cloud.google.com/appengine
Therefore, options B and C fulfill both no-ops and auto-scaling criteria, making them the correct choices.
Option A, Compute Engine with containers, does not inherently offer no-ops. Although containers aid in
application portability, users are still responsible for managing the underlying infrastructure, scaling, and
orchestration.
Option D, Compute Engine with custom instance types, focuses on customization rather than automation and
scaling. It doesn't address the no-ops requirement.
Option E, Compute Engine with managed instance groups (MIGs), provides auto-scaling but does not eliminate
the management overhead associated with the infrastructure, and hence it does not offer the no-ops
experience required in the question.
Question: 170 CertyIQ
One of your primary business objectives is being able to trust the data stored in your application. You want to log
all changes to the application data.
How can you design your logging system to verify authenticity of your logs?
A. Write the log concurrently in the cloud and on premises
B. Use a SQL database and limit who can modify the log table

C. Digitally sign each timestamp and log entry and store the signature
D. Create a JSON dump of each log entry and store it in Google Cloud Storage
Answer: C
Explanation:
The correct answer is C. Digitally sign each timestamp and log entry and store the signature. This approach
provides a verifiable way to ensure the authenticity and integrity of logs. Digital signatures use cryptographic
hashing and asymmetric encryption. When a log entry is generated, its hash is computed, then encrypted with
the private key of the logging system. The signature, along with the original log entry and timestamp, is
stored. This allows anyone with access to the corresponding public key to verify the signature, confirming that
the log wasn't tampered with and originated from the expected source. Options A, B, and D don't offer the
same level of authenticity verification. Concurrent logging (A) only provides redundancy, not proof of origin.
Limiting database access (B) helps control modification but doesn't guarantee data wasn't altered by
authorized users. JSON dumps (D) can be changed without detection. Digital signatures, as in option C, ensure
non-repudiation and protect against both unauthorized tampering and accidental modifications by providing a
cryptographic record that is difficult to forge.
Supporting concepts and authoritative links:
Digital Signatures: For an in-depth understanding of digital signatures, refer to resources explaining Public
Key Infrastructure (PKI) and the process of signing and verifying data.
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Digital_signature
Cryptography: A general understanding of cryptography is helpful, especially hashing functions, asymmetric
encryption, and the role of public and private keys. https://www.cloudflare.com/learning/ssl/what-is-public-
key-cryptography/
Immutable Logging: Understanding the concept of immutability in logging helps understand why digitally
signing is important. https://www.techtarget.com/searchsecurity/definition/immutable-logging
Question: 171 CertyIQ
Your company has a Google Workspace account and Google Cloud Organization. Some developers in the company
have created Google Cloud projects outside of the Google Cloud Organization.
You want to create an Organization structure that allows developers to create projects, but prevents them from
modifying production projects. You want to manage policies for all projects centrally and be able to set more
restrictive policies for production projects.
You want to minimize disruption to users and developers when business needs change in the future. You want to
follow Google-recommended practices. Now should you design the Organization structure?
A. 1. Create a second Google Workspace account and Organization. 2. Grant all developers the Project Creator
IAM role on the new Organization. 3. Move the developer projects into the new Organization. 4. Set the policies
for all projects on both Organizations. 5. Additionally, set the production policies on the original Organization.
B. 1. Create a folder under the Organization resource named Production. 2. Grant all developers the Project
Creator IAM role on the new Organization. 3. Move the developer projects into the new Organization. 4. Set the
policies for all projects on the Organization. 5. Additionally, set the production policies on the Production folder.
C. 1. Create folders under the Organization resource named Development and Production. 2. Grant all
developers the Project Creator IAM role on the Development folder. 3. Move the developer projects into the
Development folder. 4. Set the policies for all projects on the Organization. 5. Additionally, set the production
policies on the Production folder.
D. 1. Designate the Organization for production projects only. 2. Ensure that developers do not have the Project
Creator IAM role on the Organization. 3. Create development projects outside of the Organization using the
developer Google Workspace accounts. 4. Set the policies for all projects on the Organization. 5. Additionally,
set the production policies on the individual production projects.
Answer: C

Explanation:
The correct answer is C because it aligns with Google's best practices for resource hierarchy and policy
management within an organization. Option C proposes creating "Development" and "Production" folders
under the organization, facilitating a clear separation of environments. Granting developers "Project Creator"
on the "Development" folder allows them to create projects in their designated space, while the "Production"
folder can have stricter policies and be restricted to specific users. Applying policies at the Organization level
sets baseline rules for all projects, and more specific policies on the "Production" folder allow for tighter
control over critical resources. This hierarchical approach promotes centralized policy management and
minimal disruption. It allows for easier adjustments in the future. Option A suggests a second workspace and
organization, which is overly complex and unnecessary. Option B is missing an essential 'development' folder
and incorrectly suggests migrating the developer projects to the whole organization. Option D does not align
with Google-recommended practices for resource organization, making management complex and
unscalable. Hierarchically structured folders with distinct IAM roles and policies are crucial for good security
and management. This approach enables granular permissions and promotes the least privilege principle.
Relevant resources:
Resource Hierarchy: https://cloud.google.com/resource-manager/docs/cloud-platform-resource-hierarchy
IAM: https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/overview
Organization policies: https://cloud.google.com/resource-manager/docs/organization-policy/overview
Best practices for enterprise organizations: https://cloud.google.com/architecture/framework/organization
Question: 172 CertyIQ
Your company has an application running on Compute Engine that allows users to play their favorite music. There
are a fixed number of instances. Files are stored in Cloud Storage, and data is streamed directly to users. Users are
reporting that they sometimes need to attempt to play popular songs multiple times before they are successful.
You need to improve the performance of the application. What should you do?
A. 1. Mount the Cloud Storage bucket using gcsfuse on all backend Compute Engine instances. 2. Serve music
files directly from the backend Compute Engine instance.
B. 1. Create a Cloud Filestore NFS volume and attach it to the backend Compute Engine instances. 2. Download
popular songs in Cloud Filestore. 3. Serve music files directly from the backend Compute Engine instance.
C. 1. Copy popular songs into CloudSQL as a blob. 2. Update application code to retrieve data from CloudSQL
when Cloud Storage is overloaded.
D. 1. Create a managed instance group with Compute Engine instances. 2. Create a global load balancer and
configure it with two backends: ג—‹ Managed instance group ג—‹ Cloud Storage bucket 3. Enable Cloud CDN
on the bucket backend.
Answer: D
Explanation:
Option D is the most appropriate solution to improve application performance and address the reported
issues. Here's why:
The core issue is latency and potential overload when accessing popular songs from Cloud Storage directly.
Using a managed instance group (MIG) provides autoscaling capabilities, allowing the application to handle
increased user demand by automatically adding more compute instances. This distributes the load, preventing
any single instance from being overwhelmed. A global load balancer distributes traffic across these
instances, ensuring high availability and even traffic distribution to optimize performance. By setting up the
load balancer with the instance group and Cloud Storage, we can use Cloud CDN. Cloud CDN caches content
closer to the user, significantly reducing latency for popular content. This caching mechanism prevents the
need to repeatedly fetch the same data from Cloud Storage, improving the user experience. The load balancer

is also crucial in ensuring high availability. If there is an instance failure, traffic will be redirected to available
instances. Options A and B require storing the files locally on instances or using an NFS which does not scale
efficiently or offer the caching or load balancing capabilities we need. Option C suggests using CloudSQL for
blob storage which is an unsuitable choice. CloudSQL is a relational database and not designed for handling
large binary objects like music files efficiently or at scale. Therefore, option D provides the most scalable and
performant solution by combining autoscaling, load balancing, and content caching.
Authoritative Links:
Managed Instance Groups (MIGs): https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/instance-groups
Global Load Balancing: https://cloud.google.com/load-balancing/docs/load-balancing-overview
Cloud CDN: https://cloud.google.com/cdn/docs/overview
Question: 173 CertyIQ
The operations team in your company wants to save Cloud VPN log events for one year. You need to configure the
cloud infrastructure to save the logs. What should you do?
A. Set up a filter in Cloud Logging and a Cloud Storage bucket as an export target for the logs you want to
save.
B. Enable the Compute Engine API, and then enable logging on the firewall rules that match the traffic you
want to save.
C. Set up a Cloud Logging Dashboard titled Cloud VPN Logs, and then add a chart that queries for the VPN
metrics over a one-year time period.
D. Set up a filter in Cloud Logging and a topic in Pub/Sub to publish the logs.
Answer: A
Explanation:
The correct answer is A because it directly addresses the requirement of saving Cloud VPN logs for a year
using the appropriate Google Cloud services. Cloud Logging is the central service for managing logs within
GCP. To retain logs beyond the default retention period, they must be exported. Option A proposes a solution
that leverages Cloud Logging filters to select the specific Cloud VPN logs and then exports them to a Cloud
Storage bucket. Cloud Storage is designed for long-term, durable storage, making it suitable for the one-year
retention requirement. Filters allow you to target and export the specific log types you need.
Option B is incorrect because while firewall rules can generate logs, they don't directly capture VPN-specific
traffic, and enabling the Compute Engine API is unnecessary for VPN log management. Option C is flawed
because Cloud Logging Dashboards are used for visualization and monitoring, not for long-term log storage.
It's impossible to extract data from dashboard charts for long-term storage. Furthermore, logs are not stored
within dashboards. Option D is incorrect because while Pub/Sub is valuable for streaming logs for real-time
analysis, it is not designed for long-term storage. It doesn't provide the persistence required for a year's worth
of logs, and you would still need a separate storage mechanism like Cloud Storage for archival. Therefore,
Option A is the most aligned with best practices for long-term log storage in GCP.
Here are links for further research:
Cloud Logging Overview: https://cloud.google.com/logging/docs/overview
Cloud Logging Exports: https://cloud.google.com/logging/docs/export
Cloud Storage Overview: https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/
Cloud VPN Logging: https://cloud.google.com/vpn/docs/how-to/logging

Question: 174 CertyIQ
You are working with a data warehousing team that performs data analysis. The team needs to process data from
external partners, but the data contains personally identifiable information (PII). You need to process and store the
data without storing any of the PIIE data. What should you do?
A. Create a Dataflow pipeline to retrieve the data from the external sources. As part of the pipeline, use the
Cloud Data Loss Prevention (Cloud DLP) API to remove any PII data. Store the result in BigQuery.
B. Create a Dataflow pipeline to retrieve the data from the external sources. As part of the pipeline, store all
non-PII data in BigQuery and store all PII data in a Cloud Storage bucket that has a retention policy set.
C. Ask the external partners to upload all data on Cloud Storage. Configure Bucket Lock for the bucket. Create
a Dataflow pipeline to read the data from the bucket. As part of the pipeline, use the Cloud Data Loss
Prevention (Cloud DLP) API to remove any PII data. Store the result in BigQuery.
D. Ask the external partners to import all data in your BigQuery dataset. Create a dataflow pipeline to copy the
data into a new table. As part of the Dataflow bucket, skip all data in columns that have PII data
Answer: A
Explanation:
The most appropriate solution is A, using Dataflow with Cloud DLP to remove PII before storage in BigQuery.
This approach directly addresses the requirement of processing and storing data without retaining PII.
Dataflow's serverless nature enables efficient, scalable data transformation. Crucially, integrating Cloud DLP
within the pipeline allows for real-time identification and removal (or masking) of PII during the data ingestion
process. By performing PII scrubbing before data lands in BigQuery, you adhere to privacy and compliance
needs. BigQuery is then utilized for its intended purpose – data analysis, ensuring the analysis is performed on
