anonymized data. Option B is incorrect because storing PII data, even with a retention policy, violates the core
requirement of not storing PII. Option C is less efficient as it introduces an unnecessary storage step in Cloud
Storage, especially since only a subset of that data, that with PII already removed is needed. Bucket Lock
further complicates the process without improving the fundamental objective. Option D is not suitable
because it proposes skipping columns with PII instead of removing PII from data, leading to potential loss of
valuable data, and a lack of PII removal.
Here are links for further research:
Cloud Data Loss Prevention (DLP): https://cloud.google.com/dlp/docs
Dataflow: https://cloud.google.com/dataflow/docs
BigQuery: https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs
Dataflow Templates using DLP: https://cloud.google.com/dataflow/docs/guides/templates/provided-
templates#dlp-templates
Question: 175 CertyIQ
You want to allow your operations team to store logs from all the production projects in your Organization, without
including logs from other projects. All of the production projects are contained in a folder. You want to ensure that
all logs for existing and new production projects are captured automatically. What should you do?
A. Create an aggregated export on the Production folder. Set the log sink to be a Cloud Storage bucket in an
operations project.
B. Create an aggregated export on the Organization resource. Set the log sink to be a Cloud Storage bucket in
an operations project.
C. Create log exports in the production projects. Set the log sinks to be a Cloud Storage bucket in an operations
project.
D. Create log exports in the production projects. Set the log sinks to be BigQuery datasets in the production
projects, and grant IAM access to the operations team to run queries on the datasets.

Answer: A
Explanation:
The correct answer is A, creating an aggregated export on the Production folder, and directing logs to a Cloud
Storage bucket in an operations project. This approach best fulfills the requirement of automatically
capturing logs from all current and future production projects within a designated folder.
Here's the breakdown:
Aggregated Exports: Cloud Logging supports aggregated exports at the organization, folder, and project
levels. Aggregated exports at the folder level will capture all logs from all projects within that folder,
including new projects that are created within it.
(https://cloud.google.com/logging/docs/export/aggregated_exports)
Folder-Level Scope: Since the prompt specifies that all production projects reside within a folder, an
aggregated export at this level precisely targets the required scope, excluding logs from other projects.
Automatic Inclusion: Crucially, any new production project created within the designated folder will
automatically be included in the folder's aggregated export, eliminating the need for manual configuration
each time a new project is added.
Centralized Storage: Using a Cloud Storage bucket in a designated operations project provides a centralized,
easily accessible location for the operations team to manage and analyze all production logs.
Avoiding Project-Level Exports (Option C and D): Implementing log exports individually on each project (as
options C and D propose) requires manual configuration for each project and future project additions, which
contradicts the 'automatic' requirement. Also, storing logs in BigQuery within the production projects may not
be desirable from an administrative or access control standpoint, as specified in Option D.
By choosing an aggregated export at the folder level, combined with the central storage, this solution offers
the most streamlined, scalable, and automated log collection strategy for the given scenario.
Question: 176 CertyIQ
Your company has an application that is running on multiple instances of Compute Engine. It generates 1 TB per
day of logs. For compliance reasons, the logs need to be kept for at least two years. The logs need to be available
for active query for 30 days. After that, they just need to be retained for audit purposes. You want to implement a
storage solution that is compliant, minimizes costs, and follows Google-recommended practices. What should you
do?
A. 1. Install a Cloud Logging agent on all instances. 2. Create a sink to export logs into a regional Cloud Storage
bucket. 3. Create an Object Lifecycle rule to move files into a Coldline Cloud Storage bucket after one month. 4.
Configure a retention policy at the bucket level using bucket lock.
B. 1. Write a daily cron job, running on all instances, that uploads logs into a Cloud Storage bucket. 2. Create a
sink to export logs into a regional Cloud Storage bucket. 3. Create an Object Lifecycle rule to move files into a
Coldline Cloud Storage bucket after one month.
C. 1. Install a Cloud Logging agent on all instances. 2. Create a sink to export logs into a partitioned BigQuery
table. 3. Set a time_partitioning_expiration of 30 days.
D. 1. Create a daily cron job, running on all instances, that uploads logs into a partitioned BigQuery table. 2. Set
a time_partitioning_expiration of 30 days.
Answer: A
Explanation:
Option A is the most appropriate solution due to its effective use of Google Cloud's managed services and
cost optimization strategies. Firstly, installing the Cloud Logging agent on each Compute Engine instance
(step 1) is the recommended practice for collecting application logs. These agents automatically forward logs

to Cloud Logging, eliminating the need for custom log management scripts. Secondly, creating a sink to
export these logs to a regional Cloud Storage bucket (step 2) allows for centralized, cost-effective storage of
the logs. Cloud Storage is designed for high availability and durability. Thirdly, utilizing Object Lifecycle
Management (step 3) to move logs to a Coldline Storage bucket after 30 days addresses the cost aspect of
long-term storage. Coldline Storage is significantly cheaper than standard storage, suitable for infrequently
accessed data like older logs. Finally, configuring a retention policy at the bucket level with bucket lock (step
4) fulfills the compliance requirement of retaining logs for at least two years and prevents accidental deletion
of the data.
Option B is suboptimal because it involves manually managing logs with a cron job. This increases operational
overhead and potential for errors compared to the automated approach offered by Cloud Logging. Options C
and D store the data into BigQuery, which is not cost-optimal when the primary requirement after 30 days is
just to retain logs for audit and not for analysis. Option C does not offer any method to adhere to the required
two-year retention and option D involves running cron jobs on all instances which is not an ideal approach
compared to using Cloud Logging agents.
Therefore, option A achieves the requirements of compliance, cost-effectiveness, and adherence to Google-
recommended practices.
Authoritative Links:
Cloud Logging Agent: https://cloud.google.com/logging/docs/agent
Cloud Storage Sinks: https://cloud.google.com/logging/docs/export/configure_sinks
Cloud Storage Object Lifecycle Management: https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/lifecycle
Cloud Storage Retention Policies: https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/bucket-lock
Cloud Storage Storage Classes: https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/storage-classes
Question: 177 CertyIQ
Your company has just recently activated Cloud Identity to manage users. The Google Cloud Organization has been
configured as well. The security team needs to secure projects that will be part of the Organization. They want to
prohibit IAM users outside the domain from gaining permissions from now on. What should they do?
A. Configure an organization policy to restrict identities by domain.
B. Configure an organization policy to block creation of service accounts.
C. Configure Cloud Scheduler to trigger a Cloud Function every hour that removes all users that don't belong to
the Cloud Identity domain from all projects.
D. Create a technical user (e.g., [email protected]), and give it the project owner role at root organization level.
Write a bash script that: ¢ Lists all the IAM rules of all projects within the organization. ¢ Deletes all users that
do not belong to the company domain. Create a Compute Engine instance in a project within the Organization
and configure gcloud to be executed with technical user credentials. Configure a cron job that executes the
bash script every hour.
Answer: A
Explanation:
Option A, configuring an organization policy to restrict identities by domain, is the most effective and
appropriate solution. Organization policies provide centralized control and governance over your Google
Cloud resources. Specifically, the "constraints/iam.allowedPolicyMemberDomains" constraint allows you to
specify which domains are permitted to be granted IAM roles within the organization. By setting this policy,
you can directly prohibit users from outside your Cloud Identity domain from being added to projects,
preventing unauthorized access right from the point of configuration. This approach is proactive, automated,
and doesn't rely on reactive scripts or scheduled jobs. It aligns with the principle of least privilege and
provides consistent enforcement across the entire organization. Option B, blocking service account creation,

is not the required action since the question was specifically about user identities, not service accounts.
Option C and D, while technically feasible, are cumbersome and reactive rather than preventative. They would
incur more operational overhead, be less secure, and are less scalable. Moreover, scripts could encounter race
conditions if users are being added and removed simultaneously. Choosing a policy-based solution leverages
the inherent capabilities of Google Cloud for centralized access control, ensuring compliance and enhancing
security. Organization policies are the recommended way to manage access control at scale.
Further research:
Organization policy constraints
Using constraints
Understanding organization policies
Question: 178 CertyIQ
Your company has an application running on Google Cloud that is collecting data from thousands of physical
devices that are globally distributed. Data is published to Pub/Sub and streamed in real time into an SSD Cloud
Bigtable cluster via a Dataflow pipeline. The operations team informs you that your Cloud
Bigtable cluster has a hotspot, and queries are taking longer than expected. You need to resolve the problem and
prevent it from happening in the future. What should you do?
A. Advise your clients to use HBase APIs instead of NodeJS APIs.
B. Delete records older than 30 days.
C. Review your RowKey strategy and ensure that keys are evenly spread across the alphabet.
D. Double the number of nodes you currently have.
Answer: C
Explanation:
The correct answer is C. Review your RowKey strategy and ensure that keys are evenly spread across the
alphabet.
Here's why: Hotspots in Cloud Bigtable typically arise when writes are concentrated on a small set of rows or
regions. This happens when row keys are not properly distributed. In this scenario, the data coming from
thousands of globally distributed physical devices might be using sequential or time-based row keys, causing
all writes to gravitate towards the same region of the Bigtable cluster. Consequently, this specific region
becomes overloaded, leading to performance bottlenecks, i.e., a hotspot.
Option C directly addresses the root cause of hotspots by recommending a review of the row key strategy.
Ensuring that keys are evenly distributed across the alphabet (or any other large and diverse character set),
effectively spreads the writes and reads across different regions in Bigtable, preventing a single region from
becoming overloaded and thus resolving the hotspot issue. Using a well-distributed row key strategy is
fundamental for optimal performance with Bigtable.
Option A (using HBase APIs) is irrelevant, as the problem is not API-related but rather data layout. Option B
(deleting old records) can help with storage space but does not address the hotspot caused by poor row key
design. Option D (doubling nodes) might temporarily alleviate the problem but doesn't fix the underlying issue
of uneven data distribution; it’s an expensive band-aid, not a long-term solution. Row key optimization is
crucial for efficient data processing within a distributed system such as Bigtable.For further reading:
Cloud Bigtable Schema Design: https://cloud.google.com/bigtable/docs/schema-design
Key Visualizer: https://cloud.google.com/bigtable/docs/key-visualizer
Avoiding Hotspots: https://cloud.google.com/bigtable/docs/avoiding-hotspots

Question: 179 CertyIQ
Your company has a Google Cloud project that uses BigQuery for data warehousing. There are some tables that
contain personally identifiable information (PII).
Only the compliance team may access the PII. The other information in the tables must be available to the data
science team. You want to minimize cost and the time it takes to assign appropriate access to the tables. What
should you do?
A. 1. From the dataset where you have the source data, create views of tables that you want to share, excluding
PII. 2. Assign an appropriate project-level IAM role to the members of the data science team. 3. Assign access
controls to the dataset that contains the view.
B. 1. From the dataset where you have the source data, create materialized views of tables that you want to
share, excluding PII. 2. Assign an appropriate project-level IAM role to the members of the data science team. 3.
Assign access controls to the dataset that contains the view.
C. 1. Create a dataset for the data science team. 2. Create views of tables that you want to share, excluding PII.
3. Assign an appropriate project-level IAM role to the members of the data science team. 4. Assign access
controls to the dataset that contains the view. 5. Authorize the view to access the source dataset.
D. 1. Create a dataset for the data science team. 2. Create materialized views of tables that you want to share,
excluding PII. 3. Assign an appropriate project-level IAM role to the members of the data science team. 4.
Assign access controls to the dataset that contains the view. 5. Authorize the view to access the source
dataset.
Answer: C
Explanation:
Option C is the most appropriate solution because it effectively addresses the requirements of separating
access to PII while minimizing cost and management overhead. Here's a breakdown:
1. Creating a separate dataset for the data science team (Step 1) ensures clear segregation of
resources and simplifies access control management. It allows you to grant specific permissions at
the dataset level without impacting other data assets.
2. Creating views (Step 2) rather than materialized views is crucial for cost minimization. Views are
logical representations of data, not physical copies. They don't incur storage costs and always reflect
the latest data in the base tables.
3. Excluding PII in the views ensures that data scientists only access the information relevant to their
work, maintaining compliance with data privacy regulations.
4. Assigning an appropriate project-level IAM role (Step 3) gives the data science team the necessary
access to the project as a whole. This enables them to use BigQuery and query the views.
5. Assigning access controls to the view dataset (Step 4) isolates the access to those views only to the
members of the data science team and nobody else, this ensures clear access boundaries.
6. Authorizing the view to access the source dataset (Step 5) is essential because the view, which
resides in a different dataset, needs permission to read data from the source dataset containing PII.
BigQuery views do not inherit permissions from source tables and datasets.
Options A and B don’t segregate access to data, meaning there could be cross access by members of the data
science team. Option D is not recommended because materialized views are physical copies of data, incurring
storage costs, maintenance overheads and possible data staleness when updates to the underlying source
table occur. The choice of views keeps costs and effort to a minimum.
Relevant Cloud Computing Concepts:
Principle of Least Privilege: Option C follows this principle by granting only the necessary access to each
team. The data science team does not require access to PII; therefore, they should be limited to data views
with no PII included.
IAM (Identity and Access Management): IAM allows fine-grained control of access to GCP resources based
on roles and permissions. This is central to managing access and security in BigQuery.

Data Governance: Separating access to PII is a core aspect of data governance. Views help to enforce data
governance policies by creating a controlled view of the underlying data.
Cost Optimization: Views incur little cost compared to materialized views as they do not require storage
space.
Authoritative Links:
BigQuery views: Explanation of how BigQuery views function.
BigQuery authorized views: Explanation of how to authorize a view to access a different dataset.
BigQuery IAM roles: Overview of IAM roles and permissions for BigQuery.
Principle of Least Privilege: Google Cloud's approach to least privilege.
Question: 180 CertyIQ
Your operations team currently stores 10 TB of data in an object storage service from a third-party provider. They
want to move this data to a Cloud Storage bucket as quickly as possible, following Google-recommended
practices. They want to minimize the cost of this data migration. Which approach should they use?
A. Use the gsutil mv command to move the data.
B. Use the Storage Transfer Service to move the data.
C. Download the data to a Transfer Appliance, and ship it to Google.
D. Download the data to the on-premises data center, and upload it to the Cloud Storage bucket.
Answer: B
Explanation:
The optimal approach is to use Storage Transfer Service (STS). Here's why: STS is a fully managed, serverless
service designed specifically for large-scale data transfers into and out of Google Cloud Storage. Unlike gsutil
mv, which is primarily intended for file management within Cloud Storage, STS is optimized for transferring
data from various sources, including third-party object storage. STS offers features like automated retry
logic, scheduling, and bandwidth throttling, ensuring a reliable and efficient migration. Downloading to a
Transfer Appliance (option C) or an on-premises data center (option D) adds unnecessary steps, increasing
complexity, time, and cost. While a Transfer Appliance is suitable for extremely large datasets or situations
with limited network connectivity, 10TB doesn't warrant its use, introducing delays and expenses.
Downloading to an on-premises location (option D) is also inefficient as it requires intermediate storage and
upload processes. Given the scale of 10TB and the requirement for minimizing cost, STS provides a cost-
effective and streamlined approach. It leverages Google’s network infrastructure to deliver high-performance
transfers, and users only pay for the actual data transferred. It also allows for efficient management of the
entire process via its web console or API. STS minimizes operational overhead and offers a more secure path
for migrating data compared to manual approaches.
Authoritative Link:Storage Transfer Service Overview
Question: 181 CertyIQ
You have a Compute Engine managed instance group that adds and removes Compute Engine instances from the
group in response to the load on your application. The instances have a shutdown script that removes REDIS
database entries associated with the instance. You see that many database entries have not been removed, and
you suspect that the shutdown script is the problem. You need to ensure that the commands in the shutdown script
are run reliably every time an instance is shut down. You create a Cloud Function to remove the database entries.
What should you do next?

A. Modify the shutdown script to wait for 30 seconds before triggering the Cloud Function.
B. Do not use the Cloud Function. Modify the shutdown script to restart if it has not completed in 30 seconds.
C. Set up a Cloud Monitoring sink that triggers the Cloud Function after an instance removal log message
arrives in Cloud Logging.
D. Modify the shutdown script to wait for 30 seconds and then publish a message to a Pub/Sub queue.
Answer: C
Explanation:
Here's a detailed justification for why option C is the correct approach, along with explanations of why the
others are less suitable:
Option C: Set up a Cloud Monitoring sink that triggers the Cloud Function after an instance removal log
message arrives in Cloud Logging.
This is the most reliable solution for ensuring cleanup tasks happen during instance termination in a managed
instance group. Here's why:
Event-Driven Architecture: Cloud Logging provides near real-time log data. By creating a sink that filters for
instance removal events, we can trigger the Cloud Function after the instance is officially marked for deletion,
not during its potentially unreliable shutdown process. This is an example of an event-driven architecture.
Reliability: Shutdown scripts, as indicated in the problem, can be unreliable as they are executed by the
instance during its shutdown phase. If the instance terminates abruptly (or the script takes too long), it might
not complete execution. Using Cloud Logging and a Cloud Function removes the dependency on the
instance's health and lifecycle for running the cleanup.
Decoupling: The Cloud Function performs the cleanup outside of the instance, further decoupling the
application logic from the instance lifecycle management, leading to a more robust architecture.
Scalability: This approach scales well with a managed instance group. No matter how many instances are
being removed or scaled down, the Cloud Function handles the cleanup process automatically when a log
message indicates it.
Loose Coupling: The instance does not need to know anything about the cleanup function. This helps to limit
the complexity of the instance image.
Best Practices: Google Cloud best practices encourage using an event-driven design for lifecycle events like
instance termination.
Why other options are less suitable:
Option A: Modify the shutdown script to wait for 30 seconds before triggering the Cloud Function. This is
problematic because relying on the shutdown script to trigger anything, even after waiting, is still not reliable.
The instance might terminate before the script has a chance to complete. Also, 30 seconds might not be
enough in some cases.
Option B: Do not use the Cloud Function. Modify the shutdown script to restart if it has not completed in 30
seconds. Restarting the shutdown script creates a loop that does not actually remove the database entries,
plus it introduces more complexity to the shutdown script, and is still unreliable. It does not guarantee
completion, and the instance could still be terminated before the script completes. Moreover, it is not the
intended use of the shutdown script.
Option D: Modify the shutdown script to wait for 30 seconds and then publish a message to a Pub/Sub
queue. While slightly better than options A and B (as it's asynchronous), it still relies on the shutdown script
completing reliably, making it a less robust solution than triggering based on a log event. There's no
guarantee the message will be published before instance termination. Furthermore, introducing Pub/Sub
unnecessarily increases the complexity of the solution.
Authoritative Links:

Cloud Logging Sinks: https://cloud.google.com/logging/docs/export/configure_export_v2
Cloud Functions: https://cloud.google.com/functions/docs
Managed Instance Groups: https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/instance-groups
Shutdown Scripts: https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/startupscript#shutdownscript
In summary, using a Cloud Logging sink to trigger a Cloud Function provides the most reliable, scalable, and
decoupled approach for handling cleanup tasks during instance termination in a managed instance group.
Question: 182 CertyIQ
You are managing several projects on Google Cloud and need to interact on a daily basis with BigQuery, Bigtable,
and Kubernetes Engine using the gcloud CL tool. You are travelling a lot and work on different workstations during
the week. You want to avoid having to manage the gcloud CLI manually. What should you do?
A. Use Google Cloud Shell in the Google Cloud Console to interact with Google Cloud.
B. Create a Compute Engine instance and install gcloud on the instance. Connect to this instance via SSH to
always use the same gcloud installation when interacting with Google Cloud.
C. Install gcloud on all of your workstations. Run the command gcloud components auto-update on each
workstation
D. Use a package manager to install gcloud on your workstations instead of installing it manually.
Answer: A
Explanation:
The most effective solution for managing gcloud CLI access across multiple workstations without manual
configuration is to leverage Google Cloud Shell (option A). Google Cloud Shell is a browser-based, pre-
configured, and temporary shell environment readily accessible within the Google Cloud Console. This
eliminates the need to install and manage gcloud and its associated dependencies on individual workstations.
It provides a consistent and readily available environment regardless of the user's location or machine. Since
it's web-based, the user can access it securely from any internet-connected device with a browser, ensuring
continuity of workflow. Furthermore, the Cloud Shell instance is pre-authenticated with the user's Google
Cloud credentials, allowing direct access to managed resources without further configuration. Options B, C,
and D introduce unnecessary administrative overhead. Option B requires managing a Compute Engine
instance and establishing SSH connections, adding complexity and operational burden. Options C and D, while
providing local access, require repeated manual installation and updates on multiple machines, which is
exactly what the scenario wants to avoid. Cloud Shell's serverless, ephemeral nature makes it inherently more
suitable for managing interactions from varying locations. It also provides 5GB of persistent disk storage for
scripts and configuration files. Lastly, it simplifies access to cloud resources without exposing gcloud
credentials directly on potentially insecure machines.
Relevant Links:
Google Cloud Shell Documentation: https://cloud.google.com/shell/docs
Google Cloud CLI Documentation: https://cloud.google.com/sdk/docs/
Question: 183 CertyIQ
Your company recently acquired a company that has infrastructure in Google Cloud. Each company has its own
Google Cloud organization. Each company is using a Shared Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) to provide network
connectivity for its applications. Some of the subnets used by both companies overlap. In order for both businesses
to integrate, the applications need to have private network connectivity. These applications are not on overlapping
subnets. You want to provide connectivity with minimal re-engineering. What should you do?

A. Set up VPC peering and peer each Shared VPC together.
B. Migrate the projects from the acquired company into your company's Google Cloud organization. Re-launch
the instances in your companies Shared VPC.
C. Set up a Cloud VPN gateway in each Shared VPC and peer Cloud VPNs.
D. Configure SSH port forwarding on each application to provide connectivity between applications in the
different Shared VPCs.
Answer: C
Explanation:
The correct answer is C, establishing Cloud VPN gateways in each Shared VPC and peering them. This
approach effectively addresses the network connectivity challenge with minimal disruption. Option A, using
VPC peering directly, fails because VPC peering cannot be established between networks with overlapping
subnets. Option B, migrating projects and relaunching instances, involves significant re-engineering and
downtime, violating the requirement for minimal change. Option D, relying on SSH port forwarding, is
unsuitable for production environments due to its complexity, security risks, and performance limitations; it's
not a scalable or reliable solution for application-to-application communication. Cloud VPN, on the other hand,
allows secure IPsec tunnels to be established between the Shared VPCs, enabling private communication
even with overlapping subnets because the traffic is encapsulated and routed via the VPN gateways. This
solution also adheres to the principle of least privilege, as it focuses on the network layer rather than
modifying application configurations. Moreover, it provides a stable and secure channel for data exchange,
which is crucial for integrating two businesses. This method allows organizations to avoid IP conflict and
complexity, and maintain a secure private connection.
Authoritative Links:
Google Cloud Documentation - Cloud VPN: https://cloud.google.com/vpn/docs
Google Cloud Documentation - Shared VPC: https://cloud.google.com/vpc/docs/shared-vpc
Google Cloud Documentation - VPC Peering: https://cloud.google.com/vpc/docs/vpc-peering
Question: 184 CertyIQ
You are managing several internal applications that are deployed on Compute Engine. Business users inform you
that an application has become very slow over the past few days. You want to find the underlying cause in order to
solve the problem. What should you do first?
A. Inspect the logs and metrics from the instances in Cloud Logging and Cloud Monitoring.
B. Change the Compute Engine Instances behind the application to a machine type with more CPU and memory.
C. Restore a backup of the application database from a time before the application became slow.
D. Deploy the applications on a managed instance group with autoscaling enabled. Add a load balancer in front
of the managed instance group, and have the users connect to the IP of the load balancer.
Answer: A
Explanation:
The most appropriate first step when troubleshooting a slow application on Compute Engine is to inspect the
logs and metrics from the instances in Cloud Logging and Cloud Monitoring (A). This proactive approach
allows for a data-driven diagnosis before making potentially unnecessary and costly changes. Cloud
Monitoring provides crucial performance metrics, including CPU utilization, memory usage, disk I/O, and
network traffic, which can pinpoint resource bottlenecks. Simultaneously, Cloud Logging gathers application
and system logs, offering insights into errors, exceptions, or unusual behaviors that may contribute to the
slowdown. Analyzing both metrics and logs provides a holistic view, helping to identify the root cause –

whether it's resource contention, inefficient code, database issues, or networking problems. Attempting
solutions like increasing resources (B), restoring backups (C), or implementing autoscaling and load balancing
(D) without this initial diagnosis is premature and may not address the underlying issue, potentially resulting
in wasted resources and time. A systematic approach leveraging monitoring and logging is fundamental for
effective troubleshooting and is a best practice in cloud environments.
Further Research:
Cloud Monitoring Documentation: https://cloud.google.com/monitoring/docs
Cloud Logging Documentation: https://cloud.google.com/logging/docs
Google Cloud Best Practices for Performance Optimization:
https://cloud.google.com/architecture/framework/performance
Question: 185 CertyIQ
Your company has an application running as a Deployment in a Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) cluster. When
releasing new versions of the application via a rolling deployment, the team has been causing outages. The root
cause of the outages is misconfigurations with parameters that are only used in production. You want to put
preventive measures for this in the platform to prevent outages. What should you do?
A. Configure liveness and readiness probes in the Pod specification.
B. Configure health checks on the managed instance group.
C. Create a Scheduled Task to check whether the application is available.
D. Configure an uptime alert in Cloud Monitoring.
Answer: A
Explanation:
The correct answer is A. Configure liveness and readiness probes in the Pod specification.
Here's the justification:
Liveness and readiness probes are crucial for managing application health within a Kubernetes environment
like GKE. Liveness probes detect when an application has become unhealthy and needs to be restarted.
Readiness probes signal when an application is ready to accept traffic. By configuring these probes in the Pod
specification, Kubernetes can automatically detect and remediate issues caused by misconfigurations during
rolling deployments. When a new pod with misconfigurations starts, the liveness probe might fail, causing the
pod to be restarted, or the readiness probe might fail, preventing traffic from reaching the pod. This prevents
the new faulty pod from impacting the overall application health. These probes directly address the issue of
outages caused by misconfigured parameters that appear only in production by monitoring the application's
internal state. While options B, C, and D offer valuable monitoring and alerting capabilities, they don't actively
prevent unhealthy pods from impacting the deployment during rolling updates like liveness and readiness
probes do. For example, an uptime alert (D) would only notify about an issue after it has occurred, not prevent
it in the first place.
Authoritative links:
Kubernetes Liveness Probes: https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/configure-pod-container/configure-liveness-
readiness-startup-probes/
Kubernetes Readiness Probes: https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/configure-pod-container/configure-liveness-
readiness-startup-probes/
Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) Deployments: https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-
engine/docs/concepts/deployments

Question: 186 CertyIQ
Your company uses Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) as a platform for all workloads. Your company has a single
large GKE cluster that contains batch, stateful, and stateless workloads. The GKE cluster is configured with a
single node pool with 200 nodes. Your company needs to reduce the cost of this cluster but does not want to
compromise availability. What should you do?
A. Create a second GKE cluster for the batch workloads only. Allocate the 200 original nodes across both
clusters.
B. Configure CPU and memory limits on the namespaces in the cluster. Configure all Pods to have a CPU and
memory limits.
C. Configure a HorizontalPodAutoscaler for all stateless workloads and for all compatible stateful workloads.
Configure the cluster to use node auto scaling.
D. Change the node pool to use preemptible VMs.
Answer: C
Explanation:
Option C is the most suitable solution because it addresses both cost reduction and availability without
requiring significant architectural changes. Here's why:
Horizontal Pod Autoscaling (HPA): HPA dynamically adjusts the number of pod replicas based on observed
CPU utilization or other custom metrics. This ensures that resources are only consumed when needed, leading
to cost savings during periods of low demand. By scaling down stateless and compatible stateful workloads
during off-peak times, resource waste is minimized.
Node Autoscaling: Node autoscaling complements HPA by adding or removing nodes in the cluster based on
resource requests. If scaled pods require more resources than the current nodes can provide, the cluster will
automatically add new nodes, ensuring availability. Conversely, when demand decreases, nodes can be
removed to save costs. This dynamic adjustment keeps resources aligned with the actual requirements of the
cluster.
Resource Optimization: The combination of HPA and node autoscaling optimizes resource consumption by
scaling up or down as needed. This avoids over-provisioning that is common when a fixed number of nodes is
always running, thus reducing costs.
Availability: By using autoscaling, the cluster is more resilient. During peak usage, the system can expand
capacity to meet demand. If nodes fail, node autoscaling can quickly replace them, reducing service
disruption.
No Architectural Overhaul: Option A involves creating a second cluster, which requires significant
architectural changes and management overhead. Option B limits resources, which can negatively affect
availability during peak times if limits are too low. Option D makes use of preemptible VMs, which can be
reclaimed by GCP and are not suitable for workloads needing high availability.
Authoritative Links:
Kubernetes Horizontal Pod Autoscaling: https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/run-application/horizontal-pod-
autoscale/
GKE Node Autoscaling: https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/how-to/node-autoscaling
Best Practices for Running Cost-Effective Applications on Google Kubernetes Engine:
https://cloud.google.com/blog/products/containers-kubernetes/best-practices-for-running-cost-effective-
applications-on-gke

Question: 187 CertyIQ
Your company has a Google Cloud project that uses BigQuery for data warehousing on a pay-per-use basis. You
want to monitor queries in real time to discover the most costly queries and which users spend the most. What
should you do?
A. 1. In the BigQuery dataset that contains all the tables to be queried, add a label for each user that can launch
a query. 2. Open the Billing page of the project. 3. Select Reports. 4. Select BigQuery as the product and filter
by the user you want to check.
B. 1. Create a Cloud Logging sink to export BigQuery data access logs to BigQuery. 2. Perform a BigQuery query
on the generated table to extract the information you need.
C. 1. Create a Cloud Logging sink to export BigQuery data access logs to Cloud Storage. 2. Develop a Dataflow
pipeline to compute the cost of queries split by users.
D. 1. Activate billing export into BigQuery. 2. Perform a BigQuery query on the billing table to extract the
information you need.
Answer: B
Explanation:
Option B is the correct approach because it leverages the power of BigQuery's audit logs and its querying
capabilities to provide real-time insights into query costs and user activity.
Here's a breakdown:
1. Cloud Logging Sink: BigQuery data access logs record detailed information about queries, including
the user who executed them, the query's execution time, and the resources consumed. By creating a
Cloud Logging sink that exports these logs to another BigQuery dataset (as suggested in option B),
we can easily query this raw data. https://cloud.google.com/logging/docs/export
2. BigQuery Querying: Once the logs are in BigQuery, we can use standard SQL queries to analyze the
data. This allows us to perform aggregations, filtering, and groupings to discover which queries are
most expensive and which users spend the most on queries. We can calculate the cost based on the
processing time and other resources consumed by each query, and group that data by the user that
initiated the query. https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/querying-data
Option A is incorrect because while labels can be used, they would not automatically track the cost of queries
on a per-user basis; labels are useful for resource grouping and management. Filtering by user in the billing
page is useful for cost analysis, but it doesn't provide the detailed query-level visibility the question is asking
for.
Option C is incorrect because using Dataflow for cost analysis when the raw data is already within BigQuery is
less efficient and adds unnecessary complexity. While Dataflow is useful, it's an overkill for the requirements.
Also Dataflow will require additional costs and complexity.
Option D is incorrect because while billing export to BigQuery provides a breakdown of overall costs by
service and project, it doesn’t provide detailed information on individual BigQuery queries and the users that
generated them. It will not allow to determine which exact query is more costly and which user spends more
on the queries.
Therefore, option B offers the most direct and efficient way to gain the required insights by leveraging
logging data and BigQuery's native analytical capabilities. It allows near real-time insights into query costs,
split by user, by using the generated logs from the Cloud Logging sink exported into BigQuery.
Question: 188 CertyIQ

Your company and one of its partners each have a Google Cloud project in separate organizations. Your company's
project (prj-a) runs in Virtual Private Cloud
(vpc-a). The partner's project (prj-b) runs in vpc-b. There are two instances running on vpc-a and one instance
running on vpc-b. Subnets defined in both VPCs are not overlapping. You need to ensure that all instances
communicate with each other via internal IPs, minimizing latency and maximizing throughput. What should you do?
A. Set up a network peering between vpc-a and vpc-b.
B. Set up a VPN between vpc-a and vpc-b using Cloud VPN.
C. Configure IAP TCP forwarding on the instance in vpc-b, and then launch the following gcloud command from
one of the instances in vpc-a gcloud: gcloud compute start-iap-tunnel INSTANCE_NAME_IN_VPC_8 22 \ --
local-host-port=localhost:22
D. 1. Create an additional instance in vpc-a. 2. Create an additional instance in vpc-b. 3. Install OpenVPN in
newly created instances. 4. Configure a VPN tunnel between vpc-a and vpc-b with the help of OpenVPN.
Answer: A
Explanation:
The correct answer is A. Set up a network peering between vpc-a and vpc-b.
Here's why:
Network peering allows direct, private communication between Virtual Private Clouds (VPCs) in Google Cloud.
This establishes a secure, low-latency connection using Google's internal network. Since the subnets are non-
overlapping, peering is a feasible and optimal solution. This method avoids the overhead and complexities
associated with other options, like VPNs.
By peering vpc-a and vpc-b, instances in both projects can communicate using their internal IP addresses. This
satisfies the requirements of minimizing latency and maximizing throughput, as traffic doesn't need to
traverse the public internet. There are no extra costs associated with egress traffic to the public internet.
Peering offers a scalable approach for inter-VPC communication.
Option B, using Cloud VPN, introduces the overhead of encryption and decryption, increasing latency and
potentially reducing throughput compared to peering. While VPNs are useful for connecting to on-premise
networks, peering is better suited for VPC-to-VPC communication within Google Cloud.
Option C, employing IAP TCP forwarding, is designed to provide secure access to instances via the public
internet and is unsuitable for direct internal IP communication between VPCs. It's also more complex and less
efficient than peering for this use case.
Option D, using OpenVPN, involves manual setup, management overhead, and the potential for configuration
errors. Furthermore, relying on two new instances just for networking adds unnecessary complexity, and
OpenVPN itself is a resource-intensive option compared to the native peering option.
In summary, VPC peering is the most straightforward, efficient, and scalable solution to enable low-latency,
high-throughput communication between instances residing in separate Google Cloud VPCs with non-
overlapping subnets.
Authoritative Links:
VPC Network Peering Overview: https://cloud.google.com/vpc/docs/vpc-peering
Comparing Peering with VPNs: https://cloud.google.com/vpc/docs/choose-vpc-peering-vpn
IAP TCP Forwarding: https://cloud.google.com/iap/docs/tcp-forwarding-howto
Question: 189 CertyIQ
You want to store critical business information in Cloud Storage buckets. The information is regularly changed, but

previous versions need to be referenced on a regular basis. You want to ensure that there is a record of all changes
to any information in these buckets. You want to ensure that accidental edits or deletions can be easily rolled back.
Which feature should you enable?
A. Bucket Lock
B. Object Versioning
C. Object change notification
D. Object Lifecycle Management
Answer: B
Explanation:
The correct answer is B. Object Versioning. Object Versioning in Google Cloud Storage is specifically
designed to address the need to retain previous versions of objects, making it ideal for scenarios where data is
regularly changed and rollbacks are necessary. When enabled, every time an object is overwritten or deleted,
Cloud Storage creates a new version of the object, preserving the previous state. This ensures a historical
record of all changes. Bucket Lock, while important for compliance, prevents modifications once enabled and
isn't for regular rollbacks. Object change notification alerts you to changes but doesn't store previous
versions. Object Lifecycle Management automates object deletion or storage class changes and isn't meant
for version history. Therefore, Object Versioning perfectly fulfills the requirements of tracking all changes,
referencing past states, and easily rolling back accidental edits or deletions.
Authoritative links for further research:
Google Cloud Storage Object Versioning documentation: https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/object-
versioning
Overview of Cloud Storage Features: https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/overview
Question: 190 CertyIQ
You have a Compute Engine application that you want to autoscale when total memory usage exceeds 80%. You
have installed the Cloud Monitoring agent and configured the autoscaling policy as follows:
✑ Metric identifier: agent.googleapis.com/memory/percent_used
✑ Filter: metric.label.state = 'used'
✑ Target utilization level: 80
✑ Target type: GAUGE
You observe that the application does not scale under high load. You want to resolve this. What should you do?
A. Change the Target type to DELTA_PER_MINUTE.
B. Change the Metric identifier to agent.googleapis.com/memory/bytes_used.
C. Change the filter to metric.label.state = 'used' AND metric.label.state = 'buffered' AND metric.label.state =
'cached' AND metric.label.state = 'slab'.
D. Change the filter to metric.label.state = 'free' and the Target utilization to 20.
Answer: A
Explanation:
The issue lies in the GAUGE target type used with the percent_used memory metric. GAUGE targets a specific
absolute value at a given point in time. Memory usage, as a percentage, can fluctuate rapidly. Autoscaling
needs to react to the change in the metric over time to effectively trigger scaling. Option A, changing the
target type to DELTA_PER_MINUTE, is the correct solution. DELTA_PER_MINUTE calculates the rate of
change of the metric per minute, allowing autoscaling to respond to trends in memory usage, rather than
instantaneous peaks or valleys. This rate of change provides a more stable indicator for scaling decisions. The

original GAUGE target could be misleading, as a brief spike in usage might trigger a scale-up, but if the usage
drops immediately after, it was an inappropriate scaling response. DELTA_PER_MINUTE averages changes
over time and provides a better picture of a sustained memory pressure.
Options B, C, and D are incorrect. Option B suggests changing the metric to bytes_used, which would still be a
GAUGE value and not solve the problem of fluctuating absolute values. Option C proposes a very specific and
likely unnecessary filter that adds cached/buffered/slab memory, whereas focusing on the overall used
percentage is sufficient for autoscaling. Finally, option D’s proposal of free memory and 20% target utilization
is counterintuitive and incorrect because the goal is to scale based on used memory.
Supporting Links:
Autoscaling based on metrics: https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/autoscaling/autoscaling-metrics
(Specifically, focus on target types and usage for metrics).
Cloud Monitoring Metrics: https://cloud.google.com/monitoring/api/metrics_agent (Review the
agent.googleapis.com/memory metrics and their types).
Target types in Autoscaling: https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/autoscaling/autoscaling-
metrics#target_utilization (This clearly explains different target types including GAUGE and
DELTA_PER_MINUTE.)
Question: 191 CertyIQ
You are deploying an application to Google Cloud. The application is part of a system. The application in Google
Cloud must communicate over a private network with applications in a non-Google Cloud environment. The
expected average throughput is 200 kbps. The business requires:
✑ as close to 100% system availability as possible
✑ cost optimization
You need to design the connectivity between the locations to meet the business requirements. What should you
provision?
A. An HA Cloud VPN gateway connected with two tunnels to an on-premises VPN gateway
B. Two Classic Cloud VPN gateways connected to two on-premises VPN gateways Configure each Classic
Cloud VPN gateway to have two tunnels, each connected to different on-premises VPN gateways
C. Two HA Cloud VPN gateways connected to two on-premises VPN gateways Configure each HA Cloud VPN
gateway to have two tunnels, each connected to different on-premises VPN gateways
D. A single Cloud VPN gateway connected to an on-premises VPN gateway
Answer: A
Explanation:
The correct answer is A: An HA Cloud VPN gateway connected with two tunnels to an on-premises VPN
gateway. This solution optimally balances high availability and cost for the given requirements.
Justification:
High Availability: HA Cloud VPN utilizes two interfaces to create redundant VPN tunnels connecting to the
on-premise VPN gateway. If one tunnel or interface fails, traffic automatically shifts to the other, ensuring
continuous connectivity and maximizing system availability, which is a core business requirement.
Cost Optimization: While multiple gateways might seem like a robust solution, HA Cloud VPN offers
redundancy within a single, managed service, optimizing for cost compared to deploying multiple independent
gateways and their associated infrastructure. Using two tunnels per HA VPN gateway provides the necessary
redundancy without incurring the expense of multiple gateways.
Throughput Requirement: The 200 kbps throughput is quite low. HA Cloud VPN tunnels can easily handle this
level of traffic and are typically sized for much higher capacity, making it a suitable fit.

Avoidance of Redundant Gateways: Option B and C propose multiple gateways which increase complexity
and cost without significant added value for the given throughput and availability needs. Option D presents a
single point of failure. HA VPN inherently offers the needed redundancy.
Simplicity: Using a single HA VPN setup, as proposed in option A, simplifies the configuration and
management compared to options B and C which would require managing multiple gateways.
Relevant Cloud Computing Concepts:
High Availability (HA): Designing systems to minimize downtime and ensure continuous operation, often
through redundancy.
Virtual Private Network (VPN): A secure encrypted tunnel that creates a private connection across a public
network.
Cloud VPN: Google Cloud's service to create VPN connections to other networks using IPsec VPN tunnels.
Active-Active Redundancy: Both tunnels actively route traffic, providing more bandwidth and redundancy.
Cost-Optimization: Choosing the most efficient resource usage to minimize expenditure.
Authoritative Links:
Cloud VPN Overview: https://cloud.google.com/vpn/docs/concepts/overview
HA VPN: https://cloud.google.com/network-connectivity/docs/vpn/concepts/ha-vpn
Choosing a Cloud VPN Topology: https://cloud.google.com/network-connectivity/docs/vpn/how-to/choosing-
a-topology
In conclusion, utilizing a single HA Cloud VPN gateway with dual tunnels efficiently addresses the
requirement for both high availability and cost optimization for this specific scenario. The low bandwidth
needs do not justify a more complex and costly multi-gateway approach.
Question: 192 CertyIQ
Your company has an application running on App Engine that allows users to upload music files and share them
with other people. You want to allow users to upload files directly into Cloud Storage from their browser session.
The payload should not be passed through the backend. What should you do?
A.1. Set a CORS configuration in the target Cloud Storage bucket where the base URL of the App Engine
application is an allowed origin.
2. Use the Cloud Storage Signed URL feature to generate a POST URL.
B.1. Set a CORS configuration in the target Cloud Storage bucket where the base URL of the App Engine
application is an allowed origin.
2. Assign the Cloud Storage WRITER role to users who upload files.
C.1. Use the Cloud Storage Signed URL feature to generate a POST URL.
2. Use App Engine default credentials to sign requests against Cloud Storage.
D.1. Assign the Cloud Storage WRITER role to users who upload files.
2. Use App Engine default credentials to sign requests against Cloud Storage.
Answer: A
Explanation:
Here's a detailed justification for why option A is the correct solution, along with explanations of why the
other options are incorrect:
Justification for Option A:
Option A correctly addresses the requirement of direct browser uploads to Cloud Storage, bypassing the App
Engine backend. This is achieved through two key steps:

1. CORS Configuration: Cross-Origin Resource Sharing (CORS) is crucial for web applications to
interact with resources from different origins (domains). Since the App Engine application is hosted
on one domain and the Cloud Storage bucket on another, a CORS configuration on the bucket is
required. This configuration specifies which origins (in this case, the base URL of the App Engine
application) are permitted to make requests to the bucket. Without proper CORS, browser security
mechanisms will block direct uploads. This is an important security consideration, allowing resources
from different origins to interact with each other without having unrestricted access.
2. Signed URL: A Signed URL is a time-limited URL that grants specific permissions to access a Cloud
Storage resource. By generating a POST-type Signed URL, the application can provide a user’s
browser with the necessary credentials to directly upload a file to the designated location in Cloud
Storage. This avoids the need for the server to receive the file initially and then relay it to Cloud
Storage, reducing server load and latency.
Therefore, Option A correctly uses both CORS and Signed URLs to achieve direct browser uploads without the
backend's involvement.
Why other Options are Incorrect:
Option B: While setting a CORS configuration is correct, assigning the Cloud Storage WRITER role directly to
users is a significant security risk. Users shouldn't have direct permanent write access to the bucket. This
would violate the principle of least privilege, providing excessive access and leading to potential security
vulnerabilities.
Option C: While using Signed URLs is correct for client-side uploads, App Engine default credentials are not
intended for granting client access. They should be used only to access services from within your App Engine
application for server-to-server communication. Using them directly exposes sensitive information and
presents a security risk.
Option D: This option combines the major issues of B and C, by directly granting WRITER role and improperly
using default credentials, further amplifying the security vulnerabilities.
Authoritative Links for Further Research:
Cloud Storage CORS: https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/configuring-cors
Cloud Storage Signed URLs: https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/access-control/signed-urls
Principle of Least Privilege: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Principle_of_least_privilege
In conclusion, option A is the only solution that correctly and securely enables direct browser uploads to
Cloud Storage, adhering to best practices and avoiding potential security risks.
Question: 193 CertyIQ
You are configuring the cloud network architecture for a newly created project in Google Cloud that will host
applications in Compute Engine. Compute Engine virtual machine instances will be created in two different
subnets (sub-a and sub-b) within a single region:
•Instances in sub-a will have public IP addresses.
•Instances in sub-b will have only private IP addresses.
To download updated packages, instances must connect to a public repository outside the boundaries of Google
Cloud. You need to allow sub-b to access the external repository. What should you do?
A.Enable Private Google Access on sub-b.
B.Configure Cloud NAT and select sub-b in the NAT mapping section.
C.Configure a bastion host instance in sub-a to connect to instances in sub-b.

D.Enable Identity-Aware Proxy for TCP forwarding for instances in sub-b.
Answer: B
Explanation:
Here's a detailed justification for why option B, "Configure Cloud NAT and select sub-b in the NAT mapping
section," is the correct solution:
The core requirement is to allow instances in sub-b, which have private IP addresses, to access external
resources (a public repository) without directly exposing them to the internet. Cloud NAT (Network Address
Translation) is designed precisely for this purpose. It allows instances within a private network to initiate
outbound connections to the internet by translating their private IP addresses to a public IP address
belonging to the Cloud NAT gateway. This ensures that outbound traffic appears to originate from a public IP
address instead of the internal ones.
Option A, enabling Private Google Access, allows instances with private IP addresses to access Google Cloud
APIs and services, but it doesn't enable access to arbitrary external endpoints. Thus, it would not solve the
problem of accessing a public repository outside Google Cloud. Option C, using a bastion host, adds
unnecessary complexity. While a bastion host can provide connectivity, it requires managing an additional
instance and setting up forwarding rules. Cloud NAT is a more direct and scalable solution. Option D, Identity-
Aware Proxy, focuses on controlling access to applications and resources; it’s not relevant for basic outbound
internet connectivity.
By configuring Cloud NAT and selecting sub-b in the NAT mapping, the instances in sub-b can send traffic
through the NAT gateway. The gateway will translate the source IP addresses of these instances to a public IP
address, allowing access to the external repository. Return traffic is directed back to the private instance
using NAT. This way, instances in sub-b can access the internet without needing their own public IPs, adhering
to best practices for security and network management. Cloud NAT scales automatically based on demand
making it a robust solution.
Authoritative Links:
Cloud NAT Overview: https://cloud.google.com/nat/docs/overview
Configuring Cloud NAT: https://cloud.google.com/nat/docs/using-nat
Private Google Access: https://cloud.google.com/vpc/docs/private-access-options
Question: 194 CertyIQ
Your company is planning to migrate their Windows Server 2022 from their on-premises data center to Google
Cloud. You need to bring the licenses that are currently in use in on-premises virtual machines into the target cloud
environment. What should you do?
A.1. Create an image of the on-premises virtual machines and upload into Cloud Storage.
2. Import the image as a virtual disk on Compute Engine.
B.1. Create standard instances on Compute Engine.
2. Select as the OS the same Microsoft Windows version that is currently in use in the on-premises
environment.
C.1. Create an image of the on-premises virtual machine.
2. Import the image as a virtual disk on Compute Engine.
3. Create a standard instance on Compute Engine, selecting as the OS the same Microsoft Windows version
that is currently in use in the on-premises environment.
4. Attach a data disk that includes data that matches the created image.
D.1. Create an image of the on-premises virtual machines.
2. Import the image as a virtual disk on Compute Engine using --os=windows-2022-dc-v.
3. Create a sole-tenancy instance on Compute Engine that uses the imported disk as a boot disk.

Answer: D
Explanation:
The correct answer is D because it outlines the proper procedure for migrating Windows Server 2022 licenses
to Google Cloud using the bring-your-own-license (BYOL) model via sole-tenancy nodes. Option D starts by
creating an image of the on-premises virtual machine, capturing its operating system, applications, and
configurations. This image is then imported into Compute Engine, specifically specifying the operating system
as windows-2022-dc-v which indicates a BYOL license. The key step is then creating a sole-tenancy instance.
Sole-tenancy nodes allow customers to bring their existing Windows Server licenses, aligning with the
requirement of utilizing current on-premises licenses. BYOL is the recommended and cost-effective approach
in such scenario. Option A is incorrect as importing the image without specifying the OS does not allow proper
license mapping. Option B is incorrect because creating standard Compute Engine instances uses Google's
licenses, not your own, failing to fulfill the BYOL requirement. Option C is also incorrect. Creating a separate
standard instance and then attaching a data disk does not correctly handle Windows licenses. Instead, the OS
disk needs to be from the image you provide that has the existing windows setup, and needs to be run in a
sole-tenancy node for license purposes. Sole tenancy and BYOL are critical for compliance and cost
optimization in this scenario.
Supporting Links:
Google Cloud Sole-Tenancy Nodes: https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/nodes/sole-tenant-nodes
Bring Your Own License (BYOL): https://cloud.google.com/licensing
Importing Virtual Disks: https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/import/import-virtual-disks
Question: 195 CertyIQ
You are deploying an application to Google Cloud. The application is part of a system. The application in Google
Cloud must communicate over a private network with applications in a non-Google Cloud environment. The
expected average throughput is 200 kbps. The business requires:
•99.99% system availability
•cost optimization
You need to design the connectivity between the locations to meet the business requirements. What should you
provision?
A.An HA Cloud VPN gateway connected with two tunnels to an on-premises VPN gateway.
B.A Classic Cloud VPN gateway connected with two tunnels to an on-premises VPN gateway.
C.Two HA Cloud VPN gateways connected to two on-premises VPN gateways. Configure each HA Cloud VPN
gateway to have two tunnels, each connected to different on-premises VPN gateways.
D.A Classic Cloud VPN gateway connected with one tunnel to an on-premises VPN gateway.
Answer: A
Explanation:
Option A, an HA Cloud VPN gateway with two tunnels to an on-premises VPN gateway, is the correct choice
because it provides the required 99.99% availability and cost optimization for the described scenario. High
Availability (HA) Cloud VPN gateways inherently provide redundancy by having two interfaces, meaning that
the connection remains active even if one interface fails. The use of two tunnels enhances this redundancy,
ensuring that if one tunnel experiences issues, the other can maintain the connection. A single HA gateway is
more cost-effective than using two full HA gateways as in Option C, while still meeting the high availability
requirement. Option B, using a Classic Cloud VPN, doesn’t meet the 99.99% availability target as classic VPN
gateways lack inherent redundancy. Option D, while cost-effective, sacrifices availability with a single point of

failure. Option C is overkill for a relatively low 200 kbps throughput and doesn't contribute substantially to
increased performance. It would also unnecessarily increase costs. The HA VPN solution with two tunnels
provides a balance of reliability and cost efficiency, leveraging the HA feature for failover protection of the
primary connection. This solution ensures a stable and resilient connection for the application’s private
network communication needs while remaining within budget constraints. For further information, refer to
Google's documentation on Cloud VPN: https://cloud.google.com/vpn/docs/concepts/overview, specifically on
HA VPN configuration.
Question: 196 CertyIQ
Your company wants to migrate their 10-TB on-premises database export into Cloud Storage. You want to minimize
the time it takes to complete this activity and the overall cost. The bandwidth between the on-premises
environment and Google Cloud is 1 Gbps. You want to follow Google-recommended practices. What should you do?
A.Develop a Dataflow job to read data directly from the database and write it into Cloud Storage.
B.Use the Data Transfer appliance to perform an offline migration.
C.Use a commercial partner ETL solution to extract the data from the on-premises database and upload it into
Cloud Storage.
D.Upload the data with gcloud storage cp.
Answer: D
Explanation:
The correct answer is D. Upload the data with gcloud storage cp. Here's why:
While options A, B, and C have their place, they are not the most efficient or cost-effective solutions for a
straightforward 10TB database export transfer, given the 1 Gbps bandwidth constraint and the goal of
minimizing time and cost. Option A, using Dataflow, involves complex processing and is unnecessary for a
simple data transfer. Option B, the Data Transfer appliance, is designed for very large datasets exceeding
network bandwidth limitations and involves additional logistics, thus is an overkill for a 10TB database export
with 1 Gbps connection. Option C, relying on a commercial ETL solution, introduces costs and complexity
without adding direct value to a simple file transfer.
gcloud storage cp is a command-line tool that leverages the available network bandwidth effectively for direct
uploads to Cloud Storage. It's highly optimized, can handle large files, and is the most straightforward method
for this scenario. The tool's ability to split large files into smaller chunks for parallel transfer improves
throughput. It avoids the overhead and complexities associated with more elaborate solutions. The question
emphasizes both speed and cost reduction, and gcloud cp offers the best balance of both for transferring the
database export files directly. Cloud Storage is designed to receive large amounts of data quickly using
command line tools like gcloud storage cp.
Supporting Links:
Google Cloud Storage Documentation on gcloud storage cp:
https://cloud.google.com/sdk/gcloud/reference/storage/cp
Google Cloud Data Transfer Options: https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/data-transfer
In summary, gcloud storage cp provides a simple, direct, cost-effective and fast way to leverage the provided
1Gbps connection to transfer the database export to Cloud Storage.

Question: 197 CertyIQ
You are working at a financial institution that stores mortgage loan approval documents on Cloud Storage. Any
change to these approval documents must be uploaded as a separate approval file. You need to ensure that these
documents cannot be deleted or overwritten for the next 5 years. What should you do?
A.Create a retention policy on the bucket for the duration of 5 years. Create a lock on the retention policy.
B.Create a retention policy organizational constraint constraints/storage.retentionPolicySeconds at the
organization level. Set the duration to 5 years.
C.Use a customer-managed key for the encryption of the bucket. Rotate the key after 5 years.
D.Create a retention policy organizational constraint constraints/storage.retentionPolicySeconds at the project
level. Set the duration to 5 years.
Answer: A
Explanation:
Option A, creating a retention policy on the bucket for 5 years and then locking it, is the correct approach
because it directly addresses the requirement of preventing deletion or overwriting of objects within a Cloud
Storage bucket for a specified duration. Retention policies in Cloud Storage are designed for data
immutability and compliance. When a retention policy is locked, it becomes permanent, and neither the policy
nor the objects it covers can be deleted or modified before the policy's duration expires. This ensures
adherence to the 5-year retention requirement, aligning with regulatory obligations common in financial
institutions dealing with sensitive documents. Option B, applying a retention policy as an organizational
constraint, sets a minimum duration for all buckets within the organization. While this prevents deletion, it
doesn't guarantee a specific 5-year immutability window for this bucket, and allows a longer duration if
applied at a bucket level. Option C, using a customer-managed key (CMK) for encryption, addresses
encryption at rest, but does not prevent deletion or overwriting, meaning it does not satisfy immutability. Key
rotation has no effect on the data's deletion or overwrite status. Finally, Option D, applying a project-level
constraint, has the same weakness as Option B: it sets a minimum, not a specific duration, for all buckets in a
project. Only option A utilizes a locked retention policy on the specific bucket. This policy type achieves the
exact level of immutability required.
Authoritative Links:
Cloud Storage Retention Policies: https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/retention-policies
Locking Retention Policies: https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/locking-retention-policies
Organizational Constraints: https://cloud.google.com/resource-manager/docs/organization-
policy/understanding-constraints
Question: 198 CertyIQ
Your company has decided to make a major revision of their API in order to create better experiences for their
developers. They need to keep the old version of the API available and deployable, while allowing new customers
and testers to try out the new API. They want to keep the same SSL and DNS records in place to serve both APIs.
What should they do?
A.Configure a new load balancer for the new version of the API
B.Reconfigure old clients to use a new endpoint for the new API
C.Have the old API forward traffic to the new API based on the path
D.Use separate backend pools for each API path behind the load balancer
Answer: D

Explanation:
The correct answer is D: Use separate backend pools for each API path behind the load balancer. This
approach allows you to maintain both the old and new API versions simultaneously, under the same SSL and
DNS records. By configuring a load balancer to route traffic based on the URL path, requests to the old API
path will be directed to its corresponding backend pool, while requests to the new API path will be routed to
the new API’s backend pool. This is achieved using path-based routing, a common technique in load balancing
for managing different versions or functionalities of an application. Option A, while feasible, would require a
different DNS record or SSL, which the requirements specifically avoid. Option B is not suitable because the
goal is to support existing clients to the old API. Option C is inefficient as it puts a load on the old API. This
ensures that existing clients using the old API are not disrupted and new clients or testers can easily access
the new API without affecting the other.
Authoritative Links:
Google Cloud Load Balancing Overview: https://cloud.google.com/load-balancing/docs/load-balancing-
overview
URL Map Overview (for path-based routing): https://cloud.google.com/load-balancing/docs/url-map
Backend Services and Backend Pools: https://cloud.google.com/load-balancing/docs/backend-service
Question: 199 CertyIQ
You have a Compute Engine application that you want to autoscale when total memory usage exceeds 80%. You
have installed the Cloud Monitoring agent and configured the autoscaling policy as follows:
You observe that the application does not scale under high load. You want to resolve this. What should you do?
A.Change the Target type to DELTA_PER_MINUTE.
B.Change the Metric identifier to agent.googleapis.com/memory/bytes_used.
C.Change the filter to metric.label.state = ‘used’.
D.Change the filter to metric.label.state = ‘free’ and the Target utilization to 20.
Answer: C
Explanation:
C. Change the filter to metric.label.state = ‘used’. The current filter is set up with multiple AND conditions,
which means it's looking for a metric that simultaneously has all these states: 'used', 'buffered', 'cached', and
'slab'. This is logically impossible, as a memory location can't be in multiple states at once. Therefore, the
filter will never match any metrics, and the autoscaling policy won't trigger.

Question: 200 CertyIQ
Your company has a Google Cloud project that uses BigOuery for data warehousing. The VPN tunnel between the
on-premises environment and Google Cloud is configured with Cloud VPN. Your security team wants to avoid data
exfiltration by malicious insiders, compromised code, and accidental oversharing. What should you do?
A.Configure Private Service Connect.
B.Configure VPC Service Controls and configure Private Google Access for on-promises hosts.
C.Create a service account, grant the BigQuery JobUser role and Storage Object Viewer role to the service
account, and remove all other Identity and Access Management (IAM) access from the project.
D.Configure Private Google Access.
Answer: B
Explanation:
The correct answer is B: Configure VPC Service Controls and configure Private Google Access for on-
premises hosts. Here's a detailed justification:
Data exfiltration prevention requires a multi-layered approach, focusing on both network-level security and
resource-level access controls. VPC Service Controls (VPC SC) allows you to define a security perimeter
around Google Cloud resources, like BigQuery, limiting data access to authorized networks and identities. This
mitigates the risk of unauthorized access, even if an attacker gains credentials. VPC SC implements context-
aware access, only allowing requests from within the defined perimeter.https://cloud.google.com/vpc-service-
controls/docs/overview
Since the company's on-premises environment needs to access BigQuery, configuring Private Google Access
for on-premises hosts is crucial. Private Google Access enables on-premises systems to communicate with
Google Cloud services, including BigQuery, over a private network (e.g., the Cloud VPN tunnel), without
traversing the public internet. This further reduces the attack surface and improves security. VPC SC and
Private Google Access work in tandem to provide a comprehensive data exfiltration prevention solution.
https://cloud.google.com/vpc/docs/private-access-options
Option A, Private Service Connect, is mainly for privately accessing services hosted in other VPC networks,
not necessarily for controlling data exfiltration from your own project by internal actors. Option C,
manipulating service account roles, while a good practice, is insufficient. Restricting access to only one
service account doesn't prevent an attacker who compromises that service account from exfiltrating data
within the allowed roles. Option D, Private Google Access, only secures the network connection. It does not
enforce context-aware access controls to prevent data exfiltration by authorized but malicious users or
compromised code. Hence, option B provides a much more comprehensive solution for the given scenario. VPC
SC prevents data egress by enforcing perimeters, and Private Google Access gives secure private
connectivity to BigQuery from on-prem.
Question: 201 CertyIQ
You are managing a few hundred Ubuntu and Red Hat Enterprise Linux virtual machines running on Google
Compute Engine (GCE). You need to patch the operating systems for those VMs periodically in a secure and
scalable way. What should you do?
A. Create a Cloud Scheduler job to run a Bash script that securely connects to each VM and applies the
patches.
B. Configure Config Sync, and install the Ops Agent on each VM. Schedule a patch job to apply patches on each
VM.
C. Schedule a Cloud Build job, and use Cloud Deploy to run a patch job that applies patches on each VM.

D. Set up VM Manager, and install the OS Config agent on each VM. Schedule a patch job to apply patches on
each VM.
Answer: D
Explanation:
D — Set up VM Manager, and install the OS Config agent on each VM. Schedule a patch job to apply patches
on each VM.
When managing hundreds of Ubuntu and RHEL VMs on Google Compute Engine, you need a solution that is:
Secure (no SSH key sprawl or custom scripts)
Scalable (works across many VMs)
Automated & manageable (centralized patching, reporting, and scheduling)
Google Cloud provides a native service that does exactly this: VM Manager (OS Config).
Question: 202 CertyIQ
Your company uses Salesforce for customer relationship management (CRM). ServiceNow for IT service
management, and a Cloud SQL database to store customer transaction data You need to seamlessly connect, map.
and transform data between these systems to ensure data consistency and enable real-time reporting. You want to
follow Google-recommended practices. What should you do?
A. Leverage Workflows to connect the services and transform the data.
B. Leverage Application Integration to connect the services and transform the data.
C. Leverage Pub/Sub and BigQuery to connect the services and transform the data.
D. Leverage Pub/Sub and Datastream to connect the services and transform the data.
Answer: B
Explanation:
B. Leverage Application Integration to connect the services and transform the data.
Google Cloud's Application Integration is a fully managed, no-code/low-code Integration-Platform-as-a-
Service (iPaaS) solution designed specifically for integrating various business systems, such as CRM, ITSM,
and databases.
Question: 203 CertyIQ
You are designing the architecture for a global social media site on Google Cloud. The site serves dynamic API
content, static assets such as CSS. JS. images, and user-uploaded videos for streaming. You need to minimize
latency for all content types for users worldwide. What should you do? (Choose two.)
A. Use an external Application Load Balancer to serve your application APIs.
B. Use Cloud CDN to serve static assets of your application.
C. Use Media CDN to serve static assets of your application
D. Use Memorystore to serve your web application.
E. Use a multi-region Cloud Storage bucket to serve your entire web application.

Answer: AB
Explanation:
A. External Application Load Balancer .
It is globally distributed and uses Google’s premium global network
Routes users to the nearest healthy backend
Ideal for dynamic API traffic
Supports HTTPS, TLS termination, and advanced routing
This is the recommended front end for global, latency-sensitive web applications.
B. Cloud CDN .
Caches static assets (CSS, JS, images) at Google’s edge locations
Reduces latency and backend load
Integrates natively with the external Application Load Balancer
Serves users from the closest edge point of presence
Question: 204 CertyIQ
A Cloud Run service running your serverless application is unable to connect to an AlloyDB database created with
default configurations. You need to troubleshoot and resolve the issue as quickly as possible. What should you do?
A. Verify that the Cloud Run service and AlloyDB instance are in the same region.
B. Enable Direct VPC egress for the Cloud Run service, and send traffic directly to a VPC.
C. Disable the default public IP address of the AlloyDB instance, and use the private IP address in the
connection string.
D. Create a Cloud SQL instance, and migrate the AlloyDB database to PostgreSQL on Cloud SQL.
Answer: B
Explanation:
