B — Enable Direct VPC egress for the Cloud Run service, and send traffic directly to a VPC
By default, a Cloud Run service:
Runs in a fully managed, serverless environment
Does not have direct access to your VPC network
Uses public egress unless configured otherwise
Meanwhile, an AlloyDB instance created with default settings:
Uses a private IP
Is accessible only from within a VPC
This network mismatch prevents Cloud Run from connecting to AlloyDB.

Question: 205 CertyIQ
Your company is building containerized applications as part of their CI/CD pipeline. To improve the security and
maintainability of the build process, you need to:
•Identify potential vulnerabilities within your container images.
•Generate verifiable metadata about the builds for auditing and compliance.
•Create a comprehensive inventory of your application’s dependencies
What should you do?
A. Use Cloud Build to build container images, and then trigger Artifact Analysis on images pushed to Artifact
Registry.
B. Use Cloud Build to build container images, trigger Binary Authorization, and use Cloud Asset Inventory for
tracking and analysis.
C. Use Cloud Build to build container images, push the images to Artifact Registry, and use Security Command
Center for tracking and analysis.
D. Use Cloud Build to build container images, trigger Binary Authorization, and use Security Command Center
for tracking and analysis.
Answer: A
Explanation:
A — Use Cloud Build to build container images, and then trigger Artifact Analysis on images pushed to
Artifact Registry.
You need to address three specific goals in your CI/CD pipeline:
Identify potential vulnerabilities in container images
Generate verifiable metadata for auditing and compliance
Create a comprehensive inventory of application dependencies
Question: 206 CertyIQ
Your team is running applications on a Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) cluster with a private endpoint. You've set
up a Cloud Deploy pipeline, but deployments to the GKE cluster are failing. You need to resolve the issue. What
should you do?
A. Use VPC Service Controls with Cloud Build Update the Cloud pipeline to use Cloud Build as its execution
environment.
B. Create a Cloud Build private pool in the default VPC. Use Cloud Build to deploy the applications to the GKE
cluster.
C. Create a Cloud Build private pool that is peered with the same VPC network as your GKE cluster. Update the
Cloud Deploy pipeline to use this private pool as its execution environment.
D. Create a custom target in Cloud Deploy Update the deploy pipeline to use the custom target for the
application deployment.
Answer: C
Explanation:
C. Create a Cloud Build private pool that is peered with the same VPC network as your GKE cluster. Update
the Cloud Deploy pipeline to use this private pool as its execution environment.

When a GKE cluster has a private endpoint, its control plane is only accessible from within its own Virtual
Private Cloud (VPC) network or peered networks. The default execution environment for Cloud Deploy is a
Google-managed, multi-tenant infrastructure which does not have access to your private VPC network.
To resolve this connectivity issue, you must configure a private execution
Question: 207 CertyIQ
You are planning to migrate your on-premises compute and SAP workloads to Google Cloud. You want to follow
Google-recommended practices to quickly create a cost estimate for running these workloads in Google Cloud.
What should you do?
A. Leverage Cloud Asset Inventory to gather data and generate a cost estimate.
B. Use the Google Cloud pricing calculator, and input the estimated resource to generate a cost estimate.
C. Engage with a Google Cloud partner to perform a comprehensive assessment and provide a customized cost
estimate.
D. Gather data about your current environment, and leverage Google Cloud Migration Center to generate a cost
estimate.
Answer: C
Explanation:
Engage with a Google Cloud partner to perform a comprehensive assessment and provide a customized cost
estimate.
Question: 208 CertyIQ
Your organization has a significant amount of log data stored in Cloud Logging. The data engineering team is
accustomed to using SQL for analysis and wants the ability to create insightful dashboards for visualizing log
trends and patterns. You want to follow the recommendations of the Google Cloud Well-Architected Framework to
provide a solution for the data engineering team. What should you do?
A. Create a log sink, and export the data to BigQuery using Pub/Sub. Run queries and visualize the data with
Cloud Monitoring dashboards.
B. Enable log analytics and run queries in Cloud Monitoring. Visualize the data using Vertex AI workbench.
C. Enable log analytics and run queries in the linked log dataset in BigQuery. Visualize the data with Looker
Studio dashboards.
D. Create a log sink, and export the data to a storage bucket. Create an external table in BigQuery for the data
in the bucket. Run queries and visualize the data with Cloud Monitoring dashboards.
Answer: C
Explanation:
Log Analytics: Google Cloud's Log Analytics feature, powered by BigQuery, allows you to analyze logs in
place using standard SQL without needing to set up complex export pipelines for every query. The data
remains in Cloud Logging buckets, which are then accessible as linked datasets in BigQuery.
SQL for Analysis: This directly addresses the data engineering team's requirement to use SQL for analysis,
leveraging BigQuery's powerful, serverless analytics capabilities on petabyte-scale data.
Looker Studio for Visualization: Looker Studio (formerly Google Data Studio) has a native connector for

BigQuery, making it the recommended tool for creating interactive dashboards and visualizing the analysis
results.
Question: 209 CertyIQ
You are designing the storage architecture for a financial analytics platform. The platform ingests and stores
terabytes of transactional data daily, which is used for both real-time fraud detection and long-term historical
analysis. Transaction data from the last 30 days must be accessible with very low latency for the fraud detection
engine. Data older than 30 days is accessed infrequently for quarterly reports, where retrieval times of a few
seconds are acceptable. All data must be retained for five years to meet compliance regulations. You need to
design a solution as cost-effective as possible. What should you do?
A. Store all transaction data in a Cloud Storage bucket using the Standard storage class for the entire five-year
retention period.
B. Ingest all data into BigQuery using time-partitioned tables, and rely on BigQuery’s automatic long-term
storage pricing for data older than 90 days.
C. Configure a Cloud Storage bucket with an Object Lifecycle Management policy to transition data from the
Standard class to the Archive class after 30 days.
D. Configure a Cloud Storage bucket with an Object Lifecycle Management policy to transition data from the
Standard class to the Coldline class after 30 days.
Answer: D
Explanation:
D — Configure a Cloud Storage bucket with an Object Lifecycle Management policy to transition data from
the Standard class to the Coldline class after 30 days.
Your platform needs to handle very different access patterns:
Last 30 days of data
Very low latency
Used for real-time fraud detection
Data older than 30 days
Accessed infrequently
Retrieval time of a few seconds is acceptable
Retention
All data must be kept for 5 years
Cost
Must be as cost-effective as possible
Question: 210 CertyIQ
You are architecting a new application feature for a healthcare provider based in Qatar. The feature needs to
summarize sensitive patient notes that are submitted by clinicians. A critical requirement is that the content of
these patient notes must never be processed outside of Qatar's borders. You want to use a powerful, pre-trained
generative model for the summarization task while strictly adhering to the data residency constraint. What should

you do?
Answer:
B
Explanation:
B. Use Vertex AI Model Garden to select a Gemma model. Deploy this model to a Vertex AI Endpoint within a
Google Cloud region located in Qatar. This option allows selecting an existing, powerful model (like Gemma).
It also allows deploying it within a specific Google Cloud region in Qatar. Processing data in this region meets
the data residency requirement, ensuring sensitive information stays local
Question: 211 CertyIQ
You are migrating a critical on-premises inventory management application to Google Cloud. The application is a
monolith with a traditional relational database, and the immediate business goal is a rapid data center exit. The
monolith is exposing an API to other business critical applications.
The long-term vision is to modernize the application into globally distributed, cloud-native services to support the
company’s expansion. You need to design the initial cloud architecture to ensure that future modernization causes
the least possible disruption to other applications that depend on inventory data. The future modernization might
require the API to change structure. What should you do?
A. Use Service Directory to register the monolith's endpoint, allowing dependent applications to look up its
address and connect directly.
B. Implement a managed API facade with Apigee to handle all requests from dependent applications on behalf
of the monolith’s backend.
C. Use an internal load balancer to provide a stable IP for dependent applications to connect directly to the
monolith's native API.
D. Provide dependent applications with direct database access by creating secured SQL VIEWs on Cloud SQL
for them to query.
Answer: B
Explanation:
Decoupling and Abstraction: An API Management platform like Apigee acts as a layer of abstraction between
the dependent applications and the backend monolith. This decouples the frontend (consumers) from the
backend (the inventory application).
Future-proofing: The key requirement is to ensure future modernization causes minimal disruption. Apigee
allows you to manage the API lifecycle, meaning you can change the underlying backend services (e.g., as you
break the monolith into microservices) while maintaining a consistent, versioned API interface for consumers.
Managing API Changes: When the monolith is modernized, the native API is likely to change. Apigee can
manage these changes, potentially by offering multiple API versions simultaneously or by transforming
requests/responses to ensure backward compatibility for older clients.
Controlled Access: It provides a centralized point for managing access, security
(authentication/authorization), traffic management, and monitoring for all incoming API requests.
Question: 212 CertyIQ
Your organization uses separate Google Cloud projects for shared services, development, testing, and production.

•The shared services project hosts your private CI/CD runners and a central Artifact Registry
•The development, testing, and production projects host the GKE clusters where applications are deployed.
You need to design an architecture that allows the CI/CD runners to connect to the GKE clusters and the clusters
to pull images from Artifact Registry, all using private IP addresses. However, direct network traffic between the
development, testing, and production environments must be strictly prohibited. What should you do?
A. Create a separate VPC in each of the four projects. Connect each environment's VPC to the shared services
VPC through VPC Network Peering.
B. Expose the resources in the shared services project using an external load balancer. Implement a firewall
rule to limit access.
C. Create a separate VPC in each project. Use VPC Network Peering to create a full mesh, connecting every
VPC directly to every other VPC.
D. Configure the shared services project as a Shared VPC host. Create a single VPC in this host project and
attach the environment projects as service projects.
Answer: A
Explanation:
VPC Separation for Isolation: Creating a separate Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) for each environment
(development, testing, production) ensures strict network isolation. This inherently prevents direct network
traffic between the different environments, satisfying a key requirement of the prompt .
Centralized Shared Services: The shared services VPC hosts common resources like the CI/CD runners and
Artifact Registry.
VPC Network Peering for Controlled Connectivity: VPC Network Peering allows private IP communication
between the distinct VPCs (e.g., development VPC can peer with the shared services VPC). This enables the
runners to access GKE clusters and the clusters to pull images from the Artifact Registry using private IP
addresses.
Controlled Access: Firewall rules can be implemented within each environment's VPC to explicitly permit only
necessary traffic from the shared services VPC's ranges (e.g., allowing port 22/SSH for runners to connect to
nodes, and the necessary API/image pull traffic].
Question: 213 CertyIQ
You are designing the network architecture for a public-facing, containerized web application deployed on Cloud
Run. All incoming traffic must be inspected by a Cloud Armor web application firewall (WAF) before reaching the
application You plan to use an Application Load Balancer, which will have the Cloud Armor policy attached. You
must ensure that all public requests pass through the load balancer and any attempt to access the Cloud Run
service directly through its default *.run.app URL is blocked. What should you do?
A. Enable Identity-Aware Proxy (IAP) directly on the Cloud Run service to intercept and validate all incoming
requests
B. Create a DNS entry to route traffic to Cloud Armor. Configure Cloud Armor to deny traffic from unknown IP
addresses
C. Set the Cloud Run ingress to Allow internal traffic and Cloud Load Balancing, and use a serverless NEG
backend on the load balancer
D. Configure a VPC firewall rule with a high priority to deny all traffic that does not originate from the load
balancer
Answer: D
Explanation:

Configure a VPC firewall rule with a high priority to deny all traffic that does not originate from the load
balancer.
Question: 214 CertyIQ
To improve governance and security, your organization has structured the Google Cloud environment using folders
for different business units. Each business unit folder has subfolders for development, staging, and production
environments, which must comply with internal security controls:
•Production workloads must be protected from direct internet ingress by default unless explicitly tagged.
•The application must be accessible to customers over HTTPS.
You need to design a scalable and enforceable model that blocks internet ingress traffic to the production folders
while selectively allowing direct HTTPS traffic to the necessary virtual machines. You must also ensure that
individual project teams cannot overwrite these controls once they are implemented for all current and future
production projects. What should you do?
A. At each production folder, apply a hierarchical firewall policy to deny all ingress except for HTTPS to tagged
VMs.
B. Mandate the application teams to deploy a Terraform module to create VPC firewall rules in each project
that deny ingress and allow HTTPS.
C. At the organization root, apply a hierarchical firewall policy to deny all ingress except for HTTPS to tagged
VMs.
D. At each production folder, use an organization policy to block all external IPs and require teams to use
external HTTPS load balancers.
Answer: A
Explanation:
At each production folder, apply a hierarchical firewall policy to deny all ingress except for HTTPS to tagged
VMs.
Detailed Reasoning
To meet the requirements of enforcement, scalability, and prevention of project-level overrides, hierarchical
firewall policies are the designated tool:
Hierarchical Enforcement: Hierarchical firewall policies are applied at the folder or organization level. Rules
defined at a folder level are evaluated before any project-level VPC firewall rules. This ensures that project
teams cannot overwrite these controls with their own local rules.
Targeting and Selectivity: By applying the policy at each production folder (rather than the organization root
as in Option C), you can specifically target production environments without unintentionally impacting
development or staging folders.
Question: 215 CertyIQ
You are designing a new insurance claims processing application that will be deployed on Google Kubernetes
Engine (GKE) Your company’s compliance team requires a complete and non-repudiable audit trail for all
administrative actions from day one. Your application must capture who deploys a new container image, who
modifies the GKE cluster's configuration, and who interacts with running pods or Kubernetes secrets using
kubectl. What should you do?
A. Enable Binary Authorization on the GKE cluster, and create a policy that requires all deployed container
images to be signed by a trusted attestor.

B. Deploy a DaemonSet to every node in the GKE cluster that runs a logging agent to collect and forward all
container logs to Cloud Logging.
C. Enable GKE Audit Logging to send Kubernetes API server logs to Cloud Logging, and ensure Cloud Audit
Logs are enabled for the project.
D. Activate the Security Command Center Premium tier to analyze GKE logs and detect threats, vulnerabilities,
and misconfigurations in real time.
Answer: C
Explanation:
Enable GKE Audit Logging to send Kubernetes API server logs to Cloud Logging, and ensure Cloud Audit Logs
are enabled for the project.
Reasoning
Comprehensive Coverage: GKE Audit Logs record all interactions with the Kubernetes API server. This
includes:
Deployments: Captures who creates or updates deployments (deploys new container images).
Cluster Modifications: Records changes to cluster configuration via the API.
kubectl Interactions: Every command executed via kubectl (e.g., kubectl exec into pods or kubectl get
secrets) generates an audit entry.
Question: 216 CertyIQ
Your product team is building a critical, customer-facing application on Google Cloud. The development team
wants to use Spanner for their database to take advantage of its horizontal scalability and low operational
overhead However, the FinOps team is concerned about the direct monthly cost of Spanner and proposed using a
self-managed PostgreSQL database on Compute Engine VMs instead. You need to resolve this conflict and ensure
the project moves forward with an architecturally sound database choice that balances technical requirements
with financial constraints. What should you do?
A. Provide the development team with a reference architecture for deploying a highly available PostgreSQL
cluster on a regional managed instance group (MIG).
B. Suggest using Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL as a compromise to get a managed service at a lower cost than
Spanner.
C. Develop a total cost of ownership (TCO) analysis that includes operational overhead, and present it in a
workshop to facilitate a decision.
D. Cite the reliability and performance optimization pillars of the Google Cloud Well-Architected Framework to
formally justify the use of Spanner.
Answer: C
Explanation:
C. Develop a total cost of ownership (TCO) analysis that includes operational overhead, and present it in a
workshop to facilitate a decision.
Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) vs. Direct Cost: The FinOps team is concerned about "direct monthly cost" (the
raw line items on a bill). However, a true architectural decision must account for "operational overhead," which
includes the labor costs of managing a database (patching, backups, high availability setup), the cost of
potential downtime, and the technical debt of self-managed systems.
Data-Driven Decision Making: Developing a TCO analysis provides a quantitative, evidence-based foundation

for a decision, rather than just citing abstract frameworks.
Stakeholder Alignment: Presenting this analysis in a workshop ensures that both the technical (development)
and financial (FinOps) teams are aligned on the trade-offs between managed services (like Spanner) and self-
managed options.
Question: 217 CertyIQ
You are deploying a critical application with a stateless, containerized frontend on Cloud Run and a Cloud SQL for
PostgreSQL backend. The application experiences unpredictable traffic spikes, and the business requires the
ability to immediately roll back a failed deployment to the last known good state. You need to apply a deployment
strategy that aligns with Site Reliability Engineering (SRE) principles for both the application code and the
database schema updates, while meeting the business's requirements. What should you do?
A. Package the database schema migration script within the container to be executed on every container
startup before the application process begins.
B. Configure the CI/CD pipeline to use the :latest container tag for deployments, with database schema
changes applied manually as needed.
C. Separate CI/CD pipelines for database schema migrations from application deployments. When deploying a
new Cloud Run revision, use gradual traffic split.
D. Use a single CI/CD pipeline that first applies database schema changes and then deploys the new Cloud Run
revision.
Answer: B
Explanation:
Configure the CI/CD pipeline to use the :latest container tag for deployments, with database schema changes
applied manually as needed.
Question: 218 CertyIQ
The JencoMart security team requires that all Google Cloud Platform infrastructure is deployed using a least
privilege model with separation of duties for administration between production and development resources.
What Google domain and project structure should you recommend?
A. Create two G Suite accounts to manage users: one for development/test/staging and one for production.
Each account should contain one project for every application
B. Create two G Suite accounts to manage users: one with a single project for all development applications and
one with a single project for all production applications
C. Create a single G Suite account to manage users with each stage of each application in its own project
D. Create a single G Suite account to manage users with one project for the development/test/staging
environment and one project for the production environment
Answer: C
Explanation:
The correct answer is C. Here's why:
Option C, "Create a single G Suite account to manage users with each stage of each application in its own
project," aligns best with the principle of least privilege and separation of duties. Using a single Google
Workspace (formerly G Suite) organization provides a centralized identity management system. This simplifies
user management and ensures consistent policies.

By placing each stage of each application (e.g., development, testing, staging, production) into separate
Google Cloud Projects, we achieve strong isolation. This allows for fine-grained access controls using Identity
and Access Management (IAM). Each team or role can be granted only the specific permissions necessary for
the project they're responsible for. This minimizes the risk of accidental or malicious changes in one
environment affecting another. For example, developers can have full control over the development project
but read-only access to production.
Option A incorrectly proposes using multiple Google Workspace accounts, which complicates user
management, policy enforcement, and introduces additional overhead. Option B also suggests two Workspace
accounts and also combines all application stages within a single project, which violates the separation of
duties principle. Option D combines all development-related stages in one project, also failing to enforce the
separation of duties at a granular level.
Option C ensures that security policies can be effectively tailored for each environment, such as stricter
policies on production compared to development. This granularity is crucial for a robust security posture. This
aligns with Google's best practices for project organization.
Further Research:
Google Cloud Resource Hierarchy: https://cloud.google.com/resource-manager/docs/cloud-platform-
resource-hierarchy
IAM Best Practices: https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/best-practices-iam
Organization best practices: https://cloud.google.com/architecture/framework/security/best-practices
Question: 219 CertyIQ
A few days after JencoMart migrates the user credentials database to Google Cloud Platform and shuts down the
old server, the new database server stops responding to SSH connections. It is still serving database requests to
the application servers correctly.
What three steps should you take to diagnose the problem? (Choose three.)
A. Delete the virtual machine (VM) and disks and create a new one
B. Delete the instance, attach the disk to a new VM, and investigate
C. Take a snapshot of the disk and connect to a new machine to investigate
D. Check inbound firewall rules for the network the machine is connected to
E. Connect the machine to another network with very simple firewall rules and investigate
F. Print the Serial Console output for the instance for troubleshooting, activate the interactive console, and
investigate
Answer: CDF
Explanation:
Okay, here's a detailed justification for why options C, D, and F are the correct steps to diagnose the SSH
connection issue, along with why the other options are incorrect:
Correct Options:
C. Take a snapshot of the disk and connect to a new machine to investigate: This is a crucial first step for
troubleshooting. By creating a snapshot, you preserve the current state of the disk and its data. This allows
you to investigate the issue without risking further damage or data loss on the original instance. You can then
attach the snapshot to a new VM for analysis. This ensures a safe and non-destructive approach to diagnosing
the problem. (Link to GCP snapshot documentation)
D. Check inbound firewall rules for the network the machine is connected to: Firewall rules are a primary
cause of connectivity problems. SSH operates on port 22 (or a custom port), and a misconfigured or missing

firewall rule preventing incoming traffic on this port could explain why SSH connections are failing. It's
essential to review the rules for the network to which the database server belongs. (Link to GCP firewall rules
documentation)
F. Print the Serial Console output for the instance for troubleshooting, activate the interactive console, and
investigate: The Serial Console is a very useful tool for troubleshooting unresponsive VMs. It allows you to
access the console output of the machine without requiring SSH access. This is vital for diagnosing problems
that occur early in the boot process or prevent the SSH daemon from starting. Activating the interactive
console also provides a way to potentially login and investigate. (Link to GCP Serial Console documentation)
Incorrect Options:
A. Delete the virtual machine (VM) and disks and create a new one: Deleting the VM and disks is drastic and
should be avoided in troubleshooting scenarios. You'd lose valuable information needed for diagnosing the
root cause. It also introduces unnecessary downtime and is not a best practice for problem resolution. This is
also a destructive operation, which could lead to data loss.
B. Delete the instance, attach the disk to a new VM, and investigate: While this is less destructive than A,
deleting the instance before trying other less invasive troubleshooting steps is not recommended. You still
lose the original machine's state. It is more efficient to work with the serial console and snapshot approach for
troubleshooting purposes initially.
E. Connect the machine to another network with very simple firewall rules and investigate: Changing
network configurations is a more disruptive step and can introduce new issues. It also changes the
environment, making it more difficult to isolate the original problem. It's more effective to analyze the existing
network configuration to ensure the correct rules are in place for the server.
In Summary: The best approach to diagnosing an SSH connectivity problem is to begin with the least
disruptive steps: examining firewall rules, checking the serial console output, and taking a snapshot before
making any drastic changes like deleting or moving the machine. These steps allow you to gather information
about the problem, maintaining the original server state, while making a safe path for testing.
Question: 220 CertyIQ
JencoMart has decided to migrate user profile storage to Google Cloud Datastore and the application servers to
Google Compute Engine (GCE). During the migration, the existing infrastructure will need access to Datastore to
upload the data.
What service account key-management strategy should you recommend?
A. Provision service account keys for the on-premises infrastructure and for the GCE virtual machines (VMs)
B. Authenticate the on-premises infrastructure with a user account and provision service account keys for the
VMs
C. Provision service account keys for the on-premises infrastructure and use Google Cloud Platform (GCP)
managed keys for the VMs
D. Deploy a custom authentication service on GCE/Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) for the on-premises
infrastructure and use GCP managed keys for the VMs
Answer: C
Explanation:
The correct answer is C. Provisioning service account keys for the on-premises infrastructure and using
Google Cloud Platform (GCP) managed keys for the Compute Engine VMs is the most secure and practical
approach. On-premises infrastructure needs explicit credentials to access GCP resources, hence service
account keys are required. Directly embedding these keys into the application on the on-prem side can be
risky if the system gets compromised. However, using GCP managed keys for VMs leverages Google's
automatic key rotation and eliminates the need for manually managing sensitive credentials. Compute Engine

instances can obtain authentication tokens automatically through the metadata server using the instance's
service account, offering a more secure and convenient method. Option A is less secure as it requires
managing keys for both on-prem and GCE VMs, creating more attack vectors. Option B is inappropriate
because user accounts shouldn't be used for service-to-service authentication; they should be reserved for
human users. Option D is an unnecessary complexity, as GCP managed keys readily fulfill the purpose for GCE
instances. Furthermore, deploying a custom authentication service adds overhead and complexity without
clear security benefits over standard GCP practices. Using GCP managed keys provides a secure and less
complex solution for authenticating applications running on Compute Engine. In summary, option C strikes the
best balance between security and practicality for this hybrid scenario.
Relevant links for further research:
Service accounts
Compute Engine service accounts
IAM best practices
Authentication overview
Question: 221 CertyIQ
JencoMart has built a version of their application on Google Cloud Platform that serves traffic to Asia. You want to
measure success against their business and technical goals.
Which metrics should you track?
A. Error rates for requests from Asia
B. Latency difference between US and Asia
C. Total visits, error rates, and latency from Asia
D. Total visits and average latency for users from Asia
E. The number of character sets present in the database
Answer: C
Explanation:
Option C, "Total visits, error rates, and latency from Asia," is the most appropriate choice for measuring
success against JencoMart's business and technical goals in Asia. Total visits directly correlate with business
reach and customer engagement, crucial for understanding market penetration. Error rates are fundamental
for assessing the application's stability and reliability, indicating if the service is functioning as expected.
High error rates negatively impact user experience and can lead to lost business. Latency, or response time, is
a critical technical metric affecting user satisfaction. Slow response times can cause user frustration and
abandonment. Tracking these three metrics provides a holistic view, encompassing both business and
technical performance aspects in the targeted geographical region. The other options are either incomplete
or irrelevant. Option A only covers errors, missing crucial business insights. Option B is comparative, not
measuring actual performance in Asia. Option D lacks error rate measurement which is a critical metric.
Option E is about internal database properties which are not user facing and does not contribute towards
business and technical performance.
Authoritative links for further research:
Google Cloud Observability: https://cloud.google.com/products/operations - Provides an overview of
Google's monitoring and logging tools.
Site Reliability Engineering (SRE) Principles: https://sre.google/ - Learn about best practices for measuring
and monitoring service performance.
Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) in Cloud: https://www.bmc.com/blogs/cloud-kpis/ - Details on identifying
meaningful metrics for cloud success.

Question: 222 CertyIQ
The migration of JencoMart's application to Google Cloud Platform (GCP) is progressing too slowly. The
infrastructure is shown in the diagram. You want to maximize throughput.
What are three potential bottlenecks? (Choose three.)
A. A single VPN tunnel, which limits throughput
B. A tier of Google Cloud Storage that is not suited for this task
C. A copy command that is not suited to operate over long distances
D. Fewer virtual machines (VMs) in GCP than on-premises machines
E. A separate storage layer outside the VMs, which is not suited for this task
F. Complicated internet connectivity between the on-premises infrastructure and GCP
Answer: ACF
Explanation:
Single VPN tunnel limits throughput. Copying 20TB across long distances is a big bottleneck. VPN across
internet cannot be relied upon for high performance
Question: 223 CertyIQ
JencoMart wants to move their User Profiles database to Google Cloud Platform.
Which Google Database should they use?
A. Cloud Spanner
B. Google BigQuery

C. Google Cloud SQL
D. Google Cloud Datastore
Answer: D
Explanation:
The most suitable database for JencoMart's User Profiles is Google Cloud Datastore. Cloud Datastore is a
highly scalable, fully managed, NoSQL document database, ideal for storing user profiles due to its flexibility
and ability to handle varied data structures. Unlike Cloud SQL, which is a relational database, Datastore
doesn't impose a rigid schema, making it easier to accommodate changes in user profile attributes. Cloud
Spanner, while globally scalable, is often overkill for user profiles and incurs higher costs. BigQuery is
designed for analytical workloads, not transactional user profile storage. Datastore's serverless nature
reduces operational overhead, allowing JencoMart to focus on application development. It also supports
strong consistency for reads and writes, ensuring data integrity. The automatic scaling capabilities ensure
performance remains consistent even with a growing user base. Datastore is cost-effective for this use case
due to its pay-per-use pricing model. Furthermore, it integrates seamlessly with other Google Cloud services.
https://cloud.google.com/datastore/docs/concepts/overviewhttps://cloud.google.com/datastore/docs/https://cloud.google.com/datastore/docs/pricing
Question: 224 CertyIQ
For this question, refer to the Helicopter Racing League (HRL) case study. Your team is in charge of creating a
payment card data vault for card numbers used to bill tens of thousands of viewers, merchandise consumers, and
season ticket holders. You need to implement a custom card tokenization service that meets the following
requirements:
* It must provide low latency at minimal cost.
* It must be able to identify duplicate credit cards and must not store plaintext card numbers.
* It should support annual key rotation.
Which storage approach should you adopt for your tokenization service?
A. Store the card data in Secret Manager after running a query to identify duplicates.
B. Encrypt the card data with a deterministic algorithm stored in Firestore using Datastore mode.
C. Encrypt the card data with a deterministic algorithm and shard it across multiple Memorystore instances.
D. Use column-level encryption to store the data in Cloud SQL.
Answer: B
Explanation:
The correct answer is B. Encrypt the card data with a deterministic algorithm stored in Firestore using
Datastore mode. This approach best aligns with the HRL's requirements for a low-latency, cost-effective
tokenization service that can detect duplicates, avoid storing plaintext card numbers, and supports annual
key rotation.
Here's why:
Deterministic Encryption: A deterministic algorithm always produces the same ciphertext for the same
plaintext and encryption key. This allows for efficient duplicate detection because if two credit cards are the
same, their encrypted values will also be the same. You can easily search for identical encrypted values within
your database to find duplicates, without decrypting them. This contrasts with non-deterministic encryption,
which results in different ciphertexts each time, making duplicate detection difficult.
Firestore (Datastore Mode): Firestore in Datastore mode provides a highly scalable, schemaless NoSQL
database that offers low latency and is well-suited for storing and retrieving large amounts of data, such as

credit card tokens. It is also very cost-effective compared to a relational database. Datastore mode provides
eventual consistency which is fine for this use case.
No Plaintext Storage: The deterministic algorithm ensures that plaintext credit card numbers are never
stored. Only the encrypted tokens are persisted in the database, meeting the requirement of not storing
plaintext card numbers.
Annual Key Rotation: While the question doesn't explicitly specify how the deterministic key will be stored
and rotated, Firestore will only be storing the encrypted tokens and a well-designed key management
approach can be used to easily decrypt and re-encrypt the data with a new key. This can be achieved through
a separate secret management system, where the encryption key is securely stored, and rotated annually.
When the key rotates, the stored encrypted values will be re-encrypted using the new key.
Why other options are not suitable:
Option A (Secret Manager): Secret Manager is designed for storing sensitive secrets like API keys or
passwords and doesn't function as a database for token storage and retrieval, which the application needs for
token creation and lookup. Furthermore, it doesn't inherently solve the duplicate detection.
Option C (Memorystore): Memorystore is an in-memory data store. While it provides low latency, its
ephemeral nature and cost implications make it unsuitable as the primary database for token storage with
high durability requirements for financial data. Sharding wouldn't improve the solution either.
Option D (Cloud SQL): While Cloud SQL can use column-level encryption, it doesn't offer the same ease for
duplicate detection as deterministic encryption. Additionally, Cloud SQL is a relational database, which would
be an overkill and more expensive for this use case compared to Firestore (Datastore mode).
Supporting Links:
Deterministic Encryption: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Deterministic_encryption
Firestore Datastore Mode: https://cloud.google.com/datastore
Firestore Pricing: https://cloud.google.com/firestore/pricing
Question: 225 CertyIQ
For this question, refer to the Helicopter Racing League (HRL) case study. Recently HRL started a new regional
racing league in Cape Town, South Africa. In an effort to give customers in Cape Town a better user experience,
HRL has partnered with the Content Delivery Network provider, Fastly. HRL needs to allow traffic coming from all
of the Fastly IP address ranges into their Virtual Private Cloud network (VPC network). You are a member of the
HRL security team and you need to configure the update that will allow only the Fastly IP address ranges through
the External HTTP(S) load balancer. Which command should you use?
A.
B.
C.

D.
Answer: D

Is D:
In the GCP doc can see the same example
https://cloud.google.com/armor/docs/configure-security-policies#gcloud_11
"gcloud compute security-policies rules create 1000 \

"
Question: 226 CertyIQ
For this question, refer to the Helicopter Racing League (HRL) case study. The HRL development team releases a
new version of their predictive capability application every Tuesday evening at 3 a.m. UTC to a repository. The
security team at HRL has developed an in-house penetration test Cloud Function called
Airwolf. The security team wants to run Airwolf against the predictive capability application as soon as it is
released every Tuesday. You need to set up Airwolf to run at the recurring weekly cadence. What should you do?
A. Set up Cloud Tasks and a Cloud Storage bucket that triggers a Cloud Function.
B. Set up a Cloud Logging sink and a Cloud Storage bucket that triggers a Cloud Function.
C. Configure the deployment job to notify a Pub/Sub queue that triggers a Cloud Function.
D. Set up Identity and Access Management (IAM) and Confidential Computing to trigger a Cloud Function.
Answer: C
Explanation:
The correct answer is C. Configure the deployment job to notify a Pub/Sub queue that triggers a Cloud
Function.
Here's why:
Requirement: The security team needs to automatically run the Airwolf Cloud Function immediately after the

new application version is released on Tuesdays at 3 a.m. UTC. This necessitates a reliable event-driven
approach.
Pub/Sub for Decoupling: Pub/Sub acts as a messaging middleware, decoupling the application deployment
process from the execution of the Airwolf function. When the deployment job finishes, it publishes a message
to a pre-configured Pub/Sub topic, signaling that the new version is available. This decouples the deployment
and security processes, ensuring that if one fails the other is not impacted.
Cloud Function Trigger: The Airwolf Cloud Function is configured to listen to the Pub/Sub topic and triggers
automatically upon receiving a message. This creates the desired "as soon as it is released" behavior.
Option A (Cloud Tasks): Cloud Tasks is designed for asynchronous task execution, particularly useful for
scheduling tasks, rather than immediate execution based on an event. While you could technically use Cloud
Tasks to trigger Airwolf on a schedule, it is less reliable as it would be less responsive to the actual release
than when the release completes.
Option B (Cloud Logging sink): Logging sinks are designed to route logs, not for triggering real-time actions
based on application events. While you could potentially trigger a Cloud Function off a log entry, it is far more
complicated and unreliable. It also would require parsing of log messages to determine when the release was
complete.
Option D (IAM and Confidential Computing): IAM and Confidential Computing address security and data
protection, they do not facilitate event-driven triggers of functions. They are entirely irrelevant to this
problem.
In summary: Pub/Sub provides an efficient, reliable, and decoupled approach for triggering Cloud Functions
based on events. In this scenario the deployment job acts as a producer, publishing to a Pub/Sub topic which
acts as the communication channel. Finally the Airwolf cloud function acts as a consumer listening for
messages on the topic. This provides an elegant method of solving the problem without requiring any complex
infrastructure or scheduling.
Authoritative Links:
Google Cloud Pub/Sub: https://cloud.google.com/pubsub/docs
Google Cloud Functions: https://cloud.google.com/functions/docs
Google Cloud Tasks: https://cloud.google.com/tasks/docs
Google Cloud Logging sinks: https://cloud.google.com/logging/docs/export/configure_export
Question: 227 CertyIQ
For this question, refer to the Helicopter Racing League (HRL) case study. HRL wants better prediction accuracy
from their ML prediction models. They want you to use Google's AI Platform so HRL can understand and interpret
the predictions. What should you do?
A. Use Explainable AI.
B. Use Vision AI.
C. Use Google Cloud's operations suite.
D. Use Jupyter Notebooks.
Answer: A
Explanation:
The correct answer is A. Use Explainable AI.

Here's the justification:
HRL's primary goal is to improve the prediction accuracy of their ML models and, crucially, to understand and
interpret these predictions. Explainable AI (XAI) is specifically designed for this purpose. It provides tools and
techniques to shed light on how a model arrives at its predictions, making the model's decision-making
process transparent. This is vital for HRL because understanding why a model makes certain predictions can
highlight areas for improvement in the underlying model or the data itself.
Option B, Vision AI, is geared towards image and video analysis, which is not relevant to the prediction goals
described in the scenario. Option C, Google Cloud's operations suite (formerly Stackdriver), focuses on
monitoring, logging, and performance management of infrastructure and applications, not model
interpretability. While Jupyter Notebooks (Option D) are excellent for data analysis and model building, they
do not inherently provide the model explainability that HRL requires.
Therefore, Explainable AI provides the direct features, such as feature attribution and saliency maps, needed
to meet HRL's specific requirement for increased interpretability of model predictions. With XAI, HRL can
identify which factors have the most impact on predictions and consequently enhance their models and trust
in the results.For more on Explainable AI, you can consult:
Google Cloud AI Platform Explainable AI Documentation: https://cloud.google.com/ai-
platform/docs/explainable-ai/overview
Google Cloud Blog on Explainable AI: https://cloud.google.com/blog/topics/ai-machine-learning/what-is-
explainable-ai
Question: 228 CertyIQ
For this question, refer to the Helicopter Racing League (HRL) case study. HRL is looking for a cost-effective
approach for storing their race data such as telemetry. They want to keep all historical records, train models using
only the previous season's data, and plan for data growth in terms of volume and information collected. You need
to propose a data solution. Considering HRL business requirements and the goals expressed by CEO S. Hawke,
what should you do?
A. Use Firestore for its scalable and flexible document-based database. Use collections to aggregate race data
by season and event.
B. Use Cloud Spanner for its scalability and ability to version schemas with zero downtime. Split race data using
season as a primary key.
C. Use BigQuery for its scalability and ability to add columns to a schema. Partition race data based on season.
D. Use Cloud SQL for its ability to automatically manage storage increases and compatibility with MySQL. Use
separate database instances for each season.
Answer: C
Explanation:
The correct answer is C, using BigQuery. Here's why: HRL needs a cost-effective and scalable solution for
storing and analyzing race data, which includes telemetry. They need to keep historical records, train models
on a per-season basis, and accommodate future data growth. BigQuery excels at these requirements.
BigQuery is a serverless, highly scalable data warehouse optimized for analytical workloads. Its column-
oriented storage and partitioning capabilities make it ideal for querying large datasets efficiently. Partitioning
the data by season allows for cost optimization and faster queries when analyzing data from a specific season,
which aligns perfectly with HRL's goal of training models on previous season's data. Furthermore, BigQuery
can handle schema changes, such as adding new columns, making it well-suited for the anticipated data
growth in terms of volume and information collected. While Firestore (A) is a flexible document database, it’s
primarily for operational applications rather than analytical workloads and large-scale data analysis. Cloud
Spanner (B) is suitable for transactional data needing strong consistency and high availability, not cost-

effective analytics of historical data. Cloud SQL (D) is a managed relational database suitable for
transactional workloads, not optimized for large-scale analytical queries. Therefore, BigQuery's strengths in
scalability, cost-effectiveness, and analytical capabilities make it the most appropriate choice for HRL's
needs, perfectly addressing S. Hawke's goals.
Authoritative Links:
BigQuery Overview: https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/introduction
BigQuery Partitioned Tables: https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/partitioned-tables
Choosing a Storage Option: https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/choosing-storage-option
Question: 229 CertyIQ
For this question, refer to the Helicopter Racing League (HRL) case study. A recent finance audit of cloud
infrastructure noted an exceptionally high number of
Compute Engine instances are allocated to do video encoding and transcoding. You suspect that these Virtual
Machines are zombie machines that were not deleted after their workloads completed. You need to quickly get a
list of which VM instances are idle. What should you do?
A. Log into each Compute Engine instance and collect disk, CPU, memory, and network usage statistics for
analysis.
B. Use the gcloud compute instances list to list the virtual machine instances that have the idle: true label set.
C. Use the gcloud recommender command to list the idle virtual machine instances.
D. From the Google Console, identify which Compute Engine instances in the managed instance groups are no
longer responding to health check probes.
Answer: C
Explanation:
The correct answer is C. Use the gcloud recommender command to list the idle virtual machine instances.
Here's why:
Option C leverages Google Cloud's built-in recommendation engine, specifically designed to identify resource
optimization opportunities. This includes identifying idle Compute Engine instances that are consuming
resources without active workloads. The gcloud recommender command is the appropriate tool for surfacing
these recommendations programmatically. This approach directly addresses the problem statement by
providing a quick and efficient method to list idle VMs.
Option A is impractical and time-consuming. Logging into each VM individually to collect metrics is a manual,
inefficient process that doesn't scale. It would require significant effort and would be difficult to automate.
Option B relies on a specific label ("idle: true"), which is not automatically applied by Google Cloud. The
problem statement indicates a suspicion of undeleted VMs, not a systematic labeling error. This method
would only work if such labels were already applied, which is highly unlikely in this scenario.
Option D focuses on health check probes within managed instance groups. While this can identify
unresponsive instances, it does not directly address the problem of identifying idle running VMs. VMs could
be passing health checks but still be idle from a workload perspective.
Therefore, using the gcloud recommender command is the most efficient and direct method for identifying idle
virtual machine instances as it utilizes Google's intelligent resource analysis, making it the correct and
preferred solution. This also avoids unnecessary manual effort and focuses on the desired outcome.
Authoritative Links:

gcloud recommender: https://cloud.google.com/sdk/gcloud/reference/recommender
Recommender Overview: https://cloud.google.com/recommender/docs/overview
Compute Engine Recommendations: https://cloud.google.com/recommender/docs/compute-
recommendations
Question: 230 CertyIQ
For this question, refer to the EHR Healthcare case study. You are responsible for ensuring that EHR's use of
Google Cloud will pass an upcoming privacy compliance audit. What should you do? (Choose two.)
A. Verify EHR's product usage against the list of compliant products on the Google Cloud compliance page.
B. Advise EHR to execute a Business Associate Agreement (BAA) with Google Cloud.
C. Use Firebase Authentication for EHR's user facing applications.
D. Implement Prometheus to detect and prevent security breaches on EHR's web-based applications.
E. Use GKE private clusters for all Kubernetes workloads.
Answer: AB
Explanation:
Here's a detailed justification for why options A and B are the correct choices for ensuring EHR's Google
Cloud usage passes a privacy compliance audit, referencing the EHR Healthcare case study context:
Option A: Verify EHR's product usage against the list of compliant products on the Google Cloud
compliance page.
This step is crucial because regulatory compliance, like HIPAA in the healthcare industry (which would be
relevant to EHR), often dictates which specific services can handle protected data. Google Cloud offers a
variety of services, but not all are certified for specific compliance standards. By checking the official Google
Cloud compliance page, EHR can ensure that they are using only the services that have been audited and
approved for their compliance needs. This ensures that the underlying infrastructure and services adhere to
required privacy and security controls. Utilizing compliant products is foundational for a successful audit. This
verifies operational capability on the infrastructure end, aligning with the principle of least privilege and the
requirement of data protection.
Option B: Advise EHR to execute a Business Associate Agreement (BAA) with Google Cloud.
A Business Associate Agreement (BAA) is a legally binding contract required under regulations like HIPAA
when a covered entity (like EHR) shares protected health information (PHI) with a business associate (like
Google Cloud). The BAA outlines the responsibilities of both parties in protecting the PHI, including data
handling and security procedures. Without a BAA, EHR would be in violation of compliance rules and face
severe penalties. Therefore, executing a BAA with Google is essential to establish a clear understanding of
data protection responsibilities and ensure that Google is obligated to secure the PHI they process on EHR's
behalf. This step is vital to establishing shared responsibility for compliance, and is a legal requirement for
processing sensitive data like PHI.
Why Options C, D, and E are not the primary correct answers for this specific audit concern:
Option C: Use Firebase Authentication for EHR's user-facing applications. While Firebase Authentication is a
robust identity management tool, it's not directly relevant to the core compliance requirements related to
BAA and compliant product usage. It doesn't guarantee an audit will be successful. Secure user
authentication is important but does not replace a BAA or the use of compliant services.
Option D: Implement Prometheus to detect and prevent security breaches on EHR's web-based
applications. Prometheus is an excellent monitoring and alerting tool but doesn’t fulfill compliance

obligations. It enhances security operations; but it is secondary to establishing the foundational legal and
service agreement requirements. While detecting breaches is important, a compliance audit examines
adherence to regulatory frameworks before operational security measures.
Option E: Use GKE private clusters for all Kubernetes workloads. Using private clusters improves security,
but it doesn't address the core audit concern of BAA and compliant product usage. While a good security
practice, is not an essential element for privacy compliance audit success.
Authoritative links for further research:
Google Cloud Compliance: https://cloud.google.com/security/compliance/
Google Cloud HIPAA Compliance: https://cloud.google.com/security/compliance/hipaa
Business Associate Agreement (BAA) information: https://www.hhs.gov/hipaa/for-professionals/covered-
entities/business-associates/index.html
Question: 231 CertyIQ
For this question, refer to the EHR Healthcare case study. You need to define the technical architecture for
securely deploying workloads to Google Cloud. You also need to ensure that only verified containers are deployed
using Google Cloud services. What should you do? (Choose two.)
A. Enable Binary Authorization on GKE, and sign containers as part of a CI/CD pipeline.
B. Configure Jenkins to utilize Kritis to cryptographically sign a container as part of a CI/CD pipeline.
C. Configure Container Registry to only allow trusted service accounts to create and deploy containers from the
registry.
D. Configure Container Registry to use vulnerability scanning to confirm that there are no vulnerabilities before
deploying the workload.
Answer: AD
Explanation:
The correct answer is A and D. Let's break down why:
A. Enable Binary Authorization on GKE, and sign containers as part of a CI/CD pipeline. Binary Authorization
is a Google Cloud service designed to ensure that only verified container images are deployed to Google
Kubernetes Engine (GKE). This aligns with the requirement of deploying only verified containers. By
integrating container signing into the CI/CD pipeline, you establish a chain of trust. This means that before a
container is deployed to GKE, it must have a valid signature from a trusted entity, providing assurance that the
container hasn't been tampered with and is from an authorized source. This directly addresses the security
needs mentioned in the case study. https://cloud.google.com/binary-authorization/docs
D. Configure Container Registry to use vulnerability scanning to confirm that there are no vulnerabilities
before deploying the workload. Container Registry's vulnerability scanning helps identify potential security
flaws in container images. By enabling this feature, you ensure that containers with known vulnerabilities are
not deployed. This enhances the overall security posture, as these vulnerabilities could be exploited to
compromise the healthcare data. The scanned results can also be integrated into a CI/CD process, preventing
vulnerable images from moving to the production stage. This aligns with the stated security and deployment
requirements. https://cloud.google.com/container-registry/docs/vulnerability-scanning
Why other options are incorrect:
B. Configure Jenkins to utilize Kritis to cryptographically sign a container as part of a CI/CD pipeline. While
Kritis is a tool related to container security, Binary Authorization is the recommended solution provided by
Google for enforcing container verification directly within GKE and this option adds a third-party tool that isn't
the recommended practice. Also Kritis is not actively maintained.

C. Configure Container Registry to only allow trusted service accounts to create and deploy containers
from the registry. While service account permissions are essential for controlling access to the Container
Registry, this does not ensure that the container images themselves are secure or verified. It only governs who
can push and pull images, not what those images contain.
In essence, the combination of Binary Authorization (for verification) and vulnerability scanning in Container
Registry (for detecting flaws) creates a strong, layered security approach which directly addresses the
requirements outlined in the case study. These practices align with Google's best practices for securing
container deployments on Google Cloud.
Question: 232 CertyIQ
You need to upgrade the EHR connection to comply with their requirements. The new connection design must
support business-critical needs and meet the same network and security policy requirements. What should you
do?
A. Add a new Dedicated Interconnect connection.
B. Upgrade the bandwidth on the Dedicated Interconnect connection to 100 G.
C. Add three new Cloud VPN connections.
D. Add a new Carrier Peering connection.
Answer: A
Explanation:
The correct answer is A. Add a new Dedicated Interconnect connection. Here's why:
The scenario requires an upgrade to an existing EHR connection while maintaining business-critical needs and
existing network/security policies. Option A, adding a new Dedicated Interconnect connection, provides the
most robust solution for this situation. Dedicated Interconnect offers high bandwidth, low latency, and a
private connection between your on-premises network and Google Cloud Platform, fulfilling the business-
critical requirement. It also allows for a redundant and isolated connection, adhering to the 'same security
policy' requirement by isolating the new connection. Bandwidth upgrade on an existing connection (Option B)
might temporarily disrupt service or not fully address redundancy requirements. Cloud VPN connections
(Option C) are not suitable for high-bandwidth, low-latency needs that Dedicated Interconnect provides.
Carrier Peering (Option D) is more for connecting to Google's edge network and is not a primary method for
establishing private connectivity. Adding a new Dedicated Interconnect ensures a clear separation of the old
and new connections, allows for phased migration, and provides independent fault tolerance. By opting for a
second Dedicated Interconnect, the business maintains an active connection during the setup of the new one,
preventing downtime for the essential EHR system. This also allows for potential future scaling of bandwidth.
Authoritative Links:
Google Cloud Dedicated Interconnect: https://cloud.google.com/network-
connectivity/docs/interconnect/concepts/dedicated-interconnect
Choosing a Cloud Interconnect Option: https://cloud.google.com/network-
connectivity/docs/interconnect/how-to/choosing-interconnect
Question: 233 CertyIQ
For this question, refer to the EHR Healthcare case study. You need to define the technical architecture for hybrid
connectivity between EHR's on-premises systems and Google Cloud. You want to follow Google's recommended
practices for production-level applications. Considering the EHR Healthcare business and technical requirements,

what should you do?
A. Configure two Partner Interconnect connections in one metro (City), and make sure the Interconnect
connections are placed in different metro zones.
B. Configure two VPN connections from on-premises to Google Cloud, and make sure the VPN devices on-
premises are in separate racks.
C. Configure Direct Peering between EHR Healthcare and Google Cloud, and make sure you are peering at least
two Google locations.
D. Configure two Dedicated Interconnect connections in one metro (City) and two connections in another metro,
and make sure the Interconnect connections are placed in different metro zones.
Answer: D
Explanation:
Option D, configuring two Dedicated Interconnect connections in one metro and two in another, with
connections in different metro zones, is the most suitable solution for EHR Healthcare's hybrid connectivity
needs, prioritizing high availability and resilience. This design aligns with Google's recommended practices for
production-level applications, as it provides redundancy at multiple levels. Utilizing Dedicated Interconnect
ensures a private, high-bandwidth, and low-latency connection, vital for sensitive healthcare data. Having two
connections within each metro zone offers failover in case of a single link failure. Furthermore, using two
separate metros provides geographical redundancy against broader infrastructure disruptions. This dual
metro strategy ensures business continuity by maintaining connectivity even if one metro experiences an
outage. This design avoids single points of failure, ensuring highly reliable hybrid connectivity. Option A, using
Partner Interconnect, while cost-effective, offers less control over physical infrastructure compared to
Dedicated Interconnect and may introduce dependencies on the partner. Option B, VPN connections, do not
provide the high bandwidth and low latency required for mission-critical applications and are better suited for
less demanding workloads. Option C, Direct Peering, is typically for very large organizations and is unlikely to
fit EHR's profile, and doesn't provide the same level of redundancy as the dual dedicated interconnect
solution. The complexity and management overhead of Direct Peering are also higher than Dedicated
Interconnect.
Authoritative Links:
Google Cloud Interconnect Overview: https://cloud.google.com/interconnect/docs/overview
Choosing a Connectivity Option: https://cloud.google.com/architecture/connectivity-options-for-your-
google-cloud
Dedicated Interconnect Best Practices: https://cloud.google.com/interconnect/docs/how-to/best-practices
Question: 234 CertyIQ
For this question, refer to the EHR Healthcare case study. You are a developer on the EHR customer portal team.
Your team recently migrated the customer portal application to Google Cloud. The load has increased on the
application servers, and now the application is logging many timeout errors. You recently incorporated Pub/Sub
into the application architecture, and the application is not logging any Pub/Sub publishing errors. You want to
improve publishing latency.
What should you do?
A. Increase the Pub/Sub Total Timeout retry value.
B. Move from a Pub/Sub subscriber pull model to a push model.
C. Turn off Pub/Sub message batching.
D. Create a backup Pub/Sub message queue.
Answer: C

Explanation:
The correct answer is C. Turn off Pub/Sub message batching. Here's why:
The scenario describes increased load leading to application timeout errors, specifically mentioning that
Pub/Sub publishing is not reporting errors but latency is an issue. This suggests a bottleneck in the publishing
process, not a failure. Pub/Sub message batching, while efficient for throughput, can introduce latency. When
enabled, messages are accumulated before being sent in a batch. Under high load, this accumulation can take
longer, delaying the publish operation and leading to increased latency and perceived timeouts in the
application.
Disabling batching allows each message to be sent as soon as it's ready, reducing the delay. Options A and D
do not address the core issue of publication latency. Increasing retry timeouts (A) simply prolongs the wait,
and a backup queue (D) is not relevant to publication speed. Option B, switching to push, changes the
subscriber model, but does not influence publishing speed. Therefore, turning off batching directly tackles
the increased latency related to publishing in the context of a high-load scenario.
Further research:
Google Cloud Pub/Sub Documentation on Batching:
https://cloud.google.com/pubsub/docs/publisher#batching - This page provides official details on how
batching works and its trade-offs.
Google Cloud Pub/Sub Best Practices: https://cloud.google.com/pubsub/docs/best-practices - This page
provides more information on latency and throughput considerations, and it highlights the use cases for both
batching and non-batching.
Question: 235 CertyIQ
For this question, refer to the EHR Healthcare case study. In the past, configuration errors put public IP addresses
on backend servers that should not have been accessible from the Internet. You need to ensure that no one can put
external IP addresses on backend Compute Engine instances and that external IP addresses can only be
configured on frontend Compute Engine instances. What should you do?
A. Create an Organizational Policy with a constraint to allow external IP addresses only on the frontend
Compute Engine instances.
B. Revoke the compute.networkAdmin role from all users in the project with front end instances.
C. Create an Identity and Access Management (IAM) policy that maps the IT staff to the
compute.networkAdmin role for the organization.
D. Create a custom Identity and Access Management (IAM) role named GCE_FRONTEND with the
compute.addresses.create permission.
Answer: A
Explanation:
The correct answer is A. Create an Organizational Policy with a constraint to allow external IP addresses
only on the frontend Compute Engine instances.
Organizational Policies in Google Cloud Platform (GCP) provide centralized control over your organization's
resources. They allow you to set constraints that govern how resources can be used, ensuring consistent
security and compliance. In this scenario, the goal is to prevent accidental external IP assignment to backend
instances. This is a policy decision, not a user permission issue or specific IAM role limitation, as IAM controls
who can do what, while Organizational Policies control how resources are configured.
By using an Organizational Policy with the compute.disableDefaultExternalIPAccess constraint, you can
effectively prohibit the creation of Compute Engine instances with external IP addresses by default.

Additionally, you can define exceptions based on resource tags or other attributes, ensuring that only the
designated frontend instances can have them. This mechanism provides a robust and centralized solution to
enforce consistent configurations.
Options B, C, and D are not the best choices. Option B may hinder normal network administration tasks on
frontend instances; C isn't specific enough to prevent the problem, and Option D doesn't achieve policy
enforcement on all instances.
Here are some authoritative links for further research:
Organizational policies: https://cloud.google.com/resource-manager/docs/organization-policy/overview
Constraints for Compute Engine: https://cloud.google.com/resource-manager/docs/organization-policy/org-
policy-constraints#compute
compute.disableDefaultExternalIPAccess constraint: https://cloud.google.com/resource-
manager/docs/organization-policy/constraints/compute#compute.disableDefaultExternalIPAccess
Using Organizational Policy ensures that the desired configuration is enforced consistently across the project,
preventing accidental misconfiguration and enhancing the overall security posture by controlling external IP
usage.
Question: 236 CertyIQ
For this question, refer to the EHR Healthcare case study. You are responsible for designing the Google Cloud
network architecture for Google Kubernetes
Engine. You want to follow Google best practices. Considering the EHR Healthcare business and technical
requirements, what should you do to reduce the attack surface?
A. Use a private cluster with a private endpoint with master authorized networks configured.
B. Use a public cluster with firewall rules and Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) routes.
C. Use a private cluster with a public endpoint with master authorized networks configured.
D. Use a public cluster with master authorized networks enabled and firewall rules.
Answer: A
Explanation:
The correct answer is A: Use a private cluster with a private endpoint with master authorized networks
configured. This option best aligns with Google's best practices for security and addresses the need to reduce
the attack surface, especially critical for handling sensitive healthcare data as outlined in the EHR Healthcare
case study.
Here's why:
Private Clusters: A private cluster prevents the Kubernetes API server (the control plane) from having a
public IP address, significantly reducing its exposure to the internet. This drastically limits potential attack
vectors.
Private Endpoint: Using a private endpoint ensures that access to the master node is only possible from within
the VPC or through private network connections, further isolating it from the public internet.
Master Authorized Networks: Configuring master authorized networks allows specific IP ranges to access
the Kubernetes API server even if the endpoint is private. This provides granular control and reduces the risk
of unauthorized access.
Option B utilizes a public cluster which is directly exposed to the internet, increasing the attack surface. While
firewall rules and VPC routes add security, they aren't as effective as the complete isolation offered by

private clusters. Option C has a private cluster but a public endpoint, exposing the API server, although
restricted by authorized networks. Similarly, option D uses a public cluster again, making it insecure.
The combination of a private cluster with a private endpoint and master authorized networks offers the
strongest security posture, minimizing exposure and aligning with security best practices. For sensitive data
environments like healthcare, this approach is paramount.
Authoritative Links:
Google Kubernetes Engine Private Clusters: https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/how-
to/private-clusters
GKE Master Authorized Networks: https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/how-to/authorized-
networks
Google Cloud Security Best Practices: https://cloud.google.com/security/best-practices
Question: 237 CertyIQ
Mountkirk Games wants you to design their new testing strategy. How should the test coverage differ from their
existing backends on the other platforms?
A. Tests should scale well beyond the prior approaches
B. Unit tests are no longer required, only end-to-end tests
C. Tests should be applied after the release is in the production environment
D. Tests should include directly testing the Google Cloud Platform (GCP) infrastructure
Answer: A
Explanation:
Option A, "Tests should scale well beyond the prior approaches," is the most appropriate answer. Cloud
platforms, like Google Cloud Platform (GCP), offer significant scalability advantages. Therefore, testing
strategies should be designed to leverage this scalability and ensure the application performs efficiently
under increasing loads. Prior testing approaches might not have accounted for the dynamic resource
provisioning and scaling capabilities of GCP. Option B is incorrect because unit tests remain crucial for
verifying the functionality of individual code components, even with end-to-end tests. Option C is flawed
because testing post-release in production carries significant risks, and pre-release testing is essential for
ensuring quality and preventing downtime. Option D is not accurate; while integration testing should involve
the application's interaction with GCP services, directly testing GCP's infrastructure is not typically the
responsibility of the application development team. The focus should be on testing the application's
interaction with those services. Therefore, focusing on scalable testing methods, as suggested by option A, is
paramount to harnessing GCP's capabilities effectively.
Further research:
Google Cloud documentation on testing: https://cloud.google.com/solutions/devops/devops-tech-test-
automation
Scalable Testing: https://martinfowler.com/articles/microservice-testing/ (While focused on microservices,
this article addresses key aspects of scalable testing)
GCP documentation on software delivery: https://cloud.google.com/architecture/software-delivery-
automation
Question: 238 CertyIQ

Mountkirk Games has deployed their new backend on Google Cloud Platform (GCP). You want to create a through
testing process for new versions of the backend before they are released to the public. You want the testing
environment to scale in an economical way. How should you design the process?
A. Create a scalable environment in GCP for simulating production load
B. Use the existing infrastructure to test the GCP-based backend at scale
C. Build stress tests into each component of your application using resources internal to GCP to simulate load
D. Create a set of static environments in GCP to test different levels of load " for example, high, medium, and
low
Answer: A
Explanation:
The correct answer is A. Create a scalable environment in GCP for simulating production load.
Here's why:
Option A aligns with best practices for modern software development and cloud architecture, specifically
concerning testing in a dynamic environment. Creating a scalable environment in GCP allows Mountkirk
Games to mimic real-world traffic and load scenarios effectively. This is crucial because the production
environment will also likely scale, and testing should mirror that behavior. The benefit is that the testing
environment can dynamically adjust resources based on the simulated load, leading to cost efficiency by only
utilizing resources when needed. This contrasts with static environments (option D) which may lead to
overspending on idle resources. This method helps identify performance bottlenecks and vulnerabilities early,
providing valuable insights before a release to the public.
Option B is not ideal as production infrastructure should be shielded from testing which might create
instability. Option C is helpful but is only a component of a broader testing strategy and can't simulate
system-wide load. Option D, the use of static environments, fails to simulate a changing environment. By
simulating production load in a scalable environment, Mountkirk can get reliable information that closely
matches what would happen during actual operation.
Key Concepts:
Scalability: The ability of a system to handle increasing amounts of work by adding resources.
Cloud-based Testing: Leverage cloud resources for efficient testing of applications.
Load Testing: Simulating real-world user load to identify performance issues.
Dynamic Resource Allocation: Adjusting resources based on demand to optimize costs and performance.
DevOps: A combination of practices, tools, and philosophy for bringing together development and operations.
Authoritative Links:
Google Cloud Testing Overview: https://cloud.google.com/solutions/devops/devops-test-automation -
Provides an overview of best practices for testing in GCP.
Load Testing Best Practices: https://cloud.google.com/architecture/best-practices-for-load-testing -
Discusses best practices for load testing on GCP.
Scalability Best Practices: https://cloud.google.com/architecture/scalability - Provides guidance on building
scalable systems on Google Cloud.
Question: 239 CertyIQ
Mountkirk Games wants to set up a continuous delivery pipeline. Their architecture includes many small services
that they want to be able to update and roll back quickly. Mountkirk Games has the following requirements:
✑ Services are deployed redundantly across multiple regions in the US and Europe
✑ Only frontend services are exposed on the public internet

✑ They can provide a single frontend IP for their fleet of services
✑ Deployment artifacts are immutable
Which set of products should they use?
A. Google Cloud Storage, Google Cloud Dataflow, Google Compute Engine
B. Google Cloud Storage, Google App Engine, Google Network Load Balancer
C. Google Kubernetes Registry, Google Container Engine, Google HTTP(S) Load Balancer
D. Google Cloud Functions, Google Cloud Pub/Sub, Google Cloud Deployment Manager
Answer: C
Explanation:
The correct answer is C. Google Kubernetes Registry, Google Kubernetes Engine, Google HTTP(S) Load
Balancer. This solution aligns perfectly with Mountkirk Games' requirements for a modern continuous
delivery pipeline. Let's break down why:
Google Kubernetes Registry (GCR): GCR serves as a secure and scalable repository for storing immutable
container images, fulfilling their need for immutable deployment artifacts.
https://cloud.google.com/container-registry
Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE): GKE is a managed Kubernetes service that provides the orchestration and
deployment of containerized applications across multiple regions. It enables the redundant deployment of
microservices across the US and Europe, meeting the high availability requirement. GKE supports rolling
updates and rollbacks, allowing for rapid updates and easy reversion to previous versions.
https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine
Google HTTP(S) Load Balancer: This load balancer is a global service that can direct traffic to GKE clusters
