Which two actions should you take?
A. Create a Cloud Storage lifecycle rule with Age: 30, Storage Class: Standard, and Action: Set to Coldline, and
create a second GCS life-cycle rule with Age: 365, Storage Class: Coldline, and Action: Delete.
B. Create a Cloud Storage lifecycle rule with Age: 30, Storage Class: Coldline, and Action: Set to Nearline, and
create a second GCS life-cycle rule with Age: 91, Storage Class: Coldline, and Action: Set to Nearline.
C. Create a Cloud Storage lifecycle rule with Age: 90, Storage Class: Standard, and Action: Set to Nearline, and
create a second GCS life-cycle rule with Age: 91, Storage Class: Nearline, and Action: Set to Coldline.
D. Create a Cloud Storage lifecycle rule with Age: 30, Storage Class: Standard, and Action: Set to Coldline, and
create a second GCS life-cycle rule with Age: 365, Storage Class: Nearline, and Action: Delete.
Answer: A
Explanation:
Here's a justification for why option A is the correct approach for TerramEarth's data storage needs, focusing
on minimizing cost while retaining data for one year:
Option A uses Cloud Storage lifecycle rules to move data through storage tiers based on age. The first rule
moves files older than 30 days from the Standard tier to the Coldline tier. This transition significantly reduces
storage costs, as Coldline storage is designed for infrequent access and is less expensive than Standard. The
second rule, which deletes files older than 365 days, ensures that data is only kept for the required one year,
further optimizing cost. It's important to understand that Google Cloud Storage lifecycle rules allow for
automated transition of objects between storage classes based on defined criteria (e.g. age). Each storage
class provides different cost and access patterns, which need to be aligned to use case. The Standard tier is

ideal for frequently accessed data, while Nearline, Coldline, and Archive tiers progressively offer lower costs
for progressively less frequently accessed data. Coldline is well-suited for data accessed less than once per
quarter, so the proposed 30 day transition is a good fit for cost optimization.
Option B is incorrect because it would move the files in a way that increases the storage cost. Nearline is more
expensive than Coldline and moving files to it after 30 days isn't efficient. Option C is also inefficient as it
initially stores files in the Standard tier for 90 days and then to Nearline, which is more expensive than
Coldline. Option D would store the data for a year in the Coldline tier then delete it, it has no transition to
another more affordable tier and doesn't provide the most cost effective option.
Therefore, option A is correct as it achieves the goals set by the prompt, that is minimize file storage cost
while storing files for 1 year.
Relevant Links:
Cloud Storage Lifecycle Management: https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/lifecycle
Cloud Storage Classes: https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/storage-classes
Question: 270 CertyIQ
For this question, refer to the TerramEarth case study. You need to implement a reliable, scalable GCP solution for
the data warehouse for your company,
TerramEarth.
Considering the TerramEarth business and technical requirements, what should you do?
A. Replace the existing data warehouse with BigQuery. Use table partitioning.
B. Replace the existing data warehouse with a Compute Engine instance with 96 CPUs.
C. Replace the existing data warehouse with BigQuery. Use federated data sources.
D. Replace the existing data warehouse with a Compute Engine instance with 96 CPUs. Add an additional
Compute Engine preemptible instance with 32 CPUs.
Answer: A
Explanation:
The optimal solution for TerramEarth's data warehouse, as per the case study, is to replace the existing
system with BigQuery and leverage table partitioning (Option A). BigQuery is a fully managed, serverless data
warehouse service ideal for TerramEarth's needs of scalability, reliability, and cost-effectiveness. Unlike
Compute Engine instances (Options B and D), BigQuery eliminates the operational overhead of managing
servers, patching, and infrastructure scaling. BigQuery's architecture inherently handles large datasets,
ensuring high availability and fault tolerance. Table partitioning, specifically, enhances query performance
and reduces costs by limiting the amount of data scanned during queries. In contrast, using large Compute
Engine instances (Options B and D) requires significant upfront capacity planning, leading to potential
underutilization and higher costs. Preemptible instances (Option D) introduce instability, unsuitable for a
reliable data warehouse. Federated data sources (Option C) are beneficial for querying external data but not
as the core replacement for the entire data warehouse. TerramEarth's requirements, as per the case study,
emphasizes the need for managed services rather than managing underlying infrastructure. Thus, BigQuery
with table partitioning aligns perfectly.
For further research, refer to these official Google Cloud resources:
BigQuery Overview: https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/introduction
BigQuery Table Partitioning: https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/partitioned-tables
BigQuery Pricing: https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/pricing
Compute Engine Overview: https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/

Question: 271 CertyIQ
For this question, refer to the TerramEarth case study. A new architecture that writes all incoming data to
BigQuery has been introduced. You notice that the data is dirty, and want to ensure data quality on an automated
daily basis while managing cost.
What should you do?
A. Set up a streaming Cloud Dataflow job, receiving data by the ingestion process. Clean the data in a Cloud
Dataflow pipeline.
B. Create a Cloud Function that reads data from BigQuery and cleans it. Trigger the Cloud Function from a
Compute Engine instance.
C. Create a SQL statement on the data in BigQuery, and save it as a view. Run the view daily, and save the result
to a new table.
D. Use Cloud Dataprep and configure the BigQuery tables as the source. Schedule a daily job to clean the data.
Answer: D
Explanation:
The best approach to address TerramEarth's dirty data issue in BigQuery, with a focus on automation, cost-
effectiveness, and data quality, is option D, utilizing Cloud Dataprep.
Here's why:
Cloud Dataprep is specifically designed for data wrangling and cleaning. It offers a user-friendly interface for
data profiling, transformation, and validation, without requiring extensive coding knowledge. This makes it
easy to implement complex cleaning rules and transformations on BigQuery data. By connecting directly to
BigQuery, it avoids the complexity and costs associated with moving data to another processing service for
cleaning. Setting up a daily scheduled job within Cloud Dataprep handles the automated aspect of the
requirement. It directly works on BigQuery tables as data source and enables users to run the clean data
pipeline daily.Option A, while capable of data cleaning, involves setting up a more complex and resource-
intensive Cloud Dataflow pipeline. It also requires a streaming data ingestion pipeline setup, which does not
match the requirement of cleaning BigQuery stored data.Option B introduces unnecessary complexity by
creating a Cloud Function triggered by a Compute Engine instance to clean data within BigQuery. This
approach requires scripting data movement within BigQuery and may incur costs for both compute and data
transfer operations. It is more difficult to manage and maintain compared to a purpose built data prep
tool.Option C only creates a view which does not clean the underlying data. A view will only provide a query
result.
Cloud Dataprep is the best solution because it's purpose-built for data wrangling, is user-friendly, cost-
effective, and supports scheduled automation.
Authoritative links:
Cloud Dataprep Overview: https://cloud.google.com/dataprep/docs/concepts/overview
Data Prep on BigQuery: https://cloud.google.com/dataprep/docs/how-to/connect-to-bq
Question: 272 CertyIQ
For this question, refer to the TerramEarth case study. Considering the technical requirements, how should you
reduce the unplanned vehicle downtime in GCP?
A. Use BigQuery as the data warehouse. Connect all vehicles to the network and stream data into BigQuery
using Cloud Pub/Sub and Cloud Dataflow. Use Google Data Studio for analysis and reporting.

B. Use BigQuery as the data warehouse. Connect all vehicles to the network and upload gzip files to a Multi-
Regional Cloud Storage bucket using gcloud. Use Google Data Studio for analysis and reporting.
C. Use Cloud Dataproc Hive as the data warehouse. Upload gzip files to a Multi-Regional Cloud Storage bucket.
Upload this data into BigQuery using gcloud. Use Google Data Studio for analysis and reporting.
D. Use Cloud Dataproc Hive as the data warehouse. Directly stream data into partitioned Hive tables. Use Pig
scripts to analyze data.
Answer: A
Explanation:
Option A is the most suitable solution for reducing unplanned vehicle downtime, aligning with the technical
requirements of the TerramEarth case study. Real-time data ingestion and analysis are crucial for proactive
maintenance and minimizing downtime. Cloud Pub/Sub facilitates reliable and scalable message ingestion
from vehicles, while Cloud Dataflow enables stream processing of this data, performing transformations and
preparing it for storage. BigQuery provides a fully managed, scalable, and cost-effective data warehouse for
storing and querying this large volume of real-time data. Google Data Studio then provides a user-friendly
interface for analysis and reporting, allowing TerramEarth to monitor vehicle health and identify potential
issues before they lead to downtime.
Options B and C rely on batch processing of gzip files, which delays analysis and is unsuitable for real-time
alerts and proactive maintenance. Option B uses gcloud for uploads, which is less scalable and resilient than a
stream processing solution. Option C introduces Cloud Dataproc Hive, adding complexity and management
overhead for a data warehouse function best served by BigQuery. Option D uses Cloud Dataproc Hive, lacks
streaming capabilities, introduces complex partitioning, and requires manual analysis with Pig scripts, all
adding unnecessary operational complexity and delays. Option A provides the most effective and direct route
to identify potential issues in real-time, directly reducing unplanned downtime. The real-time streaming with
Pub/Sub, data processing with Dataflow, warehousing with BigQuery, and reporting with Data Studio form a
powerful and integrated solution ideal for proactive maintenance in TerramEarth's environment.
Authoritative Links:
Cloud Pub/Sub: https://cloud.google.com/pubsub/docs
Cloud Dataflow: https://cloud.google.com/dataflow/docs
BigQuery: https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs
Google Data Studio: https://datastudio.google.com/
Question: 273 CertyIQ
For this question, refer to the TerramEarth case study. You are asked to design a new architecture for the ingestion
of the data of the 200,000 vehicles that are connected to a cellular network. You want to follow Google-
recommended practices.
Considering the technical requirements, which components should you use for the ingestion of the data?
A. Google Kubernetes Engine with an SSL Ingress
B. Cloud IoT Core with public/private key pairs
C. Compute Engine with project-wide SSH keys
D. Compute Engine with specific SSH keys
Answer: B
Explanation:
The correct answer is B. Cloud IoT Core with public/private key pairs. Let's break down why:

TerramEarth's case study involves a large fleet of vehicles (200,000) connected over a cellular network. This
scenario screams for a managed service designed for IoT device communication. Cloud IoT Core directly
addresses this need by providing secure, scalable device management and data ingestion. It allows for
bidirectional communication, enabling not only data collection but also the sending of commands to the
vehicles if needed.
Public/private key pairs are the recommended security practice for authenticating these large number of
devices connecting to cloud services. These keys create a strong cryptographic bond ensuring only
authorized devices send data. This approach avoids complexities associated with managing individual SSH
keys for a massive number of Compute Engine instances (options C and D), thus streamlining operations.
Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) with SSL ingress (option A) is not the ideal first point of ingestion for such a
large number of devices. GKE is great for containerized applications and processing but adds unnecessary
complexity as an ingress point for device data. The focus should be on secure connectivity and efficient data
transport, and Cloud IoT Core is specifically designed for that.
In summary, Cloud IoT Core leverages MQTT and HTTP protocols for seamless data flow from devices while
ensuring security, scalability and efficient device management.
Further research:
Cloud IoT Core Documentation: https://cloud.google.com/iot/docs
Best practices for device authentication: https://cloud.google.com/iot/docs/how-tos/devices/authentication
Choosing the Right IoT Ingestion Platform: https://cloud.google.com/blog/products/iot/choosing-the-right-
google-cloud-iot-platform
Question: 274 CertyIQ
Regarding Cloud Storage, which option allows any user to access to a Cloud Storage resource for a limited time,
using a specific URL?
A. Open Buckets
B. Temporary Resources
C. Signed URLs
D. Temporary URLs
Answer: C
Explanation:
The correct answer is C. Signed URLs. Signed URLs provide a mechanism for granting temporary access to a
private Cloud Storage resource. They are URLs that are digitally signed by a user with the appropriate
permissions, allowing anyone possessing the URL to access the resource for a pre-defined duration,
regardless of whether they have a Google Cloud account or specific IAM permissions. This temporary access
control is achieved through the embedded signature, which is verified by Google Cloud Storage.
Option A, "Open Buckets," grants public and permanent access to the bucket's content, lacking any time
limitations or controlled access. Therefore, it is not suitable for granting limited-time access to specific users.
Option B, "Temporary Resources," is a generic term and not a feature specifically associated with limited
access to Cloud Storage; rather it relates to ephemeral computing resources. Option D, "Temporary URLs," is
not a standard term within Google Cloud Storage's access control mechanisms, making it an invalid choice.
Signed URLs allow for fine-grained control over access, including setting an expiration date and time, which
makes it ideal for sharing resources securely with external parties or for specific use cases requiring short-
term access. The URL's signature ensures that the user requesting the resource is authorized, given that they

possess the URL and within the set validity timeframe. This control is achieved through a cryptographic
process using a service account's private key. Signed URLs are a secure and flexible way to manage access to
Cloud Storage data for limited durations.
For further research, consult the official Google Cloud documentation:
Signed URLs: https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/access-control/signed-urls
Cloud Storage Access Control: https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/access-control
Question: 275 CertyIQ
Of the options given, which is a NoSQL database?
A. Cloud Datastore
B. Cloud SQL
C. All of the given options
D. Cloud Storage
Answer: A
Explanation:
Cloud Datastore is a NoSQL, schemaless database service, designed for storing non-relational data. It excels
at handling large, diverse, and semi-structured datasets. In contrast, Cloud SQL provides managed relational
database services, following a structured data model with predefined schemas. Relational databases like
Cloud SQL organize data into tables with rows and columns, enforcing data integrity through relationships
and constraints. Cloud Storage is an object storage service, designed for storing unstructured data like files,
images, and videos. It's not a database, lacking the querying and indexing capabilities of databases.
Therefore, while all three are Google Cloud Platform offerings, only Cloud Datastore fits the definition of a
NoSQL database. The answer "All of the given options" is incorrect because Cloud SQL is a relational
database, and Cloud Storage is not a database at all. Option A is the only correct answer. Further research on
these services and NoSQL databases can be done through Google Cloud's official documentation:
Cloud Datastore: https://cloud.google.com/datastore
Cloud SQL: https://cloud.google.com/sql
Cloud Storage: https://cloud.google.com/storage
NoSQL Databases: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/NoSQL
Question: 276 CertyIQ
Container Engine allows orchastration of what type of containers?
A. Blue Whale
B. LXC
C. BSD Jails
D. Docker
Answer: D
Explanation:
Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE), the managed service for Container Engine on Google Cloud Platform,
primarily orchestrates Docker containers. Docker has become the de facto standard for containerization,

creating portable and consistent application environments. Docker containers package an application and its
dependencies into a single image, ensuring that the application runs the same way regardless of the
underlying infrastructure. GKE leverages Kubernetes, a container orchestration system, to automate the
deployment, scaling, and management of these Docker containerized applications. The other options are not
directly supported for orchestration in GKE. While LXC is a containerization technology, it's less common in
cloud environments compared to Docker. Blue Whale is not a standard term for containers. BSD Jails, while a
form of OS-level virtualization, is not the focus of Kubernetes or GKE. Thus, GKE and Container Engine were
designed specifically to work seamlessly with Docker-based containers. This makes 'Docker' the definitive
answer.
Authoritative links:
Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) Overview: https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-
engine/docs/concepts/overview
Docker Documentation: https://docs.docker.com/
Kubernetes Documentation: https://kubernetes.io/docs/
Question: 277 CertyIQ
Regarding Cloud IAM, what type of role(s) are available?
A. Basic roles and Compiled roles
B. Primitive roles and Predefined roles
C. Simple roles
D. Basic roles and Curated roles
Answer: B
Explanation:
The correct answer is B, Primitive roles and Predefined roles. Google Cloud IAM (Identity and Access
Management) provides a hierarchical system for managing access to resources. Primitive roles are basic,
broad roles with high-level permissions, such as Owner, Editor, and Viewer. These roles offer coarse-grained
access control, granting wide-ranging permissions across a project. In contrast, Predefined roles are more
granular and specifically tailored to particular Google Cloud services. They grant fine-grained access,
adhering to the principle of least privilege by allowing users to perform only necessary tasks. For instance, a
predefined role might be Storage Admin or BigQuery Data Editor. These roles are managed by Google and are
frequently updated. Options A, C, and D are incorrect since "Compiled", "Simple", and "Curated" are not
recognized or used as role types within Cloud IAM terminology. Therefore, the primary types of roles are
"Primitive" and "Predefined", offering a balance between broad access and specific, limited privileges.
Understanding the distinction between these roles is crucial for implementing effective security policies and
managing access control within Google Cloud.
Authoritative links for further research:
1. Google Cloud IAM documentation: https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs
2. Understanding roles: https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/understanding-roles
3. Primitive roles: https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/understanding-roles#primitive_roles
4. Predefined roles: https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/understanding-roles#predefined_roles
Question: 278 CertyIQ

Which of the follow products will allow you to host a static website?
A. Cloud SDK
B. Cloud Endpoints
C. Cloud Storage
D. Cloud Datastore
Answer: C
Explanation:
The correct answer is C. Cloud Storage is Google Cloud Platform's (GCP) object storage service designed for
storing and retrieving large amounts of unstructured data, including static website assets like HTML, CSS,
JavaScript files, images, and videos. It offers the ability to configure a bucket to serve these files as a website,
directly via HTTP(S). This approach leverages the inherent scalability and reliability of Cloud Storage,
enabling a cost-effective and highly available solution for hosting static content. In contrast, Cloud SDK (A) is
a command-line tool for interacting with GCP, not a hosting service. Cloud Endpoints (B) is for developing and
deploying APIs, not for serving static web content. Cloud Datastore (D) is a NoSQL database designed for
structured data, not static files. Therefore, Cloud Storage is the only product in the list that directly provides
the necessary functionality for hosting a static website. Its object storage nature and built-in web serving
capabilities make it the ideal choice for this purpose.
Further research:
Cloud Storage documentation: https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs
Hosting a static website on Cloud Storage: https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/hosting-static-website
Question: 279 CertyIQ
Container Engine is built on which open source system?
A. Swarm
B. Kubernetes
C. Docker Orchastrate
D. Mesos
Answer: B
Explanation:
The correct answer is Kubernetes (B). Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE), Google's managed container
orchestration service, is built directly upon the open-source Kubernetes project. Kubernetes, originally
developed by Google, is a platform designed to automate deployment, scaling, and management of
containerized applications. It provides essential features like pod management, service discovery, load
balancing, and rolling updates, all crucial for operating containerized workloads effectively in cloud
environments. GKE leverages these core Kubernetes functionalities, offering a simplified and managed
experience. Swarm (A), Docker Orchestrate (C), and Mesos (D) are all alternative container orchestration
platforms but are not the underlying system for GKE. Docker Orchestrate is the previous name of Docker
Swarm. GKE does not use any other open source container orchestration platforms. While Docker is widely
used for creating containers, GKE itself uses Kubernetes for the management of those containers. Choosing
Kubernetes as the foundation allows GKE users to benefit from a widely adopted and mature platform with a
large and active community, fostering interoperability and portability of applications across different
environments. This also grants access to a large ecosystem of tools and resources.

For further exploration, please refer to:
1. Kubernetes Official Website: https://kubernetes.io/
2. Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) Documentation: https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-
engine/docs
3. Kubernetes Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kubernetes
Question: 280 CertyIQ
Cloud Source Repositories provide a hosted version of which version control system?
A. Git
B. RCS
C. SVN
D. Mercurial
Answer: A
Explanation:
Cloud Source Repositories, a service within Google Cloud Platform (GCP), is designed to host and manage
source code. Its primary purpose is to provide a scalable and reliable environment for developers to
collaborate on software projects. The core version control system upon which Cloud Source Repositories is
built is Git. Git is a distributed version control system known for its flexibility, branching capabilities, and
efficient management of code changes. Google specifically chose Git as the foundation for Cloud Source
Repositories due to its widespread adoption and familiarity within the developer community. Therefore,
developers utilizing Cloud Source Repositories interact with their code using Git commands and workflows.
This integration allows for seamless source code management within the GCP environment. While RCS, SVN,
and Mercurial are also version control systems, they are not natively supported by Cloud Source Repositories.
Google prioritizes the ease of use and broad acceptance that Git provides. Choosing Git enables a large
community of developers to quickly adopt Cloud Source Repositories, without the need to learn a new version
control system. The benefits of using Git within GCP are further enhanced by integrations with other GCP
services, including Cloud Build, Cloud Functions, and other CI/CD tools.
Here are authoritative links for further research:
Google Cloud Documentation on Cloud Source Repositories: https://cloud.google.com/source-
repositories/docs
Git Official Website: https://git-scm.com/
Question: 281 CertyIQ
Which of the following is an analytics data warehouse?
A. Cloud SQL
B. Big Query
C. Datastore
D. Cloud Storage
Answer: B
Explanation:

BigQuery is the correct answer because it is Google Cloud's fully managed, serverless data warehouse
designed for large-scale analytics. It allows for fast SQL queries on massive datasets, making it ideal for
business intelligence and data analysis. Unlike other options, BigQuery is specifically engineered to handle
analytical workloads efficiently. Cloud SQL provides managed relational databases for transactional
workloads, focusing on operational data rather than analytics. Datastore is a NoSQL document database,
suitable for application data rather than large-scale analytical processing. Cloud Storage offers object
storage for unstructured data, which is a good foundation for a data lake, but is not itself a data warehouse.
Therefore, while these services are part of the Google Cloud Platform, only BigQuery directly fits the
definition of an analytical data warehouse, supporting features like complex aggregations and ad-hoc
analysis. BigQuery's serverless architecture means users don't need to manage infrastructure, further
streamlining the analytical process.
For further research, you can explore:
BigQuery Documentation: https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs
Cloud SQL Documentation: https://cloud.google.com/sql/docs
Cloud Datastore Documentation: https://cloud.google.com/datastore/docs
Cloud Storage Documentation: https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs
Question: 282 CertyIQ
Which service offers the ability to create and run virtual machines?
A. Google Virtualization Engine
B. Compute Containers
C. VM Engine
D. Compute Engine
Answer: D
Explanation:
The correct answer is D. Compute Engine. Google Compute Engine is the Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS)
component within Google Cloud Platform (GCP) that allows users to create and manage virtual machines
(VMs). It provides the fundamental building blocks for deploying and scaling applications in the cloud. Users
can select various operating systems, machine types (CPU and memory configurations), storage options, and
network settings when configuring their VMs. Unlike containers, which share the host operating system
kernel, VMs have their own dedicated operating system, providing a higher degree of isolation and control.
Option A, "Google Virtualization Engine," is not an actual GCP service name. Option B, "Compute Containers,"
is misleading; while GCP offers container-based services like Kubernetes Engine (GKE), it doesn’t have a
service explicitly called "Compute Containers". Option C, "VM Engine," is also not a correct GCP service name.
Therefore, only "Compute Engine" directly and accurately reflects the service for creating and running virtual
machines. Compute Engine offers different VM families optimized for various workloads, such as compute-
intensive tasks, memory-intensive workloads, or accelerated computing. It allows users to provision virtual
instances based on a wide range of needs, including custom images. Through Compute Engine, developers
can build, deploy, and manage virtual servers in the cloud, giving them flexibility and control over
infrastructure resources.
Authoritative links:
Google Compute Engine Documentation: This is the official documentation for Google Compute Engine,
providing comprehensive details about features, pricing, and best practices.
Google Cloud Platform Overview: This link takes you to the main GCP page, allowing you to explore all

services offered, including Compute Engine.
Question: 283 CertyIQ
Which of the following is not helpful for mitigating the impact of an unexpected failure or reboot?
A. Use persistent disks
B. Configure tags and labels
C. Use startup scripts to re-configure the system as needed
D. Back up your data
Answer: B
Explanation:
Option B, "Configure tags and labels," is the correct answer because it does not directly contribute to
mitigating the impact of an unexpected failure or reboot. While tags and labels are valuable for resource
organization, management, and cost tracking within Google Cloud Platform (GCP), they do not play a role in
data recovery, system restoration, or service continuity following a failure. Persistent disks (Option A) are
crucial for preserving data across reboots and failures. They ensure that data written to the disk remains
available, unlike ephemeral storage that is lost. Startup scripts (Option C) are essential for automatically
reconfiguring applications and services to a desired state after a reboot, ensuring consistency and reducing
manual intervention. Backing up data (Option D) provides a safeguard against data loss due to various
unforeseen events, including failures, and allows restoration to a known-good state. Therefore, while useful
for management, tags and labels lack the crucial functionality needed for resilience to system failures, thus
making it the least helpful option in this context.
Further reading:
Google Cloud Persistent Disks: https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/disks
Google Cloud Startup Scripts: https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/startupscript
Google Cloud Backup and Disaster Recovery: https://cloud.google.com/solutions/backup-disaster-recovery
Google Cloud Tags and Labels: https://cloud.google.com/resource-manager/docs/tags/tags-overview
Question: 284 CertyIQ
Single sign-on (SSO) with G Suite is based on _____?
A. SAML2
B. JWT
C. Service accounts
D. JSON
Answer: A
Explanation:
The correct answer is A, SAML2 (Security Assertion Markup Language 2.0), because it is the standard
protocol used for federated identity and single sign-on (SSO) between G Suite (now Google Workspace) and
external identity providers. G Suite itself acts as a service provider relying on a SAML-based identity provider
to authenticate users. When a user attempts to access a Google Workspace application, the service redirects
the user to their configured identity provider. The identity provider verifies the user’s credentials and returns a

SAML assertion to Google Workspace. This assertion, containing the user's identity and attributes, allows
Google to grant the user access to the requested resource without the user having to re-enter their
credentials. While other technologies like JWT (JSON Web Tokens) are used for authorization and API access,
and service accounts are for applications, not user SSO, SAML is the standard for SSO. JSON is a data
serialization format, not a security protocol. Therefore, SAML2 is the specific technology at the core of SSO
implementation within Google Workspace. Google's documentation consistently references SAML for their
SSO implementation, confirming its central role.
Authoritative links:
Google Workspace Admin Help - Set up SAML SSO for Google Workspace:
https://support.google.com/a/answer/60751
Google Workspace Admin Help - Understand SSO with SAML: https://support.google.com/a/answer/60759
OASIS Security Assertion Markup Language (SAML) TC: https://www.oasis-
open.org/committees/tc_home.php?wg_abbrev=security
Question: 285 CertyIQ
Which tool allows you to sync data in your Google domain with Active Directory?
A. Google Cloud Directory Sync (GCDS)
B. Google Active Directory (GAD)
C. Google Domain Sync Service
D. Google LDAP Sync
Answer: A
Explanation:
The correct answer is A. Google Cloud Directory Sync (GCDS). GCDS is specifically designed to synchronize
user, group, and organizational unit data from an on-premises Microsoft Active Directory environment to
Google Workspace (formerly G Suite). This synchronization allows users to seamlessly access Google
Workspace applications with their existing Active Directory credentials, simplifying user management and
reducing administrative overhead. GCDS acts as a bridge, continuously monitoring changes in Active Directory
and replicating those changes to Google's directory service. It offers granular control over which data is
synchronized, and it can handle complex organizational structures and user attributes. Options B, C, and D are
incorrect as they either don't exist or don't serve the purpose of Active Directory synchronization. GADS is not
a valid product name, GDS, although existing in the Google ecosystem, is not relevant to AD sync, and LDAP
sync is a broader concept but not specific to the Google domain sync within the required context. GCDS is
crucial for organizations transitioning to Google Cloud, enabling a hybrid environment where on-premise
identity is integrated with cloud-based services.
For further research, refer to the official Google Cloud documentation:
Google Cloud Directory Sync (GCDS): https://support.google.com/a/answer/1069370?hl=en
About Google Cloud Directory Sync: https://support.google.com/a/answer/1069371?hl=en
Question: 286 CertyIQ
Regarding Cloud Storage: which of the following allows for time-limited access to buckets and objects without a
Google account?
A. Signed URLs

B. gsutil
C. Single sign-on
D. Temporary Storage Accounts
Answer: A
Explanation:
Signed URLs are the correct answer because they grant temporary, limited access to Google Cloud Storage
resources (buckets and objects) without requiring the requestor to have a Google account. These URLs
contain authentication information directly, allowing anyone possessing the URL to perform the specified
action (e.g., read, write) for the predefined time period. This contrasts with methods that require Google
account credentials or more complex authentication mechanisms. gsutil, a command-line tool, while useful for
interacting with Cloud Storage, doesn't itself provide temporary access without authentication. Single sign-on
(SSO) requires a user to authenticate with an identity provider, which still relies on some form of account.
Temporary Storage Accounts is not a standard GCP term; access is typically granted to existing resources via
short-lived credentials, rather than a unique, temporary account. Signed URLs provide a convenient method
for sharing objects publicly for a brief time or enabling controlled access for applications lacking full Google
account privileges. This makes them ideal for scenarios like allowing users to download files from a web
application without granting general access.
Authoritative Links:
Google Cloud Documentation on Signed URLs: https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/access-
control/signed-urls
Google Cloud Documentation on gsutil: https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/gsutil
Question: 287 CertyIQ
Which of the following is a virtual machine instance that can be terminated by Compute Engine without warning?
A. A preemptible VM
B. A shared-core VM
C. A high-cpu VM
D. A standard VM
Answer: A
Explanation:
Preemptible virtual machine (VM) instances in Google Compute Engine are designed to be cost-effective
options for batch processing and fault-tolerant workloads. Unlike regular VMs, preemptible VMs can be
terminated by Compute Engine at any time if resources are needed elsewhere. This preemption is not a
scheduled event; rather, it can occur with minimal notice, typically a 30-second warning in the instance's
metadata. This characteristic makes them unsuitable for critical or time-sensitive applications that require
continuous operation. Shared-core VMs, on the other hand, offer lower virtual CPU resources but don't have
the preemption feature. High-CPU and standard VMs also guarantee uptime and stability for applications that
require consistent compute power. Because preemptible VMs can be terminated without warning, they are the
only option listed that aligns with the question's description. This preemption policy helps Google efficiently
manage its compute resources, passing the cost savings on to the users who can tolerate interruptions. This
interruption is a key distinguishing factor between preemptible and the other VM types. Preemptible VMs are
suitable for workloads that can be easily restarted or jobs that are not impacted by short delays.

Here are some authoritative links for further research:
Google Cloud Documentation on Preemptible VMs:
https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/instances/preemptible
Google Cloud Documentation on Machine Types: https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/machine-types
Question: 288 CertyIQ
Regarding Compute Engine: What is a managed instance group?
A. A managed instance group combines existing instances of different configurations into one manageable
group
B. A managed instance group uses an instance template to create identical instances
C. A managed instance group creates a firewall around instances
D. A managed instance group is a set of servers used exclusively for batch processing
Answer: B
Explanation:
The correct answer is B: A managed instance group uses an instance template to create identical instances.
Managed Instance Groups (MIGs) in Google Compute Engine are designed for managing multiple virtual
machine (VM) instances as a single entity. This differs from a simple collection of independent VMs. At the
core of a MIG is an instance template. This template defines the configuration of the VMs within the group,
including the machine type, operating system image, disk configuration, and network settings. When a MIG is
created, it uses this template to launch multiple identical VM instances. This ensures consistency and
simplifies management across a fleet of VMs. MIGs also offer features like autoscaling (adjusting the number
of instances based on demand) and autohealing (automatically recreating unhealthy instances). Option A is
incorrect because MIGs do not combine pre-existing, differently configured VMs; rather, they create new,
identical VMs. Option C is inaccurate because while firewalls play a role in network security, MIGs are not
primarily about firewall creation. Option D is also incorrect since while MIGs can be used for batch processing,
their core function is broader, encompassing tasks requiring consistent instances like serving web
applications. In essence, MIGs provide a robust and scalable infrastructure for running applications by
leveraging instance templates to manage a homogenous group of virtual machines.
Further research can be conducted at the following links:
Google Cloud documentation on Managed Instance Groups:
https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/instance-groups/
Google Cloud documentation on Instance Templates: https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/instance-
templates
Question: 289 CertyIQ
What type of firewall rule(s) does Google Cloud's networking support?
A. deny
B. allow, deny & filtered
C. allow
D. allow & deny
Answer: D

Explanation:
Google Cloud Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) firewall rules are a fundamental aspect of network security,
controlling traffic flow to and from your virtual machine (VM) instances. These rules are stateful, meaning that
if you allow outbound traffic, the return traffic is automatically allowed, simplifying rule management. Google
Cloud firewall rules support two core actions: allow and deny. Allow rules permit traffic that matches the
specified criteria (source, destination, protocols, ports), whereas deny rules block such traffic. While there
isn't a direct "filtered" action in the way some network devices implement it, the combined effect of allow and
deny rules and their ordering provides fine-grained control, effectively "filtering" network traffic. A "deny"
rule will effectively block traffic as if filtered out. The core functionality revolves around deciding whether
traffic is permitted or blocked, which maps directly to allow and deny actions. The order in which rules are
evaluated matters, and processing stops at the first matching rule. Therefore, it's important to place more
specific rules before general ones for desired behavior. There isn't a separate, explicitly labelled 'filtered'
action type; instead, the combination and precedence of 'allow' and 'deny' rules achieve the desired traffic
filtering.
Further resources:
Google Cloud Firewall Rules Overview: https://cloud.google.com/vpc/docs/firewalls
Using Firewall Rules: https://cloud.google.com/vpc/docs/using-firewalls
Question: 290 CertyIQ
How are subnetworks different than the legacy networks?
A. They're the same, only the branding is different
B. Each subnetwork controls the IP address range used for instances that are allocated to that subnetwork
C. With subnetworks IP address allocation occurs at the global network level
D. Legacy networks are the preferred way to create networks
Answer: B
Explanation:
The correct answer is B. Each subnetwork controls the IP address range used for instances that are
allocated to that subnetwork.
Here's a detailed justification:
Subnetworks are a core component of Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) networks in Google Cloud Platform (GCP),
representing a significant departure from legacy networks. Unlike the older, global networks, subnetworks
offer a crucial level of granularity and control over IP address allocation. In GCP, a VPC network is a global,
logical construct. Within this global network, subnetworks are regional resources that define distinct IP
address ranges.
Each subnetwork is associated with a specific geographical region and has a unique CIDR (Classless Inter-
Domain Routing) block. This CIDR block determines the internal IP address range that will be available to
resources within that subnetwork, like Compute Engine instances. When an instance is created, it's assigned
an internal IP address from the subnetwork it resides in. This separation of IP ranges by region allows for
better network management, scalability, and isolation.
Option A is incorrect as subnetworks are fundamentally different from legacy networks, not just a rebranding.
Option C is also incorrect because IP address allocation is not at the global network level with subnetworks;
it's controlled at the subnetwork level based on regional CIDR block definitions. Option D is incorrect as
legacy networks are deprecated and subnetworks are the recommended approach for creating networks in

GCP. Subnetworks offer improved control, scalability, and security when compared to legacy networks.
Authoritative Links:
Google Cloud VPC Overview: https://cloud.google.com/vpc/docs/vpc - Provides a comprehensive overview of
VPC networks and subnetworks.
Google Cloud Subnetwork Documentation: https://cloud.google.com/vpc/docs/subnets - Delves into the
specifics of subnetworks, their purpose, and how to manage them.
Google Cloud VPC FAQ: https://cloud.google.com/vpc/docs/vpc-faq - Addresses common questions about
VPC networks, including the distinction between legacy and subnetwork-based networks.
Question: 291 CertyIQ
Which of the following is not a valid metric for triggering autoscaling?
A. Google Cloud Pub/Sub queuing
B. Average CPU utilization
C. Stackdriver Monitoring metrics
D. App Engine Task Queues
Answer: D
Explanation:
The correct answer is D, App Engine Task Queues, because they are not a direct metric used for triggering
autoscaling. Autoscaling in Google Cloud Platform (GCP) relies on observable performance indicators to
dynamically adjust the number of running instances. Metrics like Google Cloud Pub/Sub queue depth (A)
indicate the volume of work awaiting processing, which can trigger scaling to handle demand surges. Average
CPU utilization (B) is a fundamental metric reflecting the resource consumption of instances, directly
indicating the need for more or fewer resources. Stackdriver Monitoring metrics (C) provide a broad range of
custom and system-level metrics that can be used as triggers for autoscaling. These metrics offer granular
insights into the performance and health of applications and infrastructure, making them suitable for scaling
decisions.
App Engine Task Queues, on the other hand, are a service for managing background work and are not directly
a metric that dictates the need for autoscaling the instances processing those tasks. While task queue sizes
could indirectly influence resource usage on worker instances, they don't provide the direct, real-time, load-
related information that autoscaling requires. Autoscaling mechanisms operate based on real-time resource
consumption and performance, not the backlog of items waiting to be processed within a queue. The worker
instances handling the queued tasks would typically be scaled based on their CPU or memory usage, which
are monitored by metrics from Stackdriver or other observability tools, and not by the queue length itself.
In essence, autoscaling focuses on ensuring adequate resource capacity based on current load, while task
queues focus on reliably managing and sequencing asynchronous work.
Authoritative Links:
Autoscaling Overview: https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/autoscaling
Autoscaling based on CPU Utilization: https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/autoscaling/using-cpu-
utilization
Autoscaling using Stackdriver Monitoring: https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/autoscaling/using-
monitoring-metrics
Google Cloud Pub/Sub: https://cloud.google.com/pubsub
App Engine Task Queues: https://cloud.google.com/appengine/docs/standard/python/taskqueue/

Question: 292 CertyIQ
Which of the following features makes applying firewall settings easier?
A. Service accounts
B. Tags
C. Metadata
D. Labels
Answer: B
Explanation:
The correct answer is B. Tags.
Tags in Google Cloud Platform (GCP) provide a powerful mechanism for organizing and managing resources,
making firewall rule application much simpler and more efficient. Instead of having to specify individual
instances or IP addresses in firewall rules, you can assign tags to multiple virtual machines (VMs). These tags
then act as selectors in your firewall rules. For example, you can tag all your web servers with "web-server"
and create a single firewall rule allowing HTTP traffic to any resource with that tag. This eliminates the need
for multiple, nearly identical firewall rules and simplifies maintenance as changes to one rule impact all
tagged resources. Tags offer a flexible approach to grouping resources based on their function, environment,
or any criteria that align with your organizational strategy. This is advantageous when applying security rules
based on logical groupings. This approach significantly reduces the complexity of firewall management,
making rule sets more manageable, scalable, and auditable. Other options are not as effective in simplifying
firewall rules. Service accounts (A) primarily deal with permissions and authentication, metadata (C) provides
information about a specific instance, and labels (D) are for organizational purposes like cost tracking but are
not directly applicable in firewall rules.
Authoritative Links:
Google Cloud Documentation on Tags: https://cloud.google.com/vpc/docs/tags
Google Cloud Documentation on Firewall Rules: https://cloud.google.com/vpc/docs/firewalls
Google Cloud Blog on Using Tags for Firewall Rules: [Search Google Cloud Blog with keywords: "Google
Cloud Tags Firewall Rules"] (Note: Exact blog link may vary over time - search using keywords will reveal
relevant and recent posts).
Question: 293 CertyIQ
What option does Cloud SQL offer to help with high availability?
A. Point-in-time recovery
B. The AlwaysOn setting
C. Snapshots
D. Failover replicas
Answer: D
Explanation:
The correct answer is D. Failover replicas. Cloud SQL's primary mechanism for achieving high availability is
through failover replicas. These replicas are synchronous copies of the primary instance, continuously

mirroring data changes. In the event of a primary instance failure, a failover mechanism automatically
promotes the replica to become the new primary, ensuring minimal downtime and continued database service.
This process is typically transparent to applications connecting to the database. Options A, B, and C do not
directly address high availability. Point-in-time recovery (A) aids in data restoration but does not provide
immediate failover capabilities. There is no "AlwaysOn setting" (B) specifically mentioned in Cloud SQL
documentation, and it is a feature more commonly associated with Microsoft SQL Server. Snapshots (C) are
backups of the database at specific times and, while useful for disaster recovery, do not provide continuous
availability. Failover replicas provide active/passive redundancy, a hallmark of high-availability systems. This
design allows for automatic and rapid service restoration in case of primary instance failure, improving the
robustness and reliability of database infrastructure. By contrast, alternatives such as snapshots and backups
would require manual intervention and would involve much longer service interruptions.
Supporting links:
Google Cloud SQL High Availability
Cloud SQL Failover Overview
Cloud SQL Concepts
Question: 294 CertyIQ
Regarding Compute Engine: when executing a startup script on a Linux server which user does the instance
execute the script as?
A. ubuntu
B. The Google provided "gceinstance" user
C. Whatever user you specify in the console
D. root
Answer: D
Explanation:
The correct answer is D. root. When a startup script is executed on a Google Compute Engine Linux instance,
it runs with root privileges by default. This is because the startup script is designed to configure and initialize
the instance, often requiring elevated permissions to install software, modify system settings, and perform
other administrative tasks. The instance's operating system initiates the execution of the startup script early
in the boot process, ensuring that these critical configurations are applied before other services begin.
Running as root allows the script to interact with all parts of the system without encountering permission
issues. Although users like "ubuntu" or a custom user can be specified for general instance access, the
startup script execution is specifically handled by the root user's context to facilitate full administrative
control. Specifying a different user within the instance after the boot process does not affect the initial root
context for executing startup scripts. This default behavior underscores the importance of thoroughly
reviewing and validating startup scripts to avoid potential security risks. The principle of least privilege
suggests that a separate user should be used for typical operations, however, that user will have to be set up
by root within the startup script or instance configuration.
For further information, please see:Google Cloud Documentation on Startup Scripts - This document
discusses how to use startup scripts for Compute Engine instances, and although it does not explicitly state
the user is root, the examples provided show usage of commands requiring root privileges.Google Cloud
Community Thread - This community thread is helpful to illustrate the fact that these scripts are run as
root.Blog Post on GCE Startup Script - This blog post provides helpful guidance on understanding how these
startup scripts are run on GCE.

Question: 295 CertyIQ
Which of the follow methods will not cause a shutdown script to be executed?
A. When an instance shuts down through a request to the guest operating system
B. A preemptible instance being terminated
C. An instances.reset API call
D. Shutting down via the cloud console
Answer: C
Explanation:
The correct answer is C. An instances.reset API call. Here's why:
Shutdown scripts on Google Compute Engine instances are designed to execute during a graceful shutdown
of the virtual machine. They perform actions like cleaning up temporary files or gracefully shutting down
applications before the instance is powered off. These scripts are triggered by events that signal the
operating system's intended shutdown.
Options A, B, and D all result in a standard shutdown process that executes shutdown scripts. Option A
involves an OS-initiated shutdown, naturally triggering shutdown procedures. Preemptible instance
termination (B) is handled gracefully by Google Cloud, which sends a signal to the guest OS, prompting a
normal shutdown sequence and executing scripts. Similarly, shutting down via the Cloud Console (D) initiates
a graceful shutdown process that triggers the shutdown script.
However, an instances.reset API call is akin to a hard reboot or power cycle of a physical server. It does not
signal a graceful shutdown to the guest operating system. Instead, it abruptly restarts the instance,
bypassing the normal operating system shutdown sequence. Consequently, shutdown scripts aren't invoked
as they are meant to operate during a graceful, planned shutdown. This abrupt reboot is different from a
planned shutdown, which includes notification to the OS about a pending shutdown. Therefore, an
instances.reset does not trigger shutdown scripts.
Here are links for further reading on shutdown scripts and instance management:
Google Cloud Documentation - Shutdown scripts: https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/start/startup-
scripts/using-shutdown-scripts
Google Cloud Documentation - Instance reset: https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/instances/reset-
instance
Google Cloud Documentation - Instance states: https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/instances/instance-
lifecycle
Question: 296 CertyIQ
Which type of account would you use in code when you want to interact with Google Cloud services?
A. Google group
B. Service account
C. Code account
D. Google account
Answer: B

Explanation:
The correct answer is B. Service account. Here's why:
When code needs to interact with Google Cloud services programmatically, it requires a secure and
authorized identity. Service accounts provide this identity. They are special types of Google accounts
intended for non-human users, such as applications or virtual machines. Unlike regular user accounts (like
Google accounts), service accounts don't belong to an individual and cannot sign in interactively. Instead, they
are associated with a private key that code can use to authenticate and obtain access tokens.
Using a regular Google account (option D) in code is strongly discouraged for security reasons. Sharing a
personal Google account's credentials exposes the account to potential misuse and violates the principle of
least privilege. Google groups (option A) are for managing collections of users, not for authentication of code.
There is no "code account" (option C) concept in Google Cloud Identity and Access Management (IAM).
Service accounts allow for granular control over permissions. Through IAM, you can assign specific roles to a
service account, limiting its access to only the necessary Google Cloud resources. This promotes the principle
of least privilege and enhances security. This mechanism prevents over-privileging your automated process
and significantly reduces the attack surface.
Service account keys should be securely stored and managed, often using secret managers or environment
variables, to prevent unauthorized access. Using service accounts ensures that applications have the required
permissions without requiring human intervention, following secure coding practices.
For further information, you can refer to Google Cloud's official documentation:
Service accounts overview: https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/service-accounts
Authentication overview: https://cloud.google.com/docs/authentication
IAM roles: https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/understanding-roles
Question: 297 CertyIQ
Which of the following is not an IAM best practice?
A. Use primitive roles by default
B. Treat each component of your application as a separate trust boundary
C. Grant roles at the smallest scope needed
D. Restrict who has access to create and manage service accounts in your project
Answer: A
Explanation:
The correct answer is A. Use primitive roles by default. Primitive roles (Owner, Editor, Viewer) grant broad,
encompassing permissions across a project, violating the principle of least privilege. IAM best practices
advocate for using predefined or custom roles that offer granular access control tailored to specific tasks and
resources. Treating each application component as a separate trust boundary (B) promotes security by
limiting the impact of a potential compromise. Granting roles at the smallest scope needed (C), like at a
resource level instead of the project level, restricts access and minimizes risks. Restricting who can create
and manage service accounts (D) safeguards the identities used by applications and services, preventing
unauthorized access and privilege escalation. Employing primitive roles indiscriminately directly opposes the
fundamental principles of a secure and well-managed cloud environment. For more information on IAM best
practices, refer to Google Cloud's official documentation: https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/best-practices
and https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/understanding-roles. Using predefined or custom roles helps with
security and auditability.

Question: 298 CertyIQ
Which of the following would not reduce your recovery time in the event of a disaster?
A. Make it as easy as possible to adjust the DNS record to cut over to your warm standby server.
B. Replace your warm standby server with a hot standby server.
C. Use a highly preconfigured machine image for deploying new instances.
D. Replace your active/active hybrid production environment (on-premises and GCP) with a warm standby
server.
Answer: D
Explanation:
Option D, replacing an active/active hybrid environment with a warm standby server, would not inherently
reduce recovery time. Here's why:
Active/Active setups inherently offer faster recovery as traffic can be rerouted to a secondary active node
with minimal delay. Both locations are simultaneously handling live traffic. In contrast, a warm standby
requires a switchover period when a disaster occurs, which includes bringing the standby system online,
reconfiguring it, and then directing traffic, all of which takes time. This makes the recovery less immediate
than in an active/active architecture.
DNS adjustments (Option A) are crucial for quick cutovers, making it easier to direct traffic to the standby
server when needed and significantly speeding up the overall switch over process. This is a standard disaster
recovery strategy.
Hot standby servers (Option B) significantly reduce recovery time by remaining continuously synchronized
with the production environment, minimizing the time it takes to take over during a failure. This is because a
hot standby is already running, just not actively processing production traffic.
Preconfigured machine images (Option C) enable faster deployments by streamlining the creation of new
instances which results in faster recovery. With preconfigured images, you don't have to set up all the
dependencies or install software, saving precious time.
By moving from an active/active hybrid environment which already provides built-in redundancy, to a single
warm standby introduces a single point of failure. While a warm standby is better than cold standby in terms
of recovery time, it is still far less efficient than an active/active setup when recovering from disasters.
In essence, although a warm standby is an adequate recovery option, it doesn't contribute to a faster recovery
than an active/active system already in place.
Authoritative Links:
Google Cloud Documentation on Disaster Recovery: https://cloud.google.com/architecture/disaster-recovery
Active-Active vs Active-Passive Architecture: https://www.imperva.com/blog/active-passive-vs-active-
active-for-high-availability/
Warm Standby Disaster Recovery: https://www.veritas.com/content/support/en_US/doc/17099210-
136317244-0/v128343773
Question: 299 CertyIQ

Which of the following is not a best practice for mitigating Denial of Service attacks on your Google Cloud
infrastructure?
A. Block SYN floods using Cloud Router
B. Isolate your internal traffic from the external world
C. Scale to absorb the attack
D. Reduce the attack surface for your GCE deployment
Answer: A
Explanation:
The correct answer is A, "Block SYN floods using Cloud Router," because Cloud Router is not designed for and
cannot effectively mitigate SYN flood attacks. Cloud Router's primary function is managing dynamic routing
between your Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) networks and on-premises networks using protocols like BGP. It
doesn't operate at the layer required to inspect or filter individual TCP packets associated with SYN floods.
Options B, C, and D are all recognized best practices. Isolating internal traffic (B) reduces exposure and limits
the impact of attacks that manage to penetrate the perimeter. Scaling resources to absorb the attack (C),
such as horizontally scaling your web servers, distributes the load and prevents service outages. Reducing the
attack surface (D), by minimizing the number of exposed ports, applications, and services, limits the potential
entry points for malicious traffic.
SYN flood attacks, specifically, target the TCP handshake process. Effective mitigation involves measures like
SYN cookies or connection rate limiting, typically handled by firewalls, load balancers, or DDoS protection
services. Google Cloud Armor, for instance, provides sophisticated mechanisms for protecting against such
Layer 4 and Layer 7 attacks. Cloud Router's focus is solely on routing at the network layer, and lacks these
functionalities.
Authoritative Resources:
Google Cloud Armor Overview: https://cloud.google.com/armor/docs/overview
Google Cloud Load Balancing: https://cloud.google.com/load-balancing
Cloud Router Documentation: https://cloud.google.com/router/docs
DDoS Protection Best Practices: https://cloud.google.com/architecture/security/denial-of-service-mitigation-
best-practices
Question: 300 CertyIQ
Which is the fastest instance storage option that will still be available when an instance is stopped?
A. Local SSD
B. Standard Persistent Disk
C. SSD Persistent Disk
D. RAM disk
Answer: C
Explanation:
The correct answer is C. SSD Persistent Disk. Here's why:
Persistent disks, as their name suggests, retain data even when an instance is stopped or terminated. They are
network-attached block storage devices, meaning they're not directly tied to the physical hardware of a
specific virtual machine. Both standard and SSD persistent disks offer this durability. However, SSD

persistent disks (SSD PD) provide much faster I/O performance compared to standard persistent disks,
utilizing solid-state drives for data storage.
Local SSDs (option A) offer incredibly fast I/O speeds as they are physically attached to the instance's
hardware. However, their data is ephemeral, meaning it's deleted when the instance is stopped or terminated.
RAM disks (option D) utilize the instance's volatile memory for storage, making them the fastest option but
also the most transient; data is lost even on a simple reboot. Therefore, neither local SSD nor RAM disks
satisfy the requirement of data persistence across instance stops.
Thus, SSD Persistent Disk is the fastest storage option among those listed that persists data when an
instance is stopped, combining durability and good performance. While faster options exist within GCP, like
Hyperdisk, they weren't listed in the question, making SSD Persistent Disk the correct answer within the
provided options.
Authoritative Links for Further Research:
Google Cloud Storage Options: https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/storage-options
Compute Engine Disks: https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/disks
Persistent Disk Performance: https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/disks/performance
Choosing a Storage Option : https://cloud.google.com/architecture/choosing-storage-option
Question: 301 CertyIQ
Which of these statements about Microsoft licenses is true?
A. You can migrate your existing Microsoft application licenses to Compute Engine instances, but not your
Microsoft Windows licenses.
B. You can migrate your existing Microsoft Windows and Microsoft application licenses to Compute Engine
instances.
C. You cannot migrate your existing Microsoft Windows or Microsoft application licenses to Compute Engine
instances.
D. You can migrate your existing Microsoft Windows licenses to Compute Engine instances, but not your
Microsoft application licenses.
Answer: B
Explanation:
The correct answer is B because Google Cloud Platform (GCP) allows for the migration of both existing
Microsoft Windows Server licenses and Microsoft application licenses to Compute Engine virtual machines.
This capability is facilitated through Microsoft's License Mobility through Software Assurance program.
Essentially, if you have eligible licenses with active Software Assurance, you can bring those licenses to GCP,
avoiding the cost of paying for new licenses bundled with the cloud instances. This is a significant benefit for
organizations already invested in Microsoft technologies as it allows them to leverage existing assets while
moving to the cloud. It reduces overall costs and promotes efficient resource utilization. License mobility
ensures legal compliance and provides flexibility in deploying Microsoft workloads on GCP. This reduces
vendor lock-in and offers hybrid cloud options. Options A, C, and D are incorrect as they do not accurately
reflect the licensing options available through Microsoft and Google Cloud.
Supporting Resources:
Microsoft License Mobility through Software Assurance: https://www.microsoft.com/en-
us/licensing/licensing-programs/software-assurance-license-mobility
Google Cloud documentation on Microsoft licensing: https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/os-
licenses/microsoft

Question: 302 CertyIQ
Which database services support standard SQL queries?
A. Cloud Bigtable and Cloud SQL
B. Cloud Spanner and Cloud SQL
C. Cloud SQL and Cloud Datastore
D. Cloud SQL
Answer: B
Explanation:
The correct answer is B: Cloud Spanner and Cloud SQL. Both of these services are relational database
systems that support standard SQL queries. Cloud SQL is a fully-managed relational database service
offering MySQL, PostgreSQL, and SQL Server instances, all of which utilize SQL as their query language.
Cloud Spanner is a globally distributed, scalable database service that also uses standard SQL (with some
extensions). Cloud Bigtable, mentioned in option A, is a NoSQL wide-column store not designed for standard
SQL; it is accessed using its own APIs. Cloud Datastore, in option C, is also a NoSQL datastore, falling under
the category of document databases, and similarly doesn't support standard SQL. Options A and C incorrectly
include NoSQL databases that lack SQL support. Option D only lists Cloud SQL, while missing Cloud Spanner,
another crucial SQL-compatible service. Therefore, only option B accurately names the two managed
database services that facilitate querying data using standard SQL. This adherence to standard SQL allows
for portability and ease of development for users familiar with relational databases.
Authoritative Links:
Cloud SQL: https://cloud.google.com/sql/docs
Cloud Spanner: https://cloud.google.com/spanner/docs
Cloud Bigtable: https://cloud.google.com/bigtable/docs
Cloud Datastore: https://cloud.google.com/datastore/docs
Question: 303 CertyIQ
Which statement about IP addresses is false?
A. You are charged for a static external IP address for every hour it is in use.
B. You are not charged for ephemeral IP addresses.
C. Google Cloud Engine supports only IPv4 addresses, not IPv6.
D. You are charged for a static external IP address when it is assigned but unused.
Answer: B
Explanation:
The provided answer, B, is indeed the false statement. Here's why:
Ephemeral IP addresses in Google Cloud are not inherently free. While you are not directly charged for their
existence like you are with static external IPs, they are implicitly factored into the overall cost of resources
they are associated with, such as virtual machines or load balancers. Specifically, when a VM is running, the
cost of its ephemeral IP address is bundled into the VM's hourly or committed use cost. If a VM is stopped, its
ephemeral IP address is released and no longer accrues costs. Therefore, it’s inaccurate to say they are

completely free. Option A is true since you are indeed charged for reserving a static external IP address,
regardless of whether it's actively used, until you explicitly release it. Option C is false. Google Cloud does, in
fact, support both IPv4 and IPv6 addresses on its Compute Engine resources. Option D is also true, as
reserving a static IP incurs a charge even if it is not attached to a resource, the charge is to reserve and keep
the address available. This is because Google retains that address solely for your use. In essence, Google
charges for the reservation of the address, not for its usage. This charge is intended to ensure efficient use of
public IP address space. So, while ephemeral IPs aren't individually itemized, their cost is incorporated into the
broader resource costs.
For further information, please refer to the official Google Cloud documentation on IP
addresses:https://cloud.google.com/vpc/network-pricing#ip-address-
pricinghttps://cloud.google.com/vpc/docs/external-ip-addresseshttps://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/ip-
address/
Question: 304 CertyIQ
Which Google Cloud Platform service requires the least management because it takes care of the underlying
infrastructure for you?
A. Container Engine
B. Cloud Engine
C. App Engine
D. Docker containers running on Cloud Engine
Answer: C
Explanation:
The correct answer is C, App Engine, because it's a Platform-as-a-Service (PaaS) offering. App Engine
abstracts away the underlying infrastructure, including servers, operating systems, and patching. This allows
developers to focus solely on writing and deploying code without managing the operational aspects of the
platform. In contrast, Container Engine (A), now Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE), still requires managing the
Kubernetes cluster infrastructure, nodes, and scaling. Cloud Engine (B), now Compute Engine, is an
Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS) offering, giving users control over virtual machines but needing
management of the OS, configurations, and infrastructure. Option D, Docker containers running on Cloud
Engine, exacerbates the management burden by combining Docker container management with the
complexities of Compute Engine VMs. App Engine, being fully managed, automatically scales, load balances,
and handles the complexities of infrastructure, requiring the least user intervention. This is a key
characteristic of a true PaaS offering.
For further research, consider these links:
Google Cloud App Engine: https://cloud.google.com/appengine
Google Cloud Compute Engine: https://cloud.google.com/compute
Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE): https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine
Understanding IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS: https://azure.microsoft.com/en-us/resources/cloud-computing-
dictionary/what-is-iaas-paas-saas/
Question: 305 CertyIQ
To ensure that your application will handle the load even if an entire zone fails, what should you do?

A. Don't select the "Multizone" option when creating your managed instance group.
B. Spread your managed instance group over two zones and overprovision by 100%.
C. Create a regional unmanaged instance group and spread your instances across multiple zones.
D. Overprovision your regional managed instance group by at least 50%.
Answer: D
Explanation:
The correct answer is D. Overprovision your regional managed instance group by at least 50%. Here's the
justification:
The goal is to ensure application availability despite a complete zone failure. This necessitates distributing
resources across multiple zones, a concept known as high availability (HA). A regional managed instance
group (MIG) is crucial here because it automatically manages instances across multiple zones within a region,
providing built-in redundancy. Option A is incorrect because it specifically avoids multi-zonal deployment.
Option B suggests overprovisioning by 100% across two zones, which is valid but not necessarily the most
efficient approach for all situations. Option C, while attempting multi-zonal deployment, recommends
unmanaged instance groups, which lack the automated management and recovery features of MIGs, making
them less suitable for HA.
Option D, overprovisioning a regional MIG by at least 50%, addresses the core issue. It means having
additional capacity beyond the current load. Should one zone fail, the instances in the remaining zones can
absorb the workload from the failed zone. The 50% buffer is a common practice to ensure there's sufficient
capacity during a failure, preventing performance degradation. Regional MIGs also handle instance recreation
and redistribution across zones automatically, making them ideal for HA scenarios. Overprovisioning coupled
with a regional MIG provides both redundancy and automated recovery capabilities required for zone failure
scenarios.
For further information, refer to the official Google Cloud documentation on:
Managed Instance Groups: https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/instance-groups
High Availability: https://cloud.google.com/architecture/framework/reliability/high-availability
Regional Resources: https://cloud.google.com/resource-manager/docs/global-regional-zonal
Question: 306 CertyIQ
If you do not grant a user named Bob permission to access a Cloud Storage bucket, but then use an ACL to grant
access to an object inside that bucket to Bob, what will happen?
A. Bob will be able to access all of the objects inside the bucket because he was granted access to at least one
object in the bucket.
B. Bob will be able to access the object because bucket and object ACLs are independent of each other.
C. Bob will not be able to access the object because he does not have access to the bucket.
D. It is not possible to grant access to an object when it is inside a bucket for which a user does not have access.
Answer: B
Explanation:
The correct answer is B: Bob will be able to access the object because bucket and object ACLs are
independent of each other. This scenario highlights the critical distinction between bucket-level permissions
and object-level permissions within Google Cloud Storage (GCS). Bucket-level permissions, typically managed
using IAM (Identity and Access Management), control access to the bucket itself and all its contents as a

default. Object-level permissions, primarily managed using Access Control Lists (ACLs), apply specifically to
individual objects within a bucket. Even if a user lacks bucket-level permissions, granting an ACL on an
individual object will supersede the bucket-level default.
In this case, since Bob doesn't have IAM access to the bucket, he cannot list bucket contents or perform
bucket-level operations. However, the ACL attached to the particular object gives him explicit permission to
that single object. These ACL permissions act independently of IAM bucket permissions. GCS first checks
object ACLs; if an ACL grants access, GCS then checks if the user has the bucket level access via IAM before
granting access. Since ACL explicitly grants Bob access to the object, he will be able to access only that
object, despite not having access to the bucket itself. Option A is incorrect, as granting access to one object
does not automatically grant access to all objects. Option C is incorrect because object ACLs can indeed
grant access even if there is no access at the bucket level. Option D is also incorrect because GCS allows
independent object ACLs.
For further clarification, you can review the official Google Cloud documentation on:
Access Control Overview: https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/access-control/
IAM for Cloud Storage: https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/access-control/iam
Using ACLs: https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/access-control/using-acls
Understanding the difference between IAM and ACLs, and their hierarchical relationship, is essential for
effectively managing access control in GCS.
Question: 307 CertyIQ
To set up a virtual private network between your office network and Google Cloud Platform and have the routes
automatically updated when the network topology changes, what is the minimal number of each type of
component you need to implement?
A. 2 Cloud VPN Gateways and 1 Peer Gateway
B. 1 Cloud VPN Gateway, 1 Peer Gateway, and 1 Cloud Router
C. 2 Peer Gateways and 1 Cloud Router
D. 2 Cloud VPN Gateways and 1 Cloud Router
Answer: B
Explanation:
The correct answer is B: 1 Cloud VPN Gateway, 1 Peer Gateway, and 1 Cloud Router. Here's the breakdown:
To establish a hybrid network connection between your office (on-premises) and Google Cloud Platform (GCP)
using a VPN, you need a VPN gateway on each side. In GCP, this is represented by a Cloud VPN Gateway,
which is a managed service that provides the necessary connection endpoint. On your office side, you have a
physical or virtual router that acts as your peer gateway. This gateway isn't a GCP resource; it’s part of your
existing network infrastructure.
To enable dynamic routing and automatically update routes when your network changes, you need a Cloud
Router. Cloud Router provides BGP (Border Gateway Protocol) routing, a standard industry protocol that
allows network devices to automatically learn and share routing information. With BGP, changes in your on-
premises network are advertised to the Cloud Router, which in turn updates GCP routes, and vice versa. This
eliminates the need for manual route updates, providing a more robust and scalable solution.
Therefore, the minimum required components are one Cloud VPN Gateway on the GCP side, one peer gateway
on your office side (which is assumed as a given), and one Cloud Router for dynamic routing. Two Cloud VPN

Gateways (option A and D) is not required for a single VPN connection between one office and one VPC.
Option C, 2 peer gateways, implies setting up 2 physical offices and is out of scope.
Authoritative Links:
Cloud VPN Overview: https://cloud.google.com/network-connectivity/docs/vpn/concepts/overview
Cloud Router Overview: https://cloud.google.com/network-connectivity/docs/router/concepts/overview
BGP Routing with Cloud Router: https://cloud.google.com/network-connectivity/docs/router/concepts/bgp
Question: 308 CertyIQ
Which of the following statements about encryption on GCP is not true?
A. Google Cloud Platform encrypts customer data stored at rest by default.
B. Each encryption key is itself encrypted with a set of master keys.
C. If you want to manage your own encryption keys for data on Google Cloud Storage, the only option is
Customer-Managed Encryption Keys (CMEK) using Cloud KMS.
D. Data in Google Cloud Platform is broken into subfile chunks for storage, and each chunk is encrypted at the
storage level with an individual encryption key.
Answer: C
Explanation:
Option C is incorrect because it presents a false limitation on customer-managed encryption keys for Google
