Cloud Storage. While Customer-Managed Encryption Keys (CMEK) using Cloud KMS is a valid and popular
method for managing your own encryption keys, it is not the only option. Google Cloud Storage also supports
Customer-Supplied Encryption Keys (CSEK), which allow you to provide your own encryption keys directly,
without them being stored or managed by Google. This option is suitable for organizations that need to
maintain complete control over their encryption keys, even if it means more operational overhead. Both CMEK
and CSEK fulfill the requirement of customer management of encryption, but CSEK does not involve Google
Cloud KMS for key storage. The other options are true; GCP encrypts data at rest by default, encryption keys
are wrapped with master keys for protection, and data is chunked and encrypted individually.
Further Reading:
Google Cloud Storage Encryption: https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/encryption
Customer-Managed Encryption Keys (CMEK): https://cloud.google.com/kms/docs/cmek
Customer-Supplied Encryption Keys (CSEK): https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/encryption/customer-
supplied-keys
Question: 309 CertyIQ
Which database service requires that you configure a failover replica to make it highly available?
A. Cloud Spanner
B. Cloud SQL
C. BigQuery
D. Cloud Datastore
Answer: B
Explanation:

The correct answer is B, Cloud SQL. Unlike other options, Cloud SQL requires explicit configuration of a
failover replica to achieve high availability. Cloud Spanner, a globally distributed database, offers built-in high
availability and fault tolerance without manual replica configuration. BigQuery, a data warehousing service, is
also inherently highly available due to its architecture, which replicates data across multiple zones. Cloud
Datastore, a NoSQL document database, provides automatic replication for high availability. Cloud SQL, on
the other hand, typically involves a primary instance and a separate failover replica, which is manually
configured. This failover replica takes over if the primary instance becomes unavailable. Without a configured
failover replica, a Cloud SQL instance does not offer high availability. The setup of this failover mechanism
involves defining the replica's zone and configuring replication, making it crucial for robust application
resilience in a production environment. Therefore, Cloud SQL is the only option that explicitly necessitates the
user to set up failover replica for ensuring high availability.
Authoritative Links:
Cloud SQL High Availability: https://cloud.google.com/sql/docs/mysql/configure-ha
Cloud Spanner High Availability: https://cloud.google.com/spanner/docs/high-availability
BigQuery Availability: https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/availability
Cloud Datastore Replication and Consistency: https://cloud.google.com/datastore/docs/concepts/replication
Question: 310 CertyIQ
Which of these is not a principle you should apply when setting roles and permissions?
A. Whenever possible, assign roles to groups instead of to individuals.
B. Grant users the appropriate permissions to facilitate least privilege
C. Whenever possible, assign primitive roles rather than predefined roles.
D. Audit all policy changes by checking the Cloud Audit Logs.
Answer: C
Explanation:
The correct answer is C. "Whenever possible, assign primitive roles rather than predefined roles," is not a
principle for setting roles and permissions in Google Cloud Platform (GCP). In fact, it's the opposite of best
practice. Primitive roles (Owner, Editor, Viewer) grant very broad access, often encompassing more
permissions than necessary. This violates the principle of least privilege, which is crucial for security.
Predefined roles, on the other hand, are granular and tailored to specific services or functions. By using
predefined roles, you grant users only the necessary permissions to complete their tasks, minimizing the
potential for accidental or malicious misuse. Assigning roles to groups (A) instead of individuals streamlines
permission management and reduces administrative overhead. Granting least privilege (B) ensures that users
have just the access they need and no more, limiting potential impact from compromised accounts. Auditing
policy changes (D) via Cloud Audit Logs provides a record of access modifications, enabling security
monitoring and compliance. Using primitive roles whenever possible contradicts this approach and can
introduce unnecessary security risks.
For further information, you can consult the following official Google Cloud documentation:
Understanding roles: https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/understanding-roles
IAM best practices: https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/best-practices
Least privilege: https://cloud.google.com/security/gdpr/least-privilege

Question: 311 CertyIQ
Which of these is not a recommended method of authenticating an application with a Google Cloud service?
A. Use the gcloud and/or gsutil commands.
B. Request an OAuth2 access token and use it directly.
C. Embed the service account's credentials in the application's source code.
D. Use one of the Google Cloud Client Libraries.
Answer: C
Explanation:
The correct answer is C. Embedding a service account's credentials directly into application source code is a
highly discouraged and insecure practice. This approach, often involving placing JSON keys within the
codebase, exposes sensitive authentication information, creating significant security risks. If the code
repository or the application is compromised, the service account keys could be readily obtained, granting
unauthorized access to Google Cloud resources. Best practices prioritize robust security measures over
convenience.
Option A, using gcloud and gsutil commands, leverages pre-configured credentials or temporary tokens,
managed outside the application code, offering a safer method. Option B, requesting an OAuth2 access token,
allows applications to act on behalf of users or service accounts through short-lived, managed tokens,
enhancing security through token rotation and limited scope. Option D, utilizing Google Cloud Client Libraries,
incorporates secure and established methods for authenticating with Google Cloud services using features
like Application Default Credentials (ADC), which automatically handle authentication based on the
environment. These libraries abstract away complex credential management, promoting both developer
efficiency and security. In contrast, directly embedding credentials lacks such protection and poses severe
risks.
Therefore, while options A, B, and D are standard and recommended approaches, option C exposes long-term
sensitive secrets and should absolutely be avoided. Security principles like least privilege and secret
management dictate the use of temporary or environment-based credentials rather than embedded, static
secrets.
For further research, explore these links:
Google Cloud IAM overview: https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/overview
Google Cloud Authentication: https://cloud.google.com/docs/authentication
Service Account Best Practices: https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/service-account-best-practices
Application Default Credentials: https://cloud.google.com/docs/authentication/application-default-
credentials
Question: 312 CertyIQ
What are two different features that fully isolate groups of VM instances?
A. Firewall rules and subnetworks
B. Networks and subnetworks
C. Subnetworks and projects
D. Projects and networks
Answer: D

Explanation:
The correct answer is D, Projects and Networks. Here's why:
Projects in Google Cloud Platform (GCP) represent a fundamental level of resource isolation. Each project
acts as a separate administrative boundary, with its own set of users, permissions, and resources. VMs within
one project cannot directly interact with VMs in another project without explicit configuration and
authorization (e.g., using VPC peering, shared VPC, or load balancing). This inherent segregation provides a
high degree of security and operational independence, fulfilling the "fully isolate" requirement.
Networks (specifically, Virtual Private Cloud or VPC networks) also establish isolation. Each network is
globally scoped within a project and provides a logical boundary for resources. VM instances within the same
VPC network can communicate by default using internal IP addresses, however, VMs residing in different VPC
networks, even within the same project, are isolated at the network layer. They need routes, firewall rules, and
possibly peering/VPN to communicate.
Option A is partially correct because firewall rules do add a layer of control, but they don't fully isolate groups
of instances. They primarily manage traffic within or between networks. Subnetworks are subdivisions within
a VPC and don't inherently isolate at a larger scale. Option B is incorrect as while networks offer a level of
isolation, subnetworks further refine within a network. Option C is partially correct because subnetworks are a
mechanism for network segmentation, not a primary means of full isolation and projects do offer isolation. The
key here is full isolation at the broadest organizational levels.
Therefore, projects and networks provide two different and fully isolating features, with project isolation
being the most broad and absolute, and network isolation providing an additional layer.
For further research, refer to the following official Google Cloud documentation:
Organization and projects: https://cloud.google.com/resource-manager/docs/cloud-platform-resource-
hierarchy
VPC networks: https://cloud.google.com/vpc/docs/vpc
Firewall rules: https://cloud.google.com/vpc/docs/firewalls
Question: 313 CertyIQ
Suppose you have a web server that is working properly, but you can't connect to its instance VM over SSH. Which
of these troubleshooting methods can you use without disrupting production traffic? (Select 3 answers.)
A. Create a snapshot of the disk and use it to create a new disk; then attach the new disk to a new instance
B. Use netcat to try to connect to port 22
C. Access the serial console output
D. Create a startup script to collect information.
Answer: ABC
Explanation:
Here's a detailed justification for why options A, B, and C are correct troubleshooting methods while option D
is not, in the context of diagnosing an SSH connectivity issue to a production web server without disrupting
live traffic:
Option A (Create a snapshot of the disk and use it to create a new disk; then attach the new disk to a new
instance): This approach is non-disruptive because it operates on a copy of the disk, not the live disk itself. By
creating a snapshot, you're essentially taking a point-in-time image. You can then create a new disk from this
snapshot and attach it to a separate, new VM instance. This allows you to perform thorough analysis,

including inspecting configurations and log files, without impacting the original production server. The live
web server continues to operate on its original disk. [Google Cloud documentation on snapshots:
https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/disks/snapshots]
Option B (Use netcat to try to connect to port 22): Netcat is a versatile networking utility used to investigate
network connections. Attempting a connection to port 22 (the default SSH port) allows you to test basic
network connectivity to the server. If netcat succeeds, it indicates that the network is reachable and port 22 is
listening. If not, it implies that network connectivity or the SSH service itself may be at fault. Critically, this is
a passive test that does not interact with the running SSH process in a way that would disrupt service, and
therefore is non-disruptive to production.
Option C (Access the serial console output): The serial console provides a direct, low-level interface to the
VM instance, bypassing the need for a working network connection. You can use this to monitor boot
processes and any errors that might occur before SSH service starts. It allows you to debug problems even if
the network is completely down or SSH is malfunctioning. Accessing the console does not disrupt traffic; it’s
a passive observation point. [Google Cloud documentation on serial console:
https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/troubleshooting/troubleshooting-using-serial-console]
Option D (Create a startup script to collect information): While startup scripts can gather valuable
diagnostics data, modifying the startup script of a running production instance could lead to disruptions and
may interfere with the SSH service itself. Changing the startup script involves a full instance restart, which
clearly causes service disruption. This makes it unsuitable for the described non-disruptive troubleshooting
scenario. In a healthy process, startup scripts are executed only at the creation or reboot of a vm instance.
Therefore, the correct options are A, B, and C. They each allow you to gather information and diagnose an SSH
connectivity issue without interrupting the production web server. Option D is useful but not suitable for a
non-disruptive approach.
Question: 314 CertyIQ
To configure Stackdriver to monitor a web server and let you know if it goes down, what steps do you need to take?
(Select 2 answers.)
A. Install the Stackdriver Logging Agent on the web server
B. Create an alerting policy
C. Install the Stackdriver Monitoring Agent on the web server
D. Create an uptime check
Answer: BD
Explanation:
Here's a detailed justification for why options B and D are the correct choices for configuring Stackdriver (now
Google Cloud Observability) to monitor a web server's availability:
Option B, "Create an alerting policy," is essential because alerting policies define the conditions that trigger
notifications. Without a defined policy, Stackdriver cannot actively inform you about server downtime. You
configure these policies to specify the metric to monitor (e.g., uptime check result), the threshold that
constitutes an issue (e.g., a failed uptime check), and the notification channels (e.g., email, SMS). This is the
core of proactive incident management. Option D, "Create an uptime check," is necessary to actively probe
your web server from various locations and verify its availability and responsiveness. These uptime checks
periodically send HTTP or HTTPS requests to your server. When a check fails, Stackdriver logs this event and
potentially triggers an alert based on a corresponding alerting policy. Uptime checks provide real-time data
about server reachability from an external viewpoint, simulating end-user experience.

Option A, "Install the Stackdriver Logging Agent on the web server," is incorrect because the logging agent
collects logs for analysis and troubleshooting, not for availability monitoring. While logs may indirectly
indicate a problem, they are not the primary source for detecting server downtime. Option C, "Install the
Stackdriver Monitoring Agent on the web server," is also incorrect. Although the monitoring agent is crucial
for gathering performance metrics (CPU, memory, etc.), it doesn't directly confirm if your web server is
accessible from the outside. The agent operates inside the server and cannot detect an outage that
completely renders the server unreachable.
Therefore, a proactive approach to web server monitoring requires a combination of external uptime checks
(D) to verify availability and alert policies (B) to act upon the results of those checks. The combination allows
proactive notification when a server becomes inaccessible.
Authoritative Links:
Google Cloud Observability Documentation - Uptime Checks: https://cloud.google.com/monitoring/uptime-
checks
Google Cloud Observability Documentation - Alerting Policies: https://cloud.google.com/monitoring/alerts
Google Cloud Observability Overview: https://cloud.google.com/monitoring/docs/
Question: 315 CertyIQ
Which of these tools can you use to copy data from AWS S3 to Cloud Storage? (Select 2 answers.)
A. Cloud Storage Transfer Service
B. S3 Storage Transfer Service
C. Cloud Storage Console
D. gsutil
Answer: AD
Explanation:
Here's a detailed justification for why options A and D are the correct tools for transferring data from AWS S3
to Google Cloud Storage (GCS), along with supporting concepts and links:
A. Cloud Storage Transfer Service: This is a managed service specifically designed for moving large datasets
between various cloud storage providers, including AWS S3 and GCS. It offers features like automated
scheduling, managed transfers, and monitoring. It handles complex data migration tasks, making it efficient
and reliable for transferring bulk data. The service takes care of the underlying infrastructure and
orchestration required for a large-scale transfer, abstracting these details away from the user. This is the
best-suited service for ongoing, scheduled or large scale data transfers.
D. gsutil: This command-line tool is part of the Google Cloud SDK and provides robust functionality for
managing GCS buckets and objects. While primarily used with GCS, gsutil can be used to copy data from AWS
S3 due to its ability to access AWS through configured credentials. You'd use a command such as gsutil cp
s3://[AWS bucket]/[object] gs://[GCS bucket]/ after proper setup. It gives more granular control, making it ideal
for one-off transfers, scripting, or situations requiring advanced options.
Why B and C are incorrect:
B. S3 Storage Transfer Service: This is not a Google Cloud service, but an AWS service that allows the
transfer of data into S3, not out of it to Google Cloud. It is specifically designed for AWS to AWS transfers or
external providers into AWS.
C. Cloud Storage Console: While the GCS console is used to manage GCS resources, it does not directly

support transferring data from AWS S3. It's primarily a user interface for managing resources within Google
Cloud. It's designed to work with Google services rather than third-party services.
Key Concepts:
Cloud Interoperability: The requirement to move data between different cloud providers is common, leading
to specialized services such as Storage Transfer Service.
Command-Line Interface (CLI): The gsutil tool provides a powerful way to interact with Google Cloud services
through scripts, enabling automation.
Managed Services: Storage Transfer Service is a managed service, meaning Google handles the
infrastructure, offering reliability and ease of use.
Authoritative Links:
Cloud Storage Transfer Service: https://cloud.google.com/storage-transfer-service
gsutil: https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/gsutil
gsutil with AWS S3: https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/gsutil/commands/cp#example-copying-data-
from-amazon-s3
Question: 316 CertyIQ
What are two of the actions you can take to troubleshoot a virtual machine instance that won't start up at all?
(Select 2 answers.)
A. Increase the CPU and memory on the instance by changing the machine type.
B. Validate that your disk has a valid file system.
C. Examine your virtual machine instance's serial port output.
D. Connect to your virtual machine instance using SSH.
Answer: BC
Explanation:
Here's a justification for why options B and C are the correct actions to troubleshoot a non-starting VM
instance on Google Cloud Platform, and why options A and D are not.
Justification:
When a VM instance fails to start, initial troubleshooting should focus on fundamental issues hindering boot-
up. Option B, "Validate that your disk has a valid file system," is crucial. If the persistent disk's file system is
corrupted or invalid, the VM won't be able to mount the operating system and thus won't boot. A healthy file
system is a prerequisite for the OS to load and for the instance to function. This requires examining logs and
potentially running file system repair utilities if needed. Option C, "Examine your virtual machine instance's
serial port output," is another essential step. The serial console provides access to the instance's boot
messages, which often reveal early errors, kernel panics, or other issues that prevent the VM from starting.
This output is a low-level diagnostic tool that can reveal hardware, bootloader or kernel issues even before
network is initialized.
Option A, "Increase the CPU and memory on the instance by changing the machine type," while potentially
useful for performance issues, is not directly relevant when a VM won't start at all. Resource constraints are
typically a cause of slow performance once the VM is already running. Option D, "Connect to your virtual
machine instance using SSH," is impossible since the VM isn't started; therefore, no network connection
would be available. SSH assumes that the VM instance is running and accessible over the network. These two
actions do not address the root cause of the problem of a VM instance that doesn't start up. The key to
effectively troubleshooting a failed startup is to focus on the pre-boot phase and the fundamental operations

that must succeed before an OS can boot. Examining the disk's integrity and the serial port output provide the
insights needed to diagnose and solve the issue.
Authoritative Links:
Troubleshooting instances: https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/troubleshooting/troubleshooting-
instances
Connecting to serial consoles: https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/instances/interacting-with-serial-
console
Question: 317 CertyIQ
Which statements about application load testing are true? (Select 2 answers.)
A. You should test at the maximum load that you expect to encounter.
B. You should test at 50% more than the maximum load that you expect to encounter.
C. It is not necessary to test sudden increases in traffic since GCP scales seamlessly.
D. Your load tests should include testing sudden increases in traffic.
Answer: AD
Explanation:
Here's a justification for why options A and D are the correct choices for application load testing, and why B
and C are incorrect:
Correct Options:
A. You should test at the maximum load that you expect to encounter. This is essential because
understanding how your application performs under expected peak conditions is crucial for identifying
bottlenecks, ensuring acceptable response times, and validating resource capacity. Testing at the expected
maximum load allows you to gauge whether the application can maintain performance under realistic stress,
preventing unexpected failures or performance degradation in production. It helps determine if the system
can handle the projected user base and transaction volume.
D. Your load tests should include testing sudden increases in traffic. Real-world traffic patterns often
involve unpredictable spikes. Testing how the application handles these surges, often referred to as "burst
traffic," is vital. This enables you to evaluate the application's ability to scale, either automatically or
manually, without experiencing performance issues or crashes. Simulating these sudden increases can reveal
resource limitations or architectural weaknesses. It is particularly important in cloud environments where
auto-scaling is a common practice.
Incorrect Options:
B. You should test at 50% more than the maximum load that you expect to encounter. While testing slightly
beyond the expected maximum is sometimes good practice to discover hidden capacity, aiming for 50% over
the expected max load could unnecessarily add to testing costs. It is more crucial to thoroughly test the
expected maximum load and sudden spikes than to focus on an arbitrary overage. In most scenarios, a safety
margin built into scaling practices provides sufficient buffer for unexpected increases.
C. It is not necessary to test sudden increases in traffic since GCP scales seamlessly. While Google Cloud
Platform (GCP) offers powerful auto-scaling capabilities, relying solely on seamless scaling without testing is
risky. Applications and databases may have limitations that are not obvious to GCP's underlying scaling
infrastructure. Testing helps surface these limitations, allowing for proactive optimization. It also verifies that
scaling is configured and works correctly for specific applications. The underlying infrastructure may scale,
but applications may not always be written to take advantage of auto-scaling effectively.

Authoritative Links:
Google Cloud Documentation on Load Testing: https://cloud.google.com/load-testing
Best Practices for Load Testing: https://www.softwaretestinghelp.com/load-testing-best-practices/
Understanding Cloud Autoscaling: https://aws.amazon.com/autoscaling/ (Concepts generally apply to GCP
as well)
Question: 318 CertyIQ
Which of these statements about resilience testing are true? (Select 2 answers.)
A. In a resilience test, your application should keep running with little or no downtime.
B. To test the resilience of an autoscaling instance group, you can terminate a random instance within that
group.
C. In order for an application to survive instance failures, it should not be stateless.
D. Resilience testing is the same as disaster recovery testing.
Answer: AB
Explanation:
Here's a detailed justification for why options A and B are the correct answers, while C and D are incorrect:
Option A is correct: Resilience testing aims to verify that an application can maintain functionality and
availability, even when encountering failures or disruptions. A key indicator of resilience is minimal or no
downtime. The goal is to ensure the application continues operating smoothly despite issues, which aligns
with the definition of resilience.
Option B is correct: Terminating a random instance within an autoscaling group is a common and effective
resilience testing technique. Autoscaling groups are designed to automatically replace failed instances. By
deliberately terminating an instance, you’re testing the group’s ability to detect the failure, spin up a
replacement, and maintain overall capacity, which validates the group's resilience.
Option C is incorrect: Stateless applications are actually easier to make resilient. Stateless applications don't
store session data locally on a specific instance, allowing any instance to handle any user request. If an
instance fails, the user can seamlessly be redirected to another instance without losing session information.
Stateful applications, which retain session data, require more sophisticated strategies for resilience.
Option D is incorrect: Resilience testing and disaster recovery (DR) testing are related but different.
Resilience testing focuses on maintaining application availability under routine disruptions, like instance
failures or network issues. DR testing, conversely, focuses on recovering the entire application from large-
scale disasters like region or zone outages. Resilience is proactive, while DR is reactive. Therefore, resilience
testing and DR are not the same thing.
Authoritative Links:
Google Cloud Architecture Center - Reliability: https://cloud.google.com/architecture/reliability This page
explains the core concepts of building reliable systems, including resilience.
Google Cloud Documentation - Auto Scaling: https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/autoscaling This
resource describes how autoscaling works and why it’s important for building resilient applications.
Google Cloud Documentation - Disaster Recovery: https://cloud.google.com/solutions/disaster-recovery
Provides insights into how to handle large-scale failures and the differences from resilience testing.

Question: 319 CertyIQ
Which combination of Stackdriver services will alert you about errors generated by your applications and help you
locate the root cause in the code?
A. Monitoring, Trace, and Debugger
B. Monitoring and Error Reporting
C. Debugger and Error Reporting
D. Alerts and Debugger
Answer: C
Explanation:
The correct answer is C. Debugger and Error Reporting. Here's why:
Stackdriver Error Reporting automatically aggregates and analyzes errors produced by your applications,
providing insights into their frequency and impact. It surfaces these errors, making it easy to identify recurring
issues needing attention. This service is specifically designed to capture and display errors thrown by
application code. Google's documentation reinforces this function; see Error Reporting documentation.
Stackdriver Debugger allows you to inspect the state of your applications in production (or pre-production) at
any location in your code without stopping or slowing down your program. This service helps pinpoint the root
cause of identified errors by enabling you to examine call stacks, variable values, and execution paths at the
time of the error. Refer to Cloud Debugger documentation for more information.
While Monitoring provides system-level metrics and alerts on resource usage, it doesn’t directly tie into
application errors at the code level like Error Reporting does. Stackdriver Trace helps with performance
analysis of requests, not specific application code errors like Error Reporting or Debugger. Therefore, these
options, combined or standalone, cannot effectively identify application errors at the code and trace the root
cause as efficiently as options C. Options A, B, and D are therefore incorrect. Alerts is a feature available in
Monitoring for reacting to specific metric conditions but doesn't offer debugging capabilities of Debugger.
Option C's combination of Debugger and Error Reporting services effectively addresses both parts of the
objective: detecting application errors at a code level and tracing their origins through debugging.
Question: 320 CertyIQ
If you have configured Stackdriver Logging to export logs to BigQuery, but logs entries are not getting exported to
BigQuery, what is the most likely cause?
A. The Cloud Data Transfer Service has not been enabled.
B. There isn't a firewall rule allowing traffic between Stackdriver and BigQuery.
C. Stackdriver Logging does not have permission to write to the BigQuery dataset.
D. The size of the Stackdriver log entries being exported exceeds the maximum capacity of the BigQuery
dataset.
Answer: C
Explanation:
The most probable reason for Stackdriver Logging not exporting logs to BigQuery, despite configuration, is a
lack of proper permissions. Stackdriver Logging, as a service, needs explicit authorization to write data to a
BigQuery dataset. This authorization is typically granted through IAM (Identity and Access Management)
roles. If the service account associated with Stackdriver Logging lacks the necessary "BigQuery Data Editor"

or similar role granting write access to the target BigQuery dataset, the logs will not be exported. Options A
and B are less likely: Cloud Data Transfer Service isn't involved in this process, and Google's internal network
handles traffic between Stackdriver and BigQuery, meaning no explicit firewall rules are required. Option D is
also less probable; BigQuery is designed to handle large volumes of data, and export failures due to log size
are less common. Permissions issues are fundamental to cloud security and functionality, making option C the
most logical cause of the problem. Incorrect IAM roles are a frequent source of problems when configuring
integrated cloud services. For more on IAM roles and permissions, refer to Google Cloud's documentation:
https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/overview and specifically for BigQuery roles:
https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/access-control. Details on Stackdriver Logging exports can be found
here: https://cloud.google.com/logging/docs/export/configure_exports.
Question: 321 CertyIQ
You can use Stackdriver to monitor virtual machines on which cloud platforms?
A. Google Cloud Platform, Microsoft Azure
B. Google Cloud Platform
C. Google Cloud Platform, Microsoft Azure, Amazon Web Services
D. Google Cloud Platform, Amazon Web Services
Answer: C
Explanation:
Stackdriver (now part of Google Cloud Operations suite) is designed to be a multi-cloud monitoring and
logging solution. While it integrates deeply with Google Cloud Platform (GCP), its capabilities extend beyond
GCP. Stackdriver's agent can be installed on virtual machines (VMs) running on both Amazon Web Services
(AWS) and Microsoft Azure, enabling you to monitor their performance metrics, collect logs, and set up alerts
within the same Stackdriver console. This provides a unified view of your infrastructure, regardless of the
underlying cloud provider. This capability is particularly useful for organizations using a multi-cloud strategy.
Option A, B and D only list either GCP or a combination of two platforms which fails to encapsulate
Stackdriver's cross-cloud functionality. Option C, which correctly states that Stackdriver monitors VMs on
GCP, AWS, and Azure, is the most complete answer. Therefore, it's the accurate choice as it reflects the full
scope of Stackdriver's monitoring capabilities across major cloud providers.
Authoritative links:
Google Cloud Operations Suite (formerly Stackdriver) Documentation:
https://cloud.google.com/stackdriver/docs
Monitoring AWS EC2 instances with Cloud Monitoring:
https://cloud.google.com/stackdriver/docs/solutions/aws/ec2
Monitoring Azure VMs with Cloud Monitoring: https://cloud.google.com/stackdriver/docs/solutions/azure/vm
Question: 322 CertyIQ
To minimize the risk of someone changing your log files to hide their activities, which of the following principles
would help? (Select 3 answers.)
A. Restrict usage of the owner role for projects and log buckets.
B. Require two people to inspect the logs.
C. Implement object versioning on the log-buckets.

D. Encrypt the logs using Cloud KMS.
Answer: ACD
Explanation:
The correct answer is ACD because these options directly address log tampering and security best practices.
Restricting the use of the project owner role (A) is crucial as it has broad permissions, including modifying
logs. Limiting access to this role minimizes the number of potential actors who could manipulate log data.
Implementing object versioning on log buckets (C) provides a history of log files, allowing detection of any
changes, deletions, or unauthorized alterations. This feature enables rollback to prior versions if tampering is
discovered. Encrypting logs using Cloud KMS (D) adds another layer of security by rendering the log data
unreadable without the necessary cryptographic keys, thus preventing unauthorized access and alteration.
These three principles work together to ensure the integrity and confidentiality of the logging data. Option B,
requiring two people to inspect the logs, doesn't prevent log alteration, merely provides additional review and
isn't a primary security measure. While review is helpful, it doesn't offer the same level of protection as the
other three principles.
Supporting Links:
Cloud Logging Overview: https://cloud.google.com/logging/docs/overview - Provides detailed info on how
Cloud Logging works.
Cloud IAM Best Practices: https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/best-practices-for-using-iam - Offers guidance
on managing access rights and project roles.
Cloud Storage Object Versioning: https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/object-versioning - Explains how to
maintain version histories of your bucket data.
Cloud KMS Overview: https://cloud.google.com/kms/docs/overview - Explains how to manage and use
encryption keys.
Question: 323 CertyIQ
If network traffic between one Google Compute Engine instance and another instance is being dropped, what is
the most likely cause?
A. The instances are on a network with low bandwidth.
B. The TCP keep-alive setting is too short.
C. The instances are on a default network with no additional firewall rules.
D. A firewall rule was deleted.
Answer: D
Explanation:
The most likely cause of dropped network traffic between Google Compute Engine (GCE) instances is a
firewall rule deletion (Option D). Google Cloud Platform (GCP) firewalls act as virtual firewalls at the network
level, controlling inbound and outbound traffic based on defined rules. By default, a newly created network
has an implied deny-all rule for ingress traffic, meaning no incoming connections are allowed unless explicitly
permitted. If a firewall rule that previously allowed communication between the instances is deleted, the
traffic will be blocked and seemingly dropped. While low bandwidth (Option A) could cause slow performance,
it typically wouldn't lead to outright traffic drops. TCP keep-alive settings (Option B) impact connection
persistence, not fundamental network reachability. Option C, a default network with no additional rules, would
prevent initial connection; it would not cause an existing working connection to suddenly stop. Therefore, the
removal of a necessary rule, i.e., deletion of a firewall rule, is the most direct explanation for traffic drops
between instances that previously communicated without issue. Firewall misconfigurations are common

causes for network connectivity issues. Reviewing GCP firewall logs and configurations is the initial step for
troubleshooting these types of problems. To better understand GCP firewall rules and their importance, refer
to the official Google Cloud documentation:
https://cloud.google.com/vpc/docs/firewallshttps://cloud.google.com/vpc/docs/firewall-rules
Question: 324 CertyIQ
Which of the following practices can help you develop more secure software? (Select 3 answers.)
A. Penetration tests
B. Integrating static code analysis tools into your CI/CD pipeline
C. Encrypting your source code
D. Peer review of code
Answer: ABD
Explanation:
Here's a detailed justification for why options A, B, and D are correct, and why C is not:
A. Penetration tests: Penetration testing is a security assessment method that simulates real-world attacks
on your systems to identify vulnerabilities. By actively attempting to exploit weaknesses, penetration tests
reveal potential security flaws that might be overlooked through other means, allowing you to proactively
address them. This is a critical practice for improving software security.
B. Integrating static code analysis tools into your CI/CD pipeline: Static code analysis tools scan source
code for vulnerabilities without actually executing the program. Integrating these tools into your CI/CD
pipeline ensures that every code change is automatically checked for potential security issues. This provides
early feedback, preventing vulnerabilities from propagating into production environments. This practice shifts
security "left," making it a continuous and proactive part of the development process.
D. Peer review of code: Peer code review involves multiple developers examining each other's code for errors,
including potential security vulnerabilities. This collaborative approach leverages collective knowledge to
identify issues that individual developers might miss, fostering better code quality and security. Code reviews
also promote knowledge sharing and improved overall understanding of security best practices.
Why C. Encrypting your source code is incorrect: Encrypting source code does not directly contribute to
secure software in the context of vulnerability detection and prevention. While encryption is crucial for
protecting data at rest and in transit, source code, while proprietary intellectual property, usually isn't
particularly sensitive information, compared to the data the software will process. Rather, it's the application
after the build stage where the security concerns lie. In addition, if source code is encrypted, it cannot be
analyzed by tools nor peer reviewed, defeating the purpose of both B and D. Encrypting source code
complicates the development process without providing significant security advantages. You want your
developers to be able to access, review, modify, and compile code easily and rapidly.
Authoritative Links:
OWASP (Open Web Application Security Project): A wealth of information on web application security,
including best practices for secure development: https://owasp.org/
NIST (National Institute of Standards and Technology): Provides security guidelines and frameworks,
including those relevant to software development: https://www.nist.gov/cybersecurity
Google Cloud Security Documentation: Provides insights into Google Cloud’s security services and best
practices: https://cloud.google.com/security/

Question: 325 CertyIQ
Which two places hold information you can use to monitor the effects of a Cloud Storage lifecycle policy on
specific objects? (Select 2 answers.)
A. Cloud Storage Lifecycle Monitoring
B. Expiration time metadata
C. Access logs
D. Lifecycle config file
Answer: BC
Explanation:
Here's the justification for why options B and C are correct, and why A and D are incorrect:
B. Expiration time metadata: When a lifecycle policy deletes an object, Cloud Storage adds an expiration
time metadata to the object before the actual deletion. This allows you to verify that the object was indeed
affected by a lifecycle rule and also to identify the intended deletion date. This metadata is crucial for
monitoring and auditing the effects of lifecycle policies on individual objects.
C. Access logs: Cloud Storage access logs provide detailed records of all operations performed on your
buckets and objects, including deletions caused by lifecycle policies. You can analyze these logs to track
when specific objects were deleted and by which rule (implicitly). They offer essential insights into how the
policy is actually working and the actions it’s performing on the stored data.
Why A is incorrect:
A. Cloud Storage Lifecycle Monitoring: There isn't a specific "Cloud Storage Lifecycle Monitoring" service or
feature within Google Cloud Platform. While you can monitor overall storage usage and costs, there is no
particular tool dedicated explicitly to lifecycle monitoring itself.
Why D is incorrect:
D. Lifecycle config file: The lifecycle configuration file itself defines what the rules are, not necessarily what
has already happened. It does not provide monitoring of the effects, but rather specifies the parameters of the
policy.
Therefore, expiration time metadata attached to affected objects and access logs are the two locations that
actively track the outcomes of lifecycle policies, making options B and C the correct choices.
Supporting links for further research:
Google Cloud Storage Lifecycle Management: https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/lifecycle
Cloud Storage Access Logs: https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/access-logs
Cloud Storage Object Metadata: https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/metadata
Question: 326 CertyIQ
If you have object versioning enabled on a multi-regional bucket, what will the following lifecycle config file do?
"lifecycle":
"rule": [
"action": "type": "Delete" ,
"condition":
"age": 30,

"isLive": true ,
"action":
"type": "SetStorageClass",
"storageClass": "COLDLINE" ,
"condition":
"age": 365,
"matchesStorageClass": ["MULTI_REGIONAL"] ]
A. Archive objects older than 30 days (the second rule doesn't do anything)
B. Delete objects older than 30 days (the second rule doesn't do anything)
C. Archive objects older than 30 days and move objects to Coldline Storage after 365 days
D. Delete objects older than 30 days and move objects to Coldline Storage after 365 days
Answer: C
Explanation:
Let's break down the lifecycle configuration and determine its effect on the multi-regional bucket with
versioning enabled. The first rule specifies a "Delete" action for objects that are both "live" and older than 30
days. Because versioning is enabled, deleting a "live" object doesn't permanently remove it; instead, it creates
a noncurrent version of the object. Therefore, the rule effectively archives objects older than 30 days by
making them noncurrent. The second rule targets objects that are 365 days old and stored in
"MULTI_REGIONAL" storage, moving them to "COLDLINE" storage. This rule acts independently of the first,
impacting objects that meet its criteria. Consequently, the combined effect is that objects older than 30 days
are archived (made noncurrent), and those older than 365 days initially in "MULTI_REGIONAL" storage are
moved to "COLDLINE". Therefore, option C accurately reflects this combined behavior. The presence of object
versioning modifies how the delete action functions, causing archiving rather than permanent deletion. The
second rule, specifically targeting "MULTI_REGIONAL" objects, proceeds as expected, applying the move to
"COLDLINE" action. Therefore, options A, B and D are incorrect.
Authoritative links for further research:
Cloud Storage Object Lifecycle Management: https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/lifecycle
Cloud Storage Object Versioning: https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/object-versioning
Cloud Storage Storage Classes: https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/storage-classes
Question: 327 CertyIQ
Which of the following statements about Stackdriver Trace are true? (Select 2 answers.)
A. Stackdriver Trace tracks the performance of the virtual machines running the application.
B. Stackdriver Trace tracks the latency of incoming requests.
C. Applications in App Engine automatically submit traces to Stackdriver Trace. Applications outside of App
Engine need to use the Trace SDK or Trace API.
D. To make an application work with Stackdriver Trace, you need to add instrumentation code using the Trace
SDK or Trace API, even if the application is in App
Answer: BC
Explanation:
Here's a detailed justification for why options B and C are the correct answers regarding Stackdriver Trace,
while options A and D are incorrect:
Correct Options:

B. Stackdriver Trace tracks the latency of incoming requests. Stackdriver Trace is specifically designed to
monitor and analyze the latency of requests as they propagate through a distributed system. It helps identify
bottlenecks and performance issues by providing detailed timing information for each step in a request's
journey. This is a core functionality of trace analysis tools, focusing on request performance rather than
overall system performance metrics.
C. Applications in App Engine automatically submit traces to Stackdriver Trace. Applications outside of
App Engine need to use the Trace SDK or Trace API. Google App Engine is tightly integrated with
Stackdriver, and by default, it automatically traces requests. For applications hosted elsewhere (like Compute
Engine, Kubernetes Engine), developers need to explicitly instrument their code using the Trace SDK or API to
send trace data to Stackdriver. This ensures that trace data is available for all relevant parts of your
application.
Incorrect Options:
A. Stackdriver Trace tracks the performance of the virtual machines running the application. While the
performance of VMs can influence application performance, Stackdriver Trace does not directly monitor VM-
specific metrics like CPU utilization or memory usage. These types of metrics are captured by Stackdriver
Monitoring. Stackdriver Trace focuses solely on the lifecycle of requests, not the underlying infrastructure's
performance.
D. To make an application work with Stackdriver Trace, you need to add instrumentation code using the
Trace SDK or Trace API, even if the application is in App Engine. As stated in the justification for Option C,
App Engine has automatic trace submission. Instrumentation is not required in App Engine applications unless
you want to gain more insight at the function level with manual instrumentation using SDK or API to create
custom traces for specific sections of the code.
In summary, Stackdriver Trace is a tool primarily focused on understanding request latency within
applications. It works seamlessly with App Engine, where tracing is often automatic, but requires explicit
instrumentation using APIs or SDKs for other environments. It does not focus on general VM performance
metrics.
Authoritative links for further research:
Stackdriver Trace Overview: https://cloud.google.com/trace/docs/overview
Using Stackdriver Trace with App Engine: https://cloud.google.com/trace/docs/setup/appengine
Using the Trace API: https://cloud.google.com/trace/docs/reference/v2/rest
Using the Trace SDKs: https://cloud.google.com/trace/docs/setup/libraries
Question: 328 CertyIQ
For this question, refer to the TerramEarth case study. You start to build a new application that uses a few Cloud
Functions for the backend. One use case requires a Cloud Function func_display to invoke another Cloud Function
func_query. You want func_query only to accept invocations from func_display. You also want to follow Google's
recommended best practices. What should you do?
A. Create a token and pass it in as an environment variable to func_display. When invoking func_query, include
the token in the request. Pass the same token to func_query and reject the invocation if the tokens are
different.
B. Make func_query 'Require authentication.' Create a unique service account and associate it to func_display.
Grant the service account invoker role for func_query. Create an id token in func_display and include the token
to the request when invoking func_query.
C. Make func_query 'Require authentication' and only accept internal traffic. Create those two functions in the
same VPC. Create an ingress firewall rule for func_query to only allow traffic from func_display.
D. Create those two functions in the same project and VPC. Make func_query only accept internal traffic.
Create an ingress firewall for func_query to only allow traffic from func_display. Also, make sure both

functions use the same service account.
Answer: B
Explanation:
The correct answer is B. This option leverages Google Cloud's Identity and Access Management (IAM) and
service accounts, which are best practices for securing inter-service communication. Here's why:
Option B enforces authentication for func_query, ensuring only authorized entities can invoke it. By creating a
dedicated service account for func_display and granting it the Cloud Functions Invoker role on func_query, we
establish a secure, least-privilege access control mechanism. When func_display invokes func_query, it uses an
identity token obtained through its service account, which func_query then validates. This approach prevents
unauthorized access from other functions or external sources. This approach avoids the complexity of
maintaining and validating custom tokens, making it a more robust and scalable solution than option A. Option
C and D use VPC and firewall rules which are meant for restricting access from the network level but do not
offer robust authentication and authorization, which are better served by IAM and service accounts. Thus, B is
the best approach for secure, authenticated, and authorized function-to-function communication within
Google Cloud.
Here are some authoritative links for further research:
Google Cloud IAM: https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs
Cloud Functions Service Accounts: https://cloud.google.com/functions/docs/reference/iam/roles
Authenticating with Service Accounts: https://cloud.google.com/docs/authentication/getting-started
Cloud Functions Authentication: https://cloud.google.com/functions/docs/securing/authenticating
Question: 329 CertyIQ
For this question, refer to the TerramEarth case study. You have broken down a legacy monolithic application into a
few containerized RESTful microservices.
You want to run those microservices on Cloud Run. You also want to make sure the services are highly available
with low latency to your customers. What should you do?
A. Deploy Cloud Run services to multiple availability zones. Create Cloud Endpoints that point to the services.
Create a global HTTP(S) Load Balancing instance and attach the Cloud Endpoints to its backend.
B. Deploy Cloud Run services to multiple regions. Create serverless network endpoint groups pointing to the
services. Add the serverless NEGs to a backend service that is used by a global HTTP(S) Load Balancing
instance.
C. Deploy Cloud Run services to multiple regions. In Cloud DNS, create a latency-based DNS name that points
to the services.
D. Deploy Cloud Run services to multiple availability zones. Create a TCP/IP global load balancer. Add the Cloud
Run Endpoints to its backend service.
Answer: B
Explanation:
Here's a detailed justification for why option B is the correct approach for deploying highly available, low-
latency microservices on Cloud Run for TerramEarth, based on the provided case study context:
Option B leverages several key Google Cloud Platform (GCP) services designed for scalability and global
reach. Deploying Cloud Run services to multiple regions (not just availability zones within a single region, as in
options A and D) is crucial for high availability and disaster recovery. If one region experiences an outage,
traffic can be automatically routed to healthy regions. Serverless network endpoint groups (NEGs) act as
abstractions for Cloud Run services, allowing them to be seamlessly integrated with load balancers. A global

HTTP(S) Load Balancing instance is the centerpiece, distributing traffic across these multiple regions based
on user proximity and health checks. This ensures low latency by directing users to the closest healthy
instance. Option A uses Cloud Endpoints which are for API management, not load balancing and would not
distribute traffic as desired. Option C lacks the robust, automated failover and intelligent routing capabilities
of global load balancing. Relying solely on latency-based DNS for routing can be less responsive to failures
and less granular in traffic distribution than a true load balancer. Option D is not correct as TCP/IP load
balancing can not handle Cloud Run endpoints. Global HTTP(S) Load Balancer is the recommended way for
handling global traffic and routing to Cloud Run. Therefore, option B's combination of regional deployment,
serverless NEGs, and a global load balancer delivers the high availability and low latency required by the
TerramEarth case study, effectively handling traffic from a global customer base.
Authoritative links for further research:
Cloud Run: https://cloud.google.com/run/docs
Serverless NEGs: https://cloud.google.com/load-balancing/docs/negs/serverless-neg-concepts
Global HTTP(S) Load Balancing: https://cloud.google.com/load-balancing/docs/https
Network Endpoint Groups (NEGs): https://cloud.google.com/load-balancing/docs/negs/
Question: 330 CertyIQ
For this question, refer to the TerramEarth case study. You are migrating a Linux-based application from your
private data center to Google Cloud. The
TerramEarth security team sent you several recent Linux vulnerabilities published by Common Vulnerabilities and
Exposures (CVE). You need assistance in understanding how these vulnerabilities could impact your migration.
What should you do? (Choose two.)
A. Open a support case regarding the CVE and chat with the support engineer.
B. Read the CVEs from the Google Cloud Status Dashboard to understand the impact.
C. Read the CVEs from the Google Cloud Platform Security Bulletins to understand the impact.
D. Post a question regarding the CVE in Stack Overflow to get an explanation.
E. Post a question regarding the CVE in a Google Cloud discussion group to get an explanation.
Answer: AC
Explanation:
The correct answers are A and C. Here's why:
Option A: Open a support case regarding the CVE and chat with the support engineer. This is a valid
approach because Google Cloud Support can provide specific guidance related to how CVEs affect Google
Cloud services and offer mitigation strategies tailored to your environment. They have access to internal
knowledge and can provide the most accurate and relevant information for the migration process. This direct
interaction allows for a personalized approach to addressing your specific security concerns.
Option C: Read the CVEs from the Google Cloud Platform Security Bulletins to understand the impact.
Google publishes Security Bulletins that detail known vulnerabilities impacting Google Cloud services. These
bulletins provide authoritative information about the nature of the vulnerability, its potential impact on Google
Cloud, and recommended actions or patches. Checking these bulletins is crucial for understanding if the Linux
vulnerabilities you are concerned about are also relevant to your migrated environment or other Google Cloud
services you may be using.
Why other options are incorrect:
Option B: Read the CVEs from the Google Cloud Status Dashboard to understand the impact. The Google
Cloud Status Dashboard primarily displays the availability and operational status of Google Cloud services. It

does not typically provide detailed information about individual CVEs or their specific impact on user
configurations.
Option D: Post a question regarding the CVE in Stack Overflow to get an explanation. While Stack Overflow
is a helpful resource, it's not the best place for receiving authoritative guidance about CVE impact within
Google Cloud. Responses might not be accurate, and you might not get specific information tailored to your
Google Cloud environment.
Option E: Post a question regarding the CVE in a Google Cloud discussion group to get an explanation.
Similar to Stack Overflow, Google Cloud discussion groups offer community-based support. While you might
get help, it is not a reliable way to assess the security impact of vulnerabilities to your environment, and does
not guarantee an accurate or timely response.
In Summary:
For specific and authoritative information on how CVEs impact your Google Cloud migration, relying on
Google Cloud's official support channels (option A) and its security bulletins (option C) is the most effective
and secure strategy. This approach allows you to take proactive steps and mitigate any potential security
risks.
Authoritative Links:
Google Cloud Support: https://cloud.google.com/support/docs/
Google Cloud Platform Security Bulletins: https://cloud.google.com/support/bulletins
Question: 331 CertyIQ
For this question, refer to the TerramEarth case study. TerramEarth has a legacy web application that you cannot
migrate to cloud. However, you still want to build a cloud-native way to monitor the application. If the application
goes down, you want the URL to point to a "Site is unavailable" page as soon as possible. You also want your Ops
team to receive a notification for the issue. You need to build a reliable solution for minimum cost. What should you
do?
A. Create a scheduled job in Cloud Run to invoke a container every minute. The container will check the
application URL. If the application is down, switch the URL to the "Site is unavailable" page, and notify the Ops
team.
B. Create a cron job on a Compute Engine VM that runs every minute. The cron job invokes a Python program to
check the application URL. If the application is down, switch the URL to the "Site is unavailable" page, and
notify the Ops team.
C. Create a Cloud Monitoring uptime check to validate the application URL. If it fails, put a message in a
Pub/Sub queue that triggers a Cloud Function to switch the URL to the "Site is unavailable" page, and notify the
Ops team.
D. Use Cloud Error Reporting to check the application URL. If the application is down, switch the URL to the
"Site is unavailable" page, and notify the Ops team.
Answer: C
Explanation:
Here's a breakdown of why option C is the best solution for TerramEarth's monitoring needs:
Option C leverages Google Cloud's built-in monitoring and event-driven architecture, providing a highly
reliable and cost-effective approach. Cloud Monitoring's uptime checks are specifically designed for this
purpose – continuously validating the availability of a web endpoint. Upon detection of failure, Cloud
Monitoring can publish a message to a Pub/Sub queue, creating a decoupling point between the monitoring
service and the reaction logic. This architecture aligns with best practices for scalable and resilient systems.
A Cloud Function, triggered by the Pub/Sub message, then performs the necessary actions: switching the
DNS to point to the "Site is unavailable" page and notifying the Ops team. The use of serverless technologies,

such as Pub/Sub and Cloud Functions, ensures minimum cost as you only pay for what you use, without the
overhead of managing virtual machines or containers. Cloud Error Reporting (Option D) is designed to capture
and report application errors and isn't meant for availability checks, rendering it unsuitable for this scenario.
Options A and B require creating and maintaining custom code running on managed or unmanaged compute
resources, which increases cost and administrative overhead. Additionally, they may be less reliable than the
managed monitoring provided by Cloud Monitoring. Option C is the most efficient method because it uses
managed services and avoids deploying custom code for the monitoring process itself.
Authoritative links for further research:
Cloud Monitoring Uptime Checks: https://cloud.google.com/monitoring/uptime-checks
Cloud Pub/Sub: https://cloud.google.com/pubsub
Cloud Functions: https://cloud.google.com/functions
Cloud Error Reporting: https://cloud.google.com/error-reporting
Question: 332 CertyIQ
For this question, refer to the TerramEarth case study. You are building a microservice-based application for
TerramEarth. The application is based on Docker containers. You want to follow Google-recommended practices to
build the application continuously and store the build artifacts. What should you do?
A. Configure a trigger in Cloud Build for new source changes. Invoke Cloud Build to build container images for
each microservice, and tag them using the code commit hash. Push the images to the Container Registry.
B. Configure a trigger in Cloud Build for new source changes. The trigger invokes build jobs and build container
images for the microservices. Tag the images with a version number, and push them to Cloud Storage.
C. Create a Scheduler job to check the repo every minute. For any new change, invoke Cloud Build to build
container images for the microservices. Tag the images using the current timestamp, and push them to the
Container Registry.
D. Configure a trigger in Cloud Build for new source changes. Invoke Cloud Build to build one container image,
and tag the image with the label 'latest.' Push the image to the Container Registry.
Answer: A
Explanation:
The correct answer is A. Here's a detailed justification:
Option A aligns with Google's recommended practices for continuous integration and continuous delivery
(CI/CD) using Cloud Build and Container Registry. Cloud Build triggers enable automated builds whenever
code changes are pushed to a source repository, facilitating a continuous integration workflow. Building
separate container images for each microservice promotes modularity and independent deployment. Tagging
these images with the code commit hash provides a reliable and traceable association between the deployed
artifact and the source code, crucial for debugging and version control. Finally, pushing these images to
Container Registry, a managed Docker image registry, ensures reliable storage and access for deployments.
This entire process is a common practice in modern cloud-native application development.
Option B is incorrect because using a simple version number for tags may not be as precise as a commit hash.
It doesn't provide a direct link to the specific code revision used to build the image, potentially causing issues
in tracing back changes. Pushing images to Cloud Storage is also not the best practice as it is not designed to
be a container registry.
Option C is less efficient. Relying on Scheduler to check the repository every minute is wasteful as it
consumes resources irrespective of changes, unlike event-driven triggers. Using timestamps as tags can
result in ambiguity if multiple builds occur in close succession.
Option D is not recommended because using the 'latest' tag is considered an anti-pattern in container image

management. The 'latest' tag is mutable and can point to different images, making it difficult to track
deployed versions and potentially leading to inconsistent deployments.
Here are some authoritative links to further research:
1. Google Cloud Build Documentation: https://cloud.google.com/build/docs
2. Google Cloud Container Registry Documentation: https://cloud.google.com/container-registry/docs
3. Best Practices for Containerizing Applications: https://cloud.google.com/solutions/best-practices-
for-building-containers
4. Cloud Build Triggers Documentation: https://cloud.google.com/build/docs/automating-
builds/create-manage-triggers
In summary, Option A provides a more efficient, traceable, and best-practice-aligned approach to build and
store container images for a microservice-based application. The automatic triggering, individual images for
microservices, tagging with commit hashes, and using Container Registry all contribute to a better
development lifecycle.
Question: 333 CertyIQ
For this question, refer to the TerramEarth case study. TerramEarth has about 1 petabyte (PB) of vehicle testing
data in a private data center. You want to move the data to Cloud Storage for your machine learning team.
Currently, a 1-Gbps interconnect link is available for you. The machine learning team wants to start using the data
in a month. What should you do?
A. Request Transfer Appliances from Google Cloud, export the data to appliances, and return the appliances to
Google Cloud.
B. Configure the Storage Transfer service from Google Cloud to send the data from your data center to Cloud
Storage.
C. Make sure there are no other users consuming the 1Gbps link, and use multi-thread transfer to upload the
data to Cloud Storage.
D. Export files to an encrypted USB device, send the device to Google Cloud, and request an import of the data
to Cloud Storage.
Answer: A
Explanation:
The most appropriate solution is A, utilizing Google Cloud Transfer Appliance. Option B, using Storage
Transfer Service over a 1 Gbps link, is infeasible due to the volume of data and the time constraint.
Transferring 1 PB (1000 TB) over a 1 Gbps link would take an impractically long time, far exceeding the one-
month deadline. Even with maximized throughput and no other users on the link (Option C), the transfer would
still take several weeks. Option D, shipping data via USB drives, introduces security concerns and logistical
challenges and isn't a supported method for large-scale transfers to Google Cloud. Transfer Appliances, on
the other hand, are specifically designed for migrating large datasets. They allow for offline data transfer by
shipping physical storage appliances to the data center, loading data, and returning them to Google for import
into Cloud Storage. This method bypasses the bandwidth constraints of the 1 Gbps link and enables the
machine learning team to access their data within the given timeframe. Transfer Appliances offer a secure
and efficient solution for large-scale data migration, making Option A the best choice for TerramEarth. They
are also cost-effective when considering the alternatives.
Authoritative Links:
Google Cloud Transfer Appliance: https://cloud.google.com/transfer-appliance
Storage Transfer Service: https://cloud.google.com/storage-transfer-service

Question: 334 CertyIQ
Introductory InfoCompany Overview -
Dress4Win is a web-based company that helps their users organize and manage their personal wardrobe using a
website and mobile application. The company also cultivates an active social network that connects their users
with designers and retailers. They monetize their services through advertising, e-commerce, referrals, and a
premium app model.
Company Background -
Dress4Win's application has grown from a few servers in the founder's garage to several hundred servers and
appliances in a collocated data center. However, the capacity of their infrastructure is now insufficient for the
application's rapid growth. Because of this growth and the company's desire to innovate faster,
Dress4Win is committing to a full migration to a public cloud.
Solution Concept -
For the first phase of their migration to the cloud, Dress4Win is considering moving their development and test
environments. They are also considering building a disaster recovery site, because their current infrastructure is at
a single location. They are not sure which components of their architecture they can migrate as is and which
components they need to change before migrating them.
Existing Technical Environment -
The Dress4Win application is served out of a single data center location.
Databases:
- MySQL - user data, inventory, static data
- Redis - metadata, social graph, caching
Application servers:
- Tomcat - Java micro-services
- Nginx - static content
- Apache Beam - Batch processing
Storage appliances:
- iSCSI for VM hosts
- Fiber channel SAN - MySQL databases
- NAS - image storage, logs, backups
Apache Hadoop/Spark servers:
- Data analysis
- Real-time trending calculations
MQ servers:
- Messaging
- Social notifications
- Events
Miscellaneous servers:
- Jenkins, monitoring, bastion hosts, security scanners
Business Requirements -
Build a reliable and reproducible environment with scaled parity of production.
Improve security by defining and adhering to a set of security and Identity and Access Management (IAM) best
practices for cloud.
Improve business agility and speed of innovation through rapid provisioning of new resources.
Analyze and optimize architecture for performance in the cloud.
Migrate fully to the cloud if all other requirements are met.
Technical Requirements -
Evaluate and choose an automation framework for provisioning resources in cloud.
Support failover of the production environment to cloud during an emergency.
Identify production services that can migrate to cloud to save capacity.
Use managed services whenever possible.
Encrypt data on the wire and at rest.
Support multiple VPN connections between the production data center and cloud environment.
CEO Statement -
Our investors are concerned about our ability to scale and contain costs with our current infrastructure. They are
also concerned that a new competitor could use a public cloud platform to offset their up-front investment and
freeing them to focus on developing better features.

CTO Statement -
We have invested heavily in the current infrastructure, but much of the equipment is approaching the end of its
useful life. We are consistently waiting weeks for new gear to be racked before we can start new projects. Our
traffic patterns are highest in the mornings and weekend evenings; during other times, 80% of our capacity is
sitting idle.
CFO Statement -
Our capital expenditure is now exceeding our quarterly projections. Migrating to the cloud will likely cause an
initial increase in spending, but we expect to fully transition before our next hardware refresh cycle. Our total cost
of ownership (TCO) analysis over the next 5 years puts a cloud strategy between 30 to 50% lower than our current
model.QuestionThe Dress4Win security team has disabled external SSH access into production virtual machines
(VMs) on Google Cloud Platform (GCP).
The operations team needs to remotely manage the VMs, build and push Docker containers, and manage Google
Cloud Storage objects.
What can they do?
A.Grant the operations engineer access to use Google Cloud Shell.
B.Configure a VPN connection to GCP to allow SSH access to the cloud VMs.
C.Develop a new access request process that grants temporary SSH access to cloud VMs when an operations
engineer needs to perform a task.
D.Have the development team build an API service that allows the operations team to execute specific remote
procedure calls to accomplish their tasks.
Answer: A
Explanation:
Grant the operations engineer access to use Google Cloud Shell.
