copies of database backup files.
The database files are compressed tar files stored in their current data center.
How should he proceed?
A.Create a cron script using gsutil to copy the files to a Coldline Storage bucket.
B.Create a cron script using gsutil to copy the files to a Regional Storage bucket.
C.Create a Cloud Storage Transfer Service Job to copy the files to a Coldline Storage bucket.
D.Create a Cloud Storage Transfer Service job to copy the files to a Regional Storage bucket.
Answer: C
Explanation:
C https://cloud.google.com/storage-transfer/docs/on-prem-overviewEspecially, when Google docs explicitly
states, that custom scripts are unreliable, slow, insecure, difficult to maintain and troubleshoot.
Question: 336 CertyIQ

Dress4Win has asked you to recommend machine types they should deploy their application servers to.
How should you proceed?
A.Perform a mapping of the on-premises physical hardware cores and RAM to the nearest machine types in the
cloud.
B.Recommend that Dress4Win deploy application servers to machine types that offer the highest RAM to CPU
ratio available.
C.Recommend that Dress4Win deploy into production with the smallest instances available, monitor them over
time, and scale the machine type up until the desired performance is reached.
D.Identify the number of virtual cores and RAM associated with the application server virtual machines align
them to a custom machine type in the cloud, monitor performance, and scale the machine types up until the
desired performance is reached.
Answer: D
Explanation:
The correct approach, option D, emphasizes a performance-driven, iterative strategy. Initially, the focus is on
understanding the application server's resource requirements by examining its current virtual machine's
virtual CPU cores and RAM allocation. This information guides the selection of a custom machine type in
Google Cloud, allowing for precise resource provisioning. Custom machine types are beneficial because they
provide a high degree of customization unlike fixed predefined machine type, offering granular control over
resources and associated costs. Furthermore, deploying initially with a custom machine type that reflects
existing resource allocation allows the application to function predictably in the cloud, mirroring on-premises
behavior. Monitoring performance after deployment is crucial. This real-world data enables informed
decisions about scaling the machine types up by increasing virtual cores or memory until the application
meets desired performance levels. This iterative approach minimizes resource waste and associated costs.
Options A and B are less ideal. Option A’s “nearest machine type” can cause resource allocation mismatches
with no room for adjustments. Option B’s recommendation of maximizing the RAM:CPU ratio may lead to
overspending. Option C’s advice to start with the smallest instance might cause initial performance issues.
Thus, Option D promotes the most cost-effective and performance-optimized path.
Supporting Links:
Google Cloud Custom Machine Types: https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/instances/customize-
machine-type
Google Cloud Monitoring: https://cloud.google.com/monitoring
Right Sizing Instances: https://cloud.google.com/architecture/right-sizing-your-compute-engine-instances
Question: 337 CertyIQ
As part of Dress4Win's plans to migrate to the cloud, they want to be able to set up a managed logging and
monitoring system so they can handle spikes in their traffic load.
They want to ensure that:
* The infrastructure can be notified when it needs to scale up and down to handle the ebb and flow of usage
throughout the day
* Their administrators are notified automatically when their application reports errors.
* They can filter their aggregated logs down in order to debug one piece of the application across many hosts
Which Google StackDriver features should they use?
A.Logging, Alerts, Insights, Debug
B.Monitoring, Trace, Debug, Logging
C.Monitoring, Logging, Alerts, Error Reporting
D.Monitoring, Logging, Debug, Error Report

Answer: C
Explanation:
The correct answer is C: Monitoring, Logging, Alerts, Error Reporting. Dress4Win needs a comprehensive
solution encompassing observation, analysis, and response to application behavior and infrastructure status.
Google Cloud Monitoring allows them to track resource utilization and application performance, crucial for
scaling infrastructure based on traffic fluctuations. This addresses the need to "notify the infrastructure when
it needs to scale up and down". Stackdriver Logging aggregates logs from various sources, enabling filtering
for debugging application issues across multiple hosts. This directly addresses their requirement to filter
"aggregated logs to debug one piece of the application across many hosts." Cloud Monitoring can also trigger
alerts based on specified metrics, ensuring administrators are promptly notified about application errors. This
is satisfied by the use of Alerts in Stackdriver. Finally, Stackdriver Error Reporting provides a dedicated view
of application errors, allowing for efficient issue tracking and resolution, fulfilling the requirement that
"administrators are notified automatically when their application reports errors." Option A includes Insights
and Debug, while Debug is useful, it doesn't directly address the main concerns of scaling and monitoring;
Insights is an unspecific term in this context, unlike Error Reporting and Alerts. Options B and D also include
Debug, and Trace (in B). While Debug is useful, it's not as core to solving the specific needs of this question;
Trace (in B) is also not central, and is intended for performance analysis of requests, not error reporting and
alerts. Therefore, C provides the most complete and directly applicable set of services.
Supporting links:
Google Cloud Monitoring: https://cloud.google.com/monitoring
Google Cloud Logging: https://cloud.google.com/logging
Google Cloud Alerting: https://cloud.google.com/monitoring/alerts
Google Cloud Error Reporting: https://cloud.google.com/error-reporting
Question: 338 CertyIQ
Dress4Win would like to become familiar with deploying applications to the cloud by successfully deploying some
applications quickly, as is. They have asked for your recommendation.
What should you advise?
A.Identify self-contained applications with external dependencies as a first move to the cloud.
B.Identify enterprise applications with internal dependencies and recommend these as a first move to the
cloud.
C.Suggest moving their in-house databases to the cloud and continue serving requests to on-premise
applications.
D.Recommend moving their message queuing servers to the cloud and continue handling requests to on-
premise applications.
Answer: A
Explanation:
The most suitable approach for Dress4Win, given their goal of quickly and successfully deploying applications
to the cloud, is option A: Identify self-contained applications with external dependencies. This strategy allows
for a smoother initial cloud migration. Self-contained applications, by definition, have minimal
interdependencies with other parts of their existing infrastructure, reducing complexity and the potential for
cascading failures during the move. External dependencies, such as a connection to a third-party API, are
already often handled across networks and can often be addressed easily within a cloud context.
Migrating these applications first provides Dress4Win with valuable experience and knowledge of the cloud
environment without overwhelming them with the complexities of tightly coupled enterprise systems. Option

B, focusing on enterprise applications with internal dependencies, would involve a far more complex initial
migration, potentially leading to significant challenges and delays. Options C and D, while relevant cloud
migration strategies, do not directly address Dress4Win's goal of deploying applications quickly and
successfully. Moving databases or message queues without moving associated applications would only add
complexity and require more effort to integrate, not speed up deployment.
By prioritizing self-contained applications, Dress4Win can gain quick wins, build confidence, and develop
valuable cloud skills before tackling more complex migrations. This approach aligns with a phased migration
strategy, a best practice recommended by cloud providers. It also minimizes risks and facilitates easier
testing and validation in the new environment.
For further research, consider exploring these authoritative resources:
Google Cloud Migration Documentation: https://cloud.google.com/migrate/ - Provides information on various
migration strategies, including phased approaches.
Cloud Adoption Frameworks: Search for examples, such as those offered by Google Cloud or other cloud
providers. These frameworks often recommend starting with less complex workloads.
Gartner's Research on Cloud Migration: https://www.gartner.com/en/information-technology/topics/cloud-
computing - (Requires subscription, but provides valuable insights).
Question: 339 CertyIQ
Dress4Win has asked you for advice on how to migrate their on-premises MySQL deployment to the cloud.
They want to minimize downtime and performance impact to their on-premises solution during the migration.
Which approach should you recommend?
A.Create a dump of the on-premises MySQL master server, and then shut it down, upload it to the cloud
environment, and load into a new MySQL cluster.
B.Setup a MySQL replica server/slave in the cloud environment, and configure it for asynchronous replication
from the MySQL master server on-premises until cutover.
C.Create a new MySQL cluster in the cloud, configure applications to begin writing to both on premises and
cloud MySQL masters, and destroy the original cluster at cutover.
D.Create a dump of the MySQL replica server into the cloud environment, load it into: Google Cloud Datastore,
and configure applications to read/write to Cloud Datastore at cutover.
Answer: B
Explanation:
Option B, setting up a MySQL replica in the cloud and configuring asynchronous replication, is the most
suitable approach for minimizing downtime and performance impact during migration. This method leverages
the inherent capabilities of MySQL's replication features, allowing for a near real-time copy of the on-
premises database to be established in the cloud without interrupting the existing production system.
Asynchronous replication ensures that the on-premises master continues to operate without latency issues
associated with synchronous replication. The cloud-based replica can be synchronized gradually, minimizing
strain on the source database. During cutover, applications can be pointed to the cloud replica with minimal
interruption. Options A and C involve periods of downtime or complex dual-write scenarios, which are less
desirable in this context. Option D proposes using Cloud Datastore, a NoSQL solution, which would require a
complete application rewrite and significant data transformation, making it unsuitable for a simple database
migration. This approach aligns with best practices for database migration, emphasizing a phased, low-impact
approach.
Authoritative links:
MySQL Replication: https://dev.mysql.com/doc/refman/8.0/en/replication.html (Official MySQL

documentation on replication)
Google Cloud Database Migration: https://cloud.google.com/database-migration/docs/ (Google Cloud's
documentation on database migration strategies and tools)
Question: 340 CertyIQ
Dress4Win has configured a new uptime check with Google Stackdriver for several of their legacy services. The
Stackdriver dashboard is not reporting the services as healthy.
What should they do?
A.Install the Stackdriver agent on all of the legacy web servers.
B.In the Cloud Platform Console download the list of the uptime servers' IP addresses and create an inbound
firewall rule
C.Configure their load balancer to pass through the User-Agent HTTP header when the value matches
GoogleStackdriverMonitoring-UptimeChecks (https:// cloud.google.com/monitoring)
D.Configure their legacy web servers to allow requests that contain user-Agent HTTP header when the value
matches GoogleStackdriverMonitoring- UptimeChecks (https://cloud.google.com/monitoring)
Answer: D
Explanation:
The correct answer is D. Configure their legacy web servers to allow requests that contain user-Agent HTTP
header when the value matches GoogleStackdriverMonitoring-UptimeChecks. Here's why:
Uptime checks in Google Cloud Stackdriver (now Cloud Monitoring) function by periodically sending
HTTP/HTTPS requests to designated endpoints. These requests have a specific User-Agent header:
GoogleStackdriverMonitoring-UptimeChecks. Legacy systems, particularly those not designed with modern
monitoring in mind, might block or reject requests lacking a typical user-agent.
Option A is incorrect because the Stackdriver agent primarily collects metrics and logs from resources, not
for simple uptime checking using HTTP(S). Option B is incorrect since uptime checks are from Google-owned
IP ranges, not necessarily consistent. Firewall rules restricting access would prevent the uptime check from
reaching the legacy servers, not solve the problem. Option C is incorrect as the load balancer does not directly
process the Uptime Check. It’s the backend servers behind the load balancer that need to allow traffic from
the Uptime checks.
Therefore, the servers need to be explicitly configured to recognize and accept requests from
GoogleStackdriverMonitoring-UptimeChecks, which is most likely being blocked by a webserver or application
firewall. This ensures the Uptime Check can successfully reach the endpoint, verify its availability, and report
the service as healthy in the Stackdriver dashboard.
Relevant Links:
Cloud Monitoring Uptime Checks: https://cloud.google.com/monitoring/uptime-checks
User-Agent Header: https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Headers/User-Agent
Question: 341 CertyIQ
Introductory InfoCompany Overview -
Dress4Win is a web-based company that helps their users organize and manage their personal wardrobe using a
website and mobile application. The company also cultivates an active social network that connects their users
with designers and retailers. They monetize their services through advertising, e-commerce, referrals, and a
premium app model.

Company Background -
Dress4Win's application has grown from a few servers in the founder's garage to several hundred servers and
appliances in a collocated data center. However, the capacity of their infrastructure is now insufficient for the
application's rapid growth. Because of this growth and the company's desire to innovate faster,
Dress4Win is committing to a full migration to a public cloud.
Solution Concept -
For the first phase of their migration to the cloud, Dress4Win is considering moving their development and test
environments. They are also considering building a disaster recovery site, because their current infrastructure is at
a single location. They are not sure which components of their architecture they can migrate as is and which
components they need to change before migrating them.
Existing Technical Environment -
The Dress4Win application is served out of a single data center location.
Databases:
- MySQL - user data, inventory, static data
- Redis - metadata, social graph, caching
Application servers:
- Tomcat - Java micro-services
- Nginx - static content
- Apache Beam - Batch processing
Storage appliances:
- iSCSI for VM hosts
- Fiber channel SAN - MySQL databases
- NAS - image storage, logs, backups
Apache Hadoop/Spark servers:
- Data analysis
- Real-time trending calculations
MQ servers:
- Messaging
- Social notifications
- Events
Miscellaneous servers:
- Jenkins, monitoring, bastion hosts, security scanners
Business Requirements -
Build a reliable and reproducible environment with scaled parity of production.
Improve security by defining and adhering to a set of security and Identity and Access Management (IAM) best
practices for cloud.
Improve business agility and speed of innovation through rapid provisioning of new resources.
Analyze and optimize architecture for performance in the cloud.
Migrate fully to the cloud if all other requirements are met.
Technical Requirements -
Evaluate and choose an automation framework for provisioning resources in cloud.
Support failover of the production environment to cloud during an emergency.
Identify production services that can migrate to cloud to save capacity.
Use managed services whenever possible.
Encrypt data on the wire and at rest.
Support multiple VPN connections between the production data center and cloud environment.
CEO Statement -
Our investors are concerned about our ability to scale and contain costs with our current infrastructure. They are
also concerned that a new competitor could use a public cloud platform to offset their up-front investment and
freeing them to focus on developing better features.
CTO Statement -
We have invested heavily in the current infrastructure, but much of the equipment is approaching the end of its
useful life. We are consistently waiting weeks for new gear to be racked before we can start new projects. Our
traffic patterns are highest in the mornings and weekend evenings; during other times, 80% of our capacity is
sitting idle.
CFO Statement -
Our capital expenditure is now exceeding our quarterly projections. Migrating to the cloud will likely cause an
initial increase in spending, but we expect to fully transition before our next hardware refresh cycle. Our total cost
of ownership (TCO) analysis over the next 5 years puts a cloud strategy between 30 to 50% lower than our current

model.QuestionAs part of their new application experience, Dress4Wm allows customers to upload images of
themselves.
The customer has exclusive control over who may view these images.
Customers should be able to upload images with minimal latency and also be shown their images quickly on the
main application page when they log in.
Which configuration should Dress4Win use?
A.Store image files in a Google Cloud Storage bucket. Use Google Cloud Datastore to maintain metadata that
maps each customer's ID and their image files.
B.Store image files in a Google Cloud Storage bucket. Add custom metadata to the uploaded images in Cloud
Storage that contains the customer's unique ID.
C.Use a distributed file system to store customers' images. As storage needs increase, add more persistent
disks and/or nodes. Assign each customer a unique ID, which sets each file's owner attribute, ensuring privacy
of images.
D.Use a distributed file system to store customers' images. As storage needs increase, add more persistent
disks and/or nodes. Use a Google Cloud SQL database to maintain metadata that maps each customer's ID to
their image files.
Answer: A
Explanation:
A is correct. The whole idea is simply build and maintain an external metadata service using NoSQL database
to associate the GS object key with its metadata, in order to facilitate object findings based on attributes you
pre defined in metatdataThis AWS blog provides a solution in the context of AWS S3, but the idea behind is
applicable to Google Storage as well
https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/big-data/building-and-maintaining-an-amazon-s3-metadata-index-without-
servers/

Which additional testing methods should the developers employ to prevent an outage?
A.They should enable Google Stackdriver Debugger on the application code to show errors in the code.
B.They should add additional unit tests and production scale load tests on their cloud staging environment.
C.They should run the end-to-end tests in the cloud staging environment to determine if the code is working as

latency.

Our capital expenditure is now exceeding our quarterly projections. Migrating to the cloud will likely cause an

located in one data center.
As of a future evaluation and optimizing for performance in the cloud, Dresss4Win wants to distribute its system
architecture to multiple locations when Google cloud platform.
Which approach should they use?
A.Use regional managed instance groups and a global load balancer to increase performance because the
regional managed instance group can grow instances in each region separately based on traffic.
B.Use a global load balancer with a set of virtual machines that forward the requests to a closer group of virtual
machines managed by your operations team.
C.Use regional managed instance groups and a global load balancer to increase reliability by providing
automatic failover between zones in different regions.
D.Use a global load balancer with a set of virtual machines that forward the requests to a closer group of virtual
machines as part of a separate managed instance groups.
Answer: A
Explanation:
Use regional managed instance groups and a global load balancer to increase performance because the

regional managed instance group can grow instances in each region separately based on traffic.
Question: 345 CertyIQ
Introductory InfoCompany Overview -
Dress4Win is a web-based company that helps their users organize and manage their personal wardrobe using a
web app and mobile application. The company also cultivates an active social network that connects their users
with designers and retailers. They monetize their services through advertising, e-commerce, referrals, and a
freemium app model. The application has grown from a few servers in the founder's garage to several hundred
servers and appliances in a colocated data center. However, the capacity of their infrastructure is now insufficient
for the application's rapid growth. Because of this growth and the company's desire to innovate faster, Dress4Win
is committing to a full migration to a public cloud.
Solution Concept -
For the first phase of their migration to the cloud, Dress4Win is moving their development and test environments.
They are also building a disaster recovery site, because their current infrastructure is at a single location. They are
not sure which components of their architecture they can migrate as is and which components they need to
change before migrating them.
Existing Technical Environment -
The Dress4Win application is served out of a single data center location. All servers run Ubuntu LTS v16.04.
Databases:
MySQL. 1 server for user data, inventory, static data:
- MySQL 5.8
- 8 core CPUs
- 128 GB of RAM
- 2x 5 TB HDD (RAID 1)
Redis 3 server cluster for metadata, social graph, caching. Each server is:
- Redis 3.2
- 4 core CPUs
- 32GB of RAM
Compute:
40 Web Application servers providing micro-services based APIs and static content.
`"
- Tomcat
Java -
- Nginx
- 4 core CPUs
- 32 GB of RAM
20 Apache Hadoop/Spark servers:
- Data analysis
- Real-time trending calculations
- 8 core CPUs
- 128 GB of RAM
- 4x 5 TB HDD (RAID 1)
3 RabbitMQ servers for messaging, social notifications, and events:
- 8 core CPUs
- 32GB of RAM
Miscellaneous servers:
- Jenkins, monitoring, bastion hosts, security scanners
- 8 core CPUs
- 32GB of RAM
Storage appliances:
iSCSI for VM hosts
Fiber channel SAN `" MySQL databases
- 1 PB total storage; 400 TB available
NAS `" image storage, logs, backups
- 100 TB total storage; 35 TB available
Business Requirements -
Build a reliable and reproducible environment with scaled parity of production.
Improve security by defining and adhering to a set of security and Identity and Access Management (IAM) best

practices for cloud.
Improve business agility and speed of innovation through rapid provisioning of new resources.
Analyze and optimize architecture for performance in the cloud.
Technical Requirements -
Easily create non-production environments in the cloud.
Implement an automation framework for provisioning resources in cloud.
Implement a continuous deployment process for deploying applications to the on-premises datacenter or cloud.
Support failover of the production environment to cloud during an emergency.
Encrypt data on the wire and at rest.
Support multiple private connections between the production data center and cloud environment.
Executive Statement -
Our investors are concerned about our ability to scale and contain costs with our current infrastructure. They are
also concerned that a competitor could use a public cloud platform to offset their up-front investment and free
them to focus on developing better features. Our traffic patterns are highest in the mornings and weekend
evenings; during other times, 80% of our capacity is sitting idle.
Our capital expenditure is now exceeding our quarterly projections. Migrating to the cloud will likely cause an
initial increase in spending, but we expect to fully transition before our next hardware refresh cycle. Our total cost
of ownership (TCO) analysis over the next 5 years for a public cloud strategy achieves a cost reduction between
30% and 50% over our current model.QuestionFor this question, refer to the Dress4Win case study. Dress4Win is
expected to grow to 10 times its size in 1 year with a corresponding growth in data and traffic that mirrors the
existing patterns of usage. The CIO has set the target of migrating production infrastructure to the cloud within
the next 6 months. How will you configure the solution to scale for this growth without making major application
changes and still maximize the ROI?
A.Migrate the web application layer to App Engine, and MySQL to Cloud Datastore, and NAS to Cloud Storage.
Deploy RabbitMQ, and deploy Hadoop servers using Deployment Manager.
B.Migrate RabbitMQ to Cloud Pub/Sub, Hadoop to BigQuery, and NAS to Compute Engine with Persistent Disk
storage. Deploy Tomcat, and deploy Nginx using Deployment Manager.
C.Implement managed instance groups for Tomcat and Nginx. Migrate MySQL to Cloud SQL, RabbitMQ to
Cloud Pub/Sub, Hadoop to Cloud Dataproc, and NAS to Compute Engine with Persistent Disk storage.
D.Implement managed instance groups for the Tomcat and Nginx. Migrate MySQL to Cloud SQL, RabbitMQ to
Cloud Pub/Sub, Hadoop to Cloud Dataproc, and NAS to Cloud Storage.
Answer: D
Explanation:
Okay, let's break down why option D is the best approach for Dress4Win's scaling needs, focusing on
minimizing application changes and maximizing ROI.
Justification for Option D:
Option D proposes leveraging managed services wherever possible, which aligns perfectly with the goal of
scaling without major application rewrites and optimizing costs. Here's a detailed breakdown:
1. Managed Instance Groups for Tomcat and Nginx: Using managed instance groups (MIGs) for the
web application layer (Tomcat and Nginx) allows for automatic scaling based on traffic demands. This
ensures that Dress4Win can handle the expected 10x growth in traffic without manual intervention.
MIGs also handle health checks and instance recovery, improving reliability. [Link:
https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/instance-groups]
2. MySQL to Cloud SQL: Migrating to Cloud SQL is a logical move as it provides a fully managed
relational database service compatible with MySQL. Dress4Win can avoid the overhead of managing
database infrastructure, scaling is simplified, and they retain their familiarity with the database
technology. [Link: https://cloud.google.com/sql]
3. RabbitMQ to Cloud Pub/Sub: Replacing RabbitMQ with Cloud Pub/Sub provides a highly scalable
and reliable message queuing service. Cloud Pub/Sub is fully managed, requires no infrastructure
maintenance, and decouples producers and consumers which aids in creating a resilient system. This
is crucial for handling the expected increased messaging load. [Link:

https://cloud.google.com/pubsub]
4. Hadoop to Cloud Dataproc: Cloud Dataproc is Google Cloud's fully managed Hadoop and Spark
service. It simplifies the deployment and management of these analytics engines. Dress4Win can
leverage Dataproc's autoscaling capabilities to handle increased data processing needs. This also
means they don't have to worry about sizing and maintaining the Hadoop infrastructure. [Link:
https://cloud.google.com/dataproc]
5. NAS to Cloud Storage: Migrating the NAS to Cloud Storage provides a highly scalable, cost-
effective, and durable storage solution for images, logs, and backups. Cloud Storage offers object
storage, which is ideal for these use cases, and provides data lifecycle management to optimize
costs. It also eliminates the need for Dress4Win to maintain their physical NAS devices. [Link:
https://cloud.google.com/storage]
Why other options are not as ideal:
Option A: Migrating to App Engine is a significant architecture change that would require substantial code
refactoring. Cloud Datastore is a NoSQL solution, which is not a direct replacement for their MySQL database.
Option B: Migrating NAS to Compute Engine with Persistent Disk would mean managing storage
infrastructure instead of using a more suitable managed service like Cloud Storage. It also misses the
opportunity to fully leverage managed services for web tier like MIGs
Option C: This option is very similar to D, but misses the better service for the NAS storage which is object
storage, cloud storage.
In Summary:
Option D allows Dress4Win to scale their application by leveraging managed services that provide automatic
scaling, high availability, and reduce operational overhead. By minimizing the changes to the existing
architecture and adopting managed services, Dress4Win can rapidly scale to meet their projected growth,
accelerate their migration timeline, and maximize their return on investment by reducing the resources
needed for operations and infrastructure maintenance. The choices in option D are the best fit for Dress4Win's
requirements of minimal changes, cost optimization, and scalability.
Question: 346 CertyIQ
For this question, refer to the Dress4Win case study. Considering the given business requirements, how would you
automate the deployment of web and transactional data layers?
A.Deploy Nginx and Tomcat using Cloud Deployment Manager to Compute Engine. Deploy a Cloud SQL server
to replace MySQL. Deploy Jenkins using Cloud Deployment Manager.
B.Deploy Nginx and Tomcat using Cloud Launcher. Deploy a MySQL server using Cloud Launcher. Deploy
Jenkins to Compute Engine using Cloud Deployment Manager scripts.
C.Migrate Nginx and Tomcat to App Engine. Deploy a Cloud Datastore server to replace the MySQL server in a
high-availability configuration. Deploy Jenkins to Compute Engine using Cloud Launcher.
D.Migrate Nginx and Tomcat to App Engine. Deploy a MySQL server using Cloud Launcher. Deploy Jenkins to
Compute Engine using Cloud Launcher.
Answer: A
Explanation:
Okay, let's break down why option A is the best choice for automating the deployment of Dress4Win's web
and transactional data layers, based on the case study's likely business needs.
Option A proposes using Cloud Deployment Manager, a powerful Infrastructure-as-Code (IaC) tool, to deploy
Nginx (web server) and Tomcat (application server) to Compute Engine. This allows for repeatable, consistent

infrastructure setups. The use of Compute Engine provides fine-grained control over resources, which is
essential for performance tuning and security. This aligns with the need for a reliable and scalable platform to
handle expected growth.
Replacing the existing MySQL database with Cloud SQL provides a managed database service, reducing
administrative overhead. Cloud SQL offers features like automated backups, patching, and high availability,
which are important for business continuity. This move reduces operational complexity and allows the
Dress4Win team to focus on application development rather than database management.
Finally, deploying Jenkins using Cloud Deployment Manager ensures that the CI/CD pipeline can also be
automated and managed through code. This allows for a consistent approach across all layers of the
deployment process, promoting best practices.
Options B, C, and D have drawbacks. Cloud Launcher is not ideal for complex environments due to limited
customization options, making it unsuitable for enterprise-level needs. Migrating to App Engine (options C and
D) is not specified in the case study and likely unnecessary complexity at this stage, it would require rewriting
parts of the application and might cause other incompatibilities. Datastore is NoSQL database and would
require a significant change to the application, therefore replacing the relational database with Cloud
Datastore is not feasible.
In summary, Option A provides the best balance of scalability, automation, and manageability, aligning with
the needs of a growing e-commerce business like Dress4Win. Using infrastructure as code with Cloud
Deployment Manager allows for a repeatable, consistent, and auditable infrastructure configuration.
Supporting Links:
Google Cloud Deployment Manager: https://cloud.google.com/deployment-manager/
Google Cloud Compute Engine: https://cloud.google.com/compute/
Google Cloud SQL: https://cloud.google.com/sql/
Jenkins on Google Cloud: https://cloud.google.com/solutions/jenkins
Question: 347 CertyIQ
For this question, refer to the Dress4Win case study. Which of the compute services should be migrated as-is and
would still be an optimized architecture for performance in the cloud?
A.Web applications deployed using App Engine standard environment
B.RabbitMQ deployed using an unmanaged instance group
C.Hadoop/Spark deployed using Cloud Dataproc Regional in High Availability mode
D.Jenkins, monitoring, bastion hosts, security scanners services deployed on custom machine types
Answer: C
Explanation:
The correct answer is C, Hadoop/Spark deployed using Cloud Dataproc Regional in High Availability mode.
Here's why:
Dress4Win heavily relies on batch processing with Hadoop/Spark for data analysis. Migrating this workload to
Cloud Dataproc, a managed service for Hadoop and Spark, is a natural fit. Dataproc provides a streamlined
and optimized environment for running such jobs in the cloud. Specifically, selecting the "Regional in High
Availability" mode ensures resilience and better performance by distributing processing across multiple
zones within a region. This approach maintains the core functionality of their existing setup while leveraging
the benefits of a managed cloud service.

Option A, App Engine standard environment, is not ideal for migrating existing complex web applications as-is
because it often requires architectural changes to conform to the platform's constraints. Option B,
unmanaged instance group for RabbitMQ, while possible, doesn't offer the same level of management,
scalability, and potentially cost-efficiency compared to a managed solution on Google Cloud. Finally, option D,
while suitable for various services, isn't optimal for cloud migration. Simply replicating custom machine types
directly in the cloud might not capitalize on platform-specific optimizations. Instead, leveraging managed
services where possible ensures a more efficient cloud architecture.
Therefore, Cloud Dataproc is the most suitable "as-is" migration for the existing Hadoop/Spark workload,
providing an optimized and resilient solution in Google Cloud.
Relevant Links for further research:
Google Cloud Dataproc: https://cloud.google.com/dataproc
Dataproc High Availability: https://cloud.google.com/dataproc/docs/concepts/high-availability
Google Cloud App Engine: https://cloud.google.com/appengine
Google Cloud Instance Groups: https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/instance-groups
Question: 348 CertyIQ
For this question, refer to the Dress4Win case study. To be legally compliant during an audit, Dress4Win must be
able to give insights in all administrative actions that modify the configuration or metadata of resources on Google
Cloud.
What should you do?
A.Use Stackdriver Trace to create a Trace list analysis.
B.Use Stackdriver Monitoring to create a dashboard on the project's activity.
C.Enable Cloud Identity-Aware Proxy in all projects, and add the group of Administrators as a member.
D.Use the Activity page in the GCP Console and Stackdriver Logging to provide the required insight.
Answer: D
Explanation:
Option D, using the Activity page in the GCP Console and Stackdriver Logging, is the correct solution. Auditing
administrative actions requires a detailed record of who did what, and when, within the Google Cloud
environment. The Activity page in the GCP Console provides a basic view of recent activity, which can be
helpful for quick checks. However, for comprehensive auditing, Stackdriver Logging (now known as Cloud
Logging) is essential. Cloud Logging captures audit logs, including Admin Activity logs which track
modifications to resource configurations and metadata. This allows Dress4Win to fulfill their legal compliance
requirements by providing a granular, searchable history of changes. Option A, Stackdriver Trace, is for
tracking latency and performance of requests and is not relevant for auditing administrative actions. Option B,
Stackdriver Monitoring, focuses on system performance and metrics, not user actions and configuration
changes. Option C, Cloud Identity-Aware Proxy (IAP), is an access control mechanism that verifies user
identity before granting access; although important for security, it does not provide the detailed audit logs
needed for compliance. Therefore, combining the Activity page with Cloud Logging is the most appropriate
approach for Dress4Win to gain the necessary insights into administrative actions for audit compliance.
For further research:
1. Cloud Logging: https://cloud.google.com/logging/docs
2. Admin Activity Logs: https://cloud.google.com/logging/docs/audit
3. GCP Activity Page: https://cloud.google.com/resource-manager/docs/viewing-activity

Question: 349 CertyIQ
For this question, refer to the Dress4Win case study. You are responsible for the security of data stored in Cloud
Storage for your company, Dress4Win. You have already created a set of Google Groups and assigned the
appropriate users to those groups. You should use Google best practices and implement the simplest design to
meet the requirements.
Considering Dress4Win's business and technical requirements, what should you do?
A.Assign custom IAM roles to the Google Groups you created in order to enforce security requirements.
Encrypt data with a customer-supplied encryption key when storing files in Cloud Storage.
B.Assign custom IAM roles to the Google Groups you created in order to enforce security requirements. Enable
default storage encryption before storing files in Cloud Storage.
C.Assign predefined IAM roles to the Google Groups you created in order to enforce security requirements.
Utilize Google's default encryption at rest when storing files in Cloud Storage.
D.Assign predefined IAM roles to the Google Groups you created in order to enforce security requirements.
Ensure that the default Cloud KMS key is set before storing files in Cloud Storage.
Answer: C
Explanation:
The correct answer is C because it aligns with Google Cloud's best practices for security and simplicity.
Here's why:
Predefined IAM Roles: Google recommends using predefined IAM roles whenever possible because they are
managed by Google, regularly updated, and follow the principle of least privilege. Custom roles should only
be created when predefined roles don't adequately meet the needs. This promotes ease of management and
reduces the risk of overly permissive permissions. Creating custom roles increases complexity and requires
ongoing maintenance.
Google's Default Encryption at Rest: Google Cloud Storage automatically encrypts all data at rest using
Google-managed encryption keys. This feature is enabled by default and provides a robust level of security
without requiring additional configuration or key management on the user's part. This is the simplest and most
efficient way to secure data at rest.
Let's analyze why the other options are not the best fit:
Option A: Using custom IAM roles increases management overhead and complexity. Furthermore, customer-
supplied encryption keys (CSEK) add complexity for key management. This option goes against the principle
of simplicity outlined in the question.
Option B: While using custom IAM roles is not a best practice, enabling default storage encryption is correct.
However, option C aligns better with simplicity.
Option D: Default Cloud KMS keys do not apply to default encryption at rest for Cloud Storage. Default Cloud
Storage encryption does not rely on Cloud KMS but rather utilizes Google's encryption infrastructure.
Although Cloud KMS can be used for customer-managed encryption, it adds more complexity than default
encryption, and the question states it wants the simplest design.
In conclusion, C offers a balance of security and simplicity by using predefined IAM roles and leveraging
Google's default encryption at rest, aligning perfectly with the stated requirements.
Authoritative Links:
IAM Roles: https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/understanding-roles
Cloud Storage Encryption: https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/encryption
IAM Best Practices: https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/best-practices-for-using-iam

Question: 350 CertyIQ
For this question, refer to the Dress4Win case study. You want to ensure that your on-premises architecture meets
business requirements before you migrate your solution.
What change in the on-premises architecture should you make?
A.Replace RabbitMQ with Google Pub/Sub.
B.Downgrade MySQL to v5.7, which is supported by Cloud SQL for MySQL.
C.Resize compute resources to match predefined Compute Engine machine types.
D.Containerize the micro-services and host them in Google Kubernetes Engine.
Answer: D
Explanation:
The correct answer is D. Containerize the micro-services and host them in Google Kubernetes Engine.
Here's why:
The Dress4Win case study highlights a microservices architecture using RabbitMQ for messaging and MySQL
databases. While these components might function on-premises, they're not directly portable or optimized for
a cloud migration. Options A and B address the technology used but not the architecture itself, making them
less critical for the initial migration. Option C focuses on resource sizing, which is important but comes after
architectural adjustments. The priority for a successful lift-and-shift (a migration of the existing architecture
as is) is preparing for a portable model.
Containerizing microservices and deploying them on Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) addresses this.
Containers provide a consistent runtime environment, isolating application dependencies, and GKE offers
orchestration, scaling, and management capabilities ideal for microservice deployments in Google Cloud. This
approach allows Dress4Win to take advantage of GKE's elasticity and automated scaling, which aligns with
their stated goals of improving agility and scalability on the cloud. Before an actual cloud migration,
containerizing allows an easy and consistent move for the existing microservice architecture onto GKE. It is
key for creating portability and flexibility on their transition to Google Cloud.
Key Cloud Computing Concepts:
1. Containerization: Packaging applications and their dependencies into standardized units for
portability and consistency across environments.
2. Microservices: An architectural style that structures an application as a collection of loosely coupled
services.
3. Kubernetes (GKE): A container orchestration platform for automating deployment, scaling, and
management of containerized applications.
4. Lift and Shift Migration: Moving an existing application to the cloud with minimal changes.
Authoritative Links:
1. Containers: https://www.docker.com/resources/what-container/
2. Microservices: https://martinfowler.com/articles/microservices.html
3. Google Kubernetes Engine: https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine
4. Lift and Shift Migration: https://cloud.google.com/blog/products/gcp/lift-and-shift-to-google-cloud-
a-practical-guide-for-large-enterprises
Question: 351 CertyIQ
For this question, refer to the EHR Healthcare case study. Due to the growing partnerships with new insurance
providers, EHR Healthcare needs a solution to streamline the creation of secure Google Cloud environments for

each provider. You need to automate the provisioning and management of these secure environments, ensure that
each provider's environment is consistently configured and managed, and provide a consistent user experience
across all providers. What should you do?
A. Provision and configure each new environment and resources through the Google Cloud console.
B. Utilize Infrastructure as Code (IaC) tools, such as Terraform, to define and manage infrastructure
configurations.
C. Leverage Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) for workload deployments. Use cluster autoscaling and node
auto-provisioning features to scale the cluster based on resource usage.
D. Develop custom Cloud Run functions to automate the provisioning of new Google Cloud environments.
Incorporate security measures and pre-defined configurations within the functions to ensure consistency and
protection.
Answer: B
Explanation:
The correct answer is B. Utilize Infrastructure as Code (IaC) tools, such as Terraform, to define and manage
infrastructure configurations.
B. Utilize Infrastructure as Code (IaC) tools, such as Terraform.
Why it’s correct:
Terraform allows you to define your entire infrastructure (VPCs, IAM roles, subnets, etc.) in configuration files.
Automation: You can deploy an entire provider environment with a single command.
Consistency: Using the same code/modules ensures that Provider A's environment is identical to Provider B's.
Management: It provides a "version-controlled" history of your infrastructure, making it easy to manage
updates across all environments simultaneously.
Why other options are wrong:
A. Provision and configure each new environment through the Google Cloud console.
Why it’s incorrect: This is a manual process. It is prone to human error, does not scale efficiently as the
number of insurance providers grows, and makes it nearly impossible to ensure that every environment is
configured identically. It fails the requirement for "automation" and "consistency."
C. Leverage Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) for workload deployments.
Why it’s incorrect: While GKE is great for managing containers, this question asks about the provisioning and
management of the entire Google Cloud environment (which includes networking, security policies, and
projects). GKE helps scale applications, but it doesn't automate the creation of the underlying cloud
infrastructure for new providers.
D. Develop custom Cloud Run functions to automate provisioning.
Why it’s incorrect: While you could write custom code to call Google Cloud APIs, this is "reinventing the
wheel." It creates a high maintenance burden. Terraform is a purpose-built industry standard for this exact
task, offering built-in state management and dependency handling that custom functions lack
Question: 352 CertyIQ
For this question, refer to the EHR Healthcare case study. EHR Healthcare recently experienced unauthorized
access of their Kubernetes-based applications due to compromised credentials, resulting in a security breach. You
need to secure access between their on-premises systems and the Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) environment,

while maintaining regulatory compliance and integrating with EHR's existing identity provider. What should you
do?
A. Establish a secure VPN tunnel between the on-premises network and the GKE VPC, and implement strict
firewall rules to restrict access based on source IP addresses and ports.
B. Configure Workload Identity Federation with Active Directory to enable authentication and authorization for
applications across both Kubernetes environments.
C. Deploy an OpenID Connect (OIDC) identity provider in GKE, and integrate it with Active Directory for
centralized authentication and fine-grained authorization controls.
D. Create a service account with minimal permissions for each application running in GKE, and distribute the
credentials securely to the on-premises systems.
Answer: C
Explanation:
The EHR Healthcare case study emphasizes two critical factors:
regulatory compliance (HIPAA) and the use of an existing on-premises Active Directory (AD).Integrating GKE
with an OpenID Connect (OIDC) identity provider is the Google-recommended architectural pattern for
federating external identities (like those in Active Directory) into Kubernetes. This solution addresses the root
cause of the breach (compromised credentials) by moving away from static, long-lived keys toward short-
lived, token-based authentication. It also satisfies the compliance requirement by centralizing audit logs and
identity management within the existing enterprise system.
Why Other Options Are Incorrect:
Option A is Incorrect:
While setting up a VPN and firewall rules is a standard networking best practice, it does not solve the identity-
based breach mentioned in the prompt. The issue was "compromised credentials." An attacker with valid
credentials can often bypass firewall rules if they are accessing the system from a "trusted" IP range or if the
firewall is not identity-aware.
Option B is Incorrect:
Workload Identity Federation is primarily used to allow workloads (such as a pod in GKE or a server on-prem)
to access Google Cloud APIs (like Cloud Storage or BigQuery) without using service account keys. While it is a
powerful security tool, it is not the standard mechanism for human users or administrative systems to
authenticate against the GKE API server itself for cluster management and access.
Option D is Incorrect:
This is a security anti-pattern. Generating and distributing service account JSON keys is exactly what leads to
the "compromised credentials" scenario described in the breach. Keys are long-lived, difficult to rotate, and
often end up in insecure locations (like code repositories or local machines). This would likely fail a regulatory
compliance audit for a healthcare organization.
Question: 353 CertyIQ
For this question, refer to the EHR Healthcare case study. Due to the growing infrastructure administration costs,
you want to migrate the legacy relational database systems to Google Cloud. You need to maintain the strict
uptime requirements with limited in-house expertise. What should you do?
A. Backup and restore the databases to Cloud SQL instances with minimal changes.
B. Leverage AlloyDB to migrate the on-premises databases.

C. Set up database replication to Cloud SQL instances using native database tools, and incrementally switch
applications to use the replicas.
D. Use the Database Migration Service (DMS) to migrate the MySQL databases to Cloud SQL.
Answer: D
Explanation:
Correct Answer: D
In the EHR Healthcare case study, the organization is dealing with legacy MySQL and SQL Server databases.
When a question mentions "strict uptime" combined with "limited in-house expertise," Google Cloud’s
Database Migration Service (DMS) is almost always the intended answer.DMS is a serverless service that
simplifies the migration process by using native replication capabilities. It performs a continuous data sync
between your on-premises source and the Cloud SQL destination. This allows you to keep the legacy system
running until the final "cutover" moment, which results in minimal downtime. Because it is a managed, guided
service, it does not require the deep DBA (Database Administrator) expertise that manual replication would.
Why Other Options Are Incorrect:
Option A is Incorrect:
A Backup and Restore strategy is considered "offline" migration. You must stop writes to the database, take a
backup, move the file, and restore it. For large legacy databases, this process can take hours or even days,
which violates EHR’s strict uptime requirements.
Option B is Incorrect:
AlloyDB is a powerful, high-end database engine, but it is specifically built for PostgreSQL workloads. Since
the EHR case study focuses heavily on their existing MySQL footprint, AlloyDB is not a compatible "lift and
shift" destination without a complex and risky database conversion.
Option C is Incorrect:
While native database tools (like setting up a manual replication binlog in MySQL) can achieve low downtime,
they require significant in-house expertise to configure, monitor, and troubleshoot. The prompt explicitly
states that the team has limited expertise, making this manual approach inappropriate for their situation.
Question: 354 CertyIQ
A large healthcare provider's primary electronic health record (EHR) application runs on Compute Engine
instances with a Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL database, all located in the us-west1 region. A new regulatory mandate
requires you to implement and document a business continuity plan (BCP). This plan must ensure that the EHR
application can be fully recovered and operational in a different geographical region with a recovery time objective
(RTO) of two hours and a recovery point objective (RPO) of 15 minutes. You need to design a disaster recovery
strategy that meets these strict BCP requirements. What should you do?
A. Deploy active managed instance groups (MIGs) in both us-west1 and us-east.1, fronted by a global external
HTTP(S) Load Balancer. For the database, use a cross-region read replica in us-east1, and rely on load balancer
health checks to automatically fail over all traffic during an outage.
B. Use Terraform to define the application's compute infrastructure. During a disaster, configure the Cloud SQL
database in us-west1 to use a cross-region read replica in us-east1, build the environment in us-east1, and
promote the replica.
C. Take daily snapshots of the Compute Engine disks and Cloud SQL database. Copy these snapshots to a
Cloud Storage bucket in us-east1. During a disaster, manually restore the virtual machines (VMs) and database
from the latest snapshots.
D. Deploy a regional MIG in us-west1 for high availability, and rely on the Google Cloud SLA to ensure the region

remains online.
Answer: A
Explanation:
To meet a 15-minute RPO and a 2-hour RTO, you need a "Hot" or "Warm" standby architecture.
Option A provides exactly this.
By having Managed Instance Groups (MIGs) active in two regions and using a Global External HTTP(S) Load
Balancer, the compute layer can fail over almost instantly based on health checks (meeting the RTO). For the
data layer, a Cloud SQL cross-region read replica provides near real-time asynchronous replication. This
ensures that the data in the second region is never more than a few seconds or minutes behind the primary,
satisfying the 15-minute RPO requirement.
Why Other Options Are Incorrect:
Option B is Incorrect:
While using Terraform (Infrastructure as Code) is a best practice, building the entire environment from scratch
after the disaster has occurred is too slow. Provisioning VMs, configuring networking, and verifying the stack
manually or via script during a crisis makes it extremely difficult to guarantee a 2-hour RTO. Furthermore, the
database replica should be configured before the disaster happens to ensure data is already there.
Option C is Incorrect:
This strategy fails the RPO requirement. If you take snapshots daily, your maximum data loss (RPO) is 24
hours. If a disaster occurs right before the next scheduled snapshot, you lose a full day of patient records.
This is significantly higher than the mandated 15-minute limit.
Option D is Incorrect:
A Regional MIG only protects you against the failure of a single zone within a region. It does not provide
protection if the entire us-west1 region goes offline. Additionally, a Service Level Agreement (SLA) is a
contractual commitment for compensation; it is not a technical disaster recovery solution and does not
restore your data or services.
Question: 355 CertyIQ
Your company wants to optimize Google Cloud costs for their development and staging environments. These
environments are workstations used by developers Monday through Friday, 9:00 AM to 6:00 PM local time.
Currently, the environments run on a fleet of n1-standard-4 Compute Engine instances that operate 24/7, leading
to a high monthly cost for resources that are idle more than 70% of the time. You need to implement a solution that
significantly reduces the monthly cost of these non-production environments without impacting the development
team's productivity during work hours. What should you do?
A. Upgrade all instances to the N2 machine series.
B. Re-architect the environments to run on a regional managed instance group (MIG) with autoscaling enabled.
C. Schedule the virtual machines to start and stop to match your team's work schedule.
D. Purchase three-year committed use discounts (CUDs) for the existing n1-standard-4 instances.
Answer: C
Explanation:

Correct Answer: C
The most effective way to optimize costs for predictable, time-bound workloads is to eliminate waste during
"off-hours."Option C is the correct choice because developers only work 45 hours out of a 168-hour week
(approx. 27% of the time). By using Instance Schedules to automatically stop the VMs at 6:00 PM and start
them at 9:00 AM, you stop being billed for the CPU and RAM during the remaining 123 hours. This results in a
direct cost saving of over 70%. This approach is superior to discounts because the "zero cost" of a stopped
VM outweighs any percentage-based discount on a VM that stays running 24/7.
Why Other Options Are Incorrect:
Option A is Incorrect:
The N2 series offers a better price-to-performance ratio than the aging N1 series, but it is still a paid resource.
Upgrading the machine type does not address the fundamental issue that the company is paying for 123 hours
of idle time per week.
Option B is Incorrect:
Autoscaling in a MIG is designed for handling fluctuations in external traffic (like a web server). Developer
workstations are typically stateful (containing specific files, open applications, and local configurations).
Autoscaling might delete a VM to save costs, causing a developer to lose unsaved work. Furthermore, a MIG
won't necessarily scale down to zero on weekends unless specifically configured with complex custom
metrics.
Option D is Incorrect:
Committed Use Discounts (CUDs) provide a significant price reduction (up to 70% for a 3-year term), but they
require you to commit to paying for the resource 24 hours a day, 7 days a week, for the entire duration of the
contract. Even with a 70% discount, you are still paying for 100% of the hours. Option C is better because you
simply don't pay for the 70% of the time the machine is off, and you aren't locked into a multi-year contract
for development machines that might change in spec next year.
Question: 356 CertyIQ
Your company is expanding its AI-powered operations nationwide and has chosen accelerator-based compute for
the AI workloads. The batch image processing workloads are not time-sensitive and can tolerate interruptions. You
need to rapidly deploy cost-effective accelerator nodes for these batch tasks, ensuring rapid deployment and data
persistence when necessary. What should you do?
A. Deploy Cloud Run functions with ephemeral local SSD.
B. Deploy standard VMs with configured accelerators and attached persistent disks.
C. Deploy spot VMs with local SSD to reduce time for bursty workloads.
D. Deploy spot VMs with attached persistent disks and implement checkpoint mechanisms.
Answer: D
Explanation:
Correct Answer: D
This question tests your ability to optimize for cost while meeting hardware (accelerator) and durability
(persistence) requirements.Option D is the correct choice because Spot VMs (formerly Preemptible VMs)
provide a massive discount (up to 91%) for workloads that can tolerate being stopped by Google at any time.
Since the batch processing is "not time-sensitive," this is the most cost-effective path. By attaching Persistent

Disks (PD), your data remains available even if the VM is preempted, unlike Local SSDs which are wiped upon
termination. Finally, checkpointing is the critical software-level pattern for Spot VMs; it ensures the
application periodically saves its state so that when a new Spot VM replaces a preempted one, it can resume
from the last saved point instead of restarting the entire batch.
Why Other Options Are Incorrect:
Option A is Incorrect: Cloud Run functions are generally used for lightweight, event-driven logic. While they
have improved support for specific workloads, they do not offer the same flexibility for specialized high-
performance AI accelerators (GPUs/TPUs) as Compute Engine. Additionally, ephemeral local SSDs do not
provide the "persistence" required, as data is lost when the instance scales down or terminates.
Option B is Incorrect: While this solution is robust and easy to manage, standard VMs are significantly more
expensive than Spot VMs. The prompt specifically asks for a "cost-effective" solution for a workload that is
"not time-sensitive." Using standard VMs in this context would result in overspending for a level of availability
(99.9%+) that the application does not strictly require.
Option C is Incorrect: Local SSDs are physically attached to the host server. While they provide incredibly
high throughput and low latency, they are ephemeral. If a Spot VM is preempted, all data on a Local SSD is
lost forever. This fails the "ensure data persistence" requirement of the question.
Question: 357 CertyIQ
A financial services company is decommissioning one of its on-premises data centers. As part of this initiative, the
company needs to perform a one-time migration of 500 TB of historical transaction archives to a Cloud Storage
bucket for long-term retention. The data center's internet egress is 1 Gbps, which is shared with critical business
operations. You must complete the secure data transfer within a 60-day window to meet the decommissioning
deadline. What should you do?
A. Use Storage Transfer Service to create an agent-based transfer job that moves the data from the on-
premises file servers directly to the Cloud Storage bucket.
B. Provision a Partner Interconnect connection with a 10 Gbps capacity to accelerate the data transfer, and then
use Storage Transfer Service.
C. Order a Transfer Appliance, copy the data to the appliance using your high-speed local network, and ship it
back to Google to upload the data into your Cloud Storage bucket.
D. Write a script that uses the gcloud storage cp --parallel command to upload the data in chunks over the
public internet during off-peak hours.
Answer: C
Explanation:
Correct Answer: C
This is a classic "bandwidth vs. time" calculation question. To move 500 TB over a 1 Gbps line (assuming 100%
utilization), it would take approximately 46–50 days. However, the prompt states the 1 Gbps line is shared
with critical business operations, meaning you cannot use the full bandwidth. If you only used 20-30% of the
line to avoid impacting the business, the transfer would take over 150 days, missing the 60-day deadline.
Transfer Appliance is the best choice for "offline" data transfer when the data volume is high (hundreds of
terabytes to petabytes) and the network is slow or saturated. You load the data onto the hardware locally at
high speeds (10 Gbps or higher LAN) and ship it to Google, where it is uploaded directly to the backbone
network, bypassing your limited internet egress entirely.
Why Other Options Are Incorrect:

Option A is Incorrect:
As calculated above, an online transfer via Storage Transfer Service would take too long because your 1 Gbps
internet connection is a bottleneck and is shared with other critical traffic. You would either miss the deadline
or crash your business operations by saturating the line.
Option B is Incorrect:
While a 10 Gbps Interconnect would certainly be fast enough to move the data, provisioning a Partner
Interconnect involves third-party providers and physical cross-connects. This process can often take several
weeks to months to finalize. Given the strict 60-day decommissioning deadline, relying on a new circuit
installation is too risky compared to the immediate availability of a Transfer Appliance.
Option D is Incorrect:
Using gcloud storage cp (or the older gsutil) with parallel threads is an excellent way to optimize small-to-
medium transfers, but it still relies on your limited internet egress. Even during off-peak hours, you would
struggle to move 500 TB in the remaining time, and manual scripting is more prone to error than using
managed Google services.
Question: 358 CertyIQ
A retail company’s most critical application is its online payment processing system. The business has a
requirement that the system must be able to survive a complete zonal outage while minimizing cost. You need a
design solution that can handle a zonal failure. What should you do?
A. Deploy the application in an active-active configuration using managed instance groups (MIGs) in two
different regions, fronted by a global external HTTP(S) Load Balancer and backed by a multi-regional database
like Spanner.
B. Deploy the application on a regional MIG to provide high availability across multiple zones in the primary
region.
C. Configure the regional MIG to use only Spot VMs to aggressively minimize operational costs while
maintaining high availability.
D. Deploy the application on Compute Engine instances across multiple regions, and rely on daily snapshots for
recovery to achieve the lowest possible cost.
Answer: B
Explanation:
Correct Answer: B
In Google Cloud architecture, a Regional Managed Instance Group (MIG) is the standard solution for achieving
high availability (HA) while protecting against zonal failures. A regional MIG automatically spreads VM
instances across multiple zones within a single region. If one zone goes down, the MIG's autoscaler and self-
healing mechanisms ensure that instances are maintained in the remaining healthy zones. This satisfies the
requirement to survive a zonal outage while keeping costs significantly lower than a multi-regional setup.
Why Other Options Are Incorrect:
Option A is Incorrect:
While this architecture provides the highest possible availability (surviving a regional outage), it is not cost-
optimized. Using multiple regions and a multi-regional Spanner database is significantly more expensive than
a single-region setup. The prompt specifically asks to handle a zonal failure, making this solution overkill and
too costly for the stated requirement.

Option C is Incorrect:
Spot VMs are not suitable for "critical applications" like payment processing. Spot VMs can be reclaimed by
Google at any time with only a 30-second notice. Relying on Spot VMs for a system that must remain available
during an outage would result in unpredictable downtime, violating the requirement for high availability.
Option D is Incorrect:
Relying on daily snapshots for recovery provides a Recovery Time Objective (RTO) of hours and a Recovery
Point Objective (RPO) of up to 24 hours. For a critical online payment system, this amount of downtime and
data loss is unacceptable. Furthermore, manual recovery from snapshots does not provide the automated
"survival" of an outage that a MIG offers.
Question: 359 CertyIQ
Your company is running AI/ML workloads on graphics processing units (GPUs) within Google Kubernetes Engine
(GKE). These workloads are deployed across production and development projects, with the production
environment being business critical and operating 24/7. You need to ensure that the GPU capacity is always
available during scaling events, without being limited by overall GPU availability. You also need the ability to shift
unused GPU capacity from production to development projects as needed. You want to follow Google-
recommended best practices. What should you do?
A. Create a shared reservation in the production project, and then add the development project as a consumer
project to move capacity when needed.
B. Create a reservation in the production project, and then transfer the reservation to the development project
when needed.
C. Create a reservation in the production project, and then create a separate reservation in the development
project to secure capacity.
D. Create an owner project with shared reservations, and then configure the production project and
development project as consumer projects to consume capacity.
Answer: D
Explanation:
Correct Answer: D
To guarantee availability for specialized hardware like GPUs, you must use Compute Engine Reservations. For
scenarios involving multiple projects (Production and Development) where you need to share and shift
capacity, Google-recommended best practice is to use Shared Reservations.
Option D is the correct architectural approach. By creating a dedicated Owner Project to hold the reservation,
you centralize the billing and management of that capacity. You then designate the Production and
Development projects as Consumer Projects. This allows the GKE clusters in both projects to pull from the
same pool of reserved GPUs. If Production isn't using its full quota, the capacity is automatically available for
Development scaling events without any manual "transfer" of resources.
Why Other Options Are Incorrect:
Option A is Incorrect:
While you can share a reservation from an existing project, Google recommends using a dedicated "owner"
project for shared reservations to separate resource ownership from resource consumption. This prevents a
situation where deleting or modifying the Production project inadvertently breaks the reservation for other
environments.
Option B is Incorrect:

Reservations cannot be "transferred" between projects on the fly in the way described. Modifying a
reservation's owner or scope typically involves deleting and recreating it, which defeats the purpose of
securing "always available" capacity. Shared reservations are designed specifically to avoid this manual
overhead.
Option C is Incorrect:
Creating two separate, isolated reservations is inefficient and more expensive. If you reserve 10 GPUs for
Production and 5 for Development, and Production only uses 2, the other 8 sit idle and cannot be utilized by
Development. This fails the requirement to "shift unused GPU capacity" between environments.
Question: 360 CertyIQ
A global media company is launching a new web application. The application backend is hosted on Compute
Engine in us-central1 and serves both static assets (images, CSS, and JavaScript) and dynamic, user-specific
content from a Cloud SQL database in the same region. Early user feedback from Europe and Asia indicates
significant page load delays due to slow loading static content. You need to design a solution that minimizes
latency for all global users accessing the static content. What should you do?Choose 2 answers
A. Enable Cloud CDN for the backend service that serves the static assets, and configure it as part of a global
external HTTP(S) Load Balancer.
B. Use a regional external Network Load Balancer in us-central1 to better distribute the incoming global traffic.
C. Vertically scale the Compute Engine instances in us-central1 by increasing their machine size.
D. Deploy the application frontend service to Compute Engine managed instance groups in regions in Europe
and Asia. Use a global external HTTP(S) Load Balancer to route user traffic to the nearest region.
E. Create Cloud SQL read replicas in regions in Europe and Asia, and direct all database read traffic from those
continents to their local replica.
Answer: A,D
Explanation:
For this scenario, the primary issue is the geographic distance between the users in Europe and Asia and the
servers in us-central1. While the application handles both static and dynamic content, the specific complaint
focuses on static assets, which are ideal candidates for edge caching.
Correct Answers: A and D
Option A (Cloud CDN): This is the most effective and cost-efficient way to handle static assets globally. Cloud
CDN caches content at Google's "Points of Presence" (PoPs) all over the world. When a user in Europe
requests an image, it is served from a local cache in Europe rather than traveling all the way to us-central1.
This drastically reduces latency for static files.
Option D (Multi-regional Frontend): By deploying Managed Instance Groups (MIGs) in Europe and Asia, you
bring the web servers (the frontend) closer to the users. The Global External HTTP(S) Load Balancer uses
Anycast IP to automatically route the user to the closest healthy instance, reducing the "Time to First Byte"
(TTFB) for the initial page load.
Why Other Options Are Incorrect:
Option B is Incorrect:
A Regional Network Load Balancer is confined to a single region (us-central1). It does not help global users
because all traffic would still have to terminate in the US before being processed. It also doesn't support
Cloud CDN.

Option C is Incorrect:
Vertical scaling (making the VMs bigger) addresses CPU or RAM bottlenecks. The issue here is network
latency (the physical speed of light over fiber across oceans). A faster CPU in Kansas cannot make data travel
faster to Tokyo.
Option E is Incorrect:
While Cloud SQL read replicas in other regions are a great way to speed up dynamic data queries, the prompt
specifically highlights that the "significant page load delays" are due to static content. Optimizing the
database will not fix slow-loading images and CSS files.
Question: 361 CertyIQ
You are setting up a new regional environment for your business needs. You plan to migrate more than 2 TB of data
from the us-central1 region to the asia-southeast1 region. The transfer should occur as quickly as possible. If the
transfer is interrupted, the operation must resume where it left off and copy only new or modified objects. What
should you do?
A. Use the gcloud storage rsync --recursive command to replicate the source bucket to a new target bucket in
the us-west1 region.
B. Utilize Storage Transfer Service (STS) to migrate objects to the destination bucket in the target region. Once
the transfer is complete, delete the original source bucket objects.
C. Use the gcloud storage cp command to copy the objects. Be careful to manage potential naming conflicts to
ensure data integrity.
D. Leverage Cloud Composer to orchestrate a data pipeline that copies objects from the source bucket to the
destination bucket.
Answer: B
Explanation:
Correct Answer: B
For moving large volumes of data (terabytes or petabytes) between Google Cloud Storage buckets, Storage
Transfer Service (STS) is the Google-recommended best practice.
STS is a fully managed service that handles the scalability and reliability of the transfer. It specifically
addresses the requirements in the prompt:
-Speed: It uses Google’s high-speed internal backbone network for cross-region transfers.
-Resilience: It is designed to be fault-tolerant; if a transfer is interrupted, it automatically resumes.
-Efficiency: It performs a delta sync, meaning it compares the source and destination and only copies objects
that are new or have been modified (based on size and ETag/MD5 checksums).
-Scale: It handles more than 2 TB of data much more effectively than CLI-based tools which are limited by the
machine running them.
Why Other Options Are Incorrect:
Option A is Incorrect:
While gsutil rsync or gcloud storage rsync can perform delta synchronizations, it is a client-side tool. This
means the transfer speed is limited by the local machine's CPU and network bandwidth. Furthermore, the
option suggests moving data to us-west1, which is the wrong destination region according to the prompt (asia-

southeast1).
Option C is Incorrect:
The gcloud storage cp command is a basic copy tool. While it is faster in the new gcloud storage
implementation than the old gsutil, it does not inherently manage the "resume from where it left off" logic for
thousands of objects as robustly as STS. It would also require manual scripting to ensure only new/modified
objects are sent.
Option D is Incorrect:
Cloud Composer (managed Apache Airflow) is an orchestration tool. While you could write a DAG to move
data, it adds significant and unnecessary complexity for a standard bucket-to-bucket migration. Cloud
Composer would likely just call STS or a copy command anyway, so it is better to use the managed service
(STS) directly.
Question: 362 CertyIQ
You are developing a deep learning model that requires high-performance access to large volumes of media data
currently stored in Cloud Storage. Model training will be executed on multiple VM instances with GPUs attached,
but your application must interact with the data as if it were on the local file system. You need to minimize
complexity and cost. What should you do?
A. Create a shared persistent disk, attach the disk to your VM instances, and load data from the Cloud Storage
bucket.
B. Copy the data from Cloud Storage to Filestore, and then mount the Filestore volume as a local file system on
your VM instances.
C. Use the gcsfuse command line tool to mount the Cloud Storage bucket as a local file system, and perform
read/write operations in your bucket using standard file system semantics.
D. Use the gsutil command line tool to download the data to your VM instances.
Answer: C
Explanation:
Correct Answer: C
Cloud Storage FUSE (gcsfuse) is an open-source FUSE adapter that allows you to mount Cloud Storage
buckets as file systems on Linux or macOS systems.
This is the optimal choice because:
-Local Semantics: It allows your deep learning code to use standard POSIX-like file calls (e.g., open(), read())
to access objects in Cloud Storage, satisfying the requirement to interact with data as if it were on a local
disk.
-Minimizes Complexity: You do not need to rewrite your application to use the Cloud Storage API or client
libraries.
-Minimizes Cost: Unlike Filestore or large Persistent Disks, you only pay for the Cloud Storage capacity and
operations used. There is no need to pay for provisioned IOPS or idle disk space.
-Scalability: It works seamlessly across multiple VM instances (GPUs) simultaneously accessing the same
bucket.
Why Other Options Are Incorrect:

Option A is Incorrect:
Standard Persistent Disks can only be attached to multiple VMs in read-only mode (if using a multi-writer PD,
it is limited to specific machine types and requires a cluster-aware file system). Loading "large volumes" of
data from Cloud Storage onto disks for every training run adds significant time (latency) and storage costs,
increasing both complexity and expense.
Option B is Incorrect:
Filestore provides a fully managed NFS server which does offer local file system semantics. However,
migrating data from Cloud Storage to Filestore creates a redundant copy of the data, doubling your storage
costs. Filestore also has a higher baseline price point than Cloud Storage, which violates the "minimize cost"
requirement.
Option D is Incorrect:
Using gsutil to download data to local VMs is a common pattern, but it fails the requirement to "interact with
the data as if it were on the local file system" during the development/application phase. If the data volume is
truly "large," the VM's local boot disk may not have enough space, requiring you to provision and manage
extra disks, which increases complexity.
Question: 363 CertyIQ
A large, multinational corporation is migrating to Google Cloud. The company has several distinct business units:
Finance, Marketing, and Research and Development (R&D). The central security team has mandated governance
requirements for each business unit:
Finance: Must be restricted to deploying resources only in specific, compliant regions (us-central1 and europe-
west2). Access to their projects must be tightly controlled by a dedicated finance-admins group.
Marketing: Needs separate environments for production and development, with different teams managing each
environment.
