R&D: Requires maximum flexibility to experiment with new services but must be completely isolated to prevent
any impact on production systems.
Global Auditing: A central compliance team requires read-only access to view all resources across the entire
company for auditing purposes.
You need to design a resource hierarchy that enforces these security policies at scale according to the Google
Cloud Well-Architected Framework while providing the correct level of autonomy for each business unit. What
should you do?
A. Create a single project for each department. Apply the resource location policy directly to the Finance
project. Grant the compliance team the roles/browser role on each project individually.
B. Create a folder for each department under the root Organization node. Apply the resource location
Organization Policy on the Finance folder. Within the Marketing folder, create separate projects for mktg-prod
and mktg-dev. Grant the compliance team the roles/viewer role at the Organization level.
C. Create separate Google Cloud Organizations for each department (Finance, Marketing, and R&D). Grant the
compliance team the roles/viewer role for each organization.
D. Place all projects directly under the Organization node. Use network tags and service accounts to enforce
security boundaries between the different department workloads. Apply the resource location Organization
Policy on the Finance project.
Answer: B
Explanation:
Correct Answer: B
The Google Cloud Resource Hierarchy is designed to allow policies to be inherited from the top down.
Option B follows the Well-Architected Framework by using Folders to group resources by business unit.

-Inheritance: By applying the Resource Location Organization Policy to the Finance folder, all current and
future projects created within that folder automatically inherit the restriction to us-central1 and europe-
west2.
-Environment Separation: Marketing can have nested folders or separate projects within their department
folder to satisfy the need for production vs. development environments.
-Isolation: The folder structure provides logical administrative boundaries, ensuring R&D experiments stay
within their own project/folder scope.
-Global Auditing: Assigning roles/viewer at the Organization level ensures the compliance team has read-only
access to every folder and project in the company automatically, without having to be added to projects
individually.
Why Other Options Are Incorrect:
Option A is Incorrect:
Managing policies and permissions at the project level does not scale. As the company grows and adds
dozens or hundreds of projects, manually applying policies and adding the audit team to each project
becomes an operational nightmare and is prone to human error, leading to security gaps.
Option C is Incorrect:
Creating multiple Organizations is rarely recommended unless the business units are legally separate entities
with no shared governance. It makes central management, unified billing, and global auditing extremely
difficult. It also prevents the use of shared resources like a Shared VPC or a unified Interconnect.
Option D is Incorrect:
Placing all projects directly under the Organization node (a "flat" hierarchy) removes the ability to use Folders
as a policy enforcement point. Using network tags and service accounts provides technical isolation but does
not address the administrative and governance requirements (like regional restrictions or hierarchical IAM) as
effectively as a folder structure.
Question: 364 CertyIQ
Your organization is going to migrate applications to Kubernetes and use managed cloud services to deploy
applications. Your team is new to Kubernetes and wants to quickly onboard engineers. You want to reduce
operational overhead, so the engineering team can focus on developing consumer requirements instead of
maintaining the infrastructure. What should you do?
A. Package your application into a Docker image, and deploy it to Kubernetes on Compute Engine.
B. Leverage Cloud Build to create a container image, and deploy it automatically to Kubernetes on Compute
Engine.
C. Assess application and dependencies for containerization. Develop a migration strategy for deployment to
GKE in Standard mode.
D. Assess application and dependencies for containerization. Develop a migration strategy for deployment to
GKE in Autopilot mode.
Answer: D
Explanation:
Correct Answer: D
GKE Autopilot is the recommended solution when the primary goal is to reduce operational overhead. In

Autopilot mode, Google Cloud manages the entire underlying infrastructure, including the nodes, security,
and scaling.
Key reasons why this fits the prompt:
-Reduced Overhead: Unlike GKE Standard, where you manage node pools and machine types, Autopilot
provisions and manages nodes automatically based on your Pod specifications.
-Ease of Onboarding: For a team new to Kubernetes, Autopilot removes the complexity of cluster
administration (bin-packing, node upgrades, and OS patching), allowing them to focus purely on the
application layer.
-Cost Efficiency: You pay only for the resources (CPU, memory, storage) your Pods actually request, rather
than paying for the entire node, which simplifies budget management for new teams.
Why Other Options Are Incorrect:
Option A and B are Incorrect:
Deploying Kubernetes on Compute Engine (unmanaged/self-managed Kubernetes) represents the highest
possible operational overhead. The team would be responsible for installing Kubernetes, managing the
control plane, scaling nodes manually, and handling all security patches. This contradicts the requirement to
focus on consumer requirements rather than infrastructure.
Option C is Incorrect:
While GKE Standard is a managed service, it still leaves the responsibility of managing and configuring node
pools to the customer. The team would need to decide on machine types, autoscaling thresholds, and
maintenance windows. For a team "new to Kubernetes" looking to "reduce operational overhead," Autopilot
(Option D) is a much better fit than Standard.
Question: 365 CertyIQ
Your company runs a critical, revenue-generating ecommerce application that is served by a regional managed
instance group (MIG) behind an external HTTP(S) Load Balancer. The operations team is currently overwhelmed
with low-priority notifications and is starting to ignore alerts. Your team's service level objective (SLO) is to
maintain 99.9% availability, which is measured by the ratio of successful requests (2xx status codes) to total
requests. You want to minimize noise from non-critical events and ensure that the team is only notified of issues
that are actionable and threaten the SLO. What should you do?
A. Focus on cause-based alerts, creating alerting policies with thresholds for the Compute Engine instances,
including CPU utilization, memory usage, disk I/O, and network traffic.
B. Implement an error budget policy based on the availability of the SLO. Create a "page" alert that triggers
only when the rate of burn of the error budget predicts a full exhaustion within the next 24 hours.
C. Configure alerts based on predictive metrics. Use the instance count of the MIG as the primary metric to
trigger an alert.
D. Create log-based alerts for only the WARN and ERROR log entries generated by the application to ensure
that no potential issue is missed.
Answer: B
Explanation:
Correct Answer: B
This question tests your knowledge of Site Reliability Engineering (SRE) principles, specifically Service Level
Objectives (SLOs) and Error Budgets.

To "minimize noise" and ensure alerts are "actionable," you should move from cause-based alerting to
symptom-based alerting. Option B is the correct choice because it uses the Error Budget Burn Rate. Instead of
paging the team for every minor spike, the system calculates how fast the error budget (the 0.1% of "allowed"
failures) is being consumed. If the current failure rate is high enough to exhaust the entire monthly budget
within 24 hours, it indicates a critical threat to the SLO that requires immediate human intervention. This
significantly reduces "alert fatigue" by ignoring transient issues that don't impact the long-term reliability
goal.
Why Other Options Are Incorrect:
Option A is Incorrect:
This is traditional cause-based alerting. High CPU or disk I/O does not always mean the user is experiencing
an issue (e.g., a background cron job might spike CPU while the app remains healthy). Setting thresholds for
every individual metric is exactly what leads to the "low-priority notifications" and "overwhelmed" team
mentioned in the prompt.
Option C is Incorrect:
Instance count in a MIG is a poor indicator of service health. A MIG might scale up or down correctly based on
traffic, or it might have many instances that are all failing health checks. Alerting on the number of instances
doesn't tell the team if the availability SLO (successful requests) is being met.
Option D is Incorrect:
Log-based alerts for all WARN and ERROR entries are extremely noisy. Many applications generate warnings
or non-critical errors (like a single timed-out database connection that successfully retries) as part of normal
operations. This approach would increase the volume of low-priority notifications, worsening the current
problem.
Question: 366 CertyIQ
You manage a highly distributed, hybrid- and multi-cloud IT environment, and your developers rely heavily on
Prometheus for their workflows. You need a cloud- based, highly scalable, low-maintenance enterprise solution
that supports Prometheus Query Language (PromQL) queries, quick metric viewing, and efficient issue diagnosis.
What should you do?
A. Set up Cloud Monitoring as a single pane of glass across multi-cloud environments.
B. Enable Google Cloud Managed Service for Prometheus to monitor and alert on your workloads at scale.
C. Build a SaaS-based, Prometheus-compatible solution to display metrics for each cloud in a customizable
way.
D. Deploy a Prometheus operator in your existing Kubernetes and Serverless setup across multi-cloud
environments.
Answer: B
Explanation:
Correct Answer: B
Google Cloud Managed Service for Prometheus (GMP) is the ideal solution for this scenario. It is a fully
managed, multi-cloud, cross-project solution that allows you to monitor and alert on your workloads using
Prometheus, without having to manually manage and scale the Prometheus backend.
Key reasons why this is the correct choice:

-PromQL Support: It is built on top of the same infrastructure as Cloud Monitoring but allows you to use your
existing Prometheus queries (PromQL), dashboards, and alerts.
-Low Maintenance: As a managed service, it removes the operational burden of managing high-availability
Prometheus servers, storage (Thanos/Cortex), and retention policies.
Scalability: It can ingest millions of time series and scale globally, which is critical for highly distributed hybrid
environments.
-Hybrid/Multi-Cloud: GMP can collect metrics from GKE, on-premises environments, and even other clouds
(like AWS or Azure) using the standard Prometheus collectors.
Why Other Options Are Incorrect:
Option A is Incorrect:
While Cloud Monitoring is a single pane of glass, simply "setting it up" does not explicitly address the
requirement for PromQL support. While Cloud Monitoring has added PromQL support recently, the specific
"Managed Service for Prometheus" is the named architectural product designed to bridge the gap for
Prometheus-heavy dev teams.
Option C is Incorrect:
"Building a SaaS-based solution" goes against the requirement for a low-maintenance enterprise solution.
Creating your own platform requires significant engineering resources, maintenance, and long-term support,
which is the opposite of leveraging a cloud-managed service.
Option D is Incorrect:
Deploying a Prometheus Operator manually across various environments is a self-managed approach. This
puts the burden of scaling, storage management, and maintenance (upgrades, backup, etc.) on your team. It
does not provide the "low-maintenance enterprise solution" requested in the prompt.
Question: 367 CertyIQ
You are designing the storage architecture for a financial analytics platform. The platform ingests and stores
terabytes of transactional data daily, which is used for both real-time fraud detection and long-term historical
analysis. Transaction data from the last 30 days must be accessible with very low latency for the fraud detection
engine. Data older than 30 days is accessed infrequently for quarterly reports, where retrieval times of a few
seconds are acceptable. All data must be retained for five years to meet compliance regulations. You need to
design a solution as cost-effective as possible. What should you do?
A. Store all transaction data in a Cloud Storage bucket using the Standard storage class for the entire five-year
retention period.
B. Ingest all data into BigQuery using time-partitioned tables, and rely on BigQuery's automatic long-term
storage pricing for data older than 90 days.
C. Configure a Cloud Storage bucket with an Object Lifecycle Management policy to transition data from the
Standard class to the Archive class after 30 days.
D. Configure a Cloud Storage bucket with an Object Lifecycle Management policy to transition data from the
Standard class to the Coldline class after 30 days.
Answer: D
Explanation:
Correct Answer: D

This question evaluates your ability to use Object Lifecycle Management to reduce costs while meeting
specific Retrieval Time Objectives (RTO).
-Low Latency (0–30 days): Standard storage provides the lowest latency and is perfect for the real-time
fraud detection engine.
-Infrequent Access (30 days+): The requirement states that for quarterly reports, "retrieval times of a few
seconds are acceptable." Coldline storage fits this perfectly because it has a lower storage price than
Standard but provides millisecond access latency (the same as Standard).
-Cost-Effectiveness: Transitioning to Coldline after 30 days significantly reduces the storage cost for the
remaining 4 years and 11 months of the compliance period.
Why Other Options Are Incorrect:
Option A is Incorrect:
Storing all data in Standard storage for five years is the most expensive approach. While it meets all
performance requirements, it fails the requirement to be "as cost-effective as possible."
Option B is Incorrect:
While BigQuery is excellent for analytics, storing "terabytes daily" (petabytes over 5 years) in BigQuery
storage is typically more expensive than Cloud Storage. Furthermore, the prompt implies raw transactional
data ingestion where Cloud Storage is the standard landing zone.
Option C is Incorrect:
Archive storage is the cheapest storage class, but it is intended for data accessed less than once a year. While
the latency for Archive is still milliseconds, the retrieval costs are very high. More importantly, the prompt
asks for "quarterly reports" (accessed 4 times a year). For data accessed quarterly, Coldline is generally more
cost-effective than Archive due to the lower data access fees.
Question: 368 CertyIQ
You are migrating a large, on-premises application to Google Cloud. The application consists of several
interconnected virtual machines. You want to create a detailed migration plan to ensure a smooth migration with
minimal effort. You need to understand the existing environment, identify dependencies, and estimate the total
cost of ownership (TCO) in the cloud. What should you do?
A. Use the Google Cloud pricing calculator to input the specifications of your on-premises servers and receive a
TCO estimate.
B. Write a custom script to query the vSphere API for virtual machine information and then import it into
BigQuery for analysis.
C. Use the Google Cloud Migration Center to perform an automated discovery and assessment of the on-
premises environment.
D. Use Config Connector to declare the desired state of your Google Cloud resources in Kubernetes-style
manifests.
Answer: C
Explanation:
Correct Answer: C
Google Cloud Migration Center is the unified platform designed specifically for the assessment and planning
stages of a migration. It directly addresses every requirement in the prompt:

Understand the environment: It uses discovery tools (like the mDiscovery collector) to automatically find VMs
and servers.
-Identify dependencies: It maps the connections between different VMs, which is critical for moving
"interconnected" applications without breaking them.
-Estimate TCO: It provides a comprehensive Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) report by comparing your current
on-premises resource usage (actual CPU/RAM consumed) against the most efficient Google Cloud
equivalents (Cloud SQL, Compute Engine, etc.).
-Minimal Effort: Because it is an automated, managed platform, it removes the need for manual data entry or
custom scripting.
Why Other Options Are Incorrect:
Option A is Incorrect:
The Google Cloud Pricing Calculator is excellent for estimating the cost of specific resources once you
already know exactly what you need. However, it cannot "discover" your on-premises environment or identify
complex application dependencies. Inputting hundreds of servers manually would also violate the "minimal
effort" requirement.
Option B is Incorrect:
While querying the vSphere API is technically possible, writing and maintaining a custom script for discovery
and dependency mapping is high-effort and error-prone. Google Cloud already provides these capabilities
natively through Migration Center.
Option D is Incorrect:
Config Connector is a tool for managing Google Cloud resources using Kubernetes-style configuration
(Infrastructure as Code). It is used for provisioning resources after you have planned your migration, not for
the initial discovery, dependency mapping, or TCO estimation of an on-premises environment.
Question: 369 CertyIQ
You are migrating a critical, on-premises, three-tier ecommerce application to Google Cloud. The application has
tightly coupled dependencies between its web, application, and database tiers. The business has strict
performance and availability requirements and is concerned about the risks associated with an "all-at-once"
migration. You want to follow Google-recommended practices to create a migration plan that systematically
addresses application dependencies and validates post-migration performance before the final production
cutover. What should you do?
A. Redesign the application to be cloud-native using microservices before migration to eliminate dependencies
and simplify workload testing.
B. Prioritize the establishment of a high-bandwidth, low-latency Cloud Interconnect connection, and then plan
to migrate the database servers first.
C. Use dependency mapping to group application components into waves, create a phased migration plan, and
incorporate performance testing for each wave.
D. Plan a "lift and shift" migration for all application tiers to be executed simultaneously over a single weekend
to minimize downtime.
Answer: C
Explanation:
Correct Answer: C

For complex, tightly coupled applications, Google recommends a phased approach rather than a single "big
bang" migration.
Option C aligns with Google’s cloud migration framework:
-Dependency Mapping: Using tools (like Migration Center) to visualize which servers talk to each other
ensures that you don't accidentally move a web server while its database remains thousands of miles away
on-premises, which would cause severe latency.
-Migration Waves: By grouping related components into "waves," you reduce risk. You can migrate low-risk
dev/test environments first, then move the production tiers in a logical order.
-Performance Validation: Testing each wave allows you to confirm that the "Cloud" version of the app meets
the strict performance requirements before the final cutover, providing the business with necessary
confidence.
Why Other Options Are Incorrect:
Option A is Incorrect:
"Refactoring" or "Re-architecting" to microservices before migrating is a high-risk strategy known as "boiling
the ocean." It significantly extends the time-to-cloud and introduces too many variables. Google typically
recommends "Move then Change" (migrate first, then modernize) for critical legacy systems.
Option B is Incorrect:
While an Interconnect is likely necessary for the migration process, migrating databases first is generally a
mistake for a three-tier app. If the database moves to the cloud but the application and web tiers stay on-
premises, the "gravity" of the data will cause extreme latency for every user request that travels back and
forth across the Interconnect.
Option D is Incorrect:
The "all-at-once" or "Big Bang" migration is exactly what the business is concerned about. While it minimizes
the time the application is in a "hybrid" state, it represents the highest risk of failure. If an unforeseen
dependency issue arises, the entire application fails, leading to a complex and stressful rollback.
Question: 370 CertyIQ
To improve governance and security, your organization has structured the Google Cloud environment using folders
for different business units. Each business unit folder has subfolders for development, staging, and production
environments, which must comply with internal security controls:
Production workloads must be protected from direct internet ingress by default unless explicitly tagged.
The application must be accessible to customers over HTTPS.
You need to design a scalable and enforceable model that blocks internet ingress traffic to the production folders
while selectively allowing direct HTTPS traffic to the necessary virtual machines. You must also ensure that
individual project teams cannot overwrite these controls once they are implemented for all current and future
production projects. What should you do?
A. At each production folder, apply a hierarchical firewall policy to deny all ingress except for HTTPS to tagged
VMs.
B. Mandate the application teams to deploy a Terraform module to create VPC firewall rules in each project
that deny ingress and allow HTTPS.
C. At each production folder, use an organization policy to block all external IPs and require teams to use
external HTTPS load balancers.
D. At the organization root, apply a hierarchical firewall policy to deny all ingress except for HTTPS to tagged
VMs.

Answer: A
Explanation:
Correct Answer: A
This question tests your knowledge of Hierarchical Firewall Policies, which are the standard tool for enforcing
security at scale across a folder structure.
Option A is the correct choice because:
-Enforcement: Hierarchical firewall policies are applied at the folder level. Unlike VPC firewall rules, they are
evaluated before any project-level rules. This means a project owner cannot "override" or delete the security
controls set by the organization or folder administrators.
-Scalability: By applying the policy at the "Production" folder level, every project underneath that folder
(current and future) automatically inherits the rules.
-Selectivity: Hierarchical firewall policies support Secure Tags. You can create a broad "Deny All Ingress" rule
and a higher-priority "Allow HTTPS" rule that only applies to VMs with a specific tag (e.g., tag: "allow-https").
This allows for the "explicitly tagged" requirement mentioned in the prompt.
Why Other Options Are Incorrect:
Option B is Incorrect:
While Terraform modules promote consistency, VPC Firewall Rules are managed at the project level. An
individual project team with the proper IAM permissions could easily modify or delete these rules once they
are deployed. This fails the requirement to ensure teams "cannot overwrite these controls."
Option C is Incorrect:
Organization Policies (like "Skip Default Network Creation" or "Define allowed external IPs") are powerful but
blunt instruments. While they can block external IPs, they are not designed to manage specific port-level
traffic (like allowing only HTTPS). Relying on an Org Policy to enforce specific firewall-like behavior is less
granular and more complex to manage than a dedicated firewall policy.
Option D is Incorrect:
Applying the policy at the Organization Root would enforce these strict "Production" rules across the entire
company, including development and staging folders. The prompt specifies that these controls are for
"production folders." Applying it at the root would break development environments that might need more
flexible ingress rules for testing.
Question: 371 CertyIQ
A large enterprise is building a file processing pipeline on Google Cloud.The pipeline ingests the raw files, which
are then accessed by a fleet of hundreds of Compute Engine virtual machines (VMs) in a single region for parallel
processing.These VMs need concurrent, low-latency read and write access to a shared file system. You need to
implement a storage solution that delivers the highest possible throughput to minimize the overall processing
time. What should you do?
A. Use a multi-region Cloud Storage bucket to store the files.
B. Create a Regional Persistent Disk and attach it to all VMs in multi-writer mode.
C. Create a Filestore Zonal instance in each zone where the VMs are running. Mount the Filestore file share on
each Compute Engine VM.
D. Create a Filestore Enterprise instance in the same region as the VMs. Mount the Filestore file share on each

Compute Engine VM.
Answer: D
Explanation:
Correct Answer: D
For high-performance, concurrent file access at scale (hundreds of VMs), Filestore Enterprise is the standard
architectural choice in Google Cloud.
Option D is the correct choice because:
-Concurrent Access: Filestore provides a managed Network Attached Storage (NAS) via the NFSv3 protocol,
which allows hundreds of VMs to mount the same volume simultaneously for read/write operations.
-High Throughput: The Enterprise tier is specifically designed for high-performance computing (HPC) and
data-intensive workloads, offering significantly higher throughput and IOPS than the Basic or Zonal tiers.
-Regional Availability: Unlike the Zonal tier, the Enterprise tier is regional. This ensures that even if one zone
fails, the storage remains available to VMs in other zones within that region, meeting high availability
requirements for a large-scale pipeline.
Why Other Options Are Incorrect:
Option A is Incorrect:
While Cloud Storage is highly scalable and cost-effective, it is an object store, not a file system. While you can
use gcsfuse to mount a bucket, it does not support standard POSIX file locking or concurrent write operations
efficiently. For a high-throughput parallel processing pipeline requiring low latency, an object store will be
much slower than a dedicated NFS like Filestore.
Option B is Incorrect:
Regional Persistent Disks with multi-writer mode have very strict limitations. They can only be attached to a
maximum of two N2 VM instances simultaneously. The prompt requires access for "hundreds of VMs," making
this solution technically impossible for the stated scale.
Option C is Incorrect:
While Filestore Zonal provides good performance, creating an instance in each zone creates data silos. To
have a "shared file system" for parallel processing, all VMs need to see the same data. If you have different
Filestore instances per zone, you would have to manually synchronize data between them, which increases
complexity and latency significantly.
Question: 372 CertyIQ
You are designing the storage configuration for a critical Cloud Storage bucket. This bucket will store trade
confirmation documents for a financial services company. These documents are immutable and serve as a legal
record. Your design must meet two primary data protection requirements:1. The system must allow for the rapid
recovery of any document that is accidentally deleted or maliciously overwritten.2. Once a document is written, it
must be impossible for anyone, including administrators, to delete or modify it for seven years.
What should you do?
A. Grant only the roles/storage.objectCreator IAM role to the application's service account. Remove all other
admin permissions from the bucket.
B. Use Storage Transfer Service to create a daily replication job that synchronizes the bucket to a separate
archive bucket in another region.

C. Enable Object Versioning on the bucket. Additionally, configure the bucket with a Bucket Lock and a
retention policy of seven years.
D. Configure an Object Lifecycle Management rule on the bucket to ensure all objects are retained for at least
seven years before they can be deleted.
Answer: C
Explanation:
Correct Answer: C
This solution satisfies both requirements using native Cloud Storage features:
-Object Versioning (Requirement 1): When versioning is enabled, deleting an object does not actually remove
the data; it simply creates a "delete marker" as the current version. The previous version remains available for
rapid recovery. Similarly, overwriting an object creates a new version while preserving the old one.
-Retention Policy & Bucket Lock (Requirement 2): A retention policy defines how long objects must be kept.
Bucket Lock allows you to permanently "lock" that policy. Once locked, the policy cannot be shortened or
removed, and the objects covered by it cannot be deleted or modified by anyone (including Project Owners or
Google Cloud Admins) until the seven-year period has passed. This is a critical requirement for SEC/FINRA
compliance in financial services.
Why Other Options Are Incorrect:
Option A is Incorrect:
While restricting IAM roles follows the principle of least privilege, it does not provide a technical safeguard
against accidental deletion or malicious actions by someone who does have admin access (or a compromised
admin credential). IAM can be changed; a Locked Bucket Policy cannot.
Option B is Incorrect:
A daily replication job provides a "backup," but it is not "rapid recovery." If a document is deleted, you might
have to wait up to 24 hours for the next sync or perform a manual restore. Furthermore, replication alone does
not prevent a user with sufficient permissions from deleting the document in both locations.
Option D is Incorrect:
Object Lifecycle Management (OLM) is used for automated cost-optimization (e.g., deleting data after X days
or moving it to Archive storage). OLM is not a security or immutability feature. A user with delete permissions
can still delete an object manually before the OLM rule triggers. It does not meet the "impossible to delete"
legal requirement.
Question: 373 CertyIQ
Your company has hired an external auditing firm to perform a compliance audit. Your company’s governance
policy requires that external auditors be managed in a single Google Group that is granted temporary, read-only
access to a Cloud Storage bucket named audit-evidence-bucket. Access must be traceable to the individual
auditor’s identity and be active only for the duration of the audit engagement, which runs the entire month of
October. You need a secure access control strategy that avoids administrative overhead and complies with your
company’s governance policy. What should you do?
A. Create a service account, and grant it the roles/storage.objectViewer role on the bucket. Generate and share
Signed URLs for each object in the bucket with an expiration date of November 1.
B. Apply an IAM policy binding that grants the roles/storage.objectViewer role to the Google Group. Configure
this binding with a time-based IAM Condition that automatically grants access from October 1 to November 1.

C. Use Workforce Identity Federation to map the auditors’ group to the Google Group. Bind the
roles/storage.objectViewer role to this Google Group. Configure a 1-month session duration on the provider.
D. Use Cloud Scheduler to run a Cloud Run functions script that adds the IAM binding of
roles/storage.objectViewer to the Google Group on October 1 and another that removes the IAM binding on
November 1.
Answer: B
Explanation:
Correct Answer: B
The most secure and efficient way to handle temporary, group-based access in Google Cloud is through IAM
Conditions.
Option B is the correct choice because:
-Individual Traceability: By granting the role to a Google Group, each auditor logs in with their own corporate
identity. Cloud Audit Logs will show exactly which individual accessed which file, satisfying the "traceable to
individual identity" requirement.
-Automatic Expiration: Time-based IAM Conditions allow you to set a request.time attribute. Access will
automatically become active on October 1 and instantly expire on November 1 without any manual
intervention.
-Governance Compliance: This avoids the risk of "orphaned permissions" where an administrator forgets to
remove access after the audit is over.
Why Other Options Are Incorrect:
Option A is Incorrect:
Signed URLs provide access to anyone who has the link, which fails the requirement for individual traceability.
Audit logs would only show that the Service Account accessed the data, not which specific auditor was behind
the request. Additionally, generating links for every object in a bucket is a high administrative overhead.
Option C is Incorrect:
While Workforce Identity Federation is great for large-scale external identity integration (like OIDC/SAML), it
is overkill for a simple one-month audit involving a single Google Group. Furthermore, session duration only
controls how long a user stays logged in; it doesn't automatically manage the start and end dates of the
permissions themselves as effectively as IAM Conditions.
Option D is Incorrect:
Using Cloud Run functions and Cloud Scheduler to add/remove roles is a "brute force" automation approach.
It introduces unnecessary complexity and moving parts (scripts, service accounts, triggers). If the script fails
to run on November 1, the auditors would retain access indefinitely, creating a security risk. IAM Conditions
(Option B) are a native, declarative feature that is much more reliable.
Question: 374 CertyIQ
You are deploying a highly confidential data processing workload on Google Cloud. Your company’s compliance
framework mandates that cryptographic keys used for encrypting data at rest must be generated and stored
exclusively within a validated Hardware Security Module (HSM). You want to use a fully integrated Google Cloud
managed service to handle the lifecycle and usage of these keys.What should you do?

A. Create a new key in Cloud Key Management Service (Cloud KMS) with the HSM protection level.
B. Use Customer-Supplied Encryption Keys (CSEK) by providing your on-premises generated key with each API
request.
C. Import your on-premises HSM key material into a Cloud KMS key with the SOFTWARE protection level.
D. Configure Cloud External Key Manager (Cloud EKM) to connect to your on-premises HSM.
Answer: A
Explanation:
Correct Answer: A
This question focuses on the requirements for Hardware Security Modules (HSM) and a fully integrated
managed service.
Cloud HSM is a cloud-hosted hardware security module service that allows you to host encryption keys and
perform cryptographic operations in FIPS 140-2 Level 3 certified HSMs. By selecting the HSM protection level
within Cloud KMS, Google manages the HSM cluster for you, but the keys are generated and stay within the
hardware. This satisfies the compliance mandate while providing the "fully integrated" experience where
other GCP services (like BigQuery, Compute Engine, or Cloud Storage) can easily use the key via Service
Accounts.
Why Other Options Are Incorrect:
Option B is Incorrect:
Customer-Supplied Encryption Keys (CSEK) require you to manage the keys yourself and provide them with
every single API call (e.g., to create a disk or bucket). This is not a "fully integrated managed service" for the
key lifecycle; it places a heavy administrative and technical burden on your team to store and pass the keys
securely.
Option C is Incorrect:
The SOFTWARE protection level stores keys in software on Google’s infrastructure. Even if the material
originated from an on-premises HSM, once imported into a SOFTWARE-backed key, it no longer resides in a
validated HSM in the cloud. This would likely violate the "exclusively within a validated HSM" mandate.
Option D is Incorrect:
Cloud EKM allows you to keep your keys on an external manager (outside of Google Cloud). While this
provides maximum control, it is often used for specific "sovereignty" use cases. The prompt asks for a "fully
integrated Google Cloud managed service." Using EKM adds significant network latency and depends on the
availability of your external HSM, making it less integrated than the native Cloud HSM (Option A).
Question: 375 CertyIQ
You are designing a new insurance claims processing application that will be deployed on Google Kubernetes
Engine (GKE). Your company's compliance team requires a complete and non-repudiable audit trail for all
administrative actions from day one. Your application must capture who deploys a new container image, who
modifies the GKE cluster's configuration, and who interacts with running pods or Kubernetes secrets using
kubectl. What should you do?
A. Activate the Security Command Center Premium tier to analyze GKE logs and detect threats, vulnerabilities,
and misconfigurations in real time.
B. Deploy a DaemonSet to every node in the GKE cluster that runs a logging agent to collect and forward all
container logs to Cloud Logging.

C. Enable Binary Authorization on the GKE cluster, and create a policy that requires all deployed container
images to be signed by a trusted attestor.
D. Enable GKE Audit Logging to send Kubernetes API server logs to Cloud Logging, and ensure Cloud Audit
Logs are enabled for the project.
Answer: D
Explanation:
Correct Answer: D
To achieve a "non-repudiable audit trail" of administrative actions in Kubernetes, you must capture the calls
made to the Kubernetes API server.
Option D is the correct solution because GKE Audit Logging (which is part of Cloud Audit Logs) records all
interactions with the cluster's API. This includes:
-Administrative Activity: Who updated cluster settings or created/deleted nodes.
-Data Access: Interactions with Kubernetes objects, such as viewing or modifying Secrets and ConfigMaps.
-Workload Management: Identifying who initiated a kubectl apply or kubectl exec command.
Cloud Audit Logs are immutable and stored securely in Cloud Logging, providing the "non-repudiable"
evidence required by the insurance company's compliance team.
Why Other Options Are Incorrect:
Option A is Incorrect:
While Security Command Center (SCC) is a powerful security management and risk platform, its primary role
is to detect threats and vulnerabilities. It consumes logs to find issues, but it is not the primary mechanism for
creating the audit trail itself. You first need the logs (Option D) before SCC can analyze them.
Option B is Incorrect:
A DaemonSet for container logs captures what the application outputs (standard out/error). It does not
capture administrative actions taken by users against the Kubernetes API. For example, if a developer runs
kubectl delete pod, that action would never show up in the application logs collected by a DaemonSet.
Option C is Incorrect:
Binary Authorization is a deploy-time security control. It ensures that only "vetted" and "signed" images are
allowed to run in your cluster. While it provides excellent security for the supply chain, it does not provide an
audit trail of other administrative actions, such as modifying secrets or cluster configurations.
Question: 376 CertyIQ
Your company is building containerized applications as part of their CI/CD pipeline. To improve the security and
maintainability of the build process, you need to:1. Identify potential vulnerabilities within your container images.2.
Generate verifiable metadata about the builds for auditing and compliance.3. Create a comprehensive inventory of
your application's dependencies.
What should you do?
A. Use Cloud Build to build container images, trigger Binary Authorization, and use Security Command Center
for tracking and analysis.
B. Use Cloud Build to build container images, trigger Binary Authorization, and use Cloud Asset Inventory for
tracking and analysis.

C. Use Cloud Build to build container images, and then trigger Artifact Analysis on images pushed to Artifact
Registry.
D. Use Cloud Build to build container images, push the images to Artifact Registry, and use Security Command
Center for tracking and analysis.
Answer: C
Explanation:
Correct Answer: C
To secure a container supply chain, you need a tool that looks inside the images and records their history.
Artifact Analysis is the dedicated Google Cloud service for this purpose.
Option C is the correct choice because Artifact Analysis provides three key capabilities required by the
prompt:
-Vulnerability Scanning: It automatically scans container images for known vulnerabilities (CVEs) when they
are pushed to Artifact Registry.
-Verifiable Metadata: It stores Attestations and build provenance, which are essential for compliance and can
be used by Binary Authorization later to decide if an image is "safe" to deploy.
-Dependency Inventory (SBOM): It can generate a Software Bill of Materials (SBOM), which is the
comprehensive inventory of all packages and dependencies used to build your application.
Why Other Options Are Incorrect:
Option A and B are Incorrect:
Binary Authorization is a tool that enforces a policy at deploy-time (e.g., "Don't deploy unless scanned").
However, it does not perform the actual vulnerability scanning or dependency inventory itself. It relies on the
metadata generated by Artifact Analysis to make its decisions. Using Binary Authorization without Artifact
Analysis would not satisfy the requirement to "identify potential vulnerabilities."
Option D is Incorrect:
While Security Command Center (SCC) can display security findings, it is a management and reporting layer. It
does not natively perform deep-dive container image scanning or generate SBOMs. Artifact Registry
combined with Artifact Analysis is the standard architecture for the "build and scan" phase of a CI/CD
pipeline.
Question: 377 CertyIQ
Your ecommerce platform uses a regional Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL database to store critical order information.
The business requests a recovery time objective (RTO) of less than 10 minutes and a recovery point objective (RPO)
of 2 minutes to ensure business continuity in the event of a full regional outage. You need to design a disaster
recovery strategy for the Cloud SQL database that meets the business's strict RTO and RPO requirements. Your
design must avoid operational complexity. What should you do?
A. Configure the primary instance for high availability (HA). In the event of a regional outage, trigger a failover
to the standby instance.
B. Configure the Cloud SQL instance with a cross-region read replica. In a disaster, promote the read replica to
a standalone, primary instance.
C. Use Database Migration Service to continuously replicate the database to another instance in a different
region. In a disaster, redirect application traffic to the replica.
D. Schedule hourly automated backups of the Cloud SQL instance to a multi-regional Cloud Storage bucket. In

a disaster, restore the latest backup to a new instance in a different region.
Answer: B
Explanation:
Correct Answer: B
This question tests your understanding of Disaster Recovery (DR) versus High Availability (HA) in Google
Cloud.
-Surviving a Regional Outage: To survive the loss of an entire region, your data and compute must exist in a
different region.
-Meeting RPO (2 mins): Cross-region read replicas in Cloud SQL use asynchronous replication. Under normal
conditions, the replication lag is typically seconds, which easily satisfies the 2-minute RPO requirement.
-Meeting RTO (10 mins): "Promoting" a read replica is a single-click or single-command operation. It makes
the replica a standalone, writable database. This process usually takes only a few minutes, meeting the 10-
minute RTO goal.
-Operational Complexity: This is a native feature of Cloud SQL. It requires no custom scripts, external tools,
or complex pipeline management.
Why Other Options Are Incorrect:
Option A is Incorrect:
Cloud SQL High Availability (HA) protects against a zonal failure, not a regional failure. In an HA configuration,
the standby instance is located in a different zone within the same region. If the entire region goes offline,
both the primary and standby instances will be unavailable.
Option C is Incorrect:
Database Migration Service (DMS) is intended for migrating workloads into Google Cloud or between
instances during a transition period. While it can perform continuous replication, using it as a permanent DR
solution adds significant operational complexity compared to the native cross-region replica feature.
Option D is Incorrect:
Hourly backups fail the RPO requirement. If the region fails 59 minutes after the last backup, you would lose
59 minutes of data, violating the 2-minute RPO. Furthermore, restoring a 500GB+ database from a backup to
a new instance usually takes much longer than 10 minutes, failing the RTO requirement.
Question: 378 CertyIQ
Your product team is building a critical, customer-facing application on Google Cloud. The development team
wants to use Spanner for their database to take advantage of its horizontal scalability and low operational
overhead. However, the FinOps team is concerned about the direct monthly cost of Spanner and proposed using a
self-managed PostgreSQL database on Compute Engine VMs instead. You need to resolve this conflict and ensure
the project moves forward with an architecturally sound database choice that balances technical requirements
with financial constraints. What should you do?
A. Cite the reliability and performance optimization pillars of the Google Cloud Well-Architected Framework to
formally justify the use of Spanner.
B. Develop a total cost of ownership (TCO) analysis that includes operational overhead, and present it in a
workshop to facilitate a decision.

C. Suggest using Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL as a compromise to get a managed service at a lower cost than
Spanner.
D. Provide the development team with a reference architecture for deploying a highly available PostgreSQL
cluster on a regional managed instance group (MIG).
Answer: B
Explanation:
Correct Answer: B
This question addresses a core responsibility of a Cloud Architect: stakeholder management and financial
justification. While Spanner's monthly instance cost might be higher than Compute Engine VMs, a Total Cost
of Ownership (TCO) analysis reveals the hidden costs of a self-managed database, such as:
-Engineering Time: The cost of salary for SREs/DBAs to manage backups, patching, scaling, and high-
availability logic.
-Risk Cost: The financial impact of potential downtime or data loss if the self-managed HA logic fails.
-Opportunity Cost: The value lost when engineers spend time on infrastructure maintenance instead of
building new customer features.
Presenting this data in a collaborative workshop helps the FinOps team see the "big picture" (OpEx vs. CapEx)
and allows the team to make a data-driven decision that satisfies both technical and financial stakeholders.
Why Other Options Are Incorrect:
Option A is Incorrect:
While quoting the Well-Architected Framework provides strong theoretical backing, it rarely solves a financial
dispute. The FinOps team is concerned with the bottom line; they need numbers and financial justification, not
just architectural best practices.
Option C is Incorrect:
While Cloud SQL is a managed service, it does not offer the same "horizontal scalability" (especially for
writes) as Spanner. If the application truly requires Spanner's specific technical capabilities, suggesting Cloud
SQL is a technical downgrade that might lead to performance issues later, rather than resolving the root
conflict of how to justify the cost.
Option D is Incorrect:
Providing a reference architecture for PostgreSQL on MIGs effectively sides with the FinOps team without
addressing the development team's concerns about "low operational overhead." It increases the long-term
maintenance burden on the team and ignores the TCO benefits of using a fully managed service like Spanner.
Question: 379 CertyIQ
You need to build a continuous delivery pipeline for a containerized application in Google Cloud. You want to run all
your tests in the pipeline to improve your application's quality. What should you do?
A. *1. After the developers push the code to a central repository, trigger Cloud Build to run unit tests. If all tests
are successful, build the application container and push it to a central registry. *2. Trigger Cloud Build to deploy
the container to a testing environment and run integration tests and acceptance tests. *3. If all tests are
successful, deploy the application to the production environment and run the smoke tests.
B. *1. Run unit tests in the developer's local environment before committing and pushing the code to a central

repository. *2. After the code is pushed, trigger Cloud Build to build the application container and deploy the
container to a testing environment, and run integration tests. *3. If the integration tests are successful, deploy
the container to your production environment and run acceptance tests.
C. *1. After the developers push the code to a central repository, trigger Cloud Build to build the application
container. Then run unit tests. *2. If unit tests are successful, deploy the container to a testing environment and
run integration tests. *3. If the integration tests are successful, deploy the container to a production
environment and run acceptance tests.
D. *1. Automatically run unit tests in a local environment once the code is changed. If all tests are successful,
build a container. *2. Trigger Cloud Build to deploy the container to a testing environment, and run integration
tests and acceptance tests. *3. If all tests are successful, tag the code as production ready. Trigger Cloud Build
to build and deploy the container to the production environment.
Answer: A
Explanation:
Correct Answer: A
This option represents the industry standard for a CI/CD pipeline and follows Google-recommended best
practices for "Shift-Left" testing and automation.
-Unit Tests first: Running unit tests on the raw source code before building the container saves compute
resources and time. If the logic is broken, there is no point in waiting for a container build.
-Artifact Promotion: Once the container is built, it is pushed to a registry. From there, the same immutable
artifact is deployed to testing.
-Increasing Fidelity: Integration and Acceptance tests are run in a "Testing" environment that mimics
production.
-Production Verification: Running "Smoke Tests" after the final production deployment is critical to ensure
that the environment configuration (networking, secrets, etc.) is correct and the application is actually
reachable.
Why Other Options Are Incorrect:
Option B is Incorrect:
Relying solely on local environment tests is dangerous because "it works on my machine" does not guarantee
it will work in the build server's environment. Furthermore, Acceptance Tests should be performed in a
staging/test environment, not directly in production.
Option C is Incorrect:
Building the container before running unit tests is inefficient. Unit tests should validate the code first.
Additionally, like Option B, it suggests running Acceptance Tests in production, which is a high-risk strategy
that could impact real users.
Option D is Incorrect:
This suggests building the container locally or in an unmanaged way before the pipeline starts. It also involves
"re-building" the container for production after tagging the code, which violates the principle of immutability.
You should build the artifact once, test it, and promote that exact same image to production.
Question: 380 CertyIQ
You are planning to migrate your on-premises compute and SAP workloads to Google Cloud. You want to follow
Google-recommended practices to quickly create a cost estimate for running these workloads in Google Cloud.

What should you do?
A. Leverage Cloud Asset Inventory to gather data and generate a cost estimate.
B. Gather data about your current environment, and leverage Google Cloud Migration Center to generate a cost
estimate.
C. Engage with a Google Cloud partner to perform a comprehensive assessment and provide a customized cost
estimate.
D. Use the Google Cloud pricing calculator, and input the estimated resource to generate a cost estimate.
Answer: B
Explanation:
Correct Answer: B
Google Cloud Migration Center is the unified platform for end-to-end migration. It is specifically designed to
handle SAP Cost Estimation alongside general compute workloads.
Option B is the best choice because:
-Specialized Calculators: Migration Center includes a dedicated SAP Cost Estimator that allows you to select
predefined sizes for common SAP applications (like S/4HANA or BW/4HANA) and toggle high-availability
(HA) or disaster recovery (DR) options.
-Integrated Discovery: It allows you to gather data from your on-premises environment (via a discovery client
or file upload) and translate that directly into a Google Cloud Bill of Materials (BoM).
-Speed vs. Accuracy: It provides a "Rapid Cost Estimate" feature that uses default assumptions based on best
practices, allowing for a quick initial budget projection before a deeper assessment.
Why Other Options Are Incorrect:
Option A is Incorrect: Cloud Asset Inventory is a tool for viewing and monitoring resources that already exist
within your Google Cloud organization. It is not designed to discover on-premises environments or provide
migration cost estimates for workloads that haven't moved yet.
Option C is Incorrect: While engaging a partner is a valid strategy for the execution of a migration, the
question asks how you can quickly create a cost estimate following Google-recommended practices. Google
provides the self-service Migration Center precisely to allow customers to perform these initial estimates
independently and quickly.
Option D is Incorrect: The standard Google Cloud Pricing Calculator is excellent for pricing specific, known
resources. However, for a complex migration involving SAP and interconnected compute nodes, the Pricing
Calculator is a manual and error-prone process. It doesn't offer the automated sizing or specialized SAP
application logic found in the Migration Center.
Question: 381 CertyIQ
You are deploying a critical application with a stateless, containerized frontend on Cloud Run and a Cloud SQL for
PostgreSQL backend. The application experiences unpredictable traffic spikes, and the business requires the
ability to immediately roll back a failed deployment to the last known good state. You need to apply a deployment
strategy that aligns with Site Reliability Engineering (SRE) principles for both the application code and the
database schema updates, while meeting the business's requirements. What should you do?
A. Package the database schema migration script within the container to be executed on every container
startup before the application process begins.

B. Configure the CI/CD pipeline to use the :latest container tag for deployments, with database schema
changes applied manually as needed.
C. Separate CI/CD pipelines for database schema migrations from application deployments. When deploying a
new Cloud Run revision, use gradual traffic split.
D. Use a single CI/CD pipeline that first applies database schema changes and then deploys the new Cloud Run
revision.
Answer: C
Explanation:
Correct Answer: C
This approach follows the SRE principle of decoupling and backward compatibility:
-Decoupling Migrations: By separating the pipelines, you ensure that database changes (which are harder to
roll back) are handled independently. The gold standard is to ensure that database changes are always
backward compatible (e.g., adding a nullable column rather than renaming one).
-Gradual Traffic Splitting (Canary): Cloud Run revisions allow you to send 5% or 10% of traffic to a new
version. If errors spike, you can instantly shift 100% of traffic back to the "last known good" revision.
-Rollback Requirement: Because the schema was designed to be backward compatible with the previous
code version, rolling back the Cloud Run revision works immediately without needing to "undo" the database
migration, which is often a slow and risky process.
Why Other Options Are Incorrect:
Option A is Incorrect:
Running migrations on every container startup is a dangerous anti-pattern. If Cloud Run scales up to 100
instances during a traffic spike, 100 instances will attempt to run the migration simultaneously, leading to
database locks or corruption.
Option B is Incorrect:
Using the :latest tag is a major risk. It makes it impossible to know exactly what code is running in production
and prevents "immediate rollback" to a specific, immutable previous image hash. Manual schema changes are
also error-prone and slow.
Option D is Incorrect:
A single synchronous pipeline creates a "all-or-nothing" failure point. If the schema changes and then the
Cloud Run deployment fails, the old version of the code might be incompatible with the new schema, making
an "immediate rollback" impossible without also rolling back the database.
Question: 382 CertyIQ
You are designing a central, automated infrastructure deployment process for your organization using Terraform
and Cloud Build. The security team prohibits the use of long-lived, static service account keys in any CI/CD
pipeline. Additionally, while developers can propose infrastructure changes for peer review, they must not have
permissions to directly apply changes in the production project. You need to design a secure and automated
workflow for applying Terraform changes that meets the security team’s requirements and ensures proper
governance. What should you do?
A. Configure the pipeline to only run terraform plan. After a pull request is approved, have an authorized
developer run terraform apply from a secured workstation.

B. Create a privileged service account and store its JSON key in Secret Manager. Configure the Cloud Build
pipeline to fetch this key during execution to authenticate Terraform.
C. Configure the Cloud Build pipeline to use service account impersonation. Set up a trigger that automatically
runs terraform apply when a pull request is merged.
D. Use service account impersonation in Cloud Build. Configure the pipeline to run terraform plan on pull
requests, and require manual approval before running terraform apply.
Answer: D
Explanation:
Correct Answer: D
This solution leverages Google Cloud native security and automation features to satisfy all constraints:
-Service Account Impersonation: By using impersonation (Workload Identity or the
iam.serviceAccounts.getAccessToken permission), the Cloud Build worker identity assumes the role of a
highly privileged Terraform service account only during execution. This eliminates the need for long-lived
JSON keys, satisfying the security team's primary mandate.
-Infrastructure as Code (IaC) Governance: Running terraform plan on the pull request allows developers to
propose changes and see the impact without having the permissions to execute them.
-Manual Approvals: Cloud Build supports Approval Gates. By requiring a manual approval before the apply
step, you ensure a "second pair of eyes" (governance) and prevent developers from bypassing the production
deployment gate, even though the pipeline is automated.
Why Other Options Are Incorrect:
Option A is Incorrect:
While this prevents developers from applying changes, it breaks the "automated infrastructure deployment
process" requirement. Relying on "secured workstations" for manual applies introduces human error and a
"snowflake" environment where deployments aren't reproducible or logged centrally.
Option B is Incorrect:
Storing a key in Secret Manager is better than hardcoding it, but it is still a long-lived, static service account
key. The security team explicitly prohibited the use of such keys in any CI/CD pipeline.
Option C is Incorrect:
This configuration uses a "merge-to-master" automation. While it uses impersonation, it lacks the governance
required. If a merge automatically triggers an apply, a developer with merge rights could push changes to
production without a formal deployment approval step, violating the company's separation of duties.
Question: 383 CertyIQ
You created a Cloud Monitoring dashboard for your dev environment to view real-time application metrics. The Ops
team wants a similar dashboard to monitor the production environment. You want to minimize time and effort as
you assist the Ops team with their dashboard request. What should you do?
A. Export your dashboard to a JSON file and share it with the Ops team.
B. Grant viewer permission to the Ops team in your project so they can recreate the charts in their dashboard
from the console.
C. Create a shared document and list every step you performed to create the dashboard. The Ops team can use
the document to build their own dashboard.

D. Share your dashboard and send the shared dashboard URL to the Ops team.
Answer: A
Explanation:
Correct Answer: A
In Google Cloud Monitoring, dashboards are defined internally as JSON structures.
Option A is the most efficient and standard practice for "Dashboard as Code." By exporting the JSON:
-Speed: The Ops team can simply "Import JSON" in their production project, and the entire layout, widget
configuration, and metric queries will be instantly recreated.
-Accuracy: It eliminates human error that would occur if the team tried to manually copy settings from a
document or a different project.
-Scalability: The JSON can be stored in a version control system (like Git) to manage dashboard versions
across different environments (Dev, Staging, Prod).
Why Other Options Are Incorrect:
Option B is Incorrect:
This requires the Ops team to perform significant manual work. They would have to switch back and forth
between projects and manually configure every single chart, which is the opposite of "minimizing time and
effort."
Option C is Incorrect:
Writing a manual "how-to" guide is extremely time-consuming for you and labor-intensive for the Ops team. It
is an outdated approach to infrastructure management that lacks the precision of a direct configuration
export.
Option D is Incorrect:
Sharing a URL only allows the Ops team to view your Dev dashboard. It does not help them create a separate
dashboard for the Production environment. Additionally, dashboards are scoped to a project; a Dev dashboard
cannot simply be "pointed" to Production data via a URL without recreating the underlying queries in the
Production project.
Question: 384 CertyIQ
You are designing the observability strategy for a new microservices application running on Google Kubernetes
Engine (GKE). The application consists of multiple services (e.g., frontend, orders, payments). During load testing,
you observe an error in the frontend service's logs, but you cannot find the corresponding logs in the downstream
services to investigate the root cause because the logs are not correlated. You need to implement a solution that
allows you to follow a single user request across all microservices involved in the transaction. The solution must
not require developers to manually add correlation logic to their application code. What should you do?
A. Configure all containers to write logs to STDOUT/STDERR, and then filter logs by pod name and timestamp
in the Logs Explorer.
B. Require developers to generate a unique correlation-id at the frontend, and manually add it as a field to every
log message in all services.
C. Create custom metrics in Cloud Monitoring for error counts in each service and correlate incident spikes
using a shared dashboard.

D. Implement Cloud Trace by ensuring the traceparent header is propagated between microservice calls to link
logs to a single trace.
Answer: D
Explanation:
Correct Answer: D
To trace a single request through a complex distributed system without manual code changes, you must use
Distributed Tracing.
Option D is the correct solution because:
-Distributed Tracing (Cloud Trace): This service is specifically built to follow requests as they "hop" from one
microservice to another. It uses a unique Trace ID that represents the entire journey.
-Context Propagation (traceparent): Modern observability standards (like W3C Trace Context) use the
traceparent HTTP header to pass the Trace ID from service to service.
-Automatic Instrumentation: Many Google Cloud tools and OpenTelemetry libraries support Auto-
Instrumentation. By simply adding an agent or using a Service Mesh (like Anthos/Cloud Service Mesh), the
traceparent headers are handled automatically, satisfying the "no manual correlation logic" requirement.
-Log Integration: Cloud Logging and Cloud Trace are natively integrated. If a log entry contains the Trace ID,
you can click a "View Trace" button in the Logs Explorer to see the exact waterfall graph of that specific
request.
Why Other Options Are Incorrect:
Option A is Incorrect:
Filtering by timestamp and pod name is a manual, "best-effort" approach. In a high-traffic environment with
thousands of requests per second, thousands of logs will have the same timestamp, making it impossible to
isolate which specific log in the orders service belongs to which specific error in the frontend.
Option B is Incorrect:
This specifically violates the requirement to "not require developers to manually add correlation logic."
Manually managing correlation IDs is error-prone and creates a significant maintenance burden.
Option C is Incorrect:
Metrics are aggregated data (e.g., "how many errors happened"). They are excellent for identifying when a
problem occurred, but they cannot tell you which specific user request failed or follow the "transactional
journey" of a single request.
Question: 385 CertyIQ
Your organization has a significant amount of log data stored in Cloud Logging. The data engineering team is
accustomed to using SQL for analysis and wants the ability to create insightful dashboards for visualizing log
trends and patterns. You want to follow the recommendations of the Google Cloud Well-Architected Framework to
provide a solution for the data engineering team. What should you do?
A. Enable log analytics and run queries in Cloud Monitoring. Visualize the data using Vertex AI workbench.
B. Create a log sink, and export the data to a storage bucket. Create an external table in BigQuery for the data
in the bucket. Run queries and visualize the data with Cloud Monitoring dashboards.

C. Enable log analytics and run queries in the linked log dataset in BigQuery. Visualize the data with Looker
Studio dashboards.
D. Create a log sink, and export the data to BigQuery using Pub/Sub. Run queries and visualize the data with
Cloud Monitoring dashboards.
Answer: C
Explanation:
Correct Answer: C
The Google Cloud Well-Architected Framework recommends using Log Analytics for complex analysis and
visualization.
-Log Analytics & BigQuery Link: By upgrading a log bucket to use Log Analytics and creating a BigQuery
Linked Dataset, you allow your data engineering team to query live logs directly using standard SQL in the
BigQuery interface. This avoids the operational overhead of managing export sinks or ETL pipelines.
-SQL Compatibility: Data engineers can use their existing SQL skills to perform joins, aggregations, and
complex pattern matching on log data.
-Visualization: Looker Studio is the recommended tool for creating "insightful dashboards" from BigQuery
data. It offers superior visualization capabilities compared to standard Cloud Monitoring charts for business
intelligence (BI) and trend analysis.
Why Other Options Are Incorrect:
Option A is Incorrect:
While Log Analytics allows SQL-like queries within the Logs Explorer, it does not natively visualize those
queries in Vertex AI Workbench. Vertex AI is for ML/Data Science and adds unnecessary complexity for
standard log visualization.
Option B is Incorrect:
Exporting to a storage bucket and creating an external table is a legacy approach. It introduces latency (data
is not analyzed in real-time) and higher management overhead for schema definition and bucket maintenance.
Option D is Incorrect:
Exporting through Pub/Sub to BigQuery is useful for real-time streaming to custom applications, but it is over-
engineered for standard dashboarding. Additionally, Cloud Monitoring dashboards are limited in their ability
to visualize arbitrary SQL result sets from BigQuery.
